sections.heading: Introduction
sections.text: 27. Backup, WAL & Recovery This page explains how EvolutionDB keeps your data safe when a crash happens. It covers the full-page-image write-ahead log, the checkpoint that recycles it, two-pass replay on restart, point-in-time recovery from the WAL archive, and the base backup that clones a live database. Durability in EvolutionDB rests on one mechanism. It is a full-page-image (FPI) write-ahead log built in evolution/db/wal.c. Before any changed 4 KB page reaches the data file evosql.db, a full image of that page is added to the log file evosql.wal and flushed. If the process dies between the log write and the data write, the next start replays the logged images. No page is ever left torn. Everything else in this chapter is built on top of that one log. That includes checkpoints, recovery, the time-travel archive, and base backups. A note on terminology. The SQL keyword CHECKPOINT in EvolutionDB belongs to the LangGraph checkpoint store feature. See CHECKPOINT and CREATE CHECKPOINT STORE. It has nothing to do with the WAL checkpoint described here. The WAL checkpoint is an internal engine operation. No SQL statement triggers it. When this chapter says “checkpoint” on its own, it means the WAL checkpoint.

sections.heading: 27.1. The Write-Ahead Log
sections.text: The WAL lives in one sequential file named evosql.wal. It is created next to the data file. The page manager and buffer pool manage it fully. It is not a directory of segments. It begins with a fixed 16-byte header. After the header comes a stream of variable-length records. FieldSizeMeaning magic4 B0x57414C45, which means “WALE”. A header without it is treated as corrupt and reset. version2 BThe WAL format version. It is 1 right now. reserved2 BPadding. checkpoint_lsn4 BEvery page up to this LSN is known to be on disk in the data file. Replay skips records at or below it. next_lsn4 BThe next log sequence number to assign. It starts at 1 for a fresh log. Each record after the header is a full page image. It is wrapped in an 18-byte header and a CRC at the end. [ lsn:4B ][ page_no:4B ][ page_len:2B ][ timestamp:8B ][ page image:page_len ][ crc32:4B ] \________________ 18-byte record header _________________/ lsn A log sequence number that always increases. It is assigned under the WAL mutex when the record is written. page_no The data-file page this image belongs to. Values 0xFFFFFFFC–0xFFFFFFFE are reserved sentinels for distributed-transaction records. They are not real pages. See §27.7. page_len The number of bytes in the page payload. It is always 1–4096. A record that claims 0 or more than EVO_PAGE_SIZE ends replay. timestamp Microseconds since the Unix epoch. It is captured with gettimeofday() at log time. This is what makes the WAL archive replayable to a point in time. See §27.5. crc32 An IEEE CRC-32 over the 18-byte header plus the page image. It uses the zlib and gzip polynomial. Replay recomputes it and compares it. If it does not match, replay stops at that record. A partly written tail record is never applied. EvolutionDB logs whole pages, not logical row operations. There is no redo or undo record type. There is no per-tuple delta. There is no separate logical record stream in the WAL itself. The unit of recovery is the 4 KB page. This makes replay safe to repeat. Re-applying a page image does no harm. The cost is a larger log than a delta-based design would produce. Contrast with PostgreSQL. PostgreSQL writes typed redo records. It only writes a full-page image for the first change to a page after a checkpoint. The rest are deltas. EvolutionDB always logs the full page. PostgreSQL stores the WAL as a directory of 16 MB segment files. Their names come from the LSN. EvolutionDB uses one growing evosql.wal file. That file is cut back to its 16-byte header at each checkpoint.

sections.heading: 27.2. The Write-Ahead Rule & Commit Path
sections.text: The core rule is simple. A page's WAL record must be durable before that page is allowed to reach the data file. EvolutionDB applies this rule at commit time, not on every page write. It does this by holding back both the data-page flush and the WAL fsync until a transaction commits. An auto-commit statement or an explicit COMMIT may succeed. When it does, the server calls bp_wal_flush_dirty() in the buffer pool. This happens before the transaction is marked committed in the commit log. The steps are below. Walk the set of pages this transaction made dirty. A small array tracks them. If that array overflows, it falls back to a full buffer-pool scan. This is rare. For each dirty page, add its current image to the WAL with wal_log_page(). Run a single wal_fsync() that flushes all of those records at once. Only then assign a commit sequence number and record the commit. Batching the fsync to one flush per commit instead of one per page is the key performance choice. The source notes it is roughly 100x faster than a per-page sync. The trade-off is that the durability boundary is the commit. If the server crashes after COMMIT returns, the WAL holds the images and recovery restores them. If it crashes mid-transaction, the un-flushed dirty pages are lost and the partial work never reaches disk. Durability is at commit, not at statement. Because the WAL fsync is deferred to commit, a statement inside an open transaction is not durable until you COMMIT (see transaction control). This matches the SQL standard's notion of transactional durability and PostgreSQL's default behavior. EvolutionDB has no per-statement or asynchronous-commit knob to change it.

sections.heading: 27.3. WAL Checkpoints
sections.text: A checkpoint is the operation that lets the WAL be recycled. It declares that everything logged so far is now safely in the data file, so the log can be thrown away. wal_checkpoint() performs three steps under the WAL lock. Archive. If the active WAL holds any records, they are appended to evosql.wal.archive before being discarded. This archive is what powers point-in-time recovery (§27.5). Without it, checkpointing would permanently erase history. Advance. checkpoint_lsn is moved up to the current write position. Truncate. The active evosql.wal is truncated back to its 16-byte header, then the header is rewritten and fsync'd. Any distributed-transaction PREPARE records that are still live are re-logged into the freshly truncated WAL so prepared transactions survive the checkpoint (see §27.7). The caller is responsible for flushing dirty pages to the data file before calling wal_checkpoint(). The WAL layer itself does not flush the buffer pool. The only place this contract is honored is wal_shutdown(). It runs after pgm_shutdown() has flushed all pages. When does a checkpoint actually happen? In the current engine, a WAL checkpoint runs only at clean shutdown. When the server receives SIGTERM/SIGINT and drains, it flushes the buffer pool and then calls wal_shutdown(). This function checkpoints and closes the log. There is no periodic background checkpointer, no time- or size-triggered checkpoint, and no SQL command to force one. As a consequence, evosql.wal grows for the entire lifetime of a running process and is reclaimed only on a graceful stop. Plan log-disk headroom accordingly for long-lived servers, and prefer clean shutdowns over SIGKILL. Contrast with PostgreSQL. PostgreSQL has a dedicated checkpointer process driven by checkpoint_timeout and max_wal_size, plus a manual CHECKPOINT SQL command. EvolutionDB has none of these tuning knobs. Its checkpoint is an implicit, shutdown-time event.

sections.heading: 27.4. Crash Recovery
sections.text: Recovery is automatic and happens during pgm_init() when the data file is opened. EvolutionDB supports transparent data encryption (TDE). Page 0 (the file header) is always plaintext but data pages are encrypted. Because of this, replay is split into two passes. Pass 1, page 0 only (wal_replay_page0(), inside wal_init()) Replays only logged images of page 0. Page 0 is the file header and is never encrypted. The page manager then re-reads the recovered header. If the database is encrypted, it initializes TDE from the header. This must come first because the keys needed to decrypt data pages live in the header. Pass 2, data pages (wal_replay_remaining() → wal_replay_data()) Runs after TDE is live. Replays every non-page-0 record, encrypting each page on the way to disk when TDE is enabled. On completion it performs a checkpoint (advancing checkpoint_lsn and truncating the WAL). Within each pass the replay loop reads records sequentially from just past the header to end-of-file. For every record it does the following. Reads the record header and validates page_len is in range. If it is out of range, replay stops. This is how a torn tail record is detected. Recomputes the CRC-32 over header+payload and compares it to the stored CRC. On mismatch, replay stops. Skips any record whose lsn ≤ checkpoint_lsn (already on disk). pwrite()s the page image to page_no × 4096 in the data file, encrypting first if TDE is on and the page is not page 0. A clean start (empty WAL) recovers zero pages and proceeds normally. When recovery does apply pages, the server logs the count. [WAL] Initialized (checkpoint_lsn=0, next_lsn=1) [WAL] Pass 2: replayed 12 data page(s) [WAL] Crash recovery: 12 page(s) restored Recovery is redo-only and idempotent. There is no undo phase and no concept of replaying a transaction log up to the last commit. Replay simply re-applies page images that were logged but might not have landed in the data file. Because uncommitted work is never flushed (its WAL records are never fsync'd), there is nothing to roll back. Recovery also cannot be made to stop just before a bad transaction. For selective restore you need the time-travel archive below.

sections.heading: 27.5. Point-in-Time / Time-Travel Recovery
sections.text: Every checkpoint appends the records it is about to discard to evosql.wal.archive. Each record carries a microsecond timestamp. The archive can be replayed up to a chosen instant, restoring the database to how it looked at that time. This is implemented by wal_restore_to_timestamp(). The procedure restores a base snapshot taken before the target time, then replays archived records in order, applying each page image (TDE-encrypting as needed) and stopping at the first record whose timestamp exceeds the target. # Conceptual workflow (engine-internal; see the caveat below) # 1. Put a base copy of evosql.db in place - taken before the target time. # 2. Replay evosql.wal.archive up to target epoch-microseconds. # Records with timestamp <= target are applied; the first one past it halts replay. wal_archive_range() reports the timestamp span available in the archive. It returns the first and last record timestamps. A recovery tool can use this to confirm that the requested target falls inside the retained window before attempting a restore. Limitation: no SQL/operator surface yet. wal_restore_to_timestamp() and wal_archive_range() are implemented engine functions, but in this release nothing calls them. There is no SQL statement, server flag, or CLI command that exposes point-in-time recovery to an operator. The plumbing (timestamped records, the growing evosql.wal.archive) is in place and correct, but driving a restore requires invoking the API directly. Treat PITR as a building block, not a turnkey feature. Also note the archive only ever grows. The RETENTION clause you may see on checkpoint stores does not prune it, and there is no archive-cleanup job. Contrast with PostgreSQL. PostgreSQL drives PITR through recovery_target_time/recovery_target_lsn in the recovery configuration plus a restore_command that fetches archived segments. It can also recover to a transaction id or named restore point. EvolutionDB's archive is timestamp-keyed only, replay is whole-page, and the restore is invoked programmatically rather than through a config-driven recovery mode.

sections.heading: 27.6. Base Backups
sections.text: A base backup is a consistent physical copy of the database. It is produced by a one-shot server mode. Starting the server with --base-backup PATH initializes the engine, clones the data directory to PATH, and exits without binding any listener sockets. It never serves clients (see Server Configuration §23.7). It is the supported way to bootstrap a fresh replica from a primary. # Clone a consistent copy into a directory and exit (0 = success) evosql-server --base-backup /backups/evosql-2026-06-12 Internally repl_create_backup() first pins a consistent point. It flushes the page manager and buffer pool, then fsyncs the WAL, then records the current LSN as the backup's start LSN. It then writes one of two layouts depending on PATH. Directory mode (PATH is or becomes a directory) Produces a small bundle: evosql.db, a byte-for-byte copy of the data file. evosql.wal, a copy of the live WAL when present (best-effort; if it cannot be found the backup still succeeds). BACKUP_LABEL, a tiny text manifest: start_lsn=…, timestamp=… (epoch seconds), and format=v1. A replica uses start_lsn to know where to begin streaming WAL to catch up. Single-file mode (PATH is a plain file) A legacy path that copies just evosql.db to that file, no WAL, no label. # On success the server logs the bundle, its LSN, and the data size: [REPL] Base backup bundle created at /backups/evosql-2026-06-12 (lsn=437, 8388608 data bytes) Backup is physical, file-level, and offline-to-clients. EvolutionDB has no pg_dump-style logical/SQL backup and no streaming pg_basebackup protocol. The backup mechanism is this one-shot copy. The simplest manual alternative is to stop the server cleanly (which checkpoints and truncates the WAL) and copy evosql.db. If you want point-in-time material, also copy evosql.wal.archive using ordinary filesystem tools. Copying evosql.db from a running server without using --base-backup is unsafe, because there is no externally-triggerable checkpoint to quiesce it.

sections.heading: 27.7. WAL & Distributed Transactions
sections.text: The WAL is also where two-phase (XA) commit records are persisted so prepared transactions survive a crash. These are not page images. They reuse the record format with reserved sentinel page_no values and a small payload instead of a 4 KB page. Recordpage_no sentinelPayload XA PREPARE0xFFFFFFFExa_xid + the internal MVCC transaction id XA COMMIT0xFFFFFFFDxa_xid XA ROLLBACK0xFFFFFFFCxa_xid During replay, records with a page_no at or above 0xFFFFFFFC are recognized as XA records and are not written to the data file. They are counted as replayed but handled in memory by the XA subsystem after recovery, reconstructing the set of prepared-but-unresolved transactions. As noted in §27.3, live PREPARE records are also re-logged across a checkpoint so they are not lost when the active WAL is truncated.

sections.heading: 27.8. Quick Reference
sections.text: The files, functions, and constants that make up the backup/WAL/recovery surface. ItemWhat it isValue / location evosql.dbMain data file (4 KB pages; page 0 is the plaintext header)working directory evosql.walActive write-ahead log (single growing file, truncated at checkpoint)working directory evosql.wal.archiveAppend-only archive of pre-checkpoint records (PITR source)working directory WAL magicWAL_MAGIC “WALE”0x57414C45 WAL versionWAL_VERSION1 Page sizeEVO_PAGE_SIZE4096 bytes Record headerWAL_RECORD_HEADER_SIZE (lsn+page_no+page_len+timestamp)18 bytes Record CRCWAL_CRC_SIZE (IEEE CRC-32)4 bytes Commit-time flushbp_wal_flush_dirty() → wal_log_page() → one wal_fsync()buffer_pool.c / wal.c RecoveryTwo-pass wal_init() + wal_replay_remaining()called from pgm_init() Checkpointwal_checkpoint() (archive → advance → truncate)only via wal_shutdown() PITR (API only)wal_restore_to_timestamp(), wal_archive_range()no SQL/CLI surface Base backup--base-backup PATH → repl_create_backup()one-shot; bundle = db + wal + BACKUP_LABEL Notes & limitations Whole-page log. The WAL stores full 4 KB page images, not logical deltas. Recovery is redo-only and idempotent. There is no undo log in the WAL and no transaction-level replay target. Checkpoint only at clean shutdown. No background, timed, or size-triggered checkpointer exists, and there is no SQL command to force one. The active WAL grows for the life of the process and is reclaimed only on graceful stop. Avoid hard kills. Durability is per-commit. The WAL fsync is batched to commit time. Work in an open transaction is not durable until COMMIT. PITR has no operator surface. Time-travel restore is implemented but unexposed. No statement, flag, or tool invokes it in this release. The archive is also never pruned. Backups are physical. Use --base-backup on a running server, or copy files after a clean shutdown. There is no logical (pg_dump-style) export and no streaming basebackup protocol. TDE-aware replay. Recovery and restore re-encrypt data pages on write when the database is encrypted. Page 0 is always handled in plaintext first so keys can be loaded. See also: Server Configuration · Storage & the Buffer Pool · High Availability & Replication · Encryption (TDE) · Transaction control · CHECKPOINT (checkpoint store).

sections.heading: Introduction
sections.text: 23. Server Configuration This page shows how to start and tune evosql-server. It covers the listener ports it opens. It covers the command-line flags and environment variables it reads at boot. It also covers the limits on how many clients it will serve at once. EvolutionDB has one server executable, evosql-server. It is built from adaptor/. It is a dual-protocol server. One process starts two TCP listeners. One is the PostgreSQL wire protocol. The other is the native EVO text protocol. Both share one SQL engine and one data file, evosql.db. You set all configuration when the process starts. You use command-line flags and environment variables. PostgreSQL uses a postgresql.conf file and a reloadable GUC. EvolutionDB has neither. The settings on this page are read once in main(). They apply for the whole life of the process. Precedence. Some settings have both a flag and an environment variable. The server uses one rule for all of them. It reads environment variables first to set defaults. Then command-line flags override them. The flag always wins.

sections.heading: 23.1. Invoking the Server
sections.text: The synopsis below lists every flag the argument parser in adaptor/main.c knows about. evosql-server [--pg-port PORT] [--evo-port PORT] [--buffer-pool-size SIZE] [--replication-port PORT] [--cdc-port PORT] [--replica HOST:PORT] [--cluster node1:port,node2:port,...] [--node-id N] [--dist-port PORT] [--role primary|replica|witness] [--base-backup PATH] [PORT] You can also pass a plain number with no flag. The server reads it as the PG port. This is kept for backward compatibility. # These two are equivalent evosql-server --pg-port 5544 evosql-server 5544 The argument parser is not strict. It ignores any flag it does not know. It does not print a usage message or exit with an error for unknown options. A flag that needs a value is only used when another argument follows it.

sections.heading: 23.1.1. Startup Output
sections.text: On a normal boot the server prints some information. It prints its version, the buffer-pool size, and the TLS status. It prints the two protocol ports and the maximum connection count. It also prints an ASCII banner. Then it starts to accept connections. A typical boot looks like this. EvolutionDB v [BufferPool] 32768 pages (128 MB) [TLS] No EVOSQL_TLS_CERT / EVOSQL_TLS_KEY set - TLS disabled ============================================== EvoSQL Adaptor Server (dual-protocol) PG protocol on port 5433 EVO protocol on port 9967 TLS: disabled Max connections: 100 ============================================== [EVO] Listening on port 9967 [PG] Listening on port 5433 The PG listener runs on the main thread. It blocks forever. The EVO listener runs in a background thread. The distributed-query listener also runs in a background thread when it is configured.

sections.heading: 23.2. Ports and Listeners
sections.text: The server can open up to four kinds of TCP listeners. It always opens the two protocol listeners. It opens the other listeners only when you turn their feature on. ListenerDefault portHow to setOpened when PG wire protocol5433--pg-port (or bare positional number)Always EVO text protocol9967--evo-portAlways Replication / WAL sender9968 *--replication-port / EVOSQL_REPLICATION_PORTPort > 0 CDC streaming (JSON Lines), (off)--cdc-port / EVOSQL_CDC_PORTPort > 0 Distributed query engine9969--dist-port--cluster + --node-id given * 9968 is REPL_DEFAULT_PORT. A replica uses it as the default upstream port when you give only a host. The WAL sender itself starts only when --replication-port or EVOSQL_REPLICATION_PORT is set to a value > 0. You will set the two protocol ports most often. The default PG port is 5433. This is on purpose. It is not PostgreSQL's 5432, so both can run on one host at the same time. Each listener binds with a fixed listen() backlog of 5. # Run two engines on one box without colliding evosql-server --pg-port 5433 --evo-port 9967 evosql-server --pg-port 5443 --evo-port 9977 No bind-address control. EvolutionDB has nothing like listen_addresses. The bind address is fixed in the network layer. You cannot use configuration to limit the server to localhost. You also cannot use it to pick one interface. Use OS firewall rules or container networking to limit who can reach it.

sections.heading: 23.3. Connection Limits and the Worker Pool
sections.text: EvolutionDB sets a hard limit on the number of client connections at one time. The accept loop in server_listen() checks the live count under a mutex before it handles each connection. When the limit is already reached, the new socket is closed right away. The server also logs a rejection line. [PG] Connection rejected: limit 100 reached max_connections The most client connections allowed at one time. This count covers both protocols together. The default is built into the code and is 100. The server prints the active count and the limit on every connect and disconnect. It looks like active: 7/100. There is no startup flag for this value. You reach it through the catalog accessors get_max_connections() and set_max_connections(). The server banner shows it as Max connections. EVOSQL_POOL_SIZE (env) The size of the worker thread pool. The server starts these threads in advance to handle accepted sockets. The default is 20. Set it to 0 to turn the pool off. The server then falls back to the old one-thread-per-connection path. This is useful under Valgrind or ThreadSanitizer. The pool's task queue size follows max_connections. So a burst of connections cannot use up all the memory. MAX_SESSIONS (compile-time) The session registry has a fixed size of 256 slots. This array backs session introspection and statement cancellation. A new session still gets a valid ID when all slots are in use. But the registry does not track it. Each connection runs in its own thread. The thread comes from the pool or is spawned. The server runs queries one at a time across all threads. It uses a parser read/write lock and a DML mutex to do this. You can read more in Concurrency & Locking. So the engine still has a single writer, even though the network layer uses many threads. How this differs from PostgreSQL. In PostgreSQL max_connections is a reloadable GUC. Each connection gets its own backend process. EvolutionDB has a fixed limit of 100, set in code. Its connections are threads that share one address space. There is no limit per role or per database. There are no reserved superuser slots.

sections.heading: 23.4. Buffer Pool Size
sections.text: The Clock-Sweep buffer pool caches 4 KB pages. You can read more in Storage & the Buffer Pool. Its size has the biggest effect on memory of any setting here. It is also the only setting that accepts unit suffixes. --buffer-pool-size SIZE / EVOSQL_BUFFER_POOL_SIZE Sets how many 4 KB pages the buffer pool holds. The default is 32768 pages, which is 128 MB. This default is BP_DEFAULT_PAGES. The flag wins over the environment variable. The server reads the environment variable only when the flag is absent. The size string can use a unit suffix or a plain page count. The server reads it as follows. InputInterpreted asResulting pages 1G / 1gGigabytesval × 1024 × 1024 / 4 = 262144 128M / 128mMegabytesval × 1024 / 4 = 32768 512K / 512kKilobytesval / 4 = 128 32768Raw page count32768 # All three select a 256 MB pool evosql-server --buffer-pool-size 256M EVOSQL_BUFFER_POOL_SIZE=256M evosql-server evosql-server --buffer-pool-size 65536 # raw pages A value that comes out as zero or less counts as "unset". The server then uses the 128 MB default. It prints the final size at startup on a [BufferPool] line. The line shows pages and MB. For very small pools it shows KB.

sections.heading: 23.5. Authentication Bootstrap
sections.text: The server creates the first superuser on the first boot. It reads two environment variables to do this. The user-management layer reads them in evolution/db/UserMgmt.c. EVOSQL_USER_NAME The name of the bootstrap superuser. The default is admin. EVOSQL_PASSWORD The password for the bootstrap superuser. When this is unset or empty, the server creates a random 16-character password and prints it once at startup. Save it from the logs. The server does not store it in plaintext and does not show it again. The stored credential uses PBKDF2-SHA256. See CREATE USER and Security. # Deterministic credentials for local development EVOSQL_USER_NAME=admin EVOSQL_PASSWORD=admin evosql-server Both protocols force cleartext-password auth. The PG wire handler always sends a CleartextPassword challenge. The hashing happens on the server side. Run the server behind TLS so the cleartext password is never seen on the wire. The next section covers TLS.

sections.heading: 23.6. Transport Security (TLS)
sections.text: TLS is off by default. You turn it on only through the environment. The server sets up an OpenSSL context for the PG-wire transport at startup when both variables below point to readable PEM files. If they do not, it prints TLS disabled and runs in cleartext. TLS also needs a build made with TLS=1. EVOSQL_TLS_CERT Path to the server certificate (PEM). EVOSQL_TLS_KEY Path to the matching private key (PEM). EVOSQL_TLS_CERT=/etc/evosql/server.crt \ EVOSQL_TLS_KEY=/etc/evosql/server.key \ evosql-server A failed TLS init does not stop the server. It logs [TLS] Failed to initialise - continuing without TLS and runs in cleartext. It does not refuse to start. The replication transport can reuse the same certificate and key pair. This happens when EVOSQL_REPLICATION_TLS is truthy. See below.

sections.heading: 23.7. Replication, CDC and Cluster Boot Settings
sections.text: You can set up the replication topology fully from the environment. This is handy for containers. You can then refine it with flags. The server reads the environment first. Matching flags override it. High Availability & Replication has the full operational detail. This section only covers the boot-time settings. EVOSQL_REPLICATION_ROLE / --role One of primary, replica, or witness. This sets the role that SHOW REPLICATION STATUS reports before any peer connects. An unknown value logs a warning and leaves the role unset. EVOSQL_PRIMARY_HOST / EVOSQL_PRIMARY_PORT The upstream the server should follow when role=replica. The replica uses REPL_DEFAULT_PORT, which is 9968, when you give a host without a port. This is the same as the flag --replica HOST:PORT. EVOSQL_REPLICATION_PORT / --replication-port The port where a primary runs its WAL sender. The sender starts only when this comes out as a value > 0. EVOSQL_REPLICATION_USER / EVOSQL_REPLICATION_PASSWORD The credentials for the replication auth handshake. You must set both for them to take effect. EVOSQL_REPLICATION_TLS A truthy value turns on TLS for the replication transport. The truthy values are 1, t, T, y, and Y. It uses the same cert and key as the PG-wire context. --cdc-port / EVOSQL_CDC_PORT Starts a Change-Data-Capture streaming listener. It pushes WAL-decoded changes as JSON Lines. It is off unless the value is > 0. --cluster + --node-id A comma-separated host:port node list plus this node's zero-based index. Give both to start the Raft consensus module and the distributed query engine. The server works out each node's PG and dist ports by offsetting from this node's ports. --base-backup PATH A one-shot mode. It copies the data directory to PATH and exits without binding any listener sockets. Use it to bootstrap a fresh replica from a primary. The exit code is 0 on success and 1 on failure. # Primary with WAL sender on 9968 evosql-server --role primary --replication-port 9968 # Replica following the primary above (env-driven) EVOSQL_REPLICATION_ROLE=replica \ EVOSQL_PRIMARY_HOST=10.0.0.1 EVOSQL_PRIMARY_PORT=9968 \ evosql-server

sections.heading: 23.8. Temporal Retention
sections.text: EVOSQL_SYSTEM_TIME_RETENTION_DAYS Sets the retention window for system-versioned table history. These are temporal tables. The default is 7 days. A value of 0 turns retention pruning off. The server accepts any non-negative integer at boot. Most settings here are fixed at boot, but this one can change at runtime. Use SET SYSTEM_TIME_RETENTION = N. This overrides the boot value for the running process.

sections.heading: 23.9. Quick Reference
sections.text: This table lists every command-line flag and environment variable the server reads at startup. They are all in one place. SettingFlagEnvironment variableDefault PG port--pg-port / bare PORT, 5433 EVO port--evo-port, 9967 Buffer pool size--buffer-pool-sizeEVOSQL_BUFFER_POOL_SIZE32768 pages / 128 MB Worker pool size, EVOSQL_POOL_SIZE20 Max connections, (compiled), 100 Bootstrap user, EVOSQL_USER_NAMEadmin Bootstrap password, EVOSQL_PASSWORDrandom 16-char TLS certificate, EVOSQL_TLS_CERTnone (TLS off) TLS key, EVOSQL_TLS_KEYnone (TLS off) Replication role--roleEVOSQL_REPLICATION_ROLEunset Replication port--replication-portEVOSQL_REPLICATION_PORToff (0) Replica upstream--replica HOST:PORTEVOSQL_PRIMARY_HOST / _PORToff Replication auth, EVOSQL_REPLICATION_USER / _PASSWORDnone Replication TLS, EVOSQL_REPLICATION_TLSoff CDC port--cdc-portEVOSQL_CDC_PORToff (0) Cluster nodes--cluster, off Node ID--node-id, -1 (off) Distributed port--dist-port, 9969 Base backup (one-shot)--base-backup PATH, , Temporal retention, EVOSQL_SYSTEM_TIME_RETENTION_DAYS7 days Notes & limitations No config file. There is no postgresql.conf or evosql.conf. All configuration comes from flags and the environment. The server resolves it once at process start. Most settings cannot reload at runtime. You must restart to change ports, buffer-pool size, pool size, TLS, or the connection limit. The only documented exception is SYSTEM_TIME_RETENTION. You can change it with SET. The connection limit is built into the code and is 100. There is no startup flag for it. The server drops over-limit connections at accept time and logs a rejection. It does not send a protocol-level error message. No bind-address or interface selection. Use a firewall or container networking to limit who can reach it. Unknown flags are ignored without any message. There is no usage banner and no non-zero exit for a misspelled option. So check your spelling. Graceful shutdown happens on SIGTERM or SIGINT. The server drains the worker pool, checkpoints the WAL, flushes the buffer pool, and closes the data file before it exits. This is POSIX only. See also. Storage & the Buffer Pool · High Availability & Replication · Security · CREATE USER · SET.

sections.heading: Introduction
sections.text: 24. Managing Databases A database in EvolutionDB is a named namespace. It is registered in the catalog and owns one or more schemas. This chapter shows how to create databases. It also shows how to switch between them with USE. It explains how the multi-database layout maps onto EvolutionDB's single unified storage file.

sections.heading: 24.1. Overview
sections.text: Some systems give each database its own files. Some give each database its own cluster directory. EvolutionDB is different. It stores every database in one unified, page-based file. That file is root/evosql.db. The root/ directory contains nothing else. There are no per-database subdirectories on disk. Databases, schemas, tables, and all other metadata live inside that file's system catalog. The catalog is itself a set of B+trees. Creating a database is a pure catalog operation. It inserts a row into the catalog's database list. It does not allocate or set up any separate file. A database is best understood as a logical grouping. It is not a physical storage boundary. layers The naming hierarchy has three levels. The levels are database → schema → table. Every new database gets a starting schema named default. Every session starts out pointed at some database and some schema. Schema management is covered in detail in Chapter 25, Managing Schemas. This chapter focuses on the database level.

sections.heading: 24.2. The Default Database and Schema
sections.text: On the very first start of a fresh storage file, EvolutionDB sets up the catalog. It creates a single database named evosql. That database holds a single schema named default. A new session inherits these values if you do not specify anything else. The current database defaults to evosql. The current schema defaults to default. Default database evosql. It is created automatically the first time the catalog is set up. It always has internal database id 1. Default schema default. It is created inside every database at creation time. This includes evosql. Storage root The directory root/. It holds the single file root/evosql.db. The directory name is fixed. It is not per-database. info PostgreSQL contrast. PostgreSQL ships with postgres, template0, and template1. In PostgreSQL, CREATE DATABASE physically clones a template directory. EvolutionDB has no template databases. It has no per-database directory. So there is nothing to clone. A new database is just a catalog entry plus its own default schema.

sections.heading: 24.3. Creating a Database
sections.text: Use the CREATE DATABASE command. The only argument is the database name. EvolutionDB does not accept the per-database options found in standard SQL or PostgreSQL. Those options include owner, encoding, locale, template, tablespace, and connection limit. CREATE DATABASE name CREATE DATABASE IF NOT EXISTS name Creating a database registers it in the catalog. It also gives the database a schema named default right away. CREATE DATABASE shop; -- server responds: CREATE DATABASE shop -- The new database already contains schema "default". USE shop; CREATE TABLE products ( id INT PRIMARY KEY, name VARCHAR(100) ); -- created in shop.default.products

sections.heading: 24.3.1. IF NOT EXISTS
sections.text: Suppose you create a database whose name is already registered. Without IF NOT EXISTS, the command fails with SQLSTATE 42E04. That code means a duplicate database. With IF NOT EXISTS, the command does nothing and succeeds quietly when the database already exists. CREATE DATABASE shop; -- ok CREATE DATABASE shop; -- ERROR 42E04: database "shop" already exists CREATE DATABASE IF NOT EXISTS shop; -- ok, no change warning Note. CREATE DATABASE does not switch your session to the new database. The current database stays the same after the command returns. You must run USE on its own to start working inside the new database.

sections.heading: 24.4. Switching Databases with USE
sections.text: The USE command changes which database your next statements work on. EvolutionDB checks the name against the catalog. If the name is found, it sets that database as the session's current database. There is one side effect. The current schema is reset to default. Switching databases does not keep the schema you had selected before. USE database_name USE shop; -- server responds: USE DATABASE shop -- current database = shop, current schema reset to "default" Suppose the named database is not in the catalog. Then USE fails with SQLSTATE 3D000. That code means an undefined database. The session's current database stays the same. USE nope; -- ERROR 3D000: database "nope" does not exist

sections.heading: 24.4.1. USE Is Per-Session
sections.text: The current database and current schema are stored in each connection's own query context. They are not a global server setting. Two clients connected at the same time can point at two different databases. One connection's USE never affects another. The selection lasts only for the life of the connection. info PostgreSQL and standard-SQL contrast. PostgreSQL has no USE statement. You select a database by reconnecting. A connection is bound to one database for its whole life. EvolutionDB's USE works more like the MySQL USE statement. It is an in-session switch. But it also resets the active schema to default. MySQL does not do this. MySQL treats "database" and "schema" as the same thing.

sections.heading: 24.4.2. The Database Chosen at Connect Time
sections.text: A client can connect over the PostgreSQL wire protocol. The database name in the startup message becomes the session's first current database. A well-configured PostgreSQL client lands right in the database it asked for. It does not need an explicit USE. The same applies to a driver such as the one used by DBeaver or psql. A session that does not name a database falls back to the default. The default is evosql.

sections.heading: 24.5. The Multi-Database Layout
sections.text: All databases share root/evosql.db. Qualifying an object name is purely logical. A fully qualified table reference has this form. database.schema.table At runtime, the engine resolves unqualified names against the session's current database and current schema. The old path form root/<database>/<schema>/<table> still shows up inside the engine for backward compatibility. It is used when extracting bare table names. But it does not map to real directories on disk. It is a naming convention. It is not a filesystem layout. LevelCreated byNotes DatabaseCREATE DATABASECatalog entry only. Always starts with a default schema. SchemaCREATE SCHEMACreated inside the current database. See Chapter 25. TableCREATE TABLECreated inside the current schema of the current database.

sections.heading: 24.5.1. Practical Implications
sections.text: One file, one buffer pool Every database is paged through the same 4 KB buffer pool. They all use the same write-ahead log. Databases are a way to organize your data. They do not isolate storage, caching, or I/O from one another. No per-database tuning CREATE DATABASE accepts only a name. So there are no per-database encodings, collations, owners, or tablespaces to set. Names are case-sensitive in the catalog Database names are stored and looked up as given. However, the current-database guard compares names without case sensitivity.

sections.heading: 24.6. Dropping a Database
sections.text: The DROP DATABASE command removes a database. The removal cascades to all of its schemas and the tables inside them. DROP DATABASE name DROP DATABASE IF EXISTS name DROP DATABASE shop; -- drops shop and everything inside it DROP DATABASE IF EXISTS shop; -- no error if shop is already gone You cannot drop the database your session is currently using. Switch away first. You can also connect somewhere else. USE shop; DROP DATABASE shop; -- ERROR 55006: cannot drop the currently open database "shop" USE evosql; DROP DATABASE shop; -- ok warning Note. The drop always cascades. DROP DATABASE always removes every schema and every table the database holds. There is no RESTRICT mode that refuses to drop a non-empty database. This operation destroys data and cannot be undone. If you drop a database that does not exist, the command fails with SQLSTATE 3D000 unless you use IF EXISTS. That code means an undefined database.

sections.heading: 24.7. Error Codes
sections.text: The database-management commands report the following SQLSTATE values. SQLSTATEConditionRaised by 42E04duplicate databaseCREATE DATABASE on an existing name, when IF NOT EXISTS is not used 3D000undefined databaseUSE or DROP DATABASE on a name not in the catalog 55006object in useDROP DATABASE on the currently open database 58030I/O errorCREATE DATABASE when the catalog write fails For comparison, schema-level commands use 42E06 for a duplicate schema. They use 3F000 for an undefined schema. Those codes are documented in Chapter 25, Managing Schemas.

sections.heading: 24.8. See Also
sections.text: CREATE DATABASE. Full command reference. DROP DATABASE. Full command reference. USE. Switch the active database. Chapter 25, Managing Schemas. The next level of the namespace. CREATE SCHEMA. Add a schema to the current database.

sections.heading: Introduction
sections.text: 26. Encryption (TDE) EvolutionDB encrypts every data page on disk with AES-256. This protects the database file when it sits on disk. Inside the running server's buffer pool, the page contents stay in cleartext. Transparent Data Encryption (TDE) works below the SQL layer. Tables, indexes, the system catalog, and the write-ahead log are all stored as 4 KB pages. When TDE is active, those pages are encrypted as they go to disk. They are decrypted as they come back. The SQL you write and the rows you read do not change. From SQL the only visible effect is that the on-disk file evosql.db and its WAL cannot be read without the passphrase. This chapter describes exactly what EvolutionDB implements. EvolutionDB uses a two-layer key hierarchy called MEK and DEK. It uses AES-256 page encryption in CTR mode. It wraps keys with AES-256-GCM. It derives key material from the passphrase with PBKDF2-SHA256. This chapter also compares EvolutionDB with PostgreSQL and other TDE designs where that is helpful. The comparison is honest. It does not claim features that do not exist. Build requirement. TDE is a compile-time feature. It is guarded by the EVO_ENCRYPTION macro. The server's TLS=1 build sets this macro for you. That build also pulls in OpenSSL's EVP API for AES. A server built without EVO_ENCRYPTION turns the whole crypto path into no-op stubs. In that case the database is always plaintext. The encryption controls in this chapter then have no effect.

sections.heading: 26.1. Overview
sections.text: EvolutionDB's TDE works at the page level and covers the whole database. There is no per-table, per-column, or per-tablespace encryption. When a database is created with a passphrase set, every page except page 0 is encrypted. That choice is permanent for the life of the file. This matches the granularity of cluster-wide TDE designs. It differs from column-level encryption such as PostgreSQL's pgcrypto. That tool encrypts single values inside pages that stay otherwise plaintext. What is encryptedAll pages numbered 1 and above. This covers heap pages, B+ tree index pages, slotted-page records, the system catalog, the free list, and WAL page images written during replay. What is not encryptedPage 0 is the FileHeader. It is always plaintext. It holds the file magic, version, page size, and catalog roots. It also holds the encryption metadata itself, such as the salt, the wrapped DEK, and the IV prefix. The server needs this metadata to start decryption. Where plaintext livesIn the buffer pool. Pages are held decrypted in memory. Encryption happens only when a page is flushed to disk. Decryption happens only when a page is read from disk. The query engine never sees ciphertext. Threat modelProtection at rest. TDE defends against theft of the database file, the WAL, or the physical disk. It does not protect against an attacker who can read the server's memory. It does not protect against an attacker who holds the passphrase. The buffer pool stores plaintext and the engine works on plaintext. So TDE is invisible to queries and adds no rules to your SQL. A SELECT over an encrypted database returns the same bytes as one over a plaintext database.

sections.heading: 26.2. Key hierarchy: MEK and DEK
sections.text: EvolutionDB uses a two-layer key design in the style of Oracle TDE. KeySizeDerived / generatedStored where MEK, Master Encryption Key256 bitsDerived from the passphrase and a random 16-byte salt with PBKDF2-SHA256Never stored. It is recomputed in memory at startup. It is wiped right after use. DEK, Data Encryption Key256 bitsGenerated once from the OS CSPRNG when the database is first createdStored wrapped in the plaintext FileHeader on page 0. The MEK encrypts it. The DEK is the key that encrypts pages. The MEK exists only to protect the DEK. This extra layer makes rekeying cheap. Changing the passphrase changes the MEK. The new MEK re-wraps the same DEK. The gigabytes of already-encrypted pages never need rewriting. The passphrase comes from the EVOSQL_ENCRYPTION_KEY environment variable. The server reads it with getenv() at startup and never saves it. If the variable is unset or empty, encryption is not initialized. # Key flow at a glance EVOSQL_ENCRYPTION_KEY ──PBKDF2-SHA256(salt,100000)──▶ MEK (256-bit) MEK ──AES-256-GCM unwrap──▶ DEK (256-bit, from FileHeader) DEK ──AES-256-CTR──▶ page ciphertext (pages 1..N)

sections.heading: 26.2.1. MEK derivation (PBKDF2-SHA256)
sections.text: The MEK is derived by stretching the passphrase with PBKDF2-SHA256. EvolutionDB ships its own pure-C code for SHA-256, HMAC-SHA256, and PBKDF2. The KDF needs no external library. The code follows FIPS 180-4, RFC 2104, and RFC 2898. PRFHMAC-SHA256 IterationsPBKDF2_ITERATIONS = 100000. This value is fixed and compiled in. Salt16 random bytes. Each database gets its own salt. It is stored in the FileHeader as encryption_salt[16]. Derived key length32 bytes (256 bits) The same PBKDF2 code also backs user-password hashing. See Authentication. There the hashes are stored in the format pbkdf2:100000:<hex-salt>:<hex-hash>. For TDE the derived bytes are used straight as the MEK. They are never written to disk. Honest comparison. 100,000 PBKDF2-SHA256 iterations is a fixed, moderate work factor. It is below the project's own user-auth guidance. The security standard asks for 600,000 or more iterations for password storage. It is also well below modern memory-hard KDFs such as Argon2 or scrypt. PBKDF2 cannot match those against GPU or ASIC attackers. The best fix is a long passphrase with high entropy. With a strong passphrase the iteration count is not the weak point.

sections.heading: 26.2.2. DEK wrapping (AES-256-GCM)
sections.text: The 32-byte DEK is wrapped under the MEK with AES-256-GCM. To wrap means to encrypt. The result is a 48-byte blob. It holds 32 bytes of ciphertext and then a 16-byte GCM authentication tag. This blob is stored in the FileHeader as wrapped_dek[48]. The GCM tag also works as a passphrase verifier. At startup the server derives the MEK from the passphrase and tries to unwrap the DEK. If the passphrase is wrong, the MEK is wrong. The GCM tag check fails. The server treats that failure as a fatal "wrong encryption key" error. It does not quietly produce garbage. FATAL: Wrong encryption key for this database. The EVOSQL_ENCRYPTION_KEY does not match. The wrapping IV is the fixed 12-byte string EVO_WRAP_KEY. Reusing a fixed IV with GCM is normally dangerous. Here it is safe. Each database has its own random salt. That salt produces a unique MEK. The key and IV pair is therefore never reused across databases that share the same DEK.

sections.heading: 26.3. Page encryption (AES-256-CTR)
sections.text: Pages are encrypted with AES-256 in CTR mode using the DEK. CTR is a stream-cipher mode. It was picked on purpose for two reasons that matter to a page store. Zero storage overheadCTR ciphertext has the same length as the plaintext. A 4096-byte page encrypts to exactly 4096 bytes. So the on-disk layout, page numbering, and offsets are the same with or without TDE. There is no per-page authentication tag. Symmetric operationIn CTR mode encryption and decryption are the same operation. EvolutionDB uses one routine for both directions.

sections.heading: 26.3.1. Per-page IV construction
sections.text: Each page gets its own 16-byte CTR IV. The IV is built from a random per-database prefix and the page number. IV (16 bytes) = iv_prefix[8] || page_no_LE[4] || zeros[4] └ random, per-DB ┘ └ little-endian ┘ └ counter ┘ The 8-byte iv_prefix is generated once with the OS CSPRNG when the database is created. It is stored in the FileHeader as page_iv_prefix[8]. The prefix never changes, even when the passphrase is rotated. The page number is also part of the IV. This means no two pages share an IV. CTR mode requires exactly that. Limitation. CTR gives confidentiality, not integrity. GCM is used for the DEK wrap and has an authentication tag. CTR does not. So EvolutionDB does not detect tampering or bit-rot of encrypted pages. A flipped ciphertext bit quietly flips the matching plaintext bit. TDE here defends against reading the file. It does not defend against an attacker who can change it. Most zero-overhead page-level TDE schemes make the same trade-off. Use filesystem or volume integrity if you need tamper detection. You can also add checksums at a higher layer.

sections.heading: 26.3.2. Page 0 stays plaintext
sections.text: Page 0 is the FileHeader. It is never encrypted. Both the encrypt and decrypt entry points return early when page_no == 0. Page 0 holds the startup metadata: the magic, the salt, the wrapped DEK, and the IV prefix. The server must read this data before it can derive any key. The header does show that a database is encrypted and it shows the salt, the wrapped DEK, and the IV prefix. None of these reveal the DEK without the passphrase. EvolutionDB does not encrypt the page_no == 0 region. PostgreSQL's at-rest encryption proposals keep some control data readable for the same reason. You cannot decrypt the thing that tells you how to decrypt.

sections.heading: 26.3.3. Where encryption happens in the I/O path
sections.text: All page I/O flows through the Clock-Sweep buffer pool. See Buffer Pool. Encryption and decryption happen right at the disk boundary. PathAction Buffer pool flush (dirty page → disk)The page is copied into a scratch buffer. It is encrypted in place. Then it is written. The in-memory page stays plaintext. Buffer pool fault (disk → buffer)The page is read. Then it is decrypted in place if encryption is enabled and page_no > 0. WAL replay (recovery)Page images written to the data file during replay are encrypted before pwrite. Page 0 is the exception. Replication shippingPages are encrypted on the same code path before they are sent downstream. Every one of these checks both pcrypt_is_enabled() and page_no > 0. So a plaintext database and page 0 always skip the cipher.

sections.heading: 26.4. Enabling encryption
sections.text: TDE is decided when the database is created. It is controlled only by the EVOSQL_ENCRYPTION_KEY environment variable. There is no CREATE DATABASE ... ENCRYPTED clause. There is no SQL statement that turns encryption on for an existing plaintext file.

sections.heading: 26.4.1. A new (empty) database
sections.text: When the page manager creates a fresh evosql.db and EVOSQL_ENCRYPTION_KEY is set, it initializes encryption. It generates a random DEK, a random salt, and a random IV prefix. It derives the MEK, wraps the DEK, and records all of that in the FileHeader with encryption_enabled = 1. # docker-compose.yml - set a strong custom key BEFORE first start services: evosql: environment: EVOSQL_ENCRYPTION_KEY: "<a long, random, high-entropy passphrase>" # Or locally, when the .db file does not yet exist: export EVOSQL_ENCRYPTION_KEY="<a long, random, high-entropy passphrase>" ./adaptor/evosql-server On success the server logs [TDE] Encryption initialized for new database. From that point the file is encrypted permanently. There is no in-place "decrypt back to plaintext" operation.

sections.heading: 26.4.2. An existing encrypted database
sections.text: For an existing file, the server reads encryption_enabled from page 0. If it is set, the server derives the MEK from the supplied passphrase and unwraps the DEK. The table below shows all startup outcomes. File encryption_enabledEVOSQL_ENCRYPTION_KEYResult 1 (encrypted)correct passphraseDatabase unlocked, logs [TDE] Encryption key verified, database unlocked 1 (encrypted)wrong passphraseFATAL, GCM tag verification fails; server refuses to start 1 (encrypted)unset / emptyFATAL, "Database is encrypted but EVOSQL_ENCRYPTION_KEY.. not set" 0 (plaintext)setWarning, key ignored; existing plaintext files are not retroactively encrypted; continues without encryption 0 (plaintext)unsetNormal plaintext operation Default-key warning. The shipped docker-compose.yml sets a built-in fallback passphrase so the demo "just works." That key is publicly known. When the server detects it, it prints a prominent warning to stderr. Always override EVOSQL_ENCRYPTION_KEY with your own secret before creating a production database. A database created under the default key is no more protected than a plaintext one.

sections.heading: 26.5. Rekeying (passphrase rotation)
sections.text: The passphrase only protects the MEK. The MEK only wraps the DEK. Rotating the passphrase is therefore a fast metadata operation. A new salt is generated. A new MEK is derived from the new passphrase. The same DEK is re-wrapped under the new MEK. The IV prefix is left unchanged. No data pages are re-encrypted. Only encryption_salt and wrapped_dek in the FileHeader change. Implementation status. The rekey primitive (pcrypt_rekey) exists in the engine. In EvolutionDB 3.0 it is not exposed through SQL. There is no ALTER SYSTEM ... REKEY statement. No command-level surface in the parser or protocol layer drives it. Passphrase rotation is therefore an internal capability. It is not a user-facing operation in this release. This is a deliberate limitation, not an advertised feature.

sections.heading: 26.6. Key material in memory
sections.text: Sensitive buffers are scrubbed after use. They are not left on the stack or heap. The MEK is wiped immediately after it wraps or unwraps the DEK. SHA-256 contexts, HMAC pads, and PBKDF2 intermediates are wiped at the end of each operation. On shutdown the DEK and IV prefix are wiped from the module's static state. Scrubbing uses evo_secure_wipe(). It is hardened against the compiler optimizing away a "dead" memset (CWE-14). It prefers platform primitives where available. On Windows it uses SecureZeroMemory(). When the C11 Annex K bounds-checking interface is present, it uses memset_s(). Otherwise it falls back to a volatile function-pointer indirection. The optimizer cannot prove that call is side-effect-free, so it keeps it. Password verification also uses a constant-time comparison to avoid leaking information through timing. These protections cover the running process. While the server runs, the DEK and decrypted pages must stay in memory. That is the boundary of the at-rest threat model.

sections.heading: 26.7. Randomness source
sections.text: All key material comes from the operating system CSPRNG via crypto_random_bytes(). This includes the DEK, the MEK salt, and the IV prefix. Linux / macOSReads from /dev/urandom WindowsCryptGenRandom via CryptAcquireContext If obtaining randomness fails, encryption initialization is aborted. The server does not fall back to a weak source.

sections.heading: 26.8. Cryptographic primitives summary
sections.text: PurposeAlgorithmParametersSource Passphrase → MEKPBKDF2-SHA256100,000 iterations, 16-byte salt, 32-byte outputIn-tree pure C (crypto.c) DEK wrapping / verificationAES-256-GCMFixed 12-byte IV, 16-byte tag, 48-byte wrapped blobOpenSSL EVP Page encryptionAES-256-CTR16-byte IV = 8-byte prefix + 4-byte page no + 4 zero bytesOpenSSL EVP Hash / PRF building blockSHA-256 / HMAC-SHA256FIPS 180-4 / RFC 2104In-tree pure C (crypto.c) Random key materialOS CSPRNG, /dev/urandom · CryptGenRandom Memory scrubbingevo_secure_wipeCWE-14 hardenedIn-tree (crypto.c)

sections.heading: 26.9. On-disk metadata layout
sections.text: The encryption metadata lives in the plaintext FileHeader (page 0). It is 73 bytes total. encryption_enabled (1 byte)0 = plaintext database, 1 = AES-256 TDE active encryption_salt[16]PBKDF2 salt for MEK derivation wrapped_dek[48]DEK encrypted by the MEK. It is 32 bytes of ciphertext followed by a 16-byte GCM tag. page_iv_prefix[8]Fixed CTR IV prefix; unchanged across rekeys None of these fields reveals the DEK or any plaintext. Without the correct passphrase the wrapped DEK cannot be unwrapped. The GCM verification fails and the pages cannot be decrypted.

sections.heading: 26.10. Comparison with PostgreSQL and the SQL standard
sections.text: These notes give an honest mapping for readers coming from PostgreSQL. Cluster-wide TDECore PostgreSQL has no built-in transparent cluster encryption. At-rest encryption is typically delegated to filesystem or volume encryption, or to forks and extensions. EvolutionDB ships whole-database page TDE in the engine itself. Column-level encryptionPostgreSQL's pgcrypto encrypts individual column values. Pages stay otherwise readable and SQL changes are required. EvolutionDB has no column-level encryption. It encrypts whole pages and is invisible to SQL. Key managementEvolutionDB's MEK/DEK split resembles Oracle TDE's master-key / table-key model. The passphrase comes from an environment variable. There is no external KMS, HSM, or keystore integration. SQL standardThe SQL standard does not define transparent storage encryption. TDE here is an implementation-level feature with no standard syntax. That is why it is configured by environment variable rather than by DDL. Notes & limitations. Compile-time gated. Requires a build with EVO_ENCRYPTION (the TLS=1 build). Otherwise the crypto path is a no-op and the database is plaintext. Permanent at creation. Encryption is set when the database file is first created. You cannot encrypt an existing plaintext file in place, nor decrypt an encrypted file back to plaintext, through any supported operation. No SQL rekey. The rekey primitive exists internally but has no SQL/command surface in 3.0; passphrase rotation is not a user-facing feature. Confidentiality only. Page encryption uses CTR with no per-page authentication tag. There is no tamper detection on data pages. KDF work factor. PBKDF2 is fixed at 100,000 SHA-256 iterations; rely on a strong passphrase, not the iteration count. Default key is public. The shipped compose default passphrase is well known. Override it before creating any database you care about. Memory is the boundary. While the server runs, the DEK and decrypted pages live in memory. TDE protects the file at rest, not a live-memory attacker who already holds the key. See also: Authentication (PBKDF2 password hashing), Buffer Pool (where encrypt/decrypt is applied), Storage & Page Layout (the FileHeader and 4 KB pages), and TLS (encryption in transit, the complementary protection to encryption at rest).

sections.heading: Introduction
sections.text: 22. Installation This page shows how to get a running EvolutionDB 3.0 server. You can build the three executables from source with a C toolchain. You can also pull the prebuilt container image and start it with docker compose. EvolutionDB ships as three separate executables. They are all built from one source tree. evolution/evosql The standalone core SQL engine. It holds the parser, storage, and executor. You build it with make evolution. It is useful for embedded or single-user tests. It has no network listener. adaptor/evosql-server The dual-protocol server. It links the engine object files directly. It does not link a shared library. It serves the PostgreSQL wire protocol and the native EVO text protocol over TCP. Run this in production. You build it with make adaptor. cli/evosql-cli The interactive command-line client. It is based on GNU readline. You build it with make cli. Compared with PostgreSQL. PostgreSQL ships a configure/make build with many optional contrib modules. It also has a separate initdb step to lay out a data directory. EvolutionDB has neither of these. There is no configure script, no initdb, and no cluster directory. The server creates and manages a single page file on first start in its working directory. That file is evosql.db and it uses 4 KB pages.

sections.heading: 22.1. Requirements
sections.text: The build tools and library needs are kept small on purpose. The exact set depends on which component you build. It also depends on whether you turn on TLS. ComponentBuild toolsLibraries Core engine (evosql)GCC, Makeliblz4 Server (evosql-server)GCC, Make; Bison + Flex only to regenerate the parserliblz4; libssl/libcrypto when TLS=1 CLI client (evosql-cli)GCC, Makelibreadline; libssl/libcrypto when TLS=1 Here are notes on each library. They come from the real Makefiles. liblz4 It is always linked into the engine. It backs the TOAST overflow store. The store keeps large text rows from taking over the disk. It is a small dependency of about 150 KB. You can get it as liblz4-dev on Debian and Ubuntu. You can get it as lz4 on Homebrew and Alpine apk. You can get it as lz4-devel on RHEL and Fedora. libreadline Only the CLI client needs it. It is used for line editing and history. The history file is ~/.evosql_history. It holds up to 500 lines. OpenSSL (libssl / libcrypto) It is optional. You need it only when you build with TLS=1 for the server or TLS=1 for the CLI. The server uses it for the TLS transport and for AES-256 Transparent Data Encryption. Bison + Flex You need them only to run make generate. The generated parser and lexer files are committed to the repository. These are evoparser.tab.c/h and lex.yy.c. So an ordinary build does not need Bison or Flex.

sections.heading: 22.2. Building from source
sections.text: The top-level Makefile sits in the repository root. It drives the three sub-project Makefiles. The order matters. The engine is built first. The server and CLI link against its object files. # Build all three executables (evolution, adaptor, cli) make # Or build a single component make evolution make adaptor make cli # Clean every object file and executable make clean When make works, it prints the paths of the three binaries it built. ==================================== Build complete! evolution/evosql adaptor/evosql-server cli/evosql-cli ====================================

sections.heading: 22.2.1. Enabling TLS and encryption
sections.text: TLS support is off by default. Pass TLS=1 to compile the server with the OpenSSL transport and the page-encryption code path. This sets the EVOSQL_TLS and EVO_ENCRYPTION compile-time flags. # Server with OpenSSL TLS + Transparent Data Encryption make adaptor TLS=1 # CLI with TLS so it can connect to a TLS-enabled server make -C cli TLS=1 TLS is a build-time decision. In PostgreSQL, ssl = on is a runtime setting in postgresql.conf on a single binary. EvolutionDB works differently. It compiles the TLS code in or out. A server built without TLS=1 cannot serve TLS at all. Environment variables do not change this. See 23. Server Configuration for the runtime certificate variables EVOSQL_TLS_CERT and EVOSQL_TLS_KEY.

sections.heading: 22.2.2. Regenerating the parser
sections.text: You only need this if you have changed the grammar or the lexer. The grammar is evolution/parser/evoparser.y. The lexer is evolution/parser/evolexer.l. This step needs Bison and Flex. # Regenerate evoparser.tab.c/h and lex.yy.c, then rebuild make generate make

sections.heading: 22.2.3. Release binaries
sections.text: make release builds everything. It copies the three executables into release/. It also strips their symbol tables. make release # stripped binaries land in release/

sections.heading: 22.3. Platform notes
sections.text: The Makefiles find the platform with uname -s. They then adjust the link flags and the executable suffix to match. Linux Linux is the reference platform. Executables have no suffix. The server links -lpthread -lm -llz4. Install the dev packages with your package manager. For example, run apt-get install gcc make bison flex liblz4-dev libssl-dev libreadline-dev. macOS (Darwin) macOS builds native binaries with no suffix. Homebrew keeps liblz4, readline, and openssl off the default search path. The Makefiles add the Homebrew cellar include and lib paths for you. For example, they add -I/opt/homebrew/include. They also use brew --prefix readline and brew --prefix openssl for the keg-only formulas. Install with brew install lz4 readline openssl bison flex. Windows (MinGW / MSYS2) Windows builds .exe files. These are evosql.exe, evosql-server.exe, and evosql-cli.exe. The Makefiles send linker temp files to /tmp to work around a permission issue. They link the Winsock library -lws2_32 instead of pthreads. Honest scope. The container image and CI run on Linux. The Makefiles support macOS and MinGW too. But those are best-effort targets. There are no prebuilt OS packages. There is no .deb, no .rpm, no Homebrew tap, and no Windows installer. You build from source or use Docker.

sections.heading: 22.4. Installing with Docker
sections.text: The shipped docker-compose.yml builds the server from a multi-stage Dockerfile and starts it. The build uses a gcc:13 builder and a debian:bookworm-slim runtime. It is built with TLS=1. This is the recommended path for testing. It also works well with clients such as DBeaver, pgAdmin, or psql. # Start the server (builds the image on first run) docker compose up -d # PG:5433 EVO:9967 # Stop the server, keeping the data volume docker compose down # Stop the server and DELETE all data docker compose down -v You can also run the published image directly without the compose file. docker run -d -p 5433:5433 -p 9967:9967 \ -e EVOSQL_PASSWORD=mysecret evolutiondb/evolutiondb:latest On first start the container entrypoint makes a self-signed TLS certificate under /data/certs/ if none exists. The certificate is valid for 10 years and its CN is evosql-server. The entrypoint then exports EVOSQL_TLS_CERT and EVOSQL_TLS_KEY. After that it starts the server. The database file and certificates live in the /data volume. The data survives docker compose down. Running down -v destroys it.

sections.heading: 22.4.1. Ports
sections.text: The server is dual-protocol. The defaults below come straight from the source and the compose file. The source values are DEFAULT_PG_PORT, DEFAULT_EVO_PORT, and REPL_DEFAULT_PORT. PortProtocolDefaultHow to change PostgreSQL wirePG frontend/backend protocol, psql, DBeaver, JDBC5433--pg-port EVO nativeEvoSQL text protocol, evosql-cli, C/Python SDK9967--evo-port ReplicationWAL sender for replicas9968 (disabled unless configured)--replication-port CDC streamChange-data-capture, JSON Linesdisabled (compose maps 9970)--cdc-port / EVOSQL_CDC_PORT Use port 5433, not 5432. EvolutionDB sets its PostgreSQL-compatible listener to 5433 by default. This way it does not clash with a real PostgreSQL on the same host. Point your client at -p 5433.

sections.heading: 22.4.2. First-start credentials
sections.text: The default admin user name is admin. You set it with EVOSQL_USER_NAME. The password is controlled by EVOSQL_PASSWORD. If EVOSQL_PASSWORD is set, that password is used. The bundled docker-compose.yml sets it to admin to keep things simple. If it is not set, a random password is made and printed to stdout once on first start. Check docker logs to find it. A few other environment variables in the compose file shape the first start. The full list is in 23. Server Configuration. EVOSQL_ENCRYPTION_KEY The master passphrase for AES-256 Transparent Data Encryption. Set it to an empty string to turn encryption off. Page 0 is the file header. It is always stored in plaintext no matter what. EVOSQL_CDC_PORT Turns on the change-data-capture streaming server on the given port. The stream uses JSON Lines. EVOSQL_AUTO_RECLAIM_INTERVAL_SEC Sets how often the background reclaim and TTL tick runs. The compose file lowers it to 2 so TTL-driven row expiry shows up fast in tests. Production keeps the default of 30 s. EVOSQL_BUFFER_POOL_SIZE Sets the clock-sweep buffer pool size. It takes a raw page count or a unit suffix of K, M, or G. The suffix is read against the 4 KB page size. You can also set it with --buffer-pool-size.

sections.heading: 22.5. Running a local build
sections.text: After make, start the server and connect. The server prints its version and a banner. The banner shows the active ports. It also shows whether TLS is compiled in. # Start the server (defaults: PG 5433, EVO 9967) ./adaptor/evosql-server # Use a different PostgreSQL-wire port ./adaptor/evosql-server --pg-port 5434 # Connect with the native CLI (EVO port 9967) ./cli/evosql-cli ./cli/evosql-cli -W admin # prompt for password # Or connect any PostgreSQL client over the wire protocol psql -h 127.0.0.1 -p 5433 -U admin testdb Once you are connected you can create objects right away. There is no initdb step and no template database to bootstrap. See CREATE DATABASE and CREATE TABLE. There is also agent-memory DDL such as CREATE MEMORY STORE and CREATE CHECKPOINT STORE.

sections.heading: 22.6. Verifying the installation
sections.text: The test suite is a set of Python scripts. They speak the raw PG wire protocol. They do not need psycopg2. They need a running server. They connect to 127.0.0.1:5433 for PG or :9967 for EVO. They use the default credentials admin/admin against database testdb. # Start the server first docker compose up -d # Run individual test files python tests/test_memory_store.py python tests/test_checkpoint_store.py python tests/test_evo_protocol.py Notes and limitations. There is no configure script and no initdb. The engine creates its single 4 KB-page file evosql.db in the working directory on first run. The server links the engine's object files directly. It does not link a shared library. There is no installable libevolution. The separate C client SDK is client/libevosql-memory/. You build it with its own make -C client/libevosql-memory. TLS and page encryption are compile-time options set with TLS=1. A non-TLS build cannot be switched to TLS at runtime. Bison and Flex are needed only for make generate. They are never needed for an ordinary build. This is because the generated sources are committed. No OS-native packages are published. You get it by source build or container image only.

sections.heading: Introduction
sections.text: 28. Routine Maintenance Every EvolutionDB table needs two housekeeping jobs during its life. ANALYZE keeps the planner statistics fresh. RECLAIM sweeps out dead row versions and defragments the heap. A background daemon also runs reclamation and time-based pruning for you. EvolutionDB uses multi-version concurrency control. See MVCC. A DELETE or UPDATE never removes a row in place. The old version stays on its heap page until no running transaction could still read it. The cost-based planner also cannot estimate row counts or selectivity without sampled statistics. Routine maintenance solves both of these problems. It keeps statistics fresh and sweeps old tuples out of the heap and the indexes. Both operations are explicit SQL commands. Reclamation also runs on a background timer. Terminology. EvolutionDB calls the storage-reclamation command RECLAIM, not VACUUM. There is no VACUUM keyword. The statistics command is ANALYZE TABLE, not bare ANALYZE. Neither command appears in the SQL standard. Both are implementation-defined maintenance tools.

sections.heading: 28.1. Collecting Statistics with ANALYZE
sections.text: ANALYZE TABLE does a sequential scan of one table. It records statistics in the system catalog. The planner reads these statistics when it estimates how many rows a scan or predicate will produce. EXPLAIN also reports them. Without an ANALYZE, a table has no recorded row_count. Several maintenance rules described below then fall back to safe defaults. ANALYZE TABLE table_name [ WITH SAMPLE n { PERCENT | ROWS } ] [ UPDATE HISTOGRAM ON column [, ...] [ WITH n BUCKETS ] | DROP HISTOGRAM ON column [, ...] ] A bare ANALYZE TABLE t rebuilds every statistic for the table. The full reference covers every clause and its edge cases. It lives on the ANALYZE command page. This section sums up what is collected and how the numbers are worked out.

sections.heading: 28.1.1. What is collected
sections.text: A single full ANALYZE TABLE writes four kinds of catalog records. Table-level statistics row_count is the number of rows seen during the scan. page_count is the number of heap pages, counted by walking the page chain. last_analyzed is the wall-clock time of this run. Per-column statistics Each column gets null_count, distinct_count, min, max, and avg_width. The last one is the average value width in bytes. Per-column histograms One auto-selected histogram per column that has at least one non-NULL value. See 28.1.2. Per-index statistics For every index on the table, the server reads the B+ tree depth, leaf-page count, total entries, and average entries per leaf. These come straight from the index tree. On success the server prints a one-line summary. Here are two examples. # Full scan ANALYZE: 50000 rows, 4 columns, 412 pages # Sampled scan ANALYZE: 5012 rows sampled from 50000 (keep 10%), 4 columns, 412 pages

sections.heading: 28.1.2. Histograms
sections.text: A histogram gives the planner a distribution instead of a single distinct-count. This lets it estimate equality and range selectivity. EvolutionDB builds one of two kinds per column. It chooses the kind automatically. Frequency histogram. This is built when the number of distinct values (NDV) is no greater than the bucket budget. It stores one bucket per distinct value with its exact count, giving exact equality selectivity rather than an estimate. Equi-depth histogram. This is built when the NDV exceeds the bucket budget. Distinct values are sorted (numerically when every value parses as a number, otherwise lexicographically) and grouped into buckets that each hold roughly the same number of rows. Each bucket stores its upper bound and a cumulative count. The default bucket budget is 100. No histogram exceeds 100 buckets. UPDATE HISTOGRAM … WITH n BUCKETS overrides the default but is still capped at 100. Distinct-value tracking is capped at 10,000 values per column. Columns with more distinct values are summarized from that capped sample. This also keeps equi-depth bounds representative on very-high-cardinality columns. -- Rebuild histograms for two columns only, with a smaller bucket budget, -- leaving the per-column statistics untouched. ANALYZE TABLE orders UPDATE HISTOGRAM ON status, region WITH 32 BUCKETS; -- Forget a histogram (catalog-only, no scan). ANALYZE TABLE orders DROP HISTOGRAM ON notes;

sections.heading: 28.1.3. Sampling
sections.text: For large tables a full scan is expensive. ANALYZE can instead inspect a Bernoulli sample. Each row is kept with a fixed probability. The min and max values always track the full scan because their accuracy is free. Null counts and width sums are scaled back up to full-table estimates. Histogram bucket counts are scaled so that histogram-backed selectivity matches the reported row_count. WITH SAMPLE n PERCENT Keep each row with probability n%, clamped to 1–100. WITH SAMPLE n ROWS Target approximately n sampled rows. The target is converted to a keep percentage using the table's previously recorded row_count. With no prior ANALYZE, this falls back to a full scan. (no clause) Automatic. A full scan runs for tables under 10,000 rows (per the previously recorded row_count). Otherwise a 10% sample is used. Without a prior row count, a full scan is performed. -- Re-analyze a large fact table with a 10% sample. ANALYZE TABLE events WITH SAMPLE 10 PERCENT; Contrast with PostgreSQL. EvolutionDB requires the TABLE keyword and accepts exactly one table per statement. There is no parenthesized option list (VERBOSE, SKIP_LOCKED) and no autovacuum-style automatic statistics refresh. Sampling is controlled per-statement with WITH SAMPLE rather than globally via default_statistics_target. Histogram bucket counts are set inline with WITH n BUCKETS rather than per-column with ALTER TABLE … SET STATISTICS.

sections.heading: 28.2. Reclaiming Storage with RECLAIM
sections.text: RECLAIM TABLE sweeps obsolete MVCC row versions out of one table and physically reorganizes its heap pages and indexes. EvolutionDB never overwrites a row in place. The space used by deleted and superseded versions accumulates until a RECLAIM (manual or automatic) frees it. RECLAIM TABLE table_name The command takes no options. Its full behavior and exit messages are documented on the RECLAIM command page. The phases below explain what actually happens to the heap.

sections.heading: 28.2.1. What RECLAIM does
sections.text: For a non-empty table, RECLAIM TABLE works in this order. Dead-tuple removal and XID freeze. It walks every heap page and removes dead tuples. Dead tuples are those whose inserting transaction aborted or crashed, and those whose deleting or updating transaction committed and is older than the oldest active transaction (the removal horizon). For each removed tuple it also cleans the primary-key B+ tree, every secondary index, and any TOAST overflow chain. In the same pass it freezes the transaction id of old committed live tuples. It replaces an xmin older than the freeze cutoff with XID_FROZEN so the original id can be recycled without wraparound risk. Per-page compaction. Each surviving heap page is compacted internally so its live records become contiguous and its free space is reclaimable. Sparse-page merge. Live records are moved from tail pages into earlier pages that have room. A record is relocated only when the primary-key entry currently points at that exact version. This prevents stale or duplicate MVCC versions from being mis-repointed. Empty-page free and chain rebuild. Pages that end up with no live records are freed back to the shared page free list. The heap page chain is rebuilt over only the surviving pages. Surviving pages are then marked all-visible in the visibility map. Index fix-up. The primary-key tree is updated to the new row locations and compacted (tombstones dropped, underflowing nodes merged). Secondary indexes are kept consistent throughout. Bookkeeping. The table's dead_tuple_count is reset to zero and its frozen_xid is advanced. If the table has columns that were logically dropped with ALTER TABLE … DROP COLUMN, a final pass rewrites every tuple without those columns. It also removes the dropped-column metadata from the catalog. See ALTER TABLE. On completion the server prints a one-line summary. RECLAIM: 1240 dead tuples removed, 83 tuples frozen, compacted 38114 bytes, moved 512 records, freed 17 pages (228 pages remain) An empty table prints RECLAIM: table is empty, nothing to do and returns immediately.

sections.heading: 28.2.2. When to run it manually
sections.text: The background daemon (28.3) reclaims most tables for you. Explicit RECLAIM is mostly needed after bulk operations that the daemon's ratio test would not catch promptly. -- After a large bulk DELETE or a heavy UPDATE batch. DELETE FROM events WHERE created_at < '2025-01-01'; RECLAIM TABLE events; -- To physically finish an ALTER TABLE ... DROP COLUMN. ALTER TABLE orders DROP COLUMN legacy_note; RECLAIM TABLE orders; Dead-tuple counters are only an estimate. It is good practice to run ANALYZE TABLE after a RECLAIM that moved many rows.
