Spelling(s) acceptedToken INT8 / BIGINTBIGINT INT4 / INT / INTEGERINTEGER INT2 / SMALLINTSMALLINT INT1 / TINYINTTINYINT MIDDLEINT / MEDIUMINTMEDIUMINT NUMERIC / DEC / DECIMALDECIMAL FLOAT4 / FLOATFLOAT FLOAT8 / DOUBLEDOUBLE REALREAL CHAR / CHARACTERCHAR VARCHAR / VARCHARACTERVARCHAR TEXT, TINYTEXT, MEDIUMTEXT, LONGTEXT(respective text tokens) BINARY, VARBINARY, BIT, BLOB, MEDIUMBLOB, LONGBLOB(respective binary/blob tokens) BOOL / BOOLEANBOOLEAN DATE, TIME, DATETIME, TIMESTAMP, YEAR, INTERVALTemporal types. YEAR is special-cased, see C.4 ENUMENUM JSON / JSONBJSON (both spellings map to the same token) UUIDUUID ARRAYARRAY VECTORVECTOR, see CREATE TABLE and the vector functions in C.6 Some more words are also keywords. These include the type modifiers UNSIGNED and ZEROFILL, the SIGNAL-adjacent attributes, and the VIRTUAL and STORED generated-column markers.

sections.heading: C.3.5. Transaction, cursor and procedural keywords
sections.text: Keyword(s)Role BEGIN (token BEGINWORK), TRANSACTION, SAVEPOINT*Transaction control, see Transactions, SAVEPOINT DECLARE, CURSOR, OPEN, FETCH, CLOSE, HANDLERCursors IF, ELSE, ELSEIF, WHILE, LOOP, FOREACH, ITERATE, LEAVE, CONTINUE, EXIT, RETURN, UNTIL, DOProcedural control flow. IF is special-cased, see C.4 SIGNAL, RESIGNAL, MESSAGECondition signalling IN, OUT, INOUTRoutine parameter modes SESSION, SET, SHOW*Session settings, see SET, SHOW CHECKPOINT, RECLAIM, FORCE, QUICK, DELAYED, IGNOREMaintenance / execution hints, see CHECKPOINT, RECLAIM * The grammar recognizes the SAVEPOINT and SHOW statement entry points. The other words shown here come from the keyword rules in the lexer.

sections.heading: C.3.6. EvolutionDB-specific extension keywords
sections.text: These words have no match in the SQL standard, PostgreSQL, or MySQL in the way described here. They exist to drive EvolutionDB's own subsystems. These subsystems include LISTEN/NOTIFY, logical subscriptions, the document store, graph store, memory store, message log, checkpoint store, entity store, time-travel, scheduled jobs, sharding, and vector search. SubsystemKeywords Pub/subLISTEN, NOTIFY, UNLISTEN, CHANNEL, see LISTEN / NOTIFY Logical replicationSUBSCRIPTION, SELF, RESUME, ACK, UPTO, see CREATE SUBSCRIPTION Document storeDOCUMENT, STORE, see CREATE DOC STORE Graph storeGRAPH, NODE, EDGE, NEIGHBORS, DEPTH, RANK, see CREATE GRAPH STORE Memory storeMEMORY, EMBEDDING_DIM, DISTANCE, SEARCH, NAMESPACE, NAMESPACES, PREFIX, NS, PUT, GET, LIST, see CREATE MEMORY STORE, MEMORY PUT, MEMORY GET, MEMORY SEARCH Message logAPPEND, READ, WRITE, WRITES, RETENTION, TTL, TTL_COLUMN, see CREATE MESSAGE LOG Checkpoint storeCHECKPOINT, STORE, see CREATE CHECKPOINT STORE Entity storeENTITY, STORE, see CREATE ENTITY STORE Scheduled jobsJOB, JOBS, SCHEDULE, AT, THREAD, see CREATE JOB ShardingSHARD, SHARDS Time-travel / system versioningSYSTEM, SYSTEM_TIME, VERSIONING, see the temporal opener in SELECT Bulk loadCOPY, CSV, STDIN, STDOUT, HEADER, QUOTE, FORMAT, DELIMITER, ESCAPED, SEPARATOR, see COPY

sections.heading: C.4. Context-sensitive and special-cased words
sections.text: A plain word rule matches most keywords. A few words need special handling. The same spelling has to act in different ways based on what is around it. These cases explain parser behavior that can look surprising at first.

sections.heading: C.4.1. BETWEEN ... AND ...
sections.text: The word AND has two jobs. It is the boolean connective. It is also the separator inside a BETWEEN range. The lexer sorts this out with a start condition. When the scanner sees BETWEEN, it switches into a BTWMODE state. The next AND is then returned as the special range token. After that the scanner goes back to its starting state. Every other AND is the normal boolean connective token. -- the AND here is the BETWEEN separator, not a boolean connective SELECT * FROM orders WHERE total BETWEEN 10 AND 100;

sections.heading: C.4.2. Multi-word tokens
sections.text: A few things are matched as a single token even though you write them as two words with a space between them. They are not two separate keywords. They are one lexical unit. The patterns allow runs of spaces and tabs between the words. Two of them also allow newlines. NOT EXISTS Matched as one EXISTS token with a "negated" flag set. It is not the same as a bare EXISTS. A bare NOT followed by anything other than EXISTS is still the normal NOT keyword. ON DUPLICATE Matched as a single token. It starts the MySQL ON DUPLICATE KEY UPDATE upsert clause, see INSERT. NO ACTION Matched as one referential-action token. This is the default behavior for a foreign key.

sections.heading: C.4.3. Function-or-identifier words (trailing ( lookahead)
sections.text: Several words are keywords only when a ( comes right after them. In any other case they are normal identifiers. The lexer looks at the next character. If it is (, the word becomes the matching function token. If it is not, the word is returned as a plain NAME for a column or alias. This lets you use these names as identifiers. WordFollowed by (Otherwise COUNTaggregate COUNT(...)identifier NAME SUMaggregate SUM(...)identifier NAME AVGaggregate AVG(...)identifier NAME MINaggregate MIN(...)identifier NAME MAXaggregate MAX(...)identifier NAME RANKwindow function RANK()identifier NAME, and also a graph-store keyword IFscalar IF(...) functionIF control-flow keyword MODscalar MOD(...) functionMOD operator keyword YEARscalar YEAR(...) functionYEAR type keyword REPLACEstring REPLACE(...) functionREPLACE statement keyword This is why you can use count, sum, min, max, avg and rank as column names. If you write them right before a parenthesis, you call the function instead. -- "count" used as a column name (no following parenthesis) SELECT count FROM inventory; -- COUNT used as an aggregate (immediately followed by "(") SELECT COUNT(*) FROM inventory;

sections.heading: C.4.4. VALUE
sections.text: The word VALUE works in an unusual way. Its rule does not return a keyword token. Instead it returns an identifier token whose text is the string "VALUE". So VALUE is always treated as a normal name. It is never reserved. Its plural is VALUES. VALUES is a reserved keyword used by INSERT.

sections.heading: C.4.5. TRUE, FALSE, UNKNOWN, NULL
sections.text: The boolean literals return a single BOOL token. An integer value is attached to tell them apart. TRUE carries 1. FALSE carries 0. UNKNOWN carries -1 for three-valued logic. NULL returns the separate NULLX token. UNKNOWN appears in two rules. It can be a standalone keyword token or a boolean literal. The grammar position decides which one.

sections.heading: C.5. Using keywords as identifiers
sections.text: Sometimes you need a table, column, or alias whose name is the same as a reserved word. To do this, wrap the name in backticks. The lexer's quoted-identifier rule accepts `...`. It strips the backticks and returns the inner text as a plain NAME. This works even if that text would normally be a keyword. `any text except backtick / backslash / dot / newline` → identifier -- "order" and "select" are keywords; backticks make them column names CREATE TABLE t (`order` INT, `select` VARCHAR(20)); SELECT `order`, `select` FROM t; Note. A backtick-quoted identifier may not contain a backtick, backslash, dot, or newline. The rule's character class leaves those characters out. An opening backtick with no closing backtick before the end of the line raises "unterminated quoted name". EvolutionDB uses MySQL-style backticks for quoted identifiers. The double-quote character belongs to the string-literal rule, not the identifier rule. You cannot use it in place of a backtick to quote an identifier.

sections.heading: C.6. Built-in function names (call-position keywords)
sections.text: The lexer recognizes a large set of built-in function names. Each one is matched only when a ( comes right after it. When they are not in a call position, they are not reserved. They fall through to the identifier rule. They are listed apart from the keyword groups above and grouped here by purpose. String SUBSTR/SUBSTRING, TRIM, UPPER, LOWER, LENGTH, CHAR_LENGTH, TOKEN_LENGTH, CONCAT, LEFT, RIGHT, LPAD, RPAD, REVERSE, REPEAT, REPLACE, INSTR, LOCATE Conditional IF, IFNULL, NULLIF, COALESCE, CAST, CONVERT Numeric / math ABS, CEIL/CEILING, FLOOR, ROUND, POWER/POW, SQRT, RAND, LOG, LOG10, SIGN, PI, MOD Date / time NOW, DATE_ADD, DATE_SUB, DATEDIFF, YEAR; plus the constant openers CURRENT_DATE, CURRENT_TIME, CURRENT_TIMESTAMP, CURRENT_USER Aggregate / window COUNT, SUM, AVG, MIN, MAX, GROUP_CONCAT, ROW_NUMBER, RANK, DENSE_RANK, LEAD, LAG, NTILE, PERCENT_RANK, CUME_DIST JSON JSON_EXTRACT, JSON_UNQUOTE, JSON_TYPE, JSON_LENGTH, JSON_DEPTH, JSON_VALID, JSON_KEYS, JSON_PRETTY, JSON_QUOTE, JSON_SET, JSON_INSERT, JSON_REPLACE, JSON_REMOVE, JSON_CONTAINS, JSON_CONTAINS_PATH, JSON_SEARCH, JSON_OBJECT, JSON_ARRAY, JSON_ARRAYAGG Array / set-returning ARRAY_LENGTH, UNNEST Vector HNSW_KNN, HNSW_FILTER_KNN, HNSW_HYBRID_EXPLAIN, COSINE_DISTANCE, L2_DISTANCE, L1_DISTANCE, INNER_PRODUCT, VECTOR_DIM, VECTOR_NORM, VECTOR_NORMALIZE Sequences NEXTVAL, CURRVAL, SETVAL, LASTVAL Identity / ID generation LAST_INSERT_ID, SCOPE_IDENTITY, @@IDENTITY, @@LAST_INSERT_ID, GEN_RANDOM_UUID, GEN_RANDOM_UUID_V7, SNOWFLAKE_ID EvolutionDB internals EVO_CURRENT_XID, EVO_CALL_EXTERNAL, EVO_NOTIFY (alias PG_NOTIFY), PG_LISTENING_CHANNELS, EVO_SLEEP, EVO_JITTER Note. This list is only about the scanner. A word becomes a function token only when it sits right before (. The engine may or may not fully implement a given function. That is a separate matter. The relevant command and function pages cover it. This appendix only describes what the scanner recognizes.

sections.heading: C.7. Operator and punctuation tokens
sections.text: The scanner also turns the symbols below into tokens. They are not keywords. They complete what the lexer recognizes next to the word list above. The comparison operators all return the single COMPARISON token. A numeric sub-code is tagged on to show which operator was seen. SymbolToken / role =COMPARISON (equal) <, >, <=, >=COMPARISON (ordering) !=, <>COMPARISON (not equal) <=>COMPARISON (NULL-safe equal) &&boolean AND (same token as the keyword AND) ||boolean OR (same token as the keyword OR) <<, >>SHIFT (bit shift) :=ASSIGN ->, ->>JSON path operators (JSON_ARROW, JSON_ARROW_TEXT) <->vector L2-distance operator <#>vector negative-inner-product operator + - * / % & ~ | ^ ( ) , . ; ! [ ]arithmetic, bitwise, grouping, list, array-subscript and statement punctuation (returned as the literal character)

sections.heading: C.8. Compatibility
sections.text: EvolutionDB's keyword set mixes many dialects on purpose. Here are some honest contrasts with the systems it borrows spellings from. vs. the SQL standard. EvolutionDB has no formal reservation classes. The standard splits words into reserved words and non-reserved words. Non-reserved words can also be used as identifiers. EvolutionDB works differently. You can use any keyword as a backtick-quoted identifier. A small set of words also use the trailing-( trick of C.4.3. There is no concept of a word that is reserved "except as a function or type name" as the standard defines it. vs. PostgreSQL. PostgreSQL uses double quotes for identifiers and a keyword table sorted into groups. EvolutionDB uses MySQL-style backticks (C.5) and a flat, hand-maintained list. Many PostgreSQL spellings are still present. These include LATERAL, RETURNING, JSONB folded to the JSON token, UUID, MATERIALIZED, CONCURRENTLY, NEXTVAL/CURRVAL, GEN_RANDOM_UUID, and PG_NOTIFY as an alias. vs. MySQL. Many MySQL spellings are accepted. These include the integer aliases INT8, INT4, INT2, INT1, and MIDDLEINT, plus AUTO_INCREMENT, DISTINCTROW, STRAIGHT_JOIN, HIGH_PRIORITY/LOW_PRIORITY, SQL_BIG_RESULT and friends, the ON DUPLICATE KEY upsert, REGEXP/RLIKE, GROUP_CONCAT, and LAST_INSERT_ID. EvolutionDB-only. The extension keywords in C.3.6 are specific to EvolutionDB. They cover pub/sub, subscriptions, the document, graph, memory, message, checkpoint, and entity stores, scheduled jobs, sharding, and system-versioning. The vector operators <->, <#>, and <=> are also specific to EvolutionDB. Notes and caveats. This appendix matches the rules in evolution/parser/evolexer.l exactly as they are compiled into the parser. The keyword list is hand-written. It can drift between releases. After any lexer change, regenerate the parser with make generate and check this list again. A word being a keyword token does not mean the grammar accepts the whole statement. It also does not mean the engine fully implements the feature. The scanner only sorts words. The meaning lives on the individual command reference pages. A word is reserved everywhere. There is no per-statement reservation. If a word here is the same as a name you want, quote it with backticks (C.5). Some words show up in more than one rule on purpose. Examples are UNKNOWN, NODE, OF, STORE, RANK, and CHECKPOINT. Flex uses the first rule that matches. The order in the source file decides which token wins when the position is unclear.

sections.heading: See Also
sections.text: Appendix B. Data Types Appendix D. Error Codes SELECT CREATE TABLE

sections.heading: Introduction
sections.text: 31. evosql-cli evosql-cli is the interactive terminal client for EvolutionDB. It speaks the native EVO text protocol. It shows result sets as tables, CSV, or JSON. It supports backslash meta-commands. You can use it as an interactive shell or as a script runner. PostgreSQL's psql talks the binary PostgreSQL wire protocol on port 5432. evosql-cli is different. It connects over EvolutionDB's line-based EVO protocol on TCP port 9967. The two are not interchangeable. psql and other PostgreSQL clients like DBeaver connect to the server's PostgreSQL-compatible port 5433. evosql-cli is built for the EVO port. This page describes what the client really does. When a familiar psql feature is missing or works differently, the page says so.

sections.heading: 31.1. Invocation
sections.text: The client takes connection parameters. It also takes an optional one-shot command or a script file. If you give no command and no file, and a terminal is attached to standard input, the client starts an interactive shell. In every other case it runs in script mode. evosql-cli [OPTIONS] [-c SQL | -f FILE] -h, --host HOST The server hostname. The default is localhost. The name is resolved with gethostbyname(). If that fails, the client tries inet_addr() for a literal IPv4 address. -p, --port PORT The server EVO port. The default is 9967. This is the EVO port. It is not the PostgreSQL port 5433. -u, --user USER The username. The default is admin. -W, --password PW The password. If you leave it out, the client reads the EVOSQL_PASSWORD environment variable. If that is not set either and the server needs authentication, the client asks you for it. The password is not shown on screen while you type. -c, --command SQL Run the given SQL string and then exit. This forces script mode. -f, --file FILE Read SQL from FILE, run it, and then exit. This forces script mode. -F, --format FMT The output format. The choices are table, csv, and json. The default is table. An unknown value is rejected with exit code 2. -x, --expanded Use the expanded display. The client prints one column | value line for each field. It does not use an aligned grid. This is the same as the \x meta-command. It works with the table format only. --timing Print the client-side wall time after each statement. This is the same as \timing. --color / --no-color Turn ANSI colors on or off by force. The default is automatic. With the automatic default, colors appear only when the format is table and standard output is a TTY. On Windows, colors are always off. --help Print the usage text and exit with code 0. These examples show common ways to start the client. # interactive shell, default localhost:9967 as admin evosql-cli # connect to a remote host, prompt for password evosql-cli -h db.example.net -p 9967 -u alice # one-shot query, machine-readable output evosql-cli -F json -c "SELECT id, name FROM users" # run a script file evosql-cli -f schema.sql # pipe a script in on stdin (runs non-interactively) evosql-cli < schema.sql Password handling. The client checks for the password in this order. First it checks -W. Then it checks EVOSQL_PASSWORD. Then it shows an interactive prompt. A secret on the command line is visible to other users in the process table. For anything sensitive, use the environment variable or the prompt instead.

sections.heading: 31.2. Connecting and authentication
sections.text: When it starts, the client opens a TCP socket. Then it runs the EVO handshake. The client sends the line EVO. This says which protocol it uses. The server replies with a line that starts with HELLO. Any other greeting is a fatal error. The server then sends one of STARTTLS, AUTH_REQUIRED, or AUTH_SCRAM. TLS support is a compile-time option. If STARTTLS is offered and the client has TLS support, the connection is upgraded to TLS before any credentials are sent. The next message is the auth request. It is read over the encrypted channel. For AUTH_REQUIRED or AUTH_SCRAM, the client sends AUTH username password and waits for AUTH_OK. Any other reply means the login failed and the client exits. The password buffer and the AUTH message are wiped from memory right after the line is sent. This uses a volatile memset trick that the compiler cannot optimize away. This client advertises SCRAM but does not use it. The server may offer AUTH_SCRAM, which is SCRAM-SHA-256. Even then, evosql-cli answers with the cleartext AUTH form. The server accepts both forms. If the connection is not protected by TLS, the credentials travel over the wire in cleartext.

sections.heading: 31.2.1. TLS
sections.text: TLS is a compile-time option. Build the client with -DEVOSQL_CLI_TLS and link -lssl -lcrypto. You can also use make TLS=1. What happens at the STARTTLS step depends on whether that flag was set. Built with TLS The client accepts STARTTLS. It runs an OpenSSL handshake with TLS_client_method(). Then it does the login over the encrypted channel. On success it prints [TLS] Encrypted connection established to standard error. The interactive banner shows (TLS). Built without TLS The client sends NOTLS to decline. It continues in plaintext. It prints a warning that the credentials will be sent in cleartext. It also suggests make TLS=1. No certificate check. The TLS client creates a context and connects. It does not check the server certificate or hostname. This stops passive eavesdropping. It does not stop an active man-in-the-middle attack. There is no setting like psql's sslmode=verify-full.

sections.heading: 31.3. The interactive shell
sections.text: In interactive mode the client does several things at start. It prints a banner. It loads the command history. It fills the tab-completion cache. It installs its SIGINT handler. Then it enters the read loop. The main prompt is evosql>. While a statement is still being built up, the continuation prompt is ...>. Connected to EvoSQL at localhost:9967 Type SQL (end with ';'), or \? for help, \q to quit. evosql> SELECT 1 AS n; n - 1 (1 row) evosql>

sections.heading: 31.3.1. Statement assembly and the semicolon
sections.text: A SQL statement ends with a semicolon. It does not end when you press Enter. Lines are added to a buffer. The client keeps reading until the last meaningful character in the buffer is a ; that sits outside any quoted string. The last meaningful character is the last one that is not whitespace and not part of a comment. A semicolon inside a string literal does not submit the statement. It keeps the continuation prompt instead. evosql> INSERT INTO t(msg) ...> VALUES ('a; b; c'); INSERT 0 1 (1 row) The EVO protocol sends each query as a single line. So newlines and carriage returns inside a multi-line statement are turned into spaces before the statement is sent. The client tracks quotes. It tells single quotes ' apart from double quotes ". A -- line comment outside quotes runs to the end of the line.

sections.heading: 31.3.2. Quitting
sections.text: There are a few ways to leave the shell. You can use the \q meta-command. You can type the word quit or exit on its own. Case does not matter for these words. They are kept because they feel familiar. You can also use end-of-file, which is Ctrl-D on an empty line. If the multi-line buffer is not empty, Ctrl-D throws away the pending buffer and shows the prompt again. It does not exit.

sections.heading: 31.3.3. Ctrl-C, cancel, not quit
sections.text: evosql-cli never quits on Ctrl-C. What it does depends on what the client is doing at the time. At the prompt while typing Ctrl-C drops the current line and any pending multi-line buffer. It draws a fresh prompt, like a shell does. On POSIX this works by long-jumping out of readline after the terminal is cleaned up. rl_catch_signals is turned off, so readline never raises the signal again. While a query is running The EVO protocol has no in-band query-cancel message. So the client sets an interrupt flag. It drops the socket, which is now out of sync. Then it reconnects a fresh session on its own and prints query canceled. The connection parameters are saved at startup so this reconnect can happen quietly. The canceled statement may have changed the schema. If it contained CREATE, DROP, or ALTER, the completion cache is refreshed afterward. This is a real difference from psql. In PostgreSQL, Ctrl-C sends a cancel request on a side channel. The same session keeps going. In EvolutionDB the only way to stop a running EVO query from the prompt is to drop and reopen the socket. So any uncommitted transaction state on the old connection is lost. See transaction control.

sections.heading: 31.3.4. Command history
sections.text: History is saved to ~/.evosql_history. On Windows it is saved to %USERPROFILE%\.evosql_history. The file holds at most 500 entries. On POSIX the client uses GNU readline for history. This gives you arrow-key recall and in-line editing. On Windows the client has its own readline replacement built on the Win32 console API. It has the same 500-entry limit, up and down recall, and basic line editing. Secrets stay out of history. A finished statement may contain the PASSWORD keyword on its own. Such a statement is kept out of the history file on disk. So a cleartext password from a command like CREATE USER never reaches the disk. The keyword check looks at any non-identifier character around it. So a form like PASSWORD'secret' is caught too.

sections.heading: 31.3.5. Tab completion
sections.text: Tab completion works on POSIX only, where the client uses GNU readline. When you press Tab, the client completes against a fixed list of SQL keywords and a cached list of table names. Matching ignores the case of the prefix you typed. There is no fallback to filenames. The table-name cache is filled at startup with SHOW TABLES. It is refreshed after any statement that contains CREATE, DROP, or ALTER. It is also refreshed after a reconnect that follows a canceled DDL statement. So new tables appear and dropped tables disappear without a restart. The keyword set is fixed. It includes the common DML and DDL words, join keywords, aggregate names, and a few type names. SELECT FROM WHERE INSERT INTO VALUES UPDATE SET DELETE CREATE TABLE DROP ALTER INDEX JOIN LEFT RIGHT INNER OUTER ON GROUP BY ORDER HAVING LIMIT OFFSET DISTINCT AS AND OR NOT NULL LIKE IN BETWEEN PRIMARY KEY FOREIGN REFERENCES DEFAULT UNIQUE CHECK BEGIN COMMIT ROLLBACK DATABASE SCHEMA USER GRANT REVOKE SHOW TABLES DATABASES USERS COUNT SUM AVG MIN MAX VARCHAR INT INTEGER TIMESTAMP BOOLEAN TEXT Tab completion is not available in the Windows console build.

sections.heading: 31.4. Output formats
sections.text: You set the result format with -F or --format at startup. You can also change it with the \format meta-command while the client runs. The client supports three formats.

sections.heading: 31.4.1. table (default)
sections.text: This is an aligned grid. It has a bold header row. Then comes a dimmed -+- rule. Then the rows. At the end there is a dimmed (N row[s]) count. Column widths come from the widest value or header. A NULL value shows as the dimmed word NULL. In this format, status tags and the row count go to standard output. When \x expanded mode is on, each record gets a -[ RECORD n ]- banner. After it comes one column | value line for each field. This is handy for wide rows.

sections.heading: 31.4.2. csv
sections.text: This is CSV in the RFC 4180 style. It starts with a header row of column names. Then there is one line per row. A field is quoted only if it contains a comma, a double quote, CR, or LF. A double quote inside a field is doubled. A NULL value comes out as an empty field. You cannot tell it apart from an empty string. In CSV mode and JSON mode, only the data goes to standard output. Status tags, the row count, errors, and timing go to standard error. So a redirected stream holds clean data. evosql-cli -F csv -c "SELECT id, name FROM users" > users.csv

sections.heading: 31.4.3. json
sections.text: This is a JSON array of objects. There is one object per row. Each object is keyed by column name. Strings use only the needed escapes. These are \", \\, \n, \r, \t, and \u00XX for other control characters. A NULL value comes out as JSON null. A value comes out as an unquoted JSON number only when two things are both true. First, the column's PostgreSQL type OID is numeric. Second, the value's text is a strict JSON number. If either is not true, the value is quoted. These are the numeric OIDs the client knows. OIDType 20int8 (bigint) 21int2 (smallint) 23int4 (integer) 26oid 700float4 (real) 701float8 (double precision) 1700numeric The OID for each column comes from the EVO COL line. Its form is <name> <pg_type_oid> <type_modifier>. The column name itself may contain spaces. So the client counts in from the end and drops the two trailing integer fields. It reads the OID from the second-from-last token. evosql-cli -F json -c "SELECT id, name FROM users LIMIT 1" [ {"id": 1, "name": "alice"} ] Limits to know. In CSV you cannot tell NULL from an empty string. The whole result set is held in memory before it is printed. There is no streaming and no paging. The internal row buffer starts at 64 rows and doubles when it needs more. There is no fixed cut-off in the EVO read path. The JSON output is not pretty-printed beyond one object per line.

sections.heading: 31.5. Meta-commands
sections.text: A line that starts with a backslash is a meta-command. A SQL statement ends with a semicolon. A meta-command is different. It ends with the newline. A trailing semicolon is allowed but not needed. Run \? for the built-in summary. Here is the full set the client supports. \?, \h, \help Print the meta-command reference. \d, \dt With no argument, this lists tables. It runs SHOW TABLES. With a name, it describes that table's columns. It queries information_schema.columns for column_name, data_type, is_nullable. See SHOW. \l List databases. This runs SHOW DATABASES. See CREATE DATABASE. \du List users. This runs SHOW USERS. See CREATE USER. \dn List schemas. This runs SHOW SCHEMAS. \x Turn the expanded display on or off. It shows one column per line. It prints the new state. \timing Turn per-statement timing on or off. It prints the new state. \format F Set the output format to table, csv, or json. Case does not matter. Any other argument prints a usage line. \i FILE Read FILE and run it as a script. It uses the same splitter as -f. The file may contain meta-commands too. It may even contain a nested \i. \e Open $EDITOR on a private temporary file made with mkstemp. The default editor is vi. When the editor exits cleanly, the file's contents are run as a script. This works on POSIX only. On Windows it prints that it is not supported. \! CMD Run CMD in the system shell. \q, \quit Quit the client. Naming notes next to psql. The verbs are made to feel like psql. But the set is small and a few work a bit differently. \d and \dt are aliases. Both list tables. \d NAME describes columns. \l, \du, and \dn are thin wrappers over the matching SHOW command. There is no \copy. There is no \g. There is no variable system like \set. There is no \connect either. The connection is fixed for the life of the process. The one exception is the quiet reconnect after a Ctrl-C cancel.

sections.heading: 31.6. Scripting and non-interactive use
sections.text: The client runs in script mode in a few cases. It does so when you pass -c or -f. It also does so when standard input is not a terminal, for example when it is piped or redirected. In this mode it runs the SQL you gave. Then it sends a QUIT line, closes the connection, and exits.

sections.heading: 31.6.1. The statement splitter
sections.text: Script text can come from -c, -f, piped stdin, \i, or \e. It is split into statements by the same scanner used in interactive mode. That scanner is aware of quotes and comments. The kind of each statement is fixed by its first non-blank character. A line that starts with \ is a meta-command. It ends at the newline. Anything else is SQL. It ends at an unquoted ;. A -- line comment outside quotes runs to the end of the line. Inside a statement it becomes a single separating space. A comment line on its own is dropped. Single and double quotes are tracked. So semicolons and comment markers inside string literals are not treated as syntax. Newlines and carriage returns inside a SQL statement become spaces. The EVO protocol expects a single-line query. A last statement with no final ; or newline is still run. -- schema.sql CREATE TABLE t (id INT PRIMARY KEY, name VARCHAR(50)); INSERT INTO t VALUES (1, 'alice'), (2, 'bob'); SELECT count(*) FROM t;

sections.heading: 31.6.2. Exit status
sections.text: The script-mode exit status is built so that CI jobs and shell scripts can detect a failed query. 0 All statements ran. The server reported no error. 1 Something went wrong. A statement failed and the run hit a non-zero internal code. This happens with an unreadable -f file, a lost connection, or a Ctrl-C cancel. It also happens when the server returned an ERR for any statement. An error-seen flag tracks this. The code stays 1 even if later statements succeed. 2 An invalid --format argument was given. The client exits before it connects. evosql-cli -c "SELECT 1" && echo ok || echo failed Errors do not stop a script. In script mode a server ERR sets the error flag. The run keeps going to the next statement. At the end the process still exits 1. A lost connection with code -1 does stop the script. A Ctrl-C cancel with code -2 also stops it. This is different from psql's default and from its ON_ERROR_STOP. There is no equivalent setting here.

sections.heading: 31.7. Status tags, errors, and timing
sections.text: The EVO protocol reports the result of each statement in one of three ways. It can send a command tag, for example INSERT 0 1, SELECT, or CREATE TABLE. It can send an error line. Or it can send a result set. The client shows these as follows. Command tag Printed as is. In table format it goes to standard output. In csv and json it goes to standard error. This keeps the data stream clean. Error Printed as ERROR: message to standard error. It is red when color is on. The error-seen flag is set. This lets script-mode runs exit with a non-zero code. Row count A (N row[s]) footer follows each result set. It goes to the same place as the command tag. That is stdout for table and stderr otherwise. Timing When \timing or --timing is on, a Time: X.X ms line is printed to standard error after each statement. This is the client-side wall time around the request and response. It is measured with clock(). It is not an execution time reported by the server.

sections.heading: 31.8. Type fidelity
sections.text: For display, the client does not care about types. Every value arrives as text on a FIELD line. It is printed as is in the table and csv formats. There is only one place where type info is used. That place is JSON output. There the column's PostgreSQL type OID decides whether a number-like value is printed without quotes. The OID is carried on the EVO COL line. See §31.4.3. For the full type system and how values are stored, see the SQL reference and the data-type chapters. For query syntax see SELECT, INSERT, UPDATE, and DELETE.

sections.heading: 31.9. Notes and limitations
sections.text: EVO port only. evosql-cli connects to the EVO text protocol on port 9967. To use the PostgreSQL wire protocol on port 5433, use psql or any PostgreSQL-compatible client. evosql-cli cannot talk to that port. No in-band query cancel. When you cancel a running query, the client drops and reopens the connection. Any uncommitted transaction state on that session is lost. SCRAM not done on the client side. The client always logs in with the cleartext AUTH form. It does so even when the server advertises SCRAM-SHA-256. TLS does not check certificates. When built with TLS, the handshake stops passive eavesdropping only. Results are fully buffered. A large result set is read into memory before it is shown. There is no paging and no streaming output. One fixed connection. There is no \connect, no way to switch connections, and no variables. The host, port, user, and password are fixed for the life of the process. NULL and empty string in CSV. Both show as an empty field. You can tell them apart only in json, where NULL is null, and in table, where NULL prints as NULL. Platform differences. Tab completion and \e work on POSIX only. The Windows console build has history and basic line editing, but not these features.

sections.heading: Introduction
sections.text: 30. Connecting (PG wire & EVO) EvolutionDB accepts client connections on two protocols at the same time. The first is the PostgreSQL frontend/backend protocol version 3.0 on default TCP port 5433. The second is the native line-oriented EVO text protocol on default TCP port 9967. Both check the same user catalog before any query is accepted. A single evosql-server process starts one listener thread for each protocol. The PostgreSQL wire listener lets you connect with psql, JDBC/ODBC drivers, DBeaver, pgAdmin, and any PostgreSQL client library. The EVO listener is used by evosql-cli and by the EvoSQL language drivers. It is easier to parse. It also offers server-side prepared statements and batch execution that the simple SQL path does not have. This chapter shows only what EvolutionDB actually does. Some PostgreSQL features are missing or work in a different way. We point out each difference clearly. We do not pretend a feature works when it does not.

sections.heading: 30.1. Listeners and ports
sections.text: The server binds two ports at startup. The defaults and the flags that change them are shown below. ProtocolDefault portOverride flagTypical clients PostgreSQL wire v3.05433--pg-port PORTpsql, JDBC, ODBC, DBeaver, pgAdmin, psycopg EVO native text9967--evo-port PORTevosql-cli, EvoSQL drivers The same process starts both listeners. The PG listener runs on the main thread. The EVO listener runs on a background thread. Each new connection is handled in its own thread. # Start the dual-protocol server with default ports ./adaptor/evosql-server # Move the PG listener to 5434, leave EVO on 9967 ./adaptor/evosql-server --pg-port 5434 # A bare numeric argument is accepted as the PG port (backward compat) ./adaptor/evosql-server 5433 Note. The standard PostgreSQL port is 5432. EvolutionDB uses 5433 by default. This way it can run next to a real PostgreSQL instance on the same host. Set your client to port 5433. You can also use the port you chose with --pg-port. If you connect to 5432, you reach PostgreSQL and not EvolutionDB.

sections.heading: 30.2. Credentials
sections.text: Both protocols check the username and password against the database user catalog. They both use the same internal routine called AuthenticateUser. Passwords are stored with PBKDF2-SHA256. To learn how accounts are created and hashed, see CREATE USER and Authentication & users. EVOSQL_USER_NAME Bootstrap superuser name. Defaults to admin. EVOSQL_PASSWORD Bootstrap superuser password. If you do not set it, the server makes a random password. It prints this password to the server log one time at first start. Copy it from that line. You can also set this variable before you start the server to use a known value. The default test database is testdb. The test and example logins in this manual use admin / admin. They work with a server started with EVOSQL_PASSWORD=admin. Note. The user value in the PG startup packet picks the account to log in. There is no anonymous path and no trust path. A connection without a valid password is rejected before the query loop starts. Both protocols report login failures with SQLSTATE 28P01.

sections.heading: 30.3. PostgreSQL wire protocol (port 5433)
sections.text: EvolutionDB uses the PostgreSQL frontend/backend protocol version 3.0. The protocol number is 196608. A startup message that asks for any other version is rejected. The handshake works as described below.

sections.heading: 30.3.1. Startup and negotiation
sections.text: Before the regular startup packet, a client can send one of the special request codes. EvolutionDB handles them like this. RequestCodeServer response SSLRequest80877103The server sends S and then runs a TLS handshake if TLS is built in and available. If not, it sends N and the connection stays in plaintext. After this step the server reads the startup packet again. GSSENCRequest80877104Always declined with N. The startup packet is read again. GSSAPI encryption is not supported. CancelRequest80877102Finds the target session by its PID and secret. Sets that session's cancel flag. Then closes this connection. This follows the PG spec. See Query cancellation. StartupMessage196608Parses startup parameters, extracts user, and begins authentication.

sections.heading: 30.3.2. Authentication
sections.text: After a valid v3.0 StartupMessage, the server always sends an AuthenticationCleartextPassword request. It waits for a PasswordMessage ('p') in reply. The password is checked with AuthenticateUser. On success the server sends AuthenticationOk. On failure it sends a FATAL error with SQLSTATE 28P01 (password authentication failed). Note. The PG path uses cleartext password login only. MD5 and SCRAM-SHA-256 are not offered on the wire here. Real PostgreSQL uses scram-sha-256 by default. The password travels over the wire in clear text. So you should turn on TLS with SSLRequest. This keeps the password encrypted while it moves. The native EVO protocol offers SCRAM and does not send the password. See below.

sections.heading: 30.3.3. Parameters, key data, and ReadyForQuery
sections.text: After a successful login, the server sends a fixed set of ParameterStatus messages. Some drivers change their behavior based on server settings. These messages make sure those drivers work correctly. server_version15.0 (EvoSQL). This is the protocol-compatibility version shown to clients. server_encoding / client_encodingBoth UTF8. DateStyleISO, MDY integer_datetimeson standard_conforming_stringson TimeZoneUTC is_superuseron session_authorizationThe logged-in user name. IntervalStylepostgres Next the server sends BackendKeyData. This message carries the session-registry ID. The session-registry ID is used as the PID. It also carries a random 32-bit cancel key. After that the server sends ReadyForQuery. The transaction-status byte in ReadyForQuery can be three values. I means idle. T means inside a transaction block. E means a failed transaction that waits for ROLLBACK.

sections.heading: 30.3.4. Simple Query protocol
sections.text: A Query message ('Q') can carry several statements split by semicolons. The handler splits on semicolons but is careful with some cases. It respects single quotes, double quotes, and SQL line comments (-- ...). It also respects BEGIN ... END nesting. This way a procedure body is not cut at a semicolon inside it. The server sends one response for each statement. Then it sends a single ReadyForQuery. SELECT results are streamed one row at a time during the scan. psql "host=127.0.0.1 port=5433 user=admin dbname=testdb sslmode=disable" testdb=> CREATE TABLE t (id INT, name VARCHAR(50)); testdb=> INSERT INTO t VALUES (1, 'alice'), (2, 'bob'); testdb=> SELECT id, name FROM t WHERE id = 1;

sections.heading: 30.3.5. Extended Query protocol
sections.text: The Parse / Bind / Describe / Execute / Sync / Close sequence is supported. JDBC and psycopg's auto-prepare use this sequence. Here are the main behaviors that are special to EvolutionDB. Named statements are kept. A Parse stores the query text under a statement name. A connection can hold up to 64 named statements. A later Bind and Execute can reuse the name with no Parse before it. In that case the server rebuilds the query from the stored template. Unnamed statements use the empty name and can be replaced freely. Parameter substitution is done as text. Bound $1, $2, and so on are placed into the SQL string. A NULL parameter becomes the literal NULL. All other values are single-quoted, and quotes inside them are doubled. The server handles up to 64 parameters per Bind. Binary parameter formats are decoded for the common fixed widths. These are 4-byte int32, 2-byte int16, 8-byte int64, and 1-byte boolean. They are then turned into text. Other binary inputs are copied through as they are. Describe of a portal or statement returns a RowDescription for a SELECT with columns. Otherwise it returns NoData. Describe('S') first sends a ParameterDescription that reports zero parameter type OIDs. Close('S', name) frees the named prepared statement and drops its stored template. Close('P', …) for a portal is acknowledged but keeps nothing. Note. Parameters are bound by placing them into the string. They are not bound as separate typed values. The server handles quoting and escaping for you. But the server does not do typed parameter binding. So it does not do the type negotiation that comes with it. RowDescription reports 0 parameter OIDs in ParameterDescription. So drivers must not rely on the server to declare parameter types.

sections.heading: 30.3.6. COPY over the wire
sections.text: Both COPY ... FROM STDIN and COPY ... TO STDOUT work. They use the standard CopyIn/CopyOut subprotocol. This means CopyInResponse, CopyData, CopyDone, and CopyFail. A failed CopyIn returns SQLSTATE 57014. An unexpected message during CopyIn returns 08P01. See COPY.

sections.heading: 30.4. EVO native protocol (port 9967)
sections.text: The EVO protocol is a text protocol that uses newlines to split lines. It is made for the EvoSQL CLI and drivers. It works as request and response. The server sends a clear READY marker after every command result.

sections.heading: 30.4.1. Greeting, TLS, and authentication
sections.text: The exchange starts with a greeting. It can upgrade to TLS before any password is sent. The steps are below. The client sends the line EVO and nothing more. Anything else is rejected with ERR 08000. The server replies HELLO EvoSQL 1.0. If TLS is available, the server sends STARTTLS. If the client answers STARTTLS, the TLS handshake runs. If it answers anything else, the session stays in plaintext for backward compatibility. The server sends AUTH_SCRAM to ask for SCRAM-SHA-256. The client then picks one of two login flows. SCRAM-SHA-256 (preferred) The client sends SCRAM-CLIENT-FIRST <data>. The server replies with the server-first message. The client sends SCRAM-CLIENT-FINAL <data>. The server checks the proof and returns the server-final message. The server-final message holds the ServerSignature for mutual authentication. The password is never sent. So this is safe even over plaintext. Legacy cleartext (backward compat) The client sends AUTH <username> <password>. The password is checked directly. Use SCRAM instead, or wrap the session in TLS. On success the server sends AUTH_OK and starts the command loop. Login errors use SQLSTATE 28P01 for bad credentials or 28000 for an unexpected auth message. # Connect with the bundled CLI (EVO, port 9967) ./cli/evosql-cli # defaults to localhost:9967 ./cli/evosql-cli -W admin # prompt for a password

sections.heading: 30.4.2. Running SQL with the SQL command
sections.text: A query is sent as a length header line followed by the SQL body line. The format is shown below. SQL <byte_length> <sql text> The length must be in the range 1..65000. A value out of this range gives ERR 42000. A SELECT result is returned as a structured block shown below. RESULT COLS <n> COL <name> <type_oid> <type_modifier> # one per column ROW FIELD <value> # or: FIELD NULL ... END TAG <command_tag> READY A non-SELECT statement returns OK and then TAG <command_tag>. One example is TAG INSERT 0 2. Errors return ERR <sqlstate> <message>. Every command ends with a READY line, whether it succeeds or fails. Sending QUIT ends the session with BYE.

sections.heading: 30.4.3. Server-side prepared statements and batches
sections.text: EVO offers named prepared statements right on the protocol. You do not need a SQL-level PREPARE. A connection can hold up to 32 statements. Placeholders are $1…$9+. The parameter count comes from the highest placeholder that is seen. CommandPurpose PREPARE <name> <byte_len> + bodyStores a parameterized statement under name. Replies OK / TAG PREPARE. EXECUTE <name> <n> + n param linesFills in the parameters and runs once. Use the line \N for a SQL NULL. Other values are single-quoted, and quotes inside them are doubled. EXECUTE_BATCH <name> <rows> <cols> + param linesRuns the statement once for each row. Replies BATCH_OK <total_affected> or BATCH_ERR <row_index> <sqlstate> <msg>. EXECUTE_BATCH_BINARY <name> <rows> <cols> + binary payloadSame as above. But each parameter is sent as a typed binary value. The reply is the same BATCH_OK / BATCH_ERR. DEALLOCATE <name>Frees a prepared statement. Replies OK / TAG DEALLOCATE. Replies ERR 26000 if it is not found. The binary batch payload encodes each parameter as a one-byte type code followed by its big-endian value. The type codes are listed below. CodeTypePayload NNULLno bytes. Sent as SQL NULL iint44 bytes, big-endian lint88 bytes, big-endian dfloat88 bytes, big-endian IEEE-754 bbool1 byte. A value of 0 means false sstring4-byte big-endian length, then UTF-8 bytes with no NUL. Max 16 MB For batches, the affected-row counts are added up from the INSERT, UPDATE, and DELETE command tags. A batch stops at the first row that fails. It reports that row's index in BATCH_ERR. Note. EVO prepared statements work like the PG extended path. They place parameters as quoted SQL text. They do not bind typed values on the server. The binary batch path still decodes typed values. But it turns them into text before it places them. There is a hard limit of 32 prepared statements per EVO connection. If you go over it, you get ERR 53000. The PG path allows 64 named statements.

sections.heading: 30.4.4. COPY over EVO
sections.text: Run a COPY ... FROM STDIN through the SQL command and the server replies COPY_READY. The client then streams one CSV line per row. It ends with \. on its own line. This follows the PostgreSQL convention. For COPY ... TO STDOUT the server sends COPY_READY. Then it sends one D:<line> per row. Then it sends a \. terminator. Both finish with OK / TAG COPY <n>. See COPY.

sections.heading: 30.5. Session lifetime and limits
sections.text: Sessions on both protocols share the same lifecycle handling. Default isolation is READ COMMITTED. See SET TRANSACTION and MVCC & isolation. Idle timeout. You can set a session idle timeout. If you do, a connection that sends no data within the time window is closed. The SQLSTATE is 57P05. The message is terminating connection due to idle timeout on PG and idle timeout on EVO. Auto-rollback on disconnect. If a client drops while a transaction is open, the server rolls it back. It releases any SERIALIZABLE lock. It frees the undo log. Temporary objects. Session-scoped temporary tables and global-temp-table contents are dropped on their own when the session ends. LISTEN/NOTIFY. Asynchronous notifications are delivered between replies. On PG they come as a standard NotificationResponse. On EVO they come as a NOTIFY <sender_id> <channel> <len> framed line. See LISTEN / NOTIFY.

sections.heading: 30.6. Query cancellation
sections.text: On the PG protocol, BackendKeyData gives the client a PID and a random 32-bit secret. The PID is the session-registry ID. To cancel a running query, the client opens a second connection. It sends a CancelRequest with code 80877102 that carries that PID and secret. The server finds the session by PID and secret. It sets the cancel flag. Then it closes the cancel connection right away. This is exactly what the PostgreSQL spec says. Note. The cancel key is made fresh for each connection. It comes from a cryptographic random source and is forced to be non-zero. So a leaked key only affects the one session it was given to. The EVO protocol has no separate cancel channel. To cancel there, you rely on the idle timeout and on closing the connection.

sections.heading: 30.7. SQLSTATE codes seen during connect
sections.text: Below are the codes a client is most likely to see during connection setup and in the protocol layer. These are not query execution codes. SQLSTATEMeaningWhere 28P01Password authentication failed / invalid SCRAM proofPG & EVO auth 28000Unexpected authentication messageEVO auth 08000Expected EVO greetingEVO handshake 08006Transport read error, for example during COPY or batchPG & EVO 08P01Unexpected protocol message during COPY INPG 57P05Connection terminated due to idle timeoutPG & EVO 57014COPY from stdin failed with CopyFailPG 42000Invalid command or argument, such as a bad SQL length or a missing nameEVO 53000Too many prepared statements. The max is 32EVO 26000Prepared statement not foundEVO

sections.heading: 30.8. See also
sections.text: Authentication & users. How accounts are created and how passwords are hashed. CREATE USER and GRANT. Privileges are checked after you connect. COPY. Bulk load and unload over both protocols. PREPARE and EXECUTE. SQL-level prepared statements. LISTEN / NOTIFY. Asynchronous notifications. MVCC & isolation and SET TRANSACTION.

sections.heading: Introduction
sections.text: EvolutionDB Documentation This is the official reference manual for EvolutionDB. EvolutionDB is a SQL engine. It is built to be long-term memory for AI agents. This manual covers version . infoEvolutionDB has only one version line today. That line is 3.0. The version selector is at the top left. It will list more versions when we release them. Each version will have its own copy of this manual.

sections.heading: Manual
sections.text: school

sections.heading: I · Tutorial
sections.text: Install EvolutionDB, connect to it, and run your first queries. database

sections.heading: II · The SQL Language
sections.text: DDL, DML, queries, types, indexes, MVCC, memory, and vectors. settings

sections.heading: III · Administration
sections.text: Server config, roles, encryption, backup, and maintenance. cable

sections.heading: IV · Client Interfaces
sections.text: The PostgreSQL wire protocol, the EVO protocol, and the CLI. menu_book

sections.heading: Reference
sections.text: Every SQL command. Each one has a synopsis, parameters, and examples. bookmark

sections.heading: Appendixes

sections.heading: Introduction
sections.text: ALTER TABLE Change the definition of a table that already exists. You can add, drop, rename, or retype columns. You can add, drop, rename, enable, disable, or validate constraints. You can turn row-level security on or off. You can also set or drop a TTL column.

sections.heading: Synopsis
sections.text: ALTER TABLE table_name action where action is one of: -- columns ADD COLUMN column_name data_type [ column_attribute ... ] [ FIRST | AFTER existing_column ] DROP COLUMN column_name DROP column_name RENAME COLUMN old_column TO new_column RENAME old_column TO new_column MODIFY COLUMN column_name data_type [ column_attribute ... ] [ FIRST | AFTER existing_column ] MODIFY column_name data_type [ column_attribute ... ] [ FIRST | AFTER existing_column ] CHANGE COLUMN old_column new_column data_type [ column_attribute ... ] [ FIRST | AFTER existing_column ] CHANGE old_column new_column data_type [ column_attribute ... ] [ FIRST | AFTER existing_column ] -- constraints ADD CONSTRAINT constraint_name CHECK ( expression ) [ NOT VALIDATE ] ADD CONSTRAINT constraint_name UNIQUE ( column [, ...] ) ADD CONSTRAINT constraint_name PRIMARY KEY ( column [, ...] ) ADD CONSTRAINT constraint_name FOREIGN KEY ( column [, ...] ) REFERENCES ref_table ( ref_column [, ...] ) [ referential_action ... ] [ NOT VALIDATE ] DROP CONSTRAINT constraint_name RENAME CONSTRAINT old_name TO new_name ENABLE CONSTRAINT constraint_name DISABLE CONSTRAINT constraint_name VALIDATE CONSTRAINT constraint_name -- row-level security ENABLE ROW LEVEL SECURITY DISABLE ROW LEVEL SECURITY -- time-to-live SET TTL_COLUMN = 'column_name' DROP TTL and referential_action is one of: ON DELETE { CASCADE | SET NULL | SET DEFAULT | RESTRICT | NO ACTION } ON UPDATE { CASCADE | SET NULL | SET DEFAULT | RESTRICT | NO ACTION } MATCH { FULL | PARTIAL | SIMPLE } [ NOT ] DEFERRABLE [ INITIALLY { DEFERRED | IMMEDIATE } ]

sections.heading: Description
sections.text: ALTER TABLE changes the definition of a table that already exists. Each statement does exactly one action. EvolutionDB does not accept a list of actions separated by commas in one ALTER TABLE command. Write a separate statement for each change. If the named table does not exist, the command fails. It returns relation "table_name" does not exist with SQLSTATE 42P01. All actions work on the system catalog. The catalog stores tables, columns, and constraints in B+ trees. The column actions use two different spellings. ADD COLUMN, RENAME COLUMN ... TO, and DROP COLUMN use the ANSI and PostgreSQL spelling. MODIFY and CHANGE use the MySQL spelling. The COLUMN keyword is optional for DROP, RENAME, MODIFY, and CHANGE. MODIFY changes the type of a column in place. CHANGE renames and retypes a column in one statement. Adding, dropping, and reordering columns can rewrite the existing rows. ADD COLUMN with a FIRST or AFTER position moves the column in the catalog. It then rebuilds every tuple and fills the new column with NULL. MODIFY and CHANGE with a position clause also rewrite all tuples in the new column order. DROP COLUMN marks the column as dropped in the catalog. It does not delete the data right away. Adding a CHECK, PRIMARY KEY, or UNIQUE constraint checks the existing rows first. The command stops if any row breaks the new constraint. You can skip this check with NOT VALIDATE, which works for CHECK and FOREIGN KEY only. NOT VALIDATE records the constraint as not-validated and skips the row scan. You can run VALIDATE CONSTRAINT later to do the check that was skipped. On success, column, TTL, and RLS actions report ALTER TABLE. Constraint actions report command(s) completed successfully!...

sections.heading: Parameters
sections.text: table_name The name of the table you want to change. The name is looked up in the current database and schema. ADD COLUMN column_name data_type [ column_attribute.. ] Adds a new column to the table. The column goes at the end unless you give a position clause. The command fails with 42701 if a column with that name already exists. It fails with 54011 if the table already has the maximum number of columns. The attributes are the same as in CREATE TABLE. They include NOT NULL, NULL, DEFAULT, UNIQUE, AUTO_INCREMENT / IDENTITY, CHECK (...), COMMENT, and GENERATED ALWAYS AS (...) STORED|VIRTUAL. A DEFAULT can be a literal, CURRENT_TIMESTAMP, gen_random_uuid(), gen_random_uuid_v7(), snowflake_id(), or an expression. A column added this way is never made part of the primary key. The command fails with 23502 when the column is NOT NULL, has no DEFAULT, and the table already holds rows. This is because the existing rows would get a NULL. FIRST | AFTER existing_column An optional position clause for ADD, MODIFY, and CHANGE. FIRST puts the column at ordinal 0. AFTER existing_column puts it right after the named column. When you choose a position, every row is rewritten into the new physical column order. DROP COLUMN column_name / DROP column_name Marks a column as dropped. The command fails with 42703 if the column does not exist. It fails with 2BP01 in a few cases. These are when the column is part of the primary key, when it is the only active column left, when a secondary index uses it, or when a FOREIGN KEY or UNIQUE constraint uses it. RENAME COLUMN old_column TO new_column / RENAME old_column TO new_column Gives a column a new name. The COLUMN keyword is optional. MODIFY [COLUMN] column_name data_type [ column_attribute.. ] Changes the type or attributes of a column that already exists. The command fails with 42703 if the column does not exist. It fails with 2BP01 if the column is part of the primary key and its type would change. It fails with 42804 if the old type and the new type cannot be converted automatically. The new definition may add NOT NULL to a column that allowed NULL before. In that case the table is scanned. The command then fails with 23502 if any row holds a NULL in that column. CHANGE [COLUMN] old_column new_column data_type [ column_attribute.. ] A MySQL-style action that renames and retypes a column at the same time. It renames old_column to new_column when the names differ. It then applies the new type and attributes. It runs the same checks as MODIFY. A position clause works here too. ADD CONSTRAINT constraint_name CHECK ( expression ) Adds a named CHECK constraint. Every existing row is tested against expression. If any row fails, the command stops with 23514 and reports the row number that failed. The command fails with 42710 if a constraint with the same name already exists. ADD CONSTRAINT constraint_name UNIQUE ( column [..] ) Adds a named UNIQUE constraint over one or more columns. The command fails with 42710 if the constraint name is already used. ADD CONSTRAINT constraint_name PRIMARY KEY ( column [..] ) Adds a primary key over the listed columns. The command fails with 42710 if the table already has a primary key. It fails with 42703 if a named column does not exist. It fails with the not-null violation code if any key column holds NULL values. It fails with the unique violation code if the existing rows hold duplicate key values. ADD CONSTRAINT constraint_name FOREIGN KEY ( column [..] ) REFERENCES ref_table ( ref_column [..] ) [ referential_action.. ] Adds a foreign key that references ref_table. The command fails with 42P01 if the referenced table does not exist. It fails with 42710 if the constraint name is already used. referential_action One or more clauses that you attach to a FOREIGN KEY. You can leave them out. ON DELETE and ON UPDATE each take CASCADE, SET NULL, SET DEFAULT, RESTRICT, or NO ACTION. MATCH takes FULL, PARTIAL, or SIMPLE. You can also mark the constraint [NOT] DEFERRABLE, and add INITIALLY DEFERRED or INITIALLY IMMEDIATE. NOT VALIDATE You can use this on ADD CONSTRAINT ... CHECK and ADD CONSTRAINT ... FOREIGN KEY. It records the constraint as not-validated and skips the scan of existing rows. The constraint then applies only to later writes. It stays this way until you run VALIDATE CONSTRAINT. DROP CONSTRAINT constraint_name Removes a constraint by name. The command fails with 42704 if the table has no constraint with that name. RENAME CONSTRAINT old_name TO new_name Gives a constraint a new name. The command fails with 42704 if old_name is not found. It fails with 42710 if new_name is already in use. ENABLE CONSTRAINT constraint_name / DISABLE CONSTRAINT constraint_name Turns the enabled flag of a constraint on or off. A disabled constraint is not enforced on later writes. The command fails with 42704 if the constraint does not exist. VALIDATE CONSTRAINT constraint_name Validates a constraint that was added with NOT VALIDATE. It checks the constraint against the existing rows. It then marks the constraint as validated. ENABLE / DISABLE ROW LEVEL SECURITY Sets or clears the row-level-security flag on the table. The keywords must be exactly ROW LEVEL SECURITY. Any other word after ROW is a syntax error. SET TTL_COLUMN = 'column_name' Sets a timestamp column as the time-to-live column for the table. The value is a string in single quotes. The command fails if the named column does not exist on the table. DROP TTL Clears the TTL column setting on the table.

sections.heading: Examples
sections.text: Add a column that allows NULL. Then add a column with a default and place it after an existing column. ALTER TABLE employees ADD COLUMN nickname VARCHAR(50); ALTER TABLE employees ADD COLUMN created_at TIMESTAMP DEFAULT CURRENT_TIMESTAMP AFTER nickname; Rename and retype columns. These examples show the PostgreSQL and MySQL spellings. ALTER TABLE employees RENAME COLUMN nickname TO display_name; ALTER TABLE employees MODIFY COLUMN display_name VARCHAR(100) NOT NULL; ALTER TABLE employees CHANGE display_name full_name VARCHAR(120); This example drops a column. ALTER TABLE employees DROP COLUMN full_name; These examples add different types of constraints. ALTER TABLE employees ADD CONSTRAINT chk_age CHECK (age >= 18); ALTER TABLE employees ADD CONSTRAINT uq_email UNIQUE (email); ALTER TABLE employees ADD CONSTRAINT pk_emp PRIMARY KEY (id); ALTER TABLE orders ADD CONSTRAINT fk_emp FOREIGN KEY (emp_id) REFERENCES employees (id) ON DELETE CASCADE; Add a foreign key without checking the existing data. Then validate it later. ALTER TABLE orders ADD CONSTRAINT fk_emp2 FOREIGN KEY (emp_id) REFERENCES employees (id) NOT VALIDATE; ALTER TABLE orders VALIDATE CONSTRAINT fk_emp2; This example renames a constraint, disables it, enables it again, then drops it. ALTER TABLE employees RENAME CONSTRAINT chk_age TO chk_min_age; ALTER TABLE employees DISABLE CONSTRAINT chk_min_age; ALTER TABLE employees ENABLE CONSTRAINT chk_min_age; ALTER TABLE employees DROP CONSTRAINT chk_min_age; These examples turn row-level security on and off. They also set and drop a TTL column. ALTER TABLE sessions ENABLE ROW LEVEL SECURITY; ALTER TABLE sessions SET TTL_COLUMN = 'expires_at'; ALTER TABLE sessions DROP TTL; ALTER TABLE sessions DISABLE ROW LEVEL SECURITY;

sections.heading: Compatibility
sections.text: The SQL standard defines ALTER TABLE with the ADD, DROP, and ALTER COLUMN subforms. EvolutionDB mixes the PostgreSQL and MySQL spellings on purpose. PostgreSQL-style forms that are supported. These are ADD COLUMN, DROP COLUMN, RENAME COLUMN ... TO, named ADD CONSTRAINT for CHECK / UNIQUE / PRIMARY KEY / FOREIGN KEY, DROP CONSTRAINT, RENAME CONSTRAINT, VALIDATE CONSTRAINT, the referential MATCH and DEFERRABLE clauses, and ENABLE / DISABLE ROW LEVEL SECURITY. MySQL-style forms that are supported. These are MODIFY [COLUMN], CHANGE [COLUMN], the optional COLUMN keyword on DROP and RENAME, and the FIRST / AFTER column-position clauses. Differences from PostgreSQL. PostgreSQL spells deferred validation NOT VALID. EvolutionDB uses NOT VALIDATE. PostgreSQL writes ALTER COLUMN ... TYPE and SET/DROP NOT NULL. EvolutionDB retypes a column through MODIFY and CHANGE instead. ENABLE / DISABLE CONSTRAINT is an EvolutionDB extension. PostgreSQL has no per-constraint enable flag. EvolutionDB does not accept several actions separated by commas in one statement. EvolutionDB extensions. SET TTL_COLUMN = '...' and DROP TTL manage automatic row expiry. They have no equivalent in the SQL standard or in PostgreSQL. Not supported. Some clauses are not part of the ALTER TABLE grammar. These are ALTER COLUMN ... SET DEFAULT, SET/DROP NOT NULL, OWNER TO, SET SCHEMA, SET TABLESPACE, INHERIT, and RENAME TO. RENAME TO renames a table and is a separate statement.

sections.heading: See Also
sections.text: CREATE TABLE CREATE INDEX DROP TABLE TRUNCATE INSERT UPDATE

sections.heading: Introduction
sections.text: ANALYZE ANALYZE scans a table to collect statistics and column histograms. The query planner uses them.

sections.heading: Synopsis
sections.text: ANALYZE TABLE table_name [ WITH SAMPLE n { PERCENT | ROWS } ] [ UPDATE HISTOGRAM ON column [, ...] [ WITH n BUCKETS ] | DROP HISTOGRAM ON column [, ...] ] You can write table_name with a schema. Use the form schema.table.

sections.heading: Description
sections.text: ANALYZE TABLE does a sequential scan of table_name. It then records statistics in the system catalog. Two clauses are optional. They work on their own and do not depend on each other. The WITH SAMPLE clause controls how many rows are read. The histogram clause controls which per-column histograms are built or removed. EXPLAIN reads these statistics when it estimates row counts and picks access paths. If you use no optional clause, ANALYZE TABLE t rebuilds every statistic for the table. Table-level statistics. These are row_count, page_count, and last_analyzed. page_count is the number of heap pages. It is found by walking the page chain. last_analyzed is the wall-clock time of this run. Per-column statistics. These are null_count, distinct_count, min, max, and avg_width for each column. Per-column histograms. One histogram is chosen automatically for each column that has at least one non-NULL value. Per-index statistics. For every index on the table, this records the B+ tree depth, the leaf-page count, the total entries, and the average entries per leaf. There are two kinds of histogram. The server chooses one for each column. A frequency histogram is built when the number of distinct values, called NDV, is not larger than the bucket budget. It uses one bucket per value. This gives exact equality selectivity. If the NDV is larger, an equi-depth histogram is built instead. The distinct values are sorted first. The sort is numeric when every value parses as a number. Otherwise the sort is lexicographic. The values are then grouped into buckets that hold about the same number of rows. Each bucket stores an upper bound and a cumulative count. The default bucket budget is 100. No histogram may have more than 100 buckets. Distinct-value tracking is capped at 10,000 values per column. If a column has more distinct values, the statistics are summarized from that capped sample. Statistics are stored in the catalog no matter how many rows were scanned. On success the server prints a one-line summary. It shows the rows scanned, the columns analyzed, and the pages counted. When sampling is used, it also shows the sampled-row count and the keep percentage.

sections.heading: Parameters
sections.text: table_name The table to analyze. You may add a schema with the form schema.table. The table must exist. If it does not, the command fails with ERROR: table "…" does not exist. WITH SAMPLE n PERCENT Read each row with a probability of n%. This is Bernoulli sampling. n is clamped to the range 1–100. The sampled per-column counters are the null count and the width sums. They are scaled back up to full-table estimates. min and max are always computed from the full scan. Doing this is free. WITH SAMPLE n ROWS Aim for about n sampled rows. n has a minimum of 1. The target is turned into a keep percentage. This uses the table's last recorded row_count. There may be no prior row count because no earlier ANALYZE ran. In that case the command does a full scan instead. (no WITH SAMPLE clause) Sampling is automatic. A full scan is used for tables with fewer than 10,000 rows. Larger tables are sampled at 10%. The size is read from the last recorded row_count. If there is no prior row count, a full scan is performed. UPDATE HISTOGRAM ON column [, ...] Rebuild histograms only for the listed columns. The per-column statistics are not rewritten. The table is still scanned. This lets the server collect the value distributions of the listed columns. WITH n BUCKETS Use this with UPDATE HISTOGRAM to ask for n buckets. The default is 100. Values above 100 are reduced to 100. DROP HISTOGRAM ON column [, ...] Delete the stored histograms for the listed columns. This works on the catalog only. No table scan is done. No other statistics are changed.

sections.heading: Examples
sections.text: Rebuild all statistics, histograms, and index statistics for a table. ANALYZE TABLE orders; Analyze a table that has a schema name. ANALYZE TABLE sales.orders; Analyze a large table with a 10% Bernoulli sample. ANALYZE TABLE events WITH SAMPLE 10 PERCENT; Aim for about 5,000 sampled rows. ANALYZE TABLE events WITH SAMPLE 5000 ROWS; Rebuild histograms for two columns with a custom bucket count. Other statistics are not changed. ANALYZE TABLE orders UPDATE HISTOGRAM ON status, region WITH 32 BUCKETS; Drop the histogram for a single column. This does no scan. ANALYZE TABLE orders DROP HISTOGRAM ON notes; Combine sampling with a targeted histogram rebuild. ANALYZE TABLE events WITH SAMPLE 20 PERCENT UPDATE HISTOGRAM ON event_type; The WITH SAMPLE clause comes before the histogram clause. When you use both, the histogram is built from the sampled rows. Its bucket counts are scaled up to full-table estimates. This way the histogram-backed selectivity matches the reported row_count.

sections.heading: Compatibility
sections.text: The SQL standard has no ANALYZE statement. Each database defines how it collects statistics on its own. The EvolutionDB command needs the TABLE keyword. The form is ANALYZE TABLE name. It accepts exactly one table per statement. PostgreSQL works in a different way. There the keyword is ANALYZE without TABLE. You may give several tables and a per-table column list. Options such as VERBOSE and SKIP_LOCKED go in an option list inside parentheses. PostgreSQL has no WITH SAMPLE, UPDATE HISTOGRAM, or DROP HISTOGRAM clauses. It sets sampling globally through the default_statistics_target setting. It also sets it per column with ALTER TABLE … SET STATISTICS. EvolutionDB does not accept any of those PostgreSQL forms. The WITH SAMPLE … PERCENT form is like the standard TABLESAMPLE BERNOULLI clause used in SELECT. But here it only controls which rows feed the statistics gatherer. It does not control a query result set.

sections.heading: See Also
sections.text: CREATE INDEX DROP INDEX CREATE TABLE SELECT

sections.heading: Introduction
sections.text: CALL CALL runs a stored procedure. It runs the body one statement at a time.

sections.heading: Synopsis
sections.text: CALL procedure_name ( [ argument [, ... ] ] )

sections.heading: Description
sections.text: CALL runs a procedure that you made with CREATE PROCEDURE. EvolutionDB keeps the procedure body as raw SQL text. When you use CALL, the engine finds the procedure in the system catalog. It looks in the current database and the current schema. Then it binds your arguments to the declared parameters. Then it runs the body. The body is not compiled ahead of time. When you call the procedure, EvolutionDB does these steps. It finds procedure_name in the current database and schema. If no such procedure exists, the call fails with SQLSTATE 42883 (procedure "name" does not exist). It reads the stored parameter definitions. It checks that the number of arguments you passed matches the number of declared parameters exactly. If they do not match, the call raises SQLSTATE 42883 (procedure "name" expects N arguments, got M). It splits the body into single statements. It keeps string literals whole. It also keeps the control-flow blocks whole. These blocks are IF/ELSEIF/ELSE/END IF, WHILE … DO/END WHILE, LOOP/END LOOP, FOR … DO/END FOR, FOREACH … DO/END FOREACH, and CASE/WHEN/END CASE. Then it runs the statements in order. Before each statement runs, it replaces each declared parameter with the argument value. This works for positional references like $1, $2, and for references by parameter name. Argument values are put into the procedure's SQL as text. A string argument keeps its quotes. An integer, float, boolean, or NULL is put in as its literal text. A bare identifier is put in exactly as written. A session variable of the form @var is passed through as a reference. OUT and INOUT parameters. A parameter can be declared OUT or INOUT. If its argument is a session variable like @var, EvolutionDB writes the parameter's final value back into that variable when the procedure returns. It creates the variable if it does not exist yet. For INOUT, the variable's current value is also read in as the incoming argument. Arguments that are not @-variables do not get write-back. Limits. A CALL takes at most 16 arguments. This matches the maximum of 16 declared procedure parameters. Extra arguments past the limit are ignored at parse time. Each argument value is stored in a 256-byte buffer. The procedure name is also stored in a 256-byte buffer.

sections.heading: Parameters
sections.text: procedure_name The plain name of an existing procedure. It is found in the current database and current schema. The CALL grammar does not allow schema-qualified names. argument A value passed by position to the matching parameter. The grammar accepts these argument forms. integer literal, for example 42 string literal, for example 'hello'. The quotes are kept when the value is put in. approximate-numeric / float literal, for example 3.14 NULL, put in as the literal NULL boolean, TRUE or FALSE identifier, a bare NAME, put in exactly as written session variable, @var, which can take OUT/INOUT write-back You cannot use expressions, function calls, or arithmetic as arguments. Only the literal forms above work. The number of arguments must match the number of declared parameters.

sections.heading: Examples
sections.text: Create and call a procedure that inserts a row. CREATE PROCEDURE add_user(p_id INT, p_name VARCHAR(50)) BEGIN INSERT INTO users (id, name) VALUES ($1, $2); END; CALL add_user(1, 'Ada'); You can use parameters by name instead of by position. CREATE PROCEDURE raise_salary(emp_id INT, amount INT) BEGIN UPDATE employees SET salary = salary + amount WHERE id = emp_id; END; CALL raise_salary(7, 500); Call with no arguments. You still need the empty parentheses. CALL nightly_cleanup(); Use an OUT parameter to read a value back into a session variable. CREATE PROCEDURE count_users(OUT total INT) BEGIN SET total = (SELECT COUNT(*) FROM users); END; CALL count_users(@n); SELECT @n;

sections.heading: Compatibility
sections.text: CALL follows the idea of the SQL standard <call statement> and the PostgreSQL CALL statement. But the EvolutionDB form is narrower. Argument forms are limited to literals and variable references. PostgreSQL accepts more. EvolutionDB does not accept free-form expressions, function calls, or named-argument syntax like name => value in the argument list. The argument count must match exactly. Omitted parameters do not get default values. Procedure names are not schema-qualified in the CALL grammar. The procedure is always found in the current database and schema. Parameter binding is text substitution. It puts $N and named placeholders into the stored body. It is not a typed argument-binding system. OUT/INOUT write-back targets session variables like @var. This is an EvolutionDB extension. It is not standard SQL behavior. As in PostgreSQL and the SQL standard, only procedures can be called with CALL. These are procedures made with CREATE PROCEDURE. Functions are called inside expressions, not with CALL.

sections.heading: See Also
sections.text: CREATE PROCEDURE DROP PROCEDURE SELECT INSERT UPDATE

sections.heading: Introduction
sections.text: CHECKPOINT Write to and read from a checkpoint store. A checkpoint store is a native, table-backed key/value log of agent execution state. It works as a server-side backend for the LangGraph BaseCheckpointSaver API. Not the PostgreSQL CHECKPOINT. In PostgreSQL, CHECKPOINT is a server admin command. It forces a write-ahead-log checkpoint to disk. EvolutionDB does not use the CHECKPOINT keyword for that. Here, CHECKPOINT is the data-access verb for checkpoint stores. It inserts rows of saved agent state with PUT. It reads them back with GET and LIST. You create and destroy the stores themselves with CREATE CHECKPOINT STORE and DROP CHECKPOINT STORE.

sections.heading: Synopsis
sections.text: -- Store definition (DDL) CREATE CHECKPOINT STORE [ IF NOT EXISTS ] name [ WITH ( RETENTION = interval ) ] DROP CHECKPOINT STORE [ IF EXISTS ] name [ CASCADE ] -- Store access (DML) CHECKPOINT PUT [ WRITES ] INTO name VALUES ( thread_id, checkpoint_ns, checkpoint_id, parent_id, values, metadata, parent_config ) CHECKPOINT GET FROM name THREAD thread_id [ AT checkpoint_id ] CHECKPOINT LIST FROM name THREAD thread_id [ LIMIT count ]

sections.heading: Description
sections.text: A checkpoint store is a named object. It saves the middle state of a long-running agent run. This lets you pause, resume, branch, and audit the run. EvolutionDB builds it as ordinary catalog state on top of the regular storage engine. CREATE CHECKPOINT STORE name adds an entry in the system catalog. It also auto-creates a backing heap table named ck_name with a fixed schema. Every CHECKPOINT data statement turns into a plain INSERT or SELECT against that backing table. So checkpoint writes and reads use the same MVCC visibility, row-level security, triggers, and replication as any hand-written DML. The backing table has eight columns. They always appear in this order. thread_id VARCHAR(255) NOT NULL Names the conversation or run that a checkpoint belongs to. All reads filter on this key. checkpoint_ns VARCHAR(255) The checkpoint namespace. LangGraph uses it for nested sub-graphs. It can be null. An empty string is stored as NULL. checkpoint_id VARCHAR(255) PRIMARY KEY The unique id of this checkpoint. It is the primary key. GET … AT matches on this value. parent_id VARCHAR(255) The id of the checkpoint that this one comes from. This is what makes branching and time-travel possible. It can be null. ck_values JSON The serialized channel values and graph state. It shows up in the VALUES list as values. It is stored under the column name ck_values. This avoids the reserved word values. metadata JSON Any metadata attached to the checkpoint. This can be the source step, a writes summary, and so on. parent_config JSON The runnable config of the parent checkpoint. created_at TIMESTAMP The server sets this automatically in UTC on each PUT. You do not supply it in the VALUES list. It sets the newest-first order used by GET and LIST. CHECKPOINT PUT inserts exactly one row. The seven values in the VALUES list match the first seven columns above by position. The server fills in the eighth column, created_at. Each value slot is either a string literal or the keyword NULL. CHECKPOINT GET returns the single most recent checkpoint for the given THREAD. The rows are ordered by created_at descending with LIMIT 1. When you supply AT checkpoint_id, it instead returns the one row whose checkpoint_id matches exactly. This lets you time-travel to a specific point. CHECKPOINT LIST returns the full checkpoint history for a THREAD, newest first. An optional LIMIT count caps the number of rows returned. If the named store does not exist, all four CHECKPOINT data statements raise an error. The SQLSTATE is 42704, which means undefined object.

sections.heading: Parameters
sections.text: IF NOT EXISTS / IF EXISTS On CREATE CHECKPOINT STORE, IF NOT EXISTS hides the "already exists" error. That error is SQLSTATE 42710. It leaves the existing store as it is.
