On DROP CHECKPOINT STORE, IF EXISTS hides the "does not exist" error. That error is SQLSTATE 42704. name The store name. It must be a bare identifier. The lexer accepts the pattern [A-Za-z][A-Za-z0-9_]*, so it cannot start with an underscore. The backing table is created as ck_name in the current schema. WITH ( RETENTION = interval ) An optional retention hint kept on the store. You give it as a string literal such as '30 days'. The value is stored as-is in the catalog. The = sign is the comparison operator that the grammar accepts. CASCADE (DROP) Parsed and accepted on DROP CHECKPOINT STORE. EvolutionDB always drops the backing ck_name table along with the store. This happens whether or not you write CASCADE. The store is the only object that points to that table. PUT / PUT WRITES Both insert one checkpoint row. WRITES is an alias kept to match the LangGraph put_writes API. In this version it does not change behavior. The row is written the same way with either form. INTO name VALUES ( … ) (PUT) The target store and the seven positional values. The values are thread_id, checkpoint_ns, checkpoint_id, parent_id, values, metadata, and parent_config. Each slot is a string literal or NULL. The grammar requires all seven slots. FROM name (GET / LIST) The store to read from. THREAD thread_id A string literal that names the thread to read. This becomes the WHERE thread_id = 'thread_id' filter. AT checkpoint_id (GET) A string literal. It narrows the result to the single checkpoint with that checkpoint_id instead of the latest one. LIMIT count (LIST) An integer. Caps the number of history rows returned.

sections.heading: Examples
sections.text: Create a checkpoint store with a retention hint. CREATE CHECKPOINT STORE agent_runs WITH (RETENTION = '30 days'); Create one in an idempotent way. Running it again does no harm. CREATE CHECKPOINT STORE IF NOT EXISTS agent_runs; Write a checkpoint. The seven values are thread_id, checkpoint_ns, checkpoint_id, parent_id, values, metadata, and parent_config. The server fills in created_at. CHECKPOINT PUT INTO agent_runs VALUES ( 'thread-42', -- thread_id '', -- checkpoint_ns 'ckpt-0001', -- checkpoint_id NULL, -- parent_id (root) '{"messages":["hi"]}', -- values (JSON) '{"step":1}', -- metadata (JSON) NULL -- parent_config ); Write a follow-up checkpoint that comes from the first one. This uses the cosmetic WRITES alias. CHECKPOINT PUT WRITES INTO agent_runs VALUES ( 'thread-42', '', 'ckpt-0002', 'ckpt-0001', '{"messages":["hi","there"]}', '{"step":2}', NULL); Fetch the latest checkpoint for a thread. CHECKPOINT GET FROM agent_runs THREAD 'thread-42'; Time-travel to a specific checkpoint id. CHECKPOINT GET FROM agent_runs THREAD 'thread-42' AT 'ckpt-0001'; List a thread's history, newest first, capped at five rows. CHECKPOINT LIST FROM agent_runs THREAD 'thread-42' LIMIT 5; Every store is just a table named ck_name. So you can reach the same data with ordinary SQL. SELECT * FROM ck_agent_runs WHERE thread_id = 'thread-42' ORDER BY created_at DESC; Remove the store and its backing table. DROP CHECKPOINT STORE IF EXISTS agent_runs CASCADE;

sections.heading: Compatibility
sections.text: Not in the SQL standard. CHECKPOINT, CREATE CHECKPOINT STORE, and DROP CHECKPOINT STORE are EvolutionDB extensions. There is no match for them in the SQL standard. Different from PostgreSQL's CHECKPOINT. PostgreSQL's CHECKPOINT forces a WAL checkpoint and takes no operands. EvolutionDB's CHECKPOINT is a data-access verb for checkpoint stores. It uses PUT, PUT WRITES, GET, and LIST. It has nothing to do with WAL flushing. EvolutionDB does not offer a bare CHECKPOINT; WAL-flush statement. Based on the LangGraph BaseCheckpointSaver contract. The fixed eight-column schema mirrors the LangGraph checkpoint tuple. The columns are thread_id, checkpoint_ns, checkpoint_id, parent_id, values, metadata, parent config, and a server-set timestamp. This is a vendor convenience layer. It is not a standard interface. WRITES is accepted but cosmetic in this version. CHECKPOINT PUT and CHECKPOINT PUT WRITES behave the same way. The application layer handles any per-write meaning. CASCADE is parsed but the drop always cascades. It always drops the backing ck_name table whether or not you give the keyword. The VALUES list is fixed at seven positional slots. Unlike INSERT, you cannot supply a column list, reorder columns, or skip a slot. Pass NULL for unused fields. The created_at column is reserved for the server. The store name and backing-table name are not schema-qualified in these statements. Objects are created in the current schema and resolved against it.

sections.heading: See Also
sections.text: INSERT SELECT CREATE TABLE DROP TABLE

sections.heading: Introduction
sections.text: COPY COPY moves many rows at once between a table and a file or a client stream. It works in CSV or TEXT format.

sections.heading: Synopsis
sections.text: COPY table_name [ ( column_name [..] ) ] { FROM | TO } { 'filename' | STDIN | STDOUT } [ [ WITH ] ( option [..] ) ] where option is one of: FORMAT { CSV | TEXT } CSV DELIMITER 'delimiter_character' HEADER [ boolean ] NULL 'null_string' QUOTE 'quote_character'

sections.heading: Description
sections.text: COPY moves data between an EvolutionDB table and a file on the operating system. This is server-side file mode. It can also move data between a table and the connected client. This is stream mode. COPY FROM imports rows into a table. COPY TO exports the rows of a table. When you name a file, the EvolutionDB server process reads or writes that file directly. So the path is found on the server host. The server's filesystem permissions apply. When you give STDIN or STDOUT instead, the data moves over the open client connection. COPY FROM reads the input one line at a time. It splits each line into fields using the active dialect. Then it inserts each row through the normal insert path. So all the rules that apply to INSERT apply here too. This includes constraints, primary keys, foreign keys, and indexes. In server-side file mode the import runs as one auto-commit unit. Blank lines in the input are skipped. A row may fail for several reasons. It may have a parse error, break a constraint, or have a type mismatch. When that happens, the operation stops and reports the error. COPY TO scans the whole table. It writes each row using the active dialect. COPY TO only reads. It does not change the table. Server-side file paths are restricted. A 'filename' given to COPY must be an absolute path. It must begin with /. It must not contain a .. path component. If it does not follow these rules, the command is rejected. Relative paths are not accepted.

sections.heading: Parameters
sections.text: table_name The name of an existing table. You copy into it with FROM or out of it with TO. A target table is always required. COPY (query) TO is not supported in this grammar. Only a table name may follow COPY. ( column_name [..] ) An optional list of columns. On COPY FROM, it says which columns the incoming fields go to. It also sets the order. When you leave it out, all columns of the table are used in their declared order. FROM Says that data flows into the table. This is an import. Use it with 'filename' or STDIN. TO Says that data flows out of the table. This is an export. Use it with 'filename' or STDOUT. 'filename' The absolute path of the server-side file to read from or write to. On export the file is opened for writing. It is created if needed, or its old content is removed first. On import the file is opened for reading. The path must be absolute. It must not contain a .. component. STDIN / STDOUT Moves data over the client connection instead of a server file. Use STDIN with FROM. Use STDOUT with TO. The connection adaptor drives the stream-mode transfer after the statement is parsed. FORMAT CSV | FORMAT TEXT Picks the data format. Choosing CSV also sets the active delimiter to a comma ,. It sets the default null marker to the empty string. Choosing TEXT uses a tab delimiter and the \N null marker. If you give no format option, TEXT is the default. CSV A short form that means the same as FORMAT CSV. DELIMITER 'delimiter_character' The single character that separates fields within each line. Only the first character of the string is used. The default depends on the format. It is a tab for TEXT and a comma for CSV. HEADER [ boolean ] Controls a header line of column names. HEADER on its own turns it on. You may also follow HEADER with a boolean. The boolean can be TRUE, FALSE, or UNKNOWN. On COPY TO, a header row of column names is written first. On COPY FROM, the first input line is skipped. It is off by default. NULL 'null_string' The string that stands for a SQL NULL value. On import, a field equal to this string is stored as NULL. On export, NULL values are written as this string. The default is \N in TEXT format. It is the empty string in CSV format. QUOTE 'quote_character' The single character used to quote field values. Only the first character of the string is used. The default is the double quote ". You may write options in three ways. The grammar accepts all of them. You can write a bare list with commas. You can write a list inside parentheses ( ... ). You can use the PostgreSQL-style WITH ( ... ) form.

sections.heading: Examples
sections.text: Export a whole table to a server-side CSV file with a header row. COPY employees TO '/var/lib/evosql/export/employees.csv' WITH (FORMAT CSV, HEADER); Import a CSV file into specific columns. Skip its header line. COPY employees (id, name, dept) FROM '/var/lib/evosql/import/new_hires.csv' (FORMAT CSV, HEADER TRUE); Export in tab-delimited TEXT format. This is the default. It uses the \N null marker. COPY orders TO '/tmp/orders.tsv'; Import with a custom delimiter and a custom null string. COPY products FROM '/srv/data/products.psv' WITH (DELIMITER '|', NULL '(none)'); Stream rows to the connected client instead of a server file. COPY audit_log TO STDOUT (FORMAT CSV); Change the quote character for a CSV import. COPY notes FROM '/data/notes.csv' (CSV, QUOTE '~');

sections.heading: Compatibility
sections.text: The SQL standard has no COPY statement. It is a PostgreSQL extension. EvolutionDB copies part of it. EvolutionDB supports the FROM and TO directions. It supports server-side 'filename' mode. It supports the STDIN and STDOUT stream forms. It supports an optional column list. It supports these options. The options are FORMAT with CSV or TEXT, the bare CSV short form, DELIMITER, HEADER with an optional boolean, NULL, and QUOTE. You may give options bare, in parentheses, or in a WITH ( ... ) list. These are the main differences from PostgreSQL. No COPY (query) TO. The source of a COPY is always a table name. The grammar does not accept the result of any SELECT. Smaller option set. Some PostgreSQL options are not built in and are not recognized. These include ESCAPE, FORCE_QUOTE, FORCE_NOT_NULL, FORCE_NULL, ENCODING, FREEZE, ON_ERROR, and PROGRAM. Path restrictions. Server-side filenames must be absolute. They may not contain a .. component. Relative paths are rejected. Boolean keyword for HEADER. EvolutionDB accepts HEADER TRUE, HEADER FALSE, and HEADER UNKNOWN. The PostgreSQL HEADER MATCH mode is not supported.

sections.heading: See Also
sections.text: INSERT SELECT CREATE TABLE

sections.heading: Introduction
sections.text: CREATE CHECKPOINT STORE Register a named checkpoint store for LangGraph-style agent state. It also creates the backing table for you.

sections.heading: Synopsis
sections.text: CREATE CHECKPOINT STORE [ IF NOT EXISTS ] name [ WITH ( retention = 'interval_text' ) ]

sections.heading: Description
sections.text: CREATE CHECKPOINT STORE registers a checkpoint store named name in the system catalog. The same statement also creates a backing heap table. This table holds the real checkpoint rows. The store is EvolutionDB's native backend for the LangGraph BaseCheckpointSaver contract. It saves per-thread agent snapshots. This lets a graph run start again from where it stopped. The backing table is created in the current session schema. It has the fixed name ck_name. It uses this fixed schema. thread_id VARCHAR(255) NOT NULL The conversation or graph thread that owns the checkpoint. checkpoint_ns VARCHAR(255) The namespace inside the thread. This can be NULL. An empty string becomes NULL in this engine. checkpoint_id VARCHAR(255) PRIMARY KEY The unique id of the checkpoint. The primary-key B+ tree indexes it. parent_id VARCHAR(255) The id of the checkpoint that this one comes from. This is NULL for root checkpoints. ck_values JSON The serialized channel values payload. It is named ck_values so it does not clash with the SQL reserved word values. metadata JSON Application metadata that is attached to the checkpoint. parent_config JSON The serialized configuration of the parent checkpoint. created_at TIMESTAMP The time the row was inserted. The engine sets it for you on each write. The store is just a catalog entry over a normal table. So every standard engine feature works on it without any change. MVCC visibility, row-level security, triggers, and replication all work on the backing table. They work the same as on a table you write by hand. The statement fails if a store with the same name already exists. It returns SQLSTATE 42710. You can avoid this with IF NOT EXISTS. Then the statement does nothing and raises no error. A missing or empty name is a syntax error. It returns SQLSTATE 42601.

sections.heading: Parameters
sections.text: IF NOT EXISTS Do not raise an error if a checkpoint store with the same name already exists. When the store exists, the statement just returns. It creates nothing and changes nothing. This guard only checks for a checkpoint store with that name. It does not make sure the backing table is the same. name The name of the checkpoint store to create. It must match the engine's identifier pattern [A-Za-z][A-Za-z0-9_]*. The backing table is created as ck_name in the current schema. WITH ( retention = 'interval_text' ) This is optional. It stores a free-form retention text on the store descriptor in the catalog. For example, you can use '30 days' or '1 hour'. The value is stored as is. The outer quotes are removed. It is only descriptive metadata. This release does no automatic retention pruning. The engine does not delete old checkpoints based on this value. retention is the only key allowed inside the WITH clause.

sections.heading: Examples
sections.text: Create a checkpoint store named agent_runs. CREATE CHECKPOINT STORE agent_runs; Create it only if it does not already exist. CREATE CHECKPOINT STORE IF NOT EXISTS agent_runs; Create a store and save a retention text on it. CREATE CHECKPOINT STORE agent_runs WITH (retention = '30 days'); A normal table backs the store. So you can also read and write checkpoints straight against ck_name. INSERT INTO ck_agent_runs (thread_id, checkpoint_id, ck_values, metadata) VALUES ('thread-1', 'ckpt-001', '{"step":1}', '{"source":"loop"}'); SELECT checkpoint_id, created_at FROM ck_agent_runs WHERE thread_id = 'thread-1' ORDER BY created_at DESC; The grammar also accepts native checkpoint DML. These are CHECKPOINT PUT [WRITES] INTO name VALUES (…), CHECKPOINT GET FROM name THREAD 'id' [AT 'checkpoint_id'], and CHECKPOINT LIST FROM name THREAD 'id' [LIMIT n]. They compile down to INSERT or SELECT against the same backing table. GET returns the newest matching row. LIST returns the history with the newest first.

sections.heading: Compatibility
sections.text: CREATE CHECKPOINT STORE is an EvolutionDB extension. It is not part of the SQL standard. It has no match in PostgreSQL. PostgreSQL has no checkpoint-store object. Its CHECKPOINT command is a different thing. That command flushes WAL and buffers. EvolutionDB accepts only two clauses here. They are IF NOT EXISTS and the single-key WITH (retention = '…') option from the Synopsis. There is no TABLESPACE, owner, encoding, or other PostgreSQL CREATE … clause. The WITH clause looks like a normal storage-parameter list. But retention is the only key it knows. The engine stores it as descriptive metadata. It does not enforce it in this release.

sections.heading: See Also
sections.text: CHECKPOINT CREATE MEMORY STORE CREATE DOC STORE CREATE ENTITY STORE CREATE GRAPH STORE CREATE TABLE DROP TABLE

sections.heading: Introduction
sections.text: CREATE DATABASE Create a new, empty database and add it to the system catalog. EvolutionDB also makes a schema named default inside it.

sections.heading: Synopsis
sections.text: CREATE DATABASE [ IF NOT EXISTS ] database_name

sections.heading: Description
sections.text: CREATE DATABASE makes a new database. A database is the top container in the EvolutionDB catalog. It holds schemas. Schemas hold tables and other objects. The statement saves the new database in the system catalog. The catalog gives it a database id. When it creates the database, EvolutionDB also makes a schema named default inside it. So the database is ready for new objects right away. You do not need a separate CREATE SCHEMA step. Creating a database does not switch your session to it. Your current database stays the same. Use USE to make the new database current. Do this before you create objects in it. What happens if a database with the same name already exists? The statement fails with a "database already exists" error. The SQLSTATE is 42E04. You can avoid this with IF NOT EXISTS. Then the existing database is left as is, and the statement succeeds with no error. The statement can also fail for another reason. If the catalog cannot allocate the database, you get an I/O error. The SQLSTATE is 58030. A new instance starts with a default database named evosql. That database also has a default schema inside it. So at least one database always exists before you run any CREATE DATABASE.

sections.heading: Parameters
sections.text: IF NOT EXISTS Hides the "database already exists" error. If the catalog already has a database with this name, no new database is made. The statement still succeeds, and it does not change the existing database. Without this clause, creating a database that already exists is an error. database_name The name of the database to create. It must be unique among the databases that already exist. The name lookup against the catalog is case-sensitive.

sections.heading: Examples
sections.text: This example creates a database. CREATE DATABASE shop; Create a database only if it does not already exist. You can run this many times and get the same result. CREATE DATABASE IF NOT EXISTS analytics; Create a database. Then switch to it and start defining objects in its default schema. CREATE DATABASE reporting; USE reporting; CREATE TABLE events ( id BIGINT PRIMARY KEY AUTO_INCREMENT, name VARCHAR(120) NOT NULL );

sections.heading: Compatibility
sections.text: The SQL standard does not define CREATE DATABASE. It is a PostgreSQL and MySQL extension. EvolutionDB keeps its version of the command small on purpose. Versus PostgreSQL: PostgreSQL's CREATE DATABASE takes a long list of options. These include OWNER, TEMPLATE, ENCODING, LOCALE, LC_COLLATE, LC_CTYPE, TABLESPACE, CONNECTION LIMIT, and IS_TEMPLATE. EvolutionDB supports none of these. The grammar does not accept them. The only optional part is the IF NOT EXISTS guard. Versus MySQL: MySQL allows CREATE {DATABASE | SCHEMA} with CHARACTER SET and COLLATE options. EvolutionDB does not accept those options on CREATE DATABASE. In EvolutionDB, CREATE DATABASE and CREATE SCHEMA are not the same thing. They are separate commands at different levels. A schema lives inside the current database. EvolutionDB behavior: Creating a database also creates a schema named default inside it. The new database does not become your current database. You must run USE for that.

sections.heading: See Also
sections.text: DROP DATABASE USE CREATE SCHEMA CREATE TABLE

sections.heading: Introduction
sections.text: CREATE DOC STORE Create a document store. A document store is a collection of JSON documents. It can also have an embedding column for vector search.

sections.heading: Synopsis
sections.text: CREATE DOCUMENT STORE [ IF EXISTS ] store_name [ WITH ( option [, ...] ) ] where option is one of: embedding_dim = integer distance = 'cosine' | 'l2' | 'inner' | 'ip' | 'l1'

sections.heading: Description
sections.text: CREATE DOCUMENT STORE makes a new document store named store_name. A document store is a thin layer on top of a normal EvolutionDB table. The engine creates a backing table named doc_<store_name> in the current schema. It also adds the store to the system catalog. The store sits on a normal table. So MVCC visibility, row-level security, and the standard error path all work on its documents. The backing table always has the same fixed layout. It has these columns. id, VARCHAR(255), NOT NULL, primary key content, VARCHAR(8000), the document body meta, VARCHAR(8000), a JSON metadata string. It may be NULL. embedding, VECTOR(N), made only when embedding_dim is greater than 0 created_at, TIMESTAMP, NOT NULL, set to the UTC time of each write You can leave out embedding_dim or set it to 0. Then no embedding column is made. The store becomes a plain JSON key/value collection. In that case DOCUMENT SEARCH on the store fails. There is no vector column to score. The distance option saves which distance function DOCUMENT SEARCH uses to rank results. It does nothing for stores made without an embedding column. The command fails with an error if a store with the same name already exists. The error is SQLSTATE 42710. You can add IF EXISTS to avoid this. Then the command does nothing and succeeds. Note on IF EXISTS: EvolutionDB's grammar uses CREATE DOCUMENT STORE IF EXISTS for the idempotent form. It does not use the PostgreSQL-style IF NOT EXISTS. The meaning is the same as PostgreSQL's IF NOT EXISTS. It skips creation when a store with that name is already there.

sections.heading: Parameters
sections.text: IF EXISTS Do not raise an error if a document store with the same name already exists. The store stays the same and the command returns success. The keyword is IF EXISTS, not IF NOT EXISTS. store_name The name of the document store to create. This name goes into the catalog. It also sets the backing table name. The backing table is named doc_<store_name> and is made in the current schema. WITH ( option [..] ) An optional list of creation options. Separate the options with commas. Two options are accepted. You can use them in any order and in any combination. embedding_dim = integer The size of the vector embedding stored for each document. When it is greater than 0, the backing table gets a VECTOR(N) column named embedding. You can then query the store with DOCUMENT SEARCH ... USING VECTOR. The default is 0. That means no embedding column is made. distance = string The distance function used to rank results during DOCUMENT SEARCH. The values are case-insensitive. Accepted values are 'cosine', 'l2', 'inner', 'ip', and 'l1'. The value 'ip' is an alias for 'inner'. The default is 'cosine'. Any value that is not known falls back to 'cosine'. This setting is saved with the store. It only matters when an embedding column exists.

sections.heading: Examples
sections.text: This example creates a plain JSON document store with no vector column. CREATE DOCUMENT STORE notes; Create a store with 384-dimensional embeddings. Results are ranked by cosine distance. This is the default. CREATE DOCUMENT STORE articles WITH (embedding_dim = 384); Create a store with embeddings. Rank results by L2 distance. CREATE DOCUMENT STORE products WITH (embedding_dim = 768, distance = 'l2'); This example creates a store only if one does not already exist. CREATE DOCUMENT STORE IF EXISTS articles WITH (embedding_dim = 384, distance = 'cosine'); Write a document. Then query the store with a Mongo-style filter. DOCUMENT WRITE INTO articles VALUES ('a1', 'Vector databases explained', '{"lang":"en","tags":["db"]}'); DOCUMENT FILTER FROM articles WHERE '{"lang":"en"}' LIMIT 10;

sections.heading: Compatibility
sections.text: CREATE DOCUMENT STORE is an EvolutionDB extension. The SQL standard has no document store. PostgreSQL has no command like this one. In PostgreSQL you use a jsonb column on a normal table instead. PostgreSQL gets vector search from the third-party pgvector extension. You add it with CREATE EXTENSION and a vector column type. EvolutionDB puts both ideas into one command and a small set of DOCUMENT verbs. These are the main differences. The idempotency keyword is IF EXISTS. PostgreSQL's CREATE statements use IF NOT EXISTS. The backing table schema is fixed. It has id, content, meta, an optional embedding, and created_at. You cannot set it yourself. You cannot add extra columns through this command. Only two WITH options are accepted. They are embedding_dim and distance. PostgreSQL and pgvector tuning options are not part of this statement. For example, HNSW build parameters such as m or ef_construction are not allowed. You query, write, and remove documents with the EvolutionDB verbs DOCUMENT WRITE, DOCUMENT FILTER, DOCUMENT SEARCH, and DOCUMENT DELETE. You do not use the normal INSERT, SELECT, and DELETE. The verbs reuse the same execution machinery inside.

sections.heading: See Also
sections.text: DROP DOC STORE DOCUMENT WRITE DOCUMENT FILTER DOCUMENT SEARCH DOCUMENT DELETE CREATE TABLE

sections.heading: Introduction
sections.text: CREATE DOMAIN Make a named data type that you can use again. It is built from a base type. It can also have NOT NULL and CHECK rules.

sections.heading: Synopsis
sections.text: CREATE DOMAIN domain_name AS data_type [ DEFAULT default_expr ] [ NOT NULL ] [ CHECK ( expression ) ]

sections.heading: Description
sections.text: CREATE DOMAIN creates a domain. A domain is a named type. It joins a base data_type with rules that you choose. EvolutionDB saves the domain in its system catalog. You can then use the domain by its name. The domain does not get its own catalog entity. EvolutionDB stores it in the shared constraints B+ tree. This is under the system namespace, where table_id = 0. The constraint type is 'D'. The catalog row keeps three fields in one definition string. The string has the form "type_val|not_null|check_serialized". The three fields are the numeric type code of the base type, a 0/1 flag for NOT NULL, and the serialized check expression. The check expression is only there when a CHECK clause is given. The domain name must be unique in that system namespace. If you try to create a domain with a name that is already used, it fails with SQLSTATE 42710. This means the object is a duplicate. The base type can be any type from EvolutionDB's data_type grammar. Some examples are INTEGER, BIGINT, DECIMAL(p,s), VARCHAR(n), CHAR(n), TEXT, BOOLEAN, DATE, TIMESTAMP, UUID, JSON, ENUM(...), and array types written type[]. Honest implementation note. The parser accepts two parts of the syntax. But they do little in this release. You should not rely on them. DEFAULT default_expr is parsed but not stored. The grammar accepts the clause. But the value is thrown away. The catalog row keeps only the base type, the NOT NULL flag, and the CHECK expression. No default is saved with the domain. Domains are recorded, not yet enforced as column types. A domain is written to the catalog. But the engine does not yet resolve a domain name when it is used as a column type in CREATE TABLE. So the domain's NOT NULL and CHECK rules are not applied to columns through the domain name.

sections.heading: Parameters
sections.text: domain_name The name of the domain to create. It must be unique in the system namespace. If the name is already used, you get domain "name" already exists with SQLSTATE 42710. data_type The base type for the domain. You give it after the required AS keyword. You can use any type from EvolutionDB's data_type grammar. This includes types with parameters like VARCHAR(n) and DECIMAL(p,s). It also includes array types written type[]. The type is stored as its internal numeric code, which is type_family × 10000 + size. DEFAULT default_expr The grammar accepts this. But it is not saved in this release. The expression is parsed and then thrown away. So it does not become part of the stored domain definition. NOT NULL Marks the domain so it does not accept null values. When you use it, the not-null flag is saved in the domain's catalog definition. You can use it together with a CHECK clause. CHECK ( expression ) A boolean rule expression for the domain. When you give it, the expression is serialized and stored next to the base type in the domain's catalog row.

sections.heading: Examples
sections.text: A simple domain on an integer base type. CREATE DOMAIN postal_code AS INTEGER; A text domain that does not allow nulls. CREATE DOMAIN email_addr AS VARCHAR(255) NOT NULL; A domain with a value check. CREATE DOMAIN percent AS INTEGER CHECK (value >= 0 AND value <= 100); A non-null domain with a check together. CREATE DOMAIN positive_amount AS DECIMAL(12,2) NOT NULL CHECK (value > 0); -- The DEFAULT clause parses. But the value is not stored with the domain. CREATE DOMAIN country_code AS CHAR(2) DEFAULT 'US';

sections.heading: Compatibility
sections.text: CREATE DOMAIN is part of the SQL standard. It is also in PostgreSQL. EvolutionDB supports only a small part of it on purpose. EvolutionDB accepts DEFAULT, one NOT NULL, and one unnamed CHECK (expression). The standard and PostgreSQL allow more. They allow named constraints written CONSTRAINT name CHECK .... They allow many constraints. They allow NULL to mark a domain as nullable. They allow COLLATE. None of these are part of EvolutionDB's grammar. PostgreSQL works in a different way here. EvolutionDB does not need or accept a schema-qualified domain name in this command. The domain is registered in one system namespace. The syntax accepts DEFAULT. But in this release the default value is not kept. PostgreSQL stores and uses the default. In PostgreSQL a domain becomes a real column type. It enforces its rules on every column that uses it. In this EvolutionDB release the domain is saved in the catalog. But it is not yet linked as a column type you can reference. So its rules are not enforced through the domain name. Some code depends on default propagation. Some code depends on domain-typed columns. Treat both as not yet available. Use explicit column-level NOT NULL and CHECK rules in CREATE TABLE instead.

sections.heading: See Also
sections.text: CREATE TABLE ALTER TABLE CREATE INDEX

sections.heading: Introduction
sections.text: CREATE ENTITY STORE Create a named entity store. An entity store keeps short summaries of things, looked up by a key. It tracks how many times you wrote each entity and when you last saw it. It is built on top of a normal EvolutionDB table.

sections.heading: Synopsis
sections.text: CREATE ENTITY STORE [ IF EXISTS ] name

sections.heading: Description
sections.text: CREATE ENTITY STORE adds a new entity store named name in the current schema. An entity store sits on top of a regular EvolutionDB table. The command makes a backing table and a catalog entry. The catalog entry links the store name to that table. After this, you can use the entity DML verbs ENTITY PUT, ENTITY GET, and ENTITY RANK instead of writing raw SQL. The backing table is created for you. It is named ent_name. It always has the same five columns. entity_key VARCHAR(255) PRIMARY KEY NOT NULL The lookup key for the entity. It is the primary key. Keys are unique and indexed by a B+ tree. ENTITY PUT upserts against this key. summary VARCHAR(8000) A free-text summary of the entity. It can be null. facts VARCHAR(8000) An optional JSON-shaped string of structured facts. It can be null. It is stored as SQL NULL when you leave it out. mention_count INT NOT NULL How many times the entity has been written. It is set to 1 on the first ENTITY PUT. It goes up by one on every later put for the same key. last_seen TIMESTAMP NOT NULL The UTC ISO timestamp of the most recent put. It uses the form YYYY-MM-DD HH:MM:SS. The store is saved in the system catalog as an entity store entry. The entry holds its name and the id of the backing table. If a store with the same name already exists, the command raises an error with SQLSTATE 42710. You can stop that error with the IF EXISTS clause. In that case the existing store is left alone and the command quietly succeeds. A missing name is reported as a syntax error with SQLSTATE 42601. You can drop the store with DROP ENTITY STORE. This drops the backing ent_name table and removes the catalog entry. Note on the guard clause. The grammar accepts the exact tokens IF EXISTS here. It does not use the usual SQL form IF NOT EXISTS. The behavior still matches the normal if-not-exists meaning. When the store already exists, the statement does nothing instead of raising an error.

sections.heading: Parameters
sections.text: IF EXISTS Do not raise an error if an entity store with the same name already exists. The existing store stays in place and the command returns success. Without this clause, trying to create a store that already exists fails with entity store 'name' already exists and SQLSTATE 42710. name The name of the entity store to create. The backing table is made as ent_name in the current schema. The store is saved under this name in the catalog. This is required.

sections.heading: Examples
sections.text: Create an entity store named people. CREATE ENTITY STORE people; Create it only if it does not already exist. This makes the setup safe to run again. CREATE ENTITY STORE IF EXISTS people; Create a store and start filling it with the entity DML verbs. CREATE ENTITY STORE people; -- upsert by key: summary only ENTITY PUT INTO people VALUES ('alice', 'Lead engineer on the storage team'); -- upsert by key: summary + JSON-shaped facts ENTITY PUT INTO people VALUES ('alice', 'Storage team lead', '{"role":"lead","team":"storage"}'); -- look up one entity by key ENTITY GET FROM people WHERE KEY = 'alice'; -- rank entities by mention_count DESC (tie-broken by last_seen DESC) ENTITY RANK FROM people LIMIT 10; Drop the store and its backing table. DROP ENTITY STORE people;

sections.heading: Compatibility
sections.text: CREATE ENTITY STORE is an EvolutionDB extension. The SQL standard has no CREATE ENTITY STORE statement. PostgreSQL has no matching command. So this is not portable. The clause spelling is different from common SQL. EvolutionDB's grammar accepts IF EXISTS as the guard at create time. PostgreSQL and the wider SQL community use IF NOT EXISTS for the same "create only if absent" intent. The runtime behavior still matches the usual if-not-exists meaning. The store is not a separate storage engine. It is built fully on top of a normal EvolutionDB table named ent_name. That table has a B+ tree primary-key index on entity_key. The entity DML verbs turn into ordinary INSERT, UPDATE, and SELECT execution paths. The backing table is visible to regular SQL queries.

sections.heading: See Also
sections.text: CREATE MEMORY STORE CREATE TABLE MEMORY PUT MEMORY GET DROP TABLE

sections.heading: Introduction
sections.text: CREATE GRAPH STORE Create a named bitemporal knowledge-graph store. It comes with its own node table and edge table.

sections.heading: Synopsis
sections.text: CREATE GRAPH STORE [ IF EXISTS ] store_name

sections.heading: Description
sections.text: CREATE GRAPH STORE adds a new knowledge-graph store under store_name. It also builds the two tables that hold the store's data. This command is part of EvolutionDB's bitemporal knowledge-graph feature, Feature 224. The related commands are GRAPH UPSERT NODE, GRAPH UPSERT EDGE, and GRAPH NEIGHBORS. For a store named store_name, the command creates two tables. kg_<store_name>_nodes. It has these columns. id VARCHAR(255) PRIMARY KEY NOT NULL, type VARCHAR(64), props VARCHAR(8000), and valid_from TIMESTAMP NOT NULL. The props column holds JSON-shaped text. kg_<store_name>_edges. It has these columns. id BIGINT PRIMARY KEY AUTO_INCREMENT, src VARCHAR(255) NOT NULL, rel VARCHAR(128) NOT NULL, dst VARCHAR(255) NOT NULL, props VARCHAR(8000), valid_from TIMESTAMP NOT NULL, and invalid_at TIMESTAMP. A NULL value in invalid_at means the edge is still active. Both tables go into the current schema. The current schema comes from your current database and schema context. The store itself is saved as one row in the system catalog table CAT_SYS_GRAPH_STORES. That row holds the store name and the table ids of its nodes and edges tables. The store name is global to the catalog. It is not schema-qualified. The invalid_at column on edges is what makes the store bitemporal. Suppose you run UPSERT EDGE on a (src, rel, dst) triple that already exists. The command sets the old live row's invalid_at to NOW(). Then it adds a fresh row. Because of this, a GRAPH NEIGHBORS … AS OF query can rebuild the graph as it looked at any past timestamp. Idempotency. Without the optional clause, the command fails if the store name already exists. It raises an error with SQLSTATE 42710, which means duplicate object. With the clause present, the command leaves the existing store untouched. It then succeeds without any message. Grammar note. The optional clause means "create only if the store is not there." In the grammar it is written IF EXISTS. The full token sequence is CREATE GRAPH STORE IF EXISTS store_name. The keyword is EXISTS, but the meaning is if-not-exists. When the clause is present, an existing store is a no-op and not an error. This is different from the IF NOT EXISTS spelling used by CREATE TABLE and most other EvolutionDB DDL.

sections.heading: Parameters
sections.text: IF EXISTS Optional. When present, the command does not raise an error if a graph store with the same name already exists. Instead it does nothing and reports success. The backing tables are not rebuilt or changed. See the grammar note above. This clause acts as if-not-exists even though its keyword is EXISTS. store_name The name of the graph store to create. It must be a bare identifier. It is required. The same name is used to build the backing table names kg_<store_name>_nodes and kg_<store_name>_edges. The name is saved as is in the system catalog. If the name is missing, the command raises a syntax error with SQLSTATE 42601.

sections.heading: Examples
sections.text: Create a knowledge-graph store named kb. CREATE GRAPH STORE kb; Create it only if it is not there yet. CREATE GRAPH STORE IF EXISTS kb; Create a store and add data to it. Each VALUES argument is a single-quoted string. CREATE GRAPH STORE kb; -- nodes: (id, type, props) GRAPH UPSERT NODE INTO kb VALUES ('alice', 'person', '{"role":"engineer"}'); GRAPH UPSERT NODE INTO kb VALUES ('acme', 'org', '{"sector":"db"}'); -- edge: (src, rel, dst) GRAPH UPSERT EDGE INTO kb VALUES ('alice', 'works_at', 'acme'); -- edge with optional props: (src, rel, dst, props) GRAPH UPSERT EDGE INTO kb VALUES ('alice', 'knows', 'bob', '{"since":2021}'); Walk the graph outward from a node. The optional DEPTH clause sets how many hops to follow. The optional AS OF clause picks a point in time. GRAPH NEIGHBORS OF 'alice' IN kb; GRAPH NEIGHBORS OF 'alice' IN kb DEPTH 2; GRAPH NEIGHBORS OF 'alice' IN kb AS OF '2026-01-01 00:00:00'; Remove the store. This drops both backing tables and the catalog entry. DROP GRAPH STORE kb; DROP GRAPH STORE IF EXISTS kb;

sections.heading: Compatibility
sections.text: CREATE GRAPH STORE is an EvolutionDB extension. The SQL standard, SQL:2016, has no such statement. PostgreSQL has no CREATE GRAPH STORE command either. On PostgreSQL, graph work is done by outside extensions, not by core DDL. One example is the create_graph() function from Apache AGE. EvolutionDB gives you a built-in store with a fixed shape. The engine defines the node and edge schemas. You cannot change them when you create the store. A store always has exactly one nodes table and one edges table. The optional clause is written IF EXISTS and not IF NOT EXISTS. Still, it has if-not-exists meaning. This is specific to the graph-store and entity-store grammar. It is not how the SQL standard or PostgreSQL write a conditional CREATE. The model is bitemporal in the spirit of Zep Graphiti. It does not use the SQL standard's system-versioned tables. On update, edges are never deleted. They are closed by setting invalid_at. This is what makes point-in-time AS OF reads work. EvolutionDB does not support the SQL standard FOR SYSTEM_TIME syntax for these stores.

sections.heading: See Also
sections.text: CREATE ENTITY STORE CREATE MEMORY STORE CREATE TABLE DROP TABLE SELECT

sections.heading: Introduction
sections.text: CREATE INDEX Build a secondary index on a table. An index can cover one or more columns. It can also cover one supported expression. An index speeds up lookups, equality scans, full-text matching, and approximate nearest-neighbor search.

sections.heading: Synopsis
sections.text: CREATE [ UNIQUE ] INDEX [ CONCURRENTLY ] [ IF EXISTS ] name ON table [ USING HASH ] ( { column_name | expression } [, ...] ) -- Full-text inverted index CREATE FULLTEXT INDEX name ON table ( text_column ) -- HNSW approximate nearest-neighbor (ANN) index on a VECTOR column CREATE INDEX [ IF EXISTS ] name ON table USING HNSW ( vector_column [ opclass ] ) [ WITH ( m = N [, ef_construction = N ] ) ] where expression is one of: UPPER(col) | LOWER(col) | LENGTH(col) | CONCAT(col1, col2) where opclass is one of: vector_cosine_ops | vector_l2_ops | vector_ip_ops | vector_l1_ops

sections.heading: Description
sections.text: CREATE INDEX builds a secondary index on the named table. The default access method is a B+ tree. The tree is stored in the unified page file. Each entry is a composite key of the form indexed_value␟PK. The \x1F unit separator joins the indexed value with the row's primary key. The entry points directly at the heap row location. So a matched index entry reaches its row without a second primary-key lookup. The index is filled right away. The engine scans every existing row in the table. It builds the key for each row and inserts it. The command runs while it holds the engine's write lock. So the table is paused during the build. The one exception is when you use CONCURRENTLY. See below. An index can cover a single column. It can cover several columns. This is a composite index. Its key is the column values joined with |. An index can also cover one supported expression. Indexes on ARRAY columns are rejected in this version. EvolutionDB supports four index shapes. You pick the shape with the modifiers and the access-method clause. B+ tree. This is the default. It is an ordered index. It supports equality and the prefix scans used by the planner. Hash with USING HASH. This is an equality-only index. It is backed by an extendible-hash directory. Full-text with CREATE FULLTEXT INDEX. This is an inverted index. The text column is split into tokens. One (token, PK) pair is inserted per token. HNSW with USING HNSW. This is an in-memory graph. It is used for approximate nearest-neighbor search over a VECTOR column. An index may already exist on the same column list. If that index is not the primary-key index, CREATE INDEX fails with index already exists on column '…'. You can avoid the error with IF EXISTS. Then the statement does nothing.

sections.heading: Parameters
sections.text: UNIQUE Makes sure no two rows have the same indexed value. During the build, each key is checked for duplicates. The first duplicate stops the command with could not create unique index: duplicate key '…'. The partly built index is then destroyed. You can combine this with USING HASH. You cannot use it with FULLTEXT or USING HNSW. CONCURRENTLY Builds the index without holding the write lock for the whole scan. First the index is registered in a building state. Then it is filled in batches. The lock is released between batches. So other queries can keep running. Writes that happen during the build are saved in a modification log. They are applied in a final pass before the index is marked ready. Only the building session pays the cost. Readers ignore an index that is still building. IF EXISTS An index may already exist on the same column list. With this option, the statement does nothing instead of failing. Watch the spelling. EvolutionDB's grammar accepts the IF EXISTS token sequence here. It carries the standard "if not exists" meaning. It skips creation if a matching index is already there. name The name of the index to create. DROP INDEX looks up index names across the whole database. So pick a name that is unique across the database. table The table to create the index on. It must already exist. If it does not, the command fails with table '…' does not exist. USING HASH Picks an extendible-hash index instead of the default B+ tree. Hash indexes are created with the engine's default bucket count. They support equality lookups only. column_name [..] One or more columns to index. Each named column must exist in the table. If a column does not exist, the command fails with column '…' does not exist in table '…'. More than one column makes a composite index. Its key is the column values joined together. expression An expression index over one supported function of a column. The accepted forms are exactly UPPER(col), LOWER(col), LENGTH(col), and CONCAT(col1, col2). The expression is stored with the index. At build time and during maintenance, the engine runs it for each row to make the key. Other SQL expressions are not supported. FULLTEXT (in CREATE FULLTEXT INDEX) Builds an inverted full-text index on one text column. The column is split into tokens at build time. One entry per token is inserted. This supports MATCH queries. The full-text index is not kept up to date on INSERT, UPDATE, or DELETE in this version. After bulk DML, drop the index and create it again with CREATE FULLTEXT INDEX to refresh it. USING HNSW Builds a Hierarchical Navigable Small World graph for approximate nearest-neighbor search. The indexed column must be one VECTOR column. Composite HNSW indexes are not supported in this version. If the column is wrong, the command fails with HNSW index requires a VECTOR column… or HNSW index supports a single vector column…. The graph lives in memory only. The build parameters are saved in the catalog. So the graph can be rebuilt at server startup. opclass (HNSW) The distance function for the HNSW graph. The choices are vector_cosine_ops, vector_l2_ops, vector_ip_ops, and vector_l1_ops. The default is vector_cosine_ops. vector_ip_ops means inner product. The name vector_inner_ops is also accepted for it. An unknown opclass falls back to cosine. WITH ( m = N, ef_construction = N ) (HNSW) Tunes how the graph is built. m is the number of two-way links per node. ef_construction is the size of the candidate list at build time. The defaults are m = 16 and ef_construction = 64. For the same key, later entries in the list replace earlier ones.

sections.heading: Examples
sections.text: Create a plain B+ tree index on one column. CREATE INDEX idx_users_email ON users (email); Create a unique index. CREATE UNIQUE INDEX idx_users_username ON users (username); Create a composite index over two columns. CREATE INDEX idx_orders_cust_date ON orders (customer_id, order_date); Skip creation if a matching index is already there. CREATE INDEX IF EXISTS idx_users_email ON users (email); Create a hash index for equality lookups. CREATE INDEX idx_sessions_token ON sessions USING HASH (token); Create an expression index. This gives case-insensitive lookups on a lowercased column. CREATE INDEX idx_users_lower_email ON users (LOWER(email)); Build an index without locking the table for the whole scan. CREATE INDEX CONCURRENTLY idx_events_type ON events (event_type); Build a full-text index. Then query it. CREATE FULLTEXT INDEX idx_articles_body ON articles (body); Build an HNSW vector index with your own build parameters. CREATE INDEX idx_docs_embedding ON docs USING HNSW (embedding vector_l2_ops) WITH (m = 32, ef_construction = 128);

sections.heading: Compatibility
sections.text: CREATE INDEX is not part of the SQL standard. The standard leaves indexing to each database. EvolutionDB's syntax follows PostgreSQL closely. But it differs in a few clear ways. IF EXISTS vs IF NOT EXISTS. PostgreSQL spells the skip-if-present option IF NOT EXISTS. EvolutionDB's grammar accepts the token sequence IF EXISTS in this spot. It has the same "skip if a matching index already exists" meaning. Access methods. EvolutionDB supports the default B+ tree, USING HASH, and USING HNSW. PostgreSQL's btree, gin, gist, spgist, and brin method names are not supported. A generic USING method clause is also not supported. Full-text. EvolutionDB uses a dedicated CREATE FULLTEXT INDEX statement in the MySQL style. It does not use PostgreSQL's tsvector and GIN approach. The full-text index is not kept up to date on DML. Expression indexes. These are limited to UPPER, LOWER, LENGTH, and CONCAT(a, b). PostgreSQL allows any immutable expression and parenthesized expression lists. Unsupported clauses. EvolutionDB does not accept PostgreSQL's ASC, DESC, NULLS FIRST/LAST, COLLATE, per-column opclasses for B+ tree and hash indexes, INCLUDE, WHERE for partial indexes, TABLESPACE, or a storage-parameter WITH (...) on non-HNSW indexes. The HNSW WITH clause accepts only m and ef_construction. HNSW. The opclass names vector_cosine_ops, vector_l2_ops, vector_ip_ops, and vector_l1_ops mirror the pgvector extension. The WITH parameters mirror it too. The HNSW graph is held in memory. It is rebuilt at startup from parameters stored in the catalog.

sections.heading: See Also
sections.text: DROP INDEX CREATE TABLE SELECT

sections.heading: Introduction
sections.text: CREATE JOB CREATE JOB adds a scheduled job. The job runs a SQL statement on a cron-style timetable.

sections.heading: Synopsis
sections.text: CREATE JOB [ IF EXISTS ] name ON SCHEDULE 'min hour dom mon dow' DO 'sql_statement'

sections.heading: Description
sections.text: CREATE JOB adds a named scheduled job to the system catalog. A job joins a five-field cron expression with one SQL statement. The scheduler checks the current minute on the clock. When that minute matches the cron pattern, the job is marked as due. You give both the cron expression and the SQL body as quoted string literals. Whitespace and special characters in them follow the lexer's normal quoting rules. They do not follow the SQL grammar. The outer quotes are removed before the values are stored in the catalog. New jobs are enabled by default. Their last-run timestamp is null. You can turn jobs on and off with ALTER JOB ... ENABLE and ALTER JOB ... DISABLE. You can remove a job with DROP JOB. You can list jobs with SHOW JOBS. That command shows each job's name, cron expression, SQL body, enabled flag, and last_run_unix timestamp. Execution status. In this release the scheduler does cron matching. It also handles persistence, the ENABLE and DISABLE state, and SHOW JOBS introspection. When a job's cron pattern matches the current minute, the scheduler updates the job's last_run_unix field. You can then check that a tick fired with SHOW JOBS. It also logs a line to stderr. Running the job's SQL body is not done yet. That part comes in a future release. Do not rely on the SQL in DO being run yet.

sections.heading: Parameters
sections.text: IF EXISTS Do not raise an error if a job with the same name is already registered. When the named job exists, the statement succeeds without a message. The existing job stays the same. The cron expression and SQL body in the new statement are ignored. Note the keyword spelling. EvolutionDB's grammar accepts the bare IF EXISTS form here. It does not use PostgreSQL's IF NOT EXISTS form. name The identifier of the job to create. It must be unique among scheduled jobs. Without IF EXISTS, creating a job whose name already exists raises an error. The SQLSTATE is 42710. ON SCHEDULE 'min hour dom mon dow' A five-field cron expression given as a quoted string. The fields are minute, hour, day-of-month, month, and day-of-week. Spaces or tabs separate the fields. Each field is either * or a single non-negative integer. The * matches any value. Ranges like 1-5, comma lists like 1,15,30, and step values like */5 are not supported. Each field has a valid integer range. Minute is 0–59. Hour is 0–23. Day-of-month is 1–31. Month is 1–12. Day-of-week is 0–7, where both 0 and 7 mean Sunday. Matching is done in UTC. An expression that does not parse to exactly five valid fields raises an error. The SQLSTATE is 22023. DO 'sql_statement' The SQL statement to link with the job. You give it as a quoted string literal. It is stored in the catalog exactly as written. The DO body must not be empty.

sections.heading: Examples
sections.text: This example creates a job that runs every day at 03:00 UTC. CREATE JOB nightly_rollup ON SCHEDULE '0 3 * * *' DO 'INSERT INTO daily_totals SELECT today(), sum(amount) FROM orders'; This example creates a job that fires at the top of every hour. CREATE JOB hourly_ping ON SCHEDULE '0 * * * *' DO 'UPDATE heartbeat SET ticked_at = now()'; This example creates a job only if no job with that name already exists. CREATE JOB IF EXISTS weekly_cleanup ON SCHEDULE '30 4 * * 0' -- 04:30 every Sunday (dow 0) DO 'DELETE FROM sessions WHERE expired = TRUE'; This example shows how to inspect, disable, re-enable, and remove a job. SHOW JOBS; ALTER JOB hourly_ping DISABLE; ALTER JOB hourly_ping ENABLE; DROP JOB [ IF EXISTS ] hourly_ping;

sections.heading: Compatibility
sections.text: CREATE JOB is not part of the SQL standard. The SQL standard does not define scheduled jobs. It is also not a PostgreSQL command. Core PostgreSQL has no built-in scheduler. The closest thing in PostgreSQL is the third-party pg_cron extension. Its cron.schedule('name', '0 3 * * *', 'SQL') function call uses the same five-field cron style. EvolutionDB offers this as native DDL instead of a function. Its cron evaluator is kept simple on purpose. These are the rules. Exactly five fields. Each field is either * or a single integer. No ranges, comma lists, or step values. This is unlike pg_cron and standard crontab. Day-of-week accepts 0 and 7 for Sunday. This follows the POSIX cron style. Matching is done in UTC. The IF EXISTS spelling here is different from PostgreSQL's usual IF NOT EXISTS guard on CREATE statements. In this release the job's SQL body is matched and timestamped but not yet run. See the note under Description.

sections.heading: See Also
sections.text: SHOW CREATE PROCEDURE CALL CREATE TRIGGER

sections.heading: Introduction
sections.text: CREATE MEMORY STORE Create a new long-term agent memory store. It is a namespaced key/value store. It can also hold vector embeddings for semantic search.

sections.heading: Synopsis
sections.text: CREATE MEMORY STORE [ IF NOT EXISTS ] store_name [ WITH ( option [, ... ] ) ] where option is one of: embedding_dim = N distance = 'cosine' | 'l2' | 'inner' | 'ip' | 'l1'

sections.heading: Description
sections.text: CREATE MEMORY STORE adds a new memory store in the current schema. It also creates the table behind it. A memory store is made for agent long-term memory. Each record has a namespace and a key. Each record holds a JSON value. Each record can also hold a vector embedding for semantic similarity search. The store is backed by a normal table. The table is named mem_<store_name> and is created in the current schema. The table has these columns. mem_namespace, VARCHAR(255) NOT NULL mem_key, VARCHAR(255) NOT NULL pk, VARCHAR(512) PRIMARY KEY. It joins the namespace and the key. mem_value, JSON. It can be null. embedding, VECTOR(N). It exists only when embedding_dim is greater than 0. created_at, TIMESTAMP ttl_at, TIMESTAMP. It can be null. When you leave out embedding_dim or set it to 0, there is no embedding column. The store then works as a plain namespaced JSON key/value table. In that case MEMORY SEARCH on the store fails. There is no embedding column to score against. The backing table is a normal table. So everything that works for tables works for a memory store. This includes MVCC, primary-key uniqueness on (namespace, key), row-level security policies, triggers, and foreign-key cascades. The MEMORY PUT, GET, SEARCH, DELETE, and LIST NAMESPACES commands are simple wrappers. They turn into normal INSERT/SELECT/DELETE statements on mem_<store_name>. To create a store, no memory store with the same name may exist yet. With IF NOT EXISTS, no error is raised if the store already exists. The existing store stays the same.

sections.heading: Parameters
sections.text: IF NOT EXISTS Do nothing if a memory store with the same name already exists. No error is raised. The existing store is not changed. Its embedding_dim and distance settings stay the same. store_name The name of the memory store to create. The backing table is named mem_<store_name> and is created in the current schema. embedding_dim = N The number of dimensions in the vector embedding kept with each record. When N > 0, an embedding VECTOR(N) column is added to the backing table. The store then supports MEMORY SEARCH ... USING VECTOR. When you leave out this option, the default is 0. No embedding column is created. The store is a plain JSON key/value store. distance = 'metric' The distance metric used by MEMORY SEARCH to rank results against the query vector. This setting is saved on the store. It picks which vector function scores each row at search time. These are the accepted values. 'cosine' is cosine distance. This is the default. It is also used when you leave out the option or give a value that is not known. 'l2' is Euclidean distance. It is also called L2. 'inner' or 'ip' is inner product. 'l1' is Manhattan distance. It is also called L1. The metric matters only when embedding_dim > 0.

sections.heading: Examples
sections.text: Create a plain JSON key/value memory store. It has no semantic search. CREATE MEMORY STORE agent_notes; Create a store with 1536-dimension embeddings. It uses the default cosine metric. CREATE MEMORY STORE agent_memory WITH (embedding_dim = 1536); Create a store with embeddings ranked by L2 distance. Create it only if it does not already exist. CREATE MEMORY STORE IF NOT EXISTS chat_recall WITH (embedding_dim = 768, distance = 'l2'); This example populates a store and queries it after creating it. -- store a value (upsert on namespace+key) MEMORY PUT INTO agent_memory VALUES ('user:42', 'pref:lang', '{"lang":"tr"}', '[0.1,0.2,...]'); -- read it back MEMORY GET FROM agent_memory WHERE NS = 'user:42' AND KEY = 'pref:lang'; -- semantic search (requires embedding_dim > 0) MEMORY SEARCH agent_memory USING VECTOR '[0.1,0.2,...]' LIMIT 5;

sections.heading: Compatibility
sections.text: CREATE MEMORY STORE is an EvolutionDB extension. It is not part of the SQL standard. PostgreSQL has no matching statement. The closest thing in PostgreSQL is to create a table and add a pgvector column by hand. The EvolutionDB memory store puts the namespaced key/value schema, the optional embedding column, and the distance metric into one simple command. Here are some notes on the EvolutionDB behavior. The WITH clause accepts only the embedding_dim and distance options. There is no other storage-parameter syntax. PostgreSQL-style table options are not accepted here. The only known distances are 'cosine', 'l2', 'inner'/'ip', and 'l1'. Any other string quietly falls back to 'cosine'. The backing table mem_<store_name> is a real table. It is fully visible to normal SQL. You can read, secure, or trigger its rows just like any user table.

sections.heading: See Also
sections.text: CREATE TABLE CREATE POLICY CREATE TRIGGER SELECT INSERT DELETE

sections.heading: Introduction
sections.text: CREATE MESSAGE LOG Create an append-only message log. This is a managed store for chat and conversation history. It is keyed by session.

sections.heading: Synopsis
sections.text: CREATE MESSAGE LOG name [ WITH ( ttl = 'N days' ) ]

sections.heading: Description
sections.text: CREATE MESSAGE LOG registers a new append-only message log. A message log is built for storing conversation history. This means agent chat turns, session transcripts, and similar work where you mostly add new rows. You reach the data by a session identifier and not by raw SQL row operations. The command creates a normal backing table named ml_name in the current schema. It also adds an entry in the system catalog. That entry links the log name to the table. The backing table has these fixed columns. seq, BIGINT Primary key. It auto-increments and starts at 1 with a step of 1. It sets the append order of messages. session_id, VARCHAR(255) NOT NULL The session that the message belongs to. All read, truncate, and count operations filter on this column. role, VARCHAR(64) The message author or role. For example user, assistant, or system. content, VARCHAR(8000) The message body. meta, VARCHAR(8000) Optional metadata. It is stored as a JSON-shaped string. It is NULL when you leave it out on append. created_at, TIMESTAMP NOT NULL An ISO timestamp in UTC. The engine creates it at append time. The log is backed by a regular table. So all of EvolutionDB's existing constraint, MVCC, and snapshot machinery still applies. Appends go through the standard INSERT path and fill seq by auto-increment. Reads go through SELECT. Truncation goes through DELETE. The keyword LOG is not a reserved word in the lexer. The parser action checks it instead. If you write CREATE MESSAGE name name with any word other than LOG in that spot, it raises expected LOG after MESSAGE in CREATE MESSAGE LOG. After you create the log, you fill it and query it with the companion MESSAGE DML statements. These are MESSAGE APPEND, MESSAGE READ, MESSAGE TRUNCATE, and MESSAGE COUNT.

sections.heading: Parameters
sections.text: name The name of the message log to create. The backing table is created as ml_name in the current schema. If a message log with this name already exists, an error is raised. The SQLSTATE is 42710 and the message is message log 'name' already exists. WITH ( ttl = 'N days' ) Optional. It sets a time-to-live retention hint in days. The hint is recorded with the log. The parser reads the literal by taking the first integer it contains. So both '7 days' and a bare '7' resolve to 7. When you leave out the clause, the TTL defaults to 0 and no TTL is recorded. ttl is the only option accepted inside the WITH (...) list.

sections.heading: Examples
sections.text: This example creates a basic message log. CREATE MESSAGE LOG chat_history; This example creates a log with a retention hint of 7 days. CREATE MESSAGE LOG agent_sessions WITH (ttl = '7 days'); This example appends messages to a session and then reads them back. MESSAGE APPEND INTO chat_history VALUES ('sess-42', 'user', 'What is EvolutionDB?'); -- with optional JSON metadata as a 4th value MESSAGE APPEND INTO chat_history VALUES ('sess-42', 'assistant', 'A SQL engine in C.', '{"tokens":7}'); -- read the whole session (ordered by seq) MESSAGE READ FROM chat_history WHERE SESSION = 'sess-42'; -- read only the most recent 10 turns MESSAGE READ FROM chat_history WHERE SESSION = 'sess-42' LAST 10; This example counts and prunes a session. -- how many messages in the session MESSAGE COUNT FROM chat_history WHERE SESSION = 'sess-42'; -- delete everything for the session MESSAGE TRUNCATE FROM chat_history WHERE SESSION = 'sess-42'; -- delete only rows older than seq 1000 (seq < 1000) MESSAGE TRUNCATE FROM chat_history WHERE SESSION = 'sess-42' BEFORE 1000; This example removes the log and its backing table. DROP MESSAGE LOG chat_history; DROP MESSAGE LOG IF EXISTS chat_history;

sections.heading: Compatibility
sections.text: CREATE MESSAGE LOG is an EvolutionDB extension. It is not part of the SQL standard. It has no equivalent in PostgreSQL. PostgreSQL has no MESSAGE LOG object type and no MESSAGE DML family. In any SQL system, the closest standard way would be to build your own table by hand and write your own INSERT, SELECT, and DELETE queries. CREATE MESSAGE LOG just wraps that pattern in a session-oriented command set. EvolutionDB's WITH clause here is kept small on purpose. The only option it knows is ttl. Unlike PostgreSQL storage-parameter lists, it has no comma-separated set of extra options.

sections.heading: See Also
sections.text: CREATE TABLE DROP TABLE CREATE MEMORY STORE CREATE DOC STORE INSERT SELECT DELETE

sections.heading: Introduction
sections.text: CREATE POLICY Create a row-level security policy. It limits which rows users can read or write on a table.

sections.heading: Synopsis
sections.text: CREATE POLICY name ON table_name [ AS { PERMISSIVE | RESTRICTIVE } ] [ FOR { ALL | SELECT | INSERT | UPDATE | DELETE } ] [ TO role_name [, ...] ] [ USING ( using_expression ) ] [ WITH CHECK ( check_expression ) ] -- Memory-store variant (sugar; resolves to the backing table) CREATE POLICY name ON MEMORY STORE store_name [ AS { PERMISSIVE | RESTRICTIVE } ] [ FOR { ALL | SELECT | INSERT | UPDATE | DELETE } ] [ TO role_name [, ...] ] [ USING ( using_expression ) ] [ WITH CHECK ( check_expression ) ]

sections.heading: Description
sections.text: CREATE POLICY creates a row-level security (RLS) policy. It stores the policy in the system catalog against the target table. A policy holds a row-filter expression. EvolutionDB applies this expression on its own when ordinary users run SELECT, INSERT, UPDATE, or DELETE on the table. Creating a policy on its own does not change how queries behave. Row-level security only takes effect after you turn it on for the table with ALTER TABLE table_name ENABLE ROW LEVEL SECURITY. While RLS is on, every policy that applies is checked on each affected row. At other times the policies stay inactive. The admin account is a superuser. It skips all policies. It always sees and can write every row. This is true no matter how many policies exist or whether RLS is on. The expressions combine like this for a given statement and user. Permissive policies are the default. They are joined with OR. A row is visible or writable if it passes any one permissive policy that applies to the user and command. Restrictive policies are joined with AND. They are added on top of the permissive result. A row must pass every restrictive policy that applies. It must also pass the permissive set. Sometimes no permissive policy applies to the current user and command. Then the result is "no rows". The table looks empty for reads. Writes are rejected. At least one permissive policy must match before any access is allowed. USING controls which existing rows a command can see. It is added to the query's WHERE filter for SELECT, UPDATE, and DELETE. WITH CHECK controls which new or changed row values can be written by INSERT and UPDATE. A write is rejected if its new row fails the check. When you leave out WITH CHECK, the USING expression is used as the write check. This matches PostgreSQL. The ON MEMORY STORE store_name form is a shortcut for protecting a memory store. It resolves the store to its backing mem_store_name table. Then it creates an ordinary table policy there. Policy expressions can use the backing columns like any other column. These columns include mem_namespace, mem_key, and mem_value.

sections.heading: Parameters
sections.text: name The name of the new policy. Policy names belong to their table. You can use the same name on different tables. If you create a policy with a name that already exists on the same table, you get a duplicate-object error. table_name The existing table the policy applies to. If the relation does not exist, the command fails with relation "table_name" does not exist. ON MEMORY STORE store_name Apply the policy to a memory store instead of a regular table. EvolutionDB looks up the store. Then it targets the store's backing mem_store_name table. If the store is not found, the command fails with the same relation-not-found error. AS PERMISSIVE | AS RESTRICTIVE Sets how the policy combines with others. PERMISSIVE policies are joined with OR. RESTRICTIVE policies are joined with AND on top. When you leave out AS, the policy is PERMISSIVE. FOR ALL | FOR SELECT | FOR INSERT | FOR UPDATE | FOR DELETE Limits the policy to one command type, or to all commands. When you leave out FOR, the policy applies to ALL commands. Only the single keywords shown here are accepted. Comma-separated command lists are not supported. TO role_name [..] The roles the policy applies to. Roles are users. Role matching is case-insensitive against the connected username. The special role public matches every user. When you leave out TO, the policy applies to all users. USING ( using_expression ) A boolean expression. It is checked for each row to decide row visibility for SELECT, UPDATE, and DELETE. Rows where it is false are hidden from the user. The expression can use the table's columns. The parsed expression is serialized and stored in the catalog. WITH CHECK ( check_expression ) A boolean expression. It is checked against the proposed new row for INSERT and UPDATE. A write is rejected if its resulting row fails the check. If you leave it out, the USING expression is used as the check. Note. A policy does nothing until you run ALTER TABLE table_name ENABLE ROW LEVEL SECURITY. For a memory store, use ALTER MEMORY STORE store_name ENABLE ROW LEVEL SECURITY.

sections.heading: Examples
sections.text: This example limits a table so each tenant sees only its own rows. It then turns on enforcement. CREATE POLICY tenant_isolation ON orders USING (tenant_id = current_tenant); ALTER TABLE orders ENABLE ROW LEVEL SECURITY; This example creates a read-only policy for one role. It also adds a write check that stops back-dated inserts. CREATE POLICY analyst_read ON events FOR SELECT TO analyst, auditor USING (region = 'EU'); CREATE POLICY no_backdating ON events FOR INSERT WITH CHECK (created_at >= '2026-01-01'); This example adds a restrictive policy on top of permissive ones. A row must pass both. It applies to everyone through public. CREATE POLICY hide_archived ON documents AS RESTRICTIVE TO public USING (archived = false); This example protects a memory store. The policy is created on its backing table. CREATE POLICY session_scope ON MEMORY STORE sessions USING (mem_namespace = owner_namespace); ALTER MEMORY STORE sessions ENABLE ROW LEVEL SECURITY; This example removes a policy. DROP POLICY tenant_isolation ON orders;

sections.heading: Compatibility
sections.text: CREATE POLICY is not part of the SQL standard. It follows PostgreSQL's row-level security extension. EvolutionDB supports a useful subset of that extension. Supported. AS PERMISSIVE and RESTRICTIVE with permissive as the default. FOR ALL, SELECT, INSERT, UPDATE, and DELETE with all as the default. TO role lists with the public pseudo-role. USING. WITH CHECK. As in PostgreSQL, a missing WITH CHECK falls back to USING. Permissive policies join with OR. Restrictive policies join with AND on top. Differences. FOR accepts only a single command keyword. There is no ALTER POLICY command. To change a policy, drop it and create it again. The ON MEMORY STORE form is an EvolutionDB extension. PostgreSQL has no equivalent. Role matching is case-insensitive against the connected username. The built-in admin account is always a superuser. It bypasses all policies. As in PostgreSQL, policies have no effect until row-level security is enabled on the table. A table with RLS enabled but no permissive policy that applies denies all access to non-superusers.

sections.heading: See Also
sections.text: ALTER TABLE. It turns row-level security on or off for a table. CREATE TABLE SELECT INSERT UPDATE DELETE

sections.heading: Introduction
sections.text: CREATE PROCEDURE Create a new stored procedure. The body is a block of SQL statements. EvolutionDB stores it as raw text. It runs the statements one by one when you call the procedure with CALL.

sections.heading: Synopsis
sections.text: CREATE [ OR REPLACE ] PROCEDURE name ( [ argument [, ...] ] ) [ AS ] BEGIN statements END where argument is: [ IN | OUT | INOUT ] arg_name arg_type

sections.heading: Description
sections.text: CREATE PROCEDURE adds a named procedure to the current database and schema. EvolutionDB does not compile the body. It keeps the raw text between the opening BEGIN and its matching END exactly as you wrote it. It saves this text in the system catalog. When you run CALL, EvolutionDB splits the stored text into single statements. It runs each one through the normal query path. The procedure is created in the database and schema that are current when you define it. EvolutionDB resolves these through the system catalog. A procedure with the same name may already exist in that schema. In that case the statement fails with a "procedure already exists" error. The SQLSTATE is 42P07. To avoid the error, use OR REPLACE. Then EvolutionDB drops the old definition first and creates it again. The body sits between a matching BEGIN and END pair. EvolutionDB tracks nested blocks while it looks for the closing keyword. Each inner BEGIN raises the nesting depth. Each plain END lowers it. Some terminators are compound, such as END IF, END WHILE, END LOOP, END FOR, END FOREACH and END CASE. EvolutionDB knows these and they do not close the procedure. The outer END at depth zero ends the definition. EvolutionDB ignores text inside single-quoted string literals during this scan. Parameters in the argument list become substitution targets inside the body. When you call the procedure, EvolutionDB replaces two things before it runs each statement. It replaces positional placeholders like $1 and $2. It also replaces plain references to a parameter's name. The values come from the arguments you pass to CALL. A reference that matches nothing stays as it is. Text inside string literals is copied as-is with no substitution. The body may hold ordinary SQL statements. It may also hold control-flow blocks. These include IF … END IF, WHILE … END WHILE, LOOP … END LOOP, FOR … END FOR, FOREACH … END FOREACH and CASE … END CASE. The engine splits these and builds a block map at call time. The grammar accepts argument modes. These are IN, OUT and INOUT. EvolutionDB records them. But procedures run only by text substitution at CALL time. There is no separate way to bind output values. A procedure with no parameters still needs the empty parentheses.

sections.heading: Parameters
sections.text: OR REPLACE Replace a procedure that already has the same name in the current schema. This way you do not get an error. If you leave it out, defining over an existing procedure fails with SQLSTATE 42P07. name The name of the procedure to create. EvolutionDB adds it under the current database and schema. IN | OUT | INOUT The optional mode of the argument that follows. If you give no mode keyword, the argument is IN by default. EvolutionDB stores the mode with the parameter. It does not change how text substitution runs. arg_name The name of an argument. Inside the body you can refer to it by this name. You can also use its positional placeholder. $1 is the first argument. $2 is the second argument, and so on. Name matching is case-insensitive. arg_type The data type of the argument. For example INT, VARCHAR(50), TEXT or BOOLEAN. It shows the kind of argument you expect. EvolutionDB parses it like a normal column type. AS An optional keyword you may place right before BEGIN. It has no effect. The definition is the same with or without AS. statements The procedure body. It is a series of SQL statements and control-flow blocks between BEGIN and the matching outer END. You separate statements with semicolons. EvolutionDB stores the text as-is. It runs the text at CALL time.

sections.heading: Examples
sections.text: A procedure with no arguments that runs an insert. CREATE PROCEDURE add_default_user() BEGIN INSERT INTO users (name, active) VALUES ('guest', TRUE); END; A procedure that uses named parameters. EvolutionDB puts them into the body at call time. CREATE PROCEDURE raise_salary(dept_id INT, pct INT) AS BEGIN UPDATE employees SET salary = salary + (salary * pct / 100) WHERE department = dept_id; END; -- invoke it CALL raise_salary(3, 10); The same body with positional placeholders instead of names. CREATE PROCEDURE raise_salary(dept_id INT, pct INT) BEGIN UPDATE employees SET salary = salary + (salary * $2 / 100) WHERE department = $1; END; Redefining a procedure with OR REPLACE and a control-flow body. CREATE OR REPLACE PROCEDURE archive_or_skip(min_age INT) BEGIN IF min_age > 0 THEN DELETE FROM sessions WHERE age > min_age; END IF; END;

sections.heading: Compatibility
sections.text: The EvolutionDB CREATE PROCEDURE looks more like the SQL/PSM persistent stored modules form and the MySQL procedure syntax than the PostgreSQL one. Here are the main differences. No language clause. PostgreSQL needs LANGUAGE. It also wraps the body in a dollar-quoted or single-quoted string. EvolutionDB has no LANGUAGE clause. It does not quote the body. You write the body straight as a BEGIN … END block. AS is just an optional word with no effect. Textual parameters. EvolutionDB applies arguments by plain text substitution. It uses $n placeholders or parameter names. It does not bind them as typed values. EvolutionDB records argument types and the IN, OUT and INOUT modes. But they do not set up typed binding or output parameters. Unsupported PostgreSQL options. The EvolutionDB grammar does not include some clauses. These include SECURITY DEFINER/INVOKER, SET configuration, TRANSFORM and EXTERNAL. It also does not allow parameters with DEFAULT values. EvolutionDB does not accept any of these. Single-name resolution. You cannot overload procedures by argument signature. EvolutionDB finds a procedure by its name within a database and schema. DROP PROCEDURE takes only the bare name. It needs no argument list. The related CREATE FUNCTION form uses this same grammar. It adds a RETURNS type clause. The same engine stores and runs both.

sections.heading: See Also
sections.text: CREATE TRIGGER CREATE TABLE INSERT UPDATE DELETE

sections.heading: Introduction
sections.text: CREATE SEQUENCE Create a new sequence generator. A sequence is a named counter that is saved on disk. It gives you a series of BIGINT values that always go in one direction. You read these values with the nextval(), currval(), setval() and lastval() functions.

sections.heading: Synopsis
sections.text: CREATE SEQUENCE [ IF NOT EXISTS ] name [ INCREMENT [ BY ] increment ] [ MINVALUE minvalue ] [ MAXVALUE maxvalue ] [ START [ WITH ] start ] [ CYCLE | NOT CYCLE ]

sections.heading: Description
sections.text: CREATE SEQUENCE creates a new sequence-number generator. The sequence is saved as an object in the system catalog of the current schema. It stays on disk after a server restart. Once the sequence exists, you work with it through the sequence functions. nextval(name) moves the sequence forward and returns the new value. The first call to nextval() on a brand new sequence returns the START value. At that point the sequence has not been used yet. Each later call adds increment to the current value. currval() returns the value you got most recently. lastval() returns the value of the sequence you used most recently in this session. setval() sets the current value to a number you choose. If the given name already belongs to a sequence in the current schema, you get an error. To avoid this, use IF NOT EXISTS. Then you get a notice and no new sequence is created.
