Keep in mind that IF NOT EXISTS only checks if a sequence with that name exists. It does not check if the old definition matches the new one you asked for. Each call to nextval() runs one at a time under an internal mutex. The new value is saved to the catalog right away. Because of this, two sessions never get the same value. The new value is saved before it is returned. So sequence values used inside a transaction are not rolled back if that transaction fails. This means gaps in the series are normal and expected. When increment is positive, the sequence counts up. When it is negative, the sequence counts down. An ascending sequence may reach maxvalue. A descending sequence may reach minvalue. A sequence with CYCLE then wraps around. An ascending one goes back to minvalue. A descending one goes back to maxvalue. A sequence without cycling raises an error instead.

sections.heading: Parameters
sections.text: IF NOT EXISTS Do not raise an error if a sequence with the same name already exists in the current schema. You get a notice instead. The old sequence is left unchanged. name The name of the sequence to create. The sequence is created in the current schema. You cannot add a schema name to it in this form. INCREMENT [ BY ] increment The value added to the current sequence value on each call to nextval(). A positive value makes the sequence count up. A negative value makes it count down. The BY keyword is optional. The default is 1. MINVALUE minvalue The smallest value the sequence can produce. The default is 1. For a descending sequence, this is the wrap target when CYCLE is set. For a descending sequence without cycling, this is the floor where it raises an error. MAXVALUE maxvalue The largest value the sequence can produce. The default is 2147483647. This is 231−1. For an ascending sequence, this is the wrap target when CYCLE is set. For an ascending sequence without cycling, this is the ceiling where it raises an error. START [ WITH ] start The first value the sequence returns. This is the value you get from the first nextval() call. The WITH keyword is optional. If you leave it out, the start value is minvalue. CYCLE / NOT CYCLE CYCLE lets the sequence wrap around when it reaches a limit. An ascending sequence wraps at maxvalue. A descending sequence wraps at minvalue. NOT CYCLE is the default. With it, nextval() raises an error once a limit is reached. Note. The clause order in the Synopsis is just a convention. It is not required. The grammar accepts the options in any order. If you give the same option twice, the later one wins. Sequence values are 64-bit signed integers. This is the BIGINT type. But the default MAXVALUE is 2147483647. Set MAXVALUE yourself if you need the full 64-bit range.

sections.heading: Examples
sections.text: Create an ascending sequence that starts at 1 and goes up by 1. This uses all the defaults. CREATE SEQUENCE order_id_seq; This example reads values from the sequence. SELECT nextval('order_id_seq'); -- 1 (the START value) SELECT nextval('order_id_seq'); -- 2 SELECT currval('order_id_seq'); -- 2 Create a sequence that starts at 1000 and counts up by 10. CREATE SEQUENCE invoice_seq START WITH 1000 INCREMENT BY 10; Create a sequence that has limits and cycles. It goes from 1 to 5, then back to 1. CREATE SEQUENCE day_slot_seq MINVALUE 1 MAXVALUE 5 CYCLE; Create a sequence that counts down. CREATE SEQUENCE countdown_seq START 100 INCREMENT -1 MINVALUE 0; Create the sequence only if it does not exist yet. If it already exists, skip it. CREATE SEQUENCE IF NOT EXISTS session_seq; Move a sequence to a new spot with setval(). After this, the next nextval() call returns 200. SELECT setval('invoice_seq', 200);

sections.heading: Compatibility
sections.text: CREATE SEQUENCE follows the spirit of the SQL standard. EvolutionDB has a few differences from it. They are listed below. EvolutionDB supports the clauses INCREMENT [BY], MINVALUE, MAXVALUE, START [WITH] and CYCLE / NOT CYCLE. It also adds the extra clause IF NOT EXISTS. The standard uses the keyword NO CYCLE. In EvolutionDB you write NOT CYCLE instead. The forms NO MINVALUE and NO MAXVALUE are not supported. Leave the clause out to use the default. The grammar does not accept the PostgreSQL clauses AS data_type, CACHE, OWNED BY and TEMPORARY/UNLOGGED. Sequences are always saved BIGINT generators. Every nextval() writes straight to the catalog. There is no client-side cache. The standard treats nextval/currval as expressions. Like PostgreSQL, EvolutionDB makes them functions. You pass the sequence name as a string. For example, nextval('seq'). The default MAXVALUE is 2147483647, not the 64-bit maximum. Set it yourself when you need a higher ceiling.

sections.heading: See Also
sections.text: DROP SEQUENCE ALTER SEQUENCE CREATE TABLE SELECT INSERT

sections.heading: Introduction
sections.text: CREATE SUBSCRIPTION Set up a durable mailbox. It keeps every NOTIFY committed on a channel. A consumer can disconnect, come back later, and read the backlog in order.

sections.heading: Synopsis
sections.text: CREATE SUBSCRIPTION [ IF NOT EXISTS ] subscription_name FOR CHANNEL channel_name

sections.heading: Description
sections.text: CREATE SUBSCRIPTION creates a durable subscription. It sits on top of EvolutionDB's in-memory LISTEN/NOTIFY mechanism. A plain LISTEN only delivers a notification if a session is connected and listening at the moment a NOTIFY fires. Anything sent while the consumer is offline is lost. A subscription fixes this gap. It binds a named, lasting mailbox to a channel. Once the subscription exists, every NOTIFY that commits on channel_name adds a row to a private backing table. Each row holds a sequence number that always goes up. This is the seq value. The row also holds the notification payload and a posted_at timestamp. The consumer reads the backlog with RESUME SUBSCRIPTION. That command returns every row whose seq is greater than the acknowledged watermark, sorted by seq from low to high. Then ACK SUBSCRIPTION ... UPTO N moves the watermark forward. Creating the subscription does two things behind the scenes. It creates a backing table named sub_<subscription_name> in the current schema. The table has these columns. seq BIGINT PRIMARY KEY. payload VARCHAR(8000), which can be null. And posted_at TIMESTAMP NOT NULL. It writes a catalog entry. The entry records the channel binding and the backing-table id. It records the acknowledgement watermark, called last_ack_seq, which starts at 0. It records the next sequence number to assign, called next_seq, which starts at 1. It also records a retention window of 7 days. The auto-RECLAIM daemon uses this window when it removes acknowledged and expired rows. Durable writes happen on the NOTIFY commit path. They run after the in-memory listener fan-out. So live consumers do not pay the cost of the on-disk write when no subscription is set up on the channel. The channel binding matches the channel named in NOTIFY. The match is case-insensitive. A subscription only keeps notifications committed after it is created. It does not go back and pick up notifications that fired earlier.

sections.heading: Parameters
sections.text: IF NOT EXISTS Do nothing if a subscription with the same name already exists. It does not raise an error. This does not promise that the existing subscription is bound to the same channel. It only hides the duplicate-name error. The lexer reads the phrase as one NOT EXISTS token. So the spelling is fixed. subscription_name The name of the new subscription. The name must be unique among subscriptions. If you try to create a duplicate without IF NOT EXISTS, the command raises SQLSTATE 42710. This means duplicate object. The name is also used to build the backing table name sub_<subscription_name>. FOR CHANNEL channel_name The notification channel this subscription keeps. This clause is required. If you leave it out, the command raises a syntax error with SQLSTATE 42601. Every NOTIFY committed on this channel name adds one row to the subscription's mailbox. Not supported. EvolutionDB's CREATE SUBSCRIPTION has no CONNECTION, PUBLICATION, or WITH (...) clause. The grammar accepts only the two forms shown in the Synopsis. The retention window has a fixed default of 7 days. You cannot change it through the statement right now.

sections.heading: Examples
sections.text: Create a durable subscription bound to the orders channel. CREATE SUBSCRIPTION orders_mailbox FOR CHANNEL orders; Create one only if it does not already exist. This makes the setup idempotent. CREATE SUBSCRIPTION IF NOT EXISTS orders_mailbox FOR CHANNEL orders; A producer sends a message to the channel. The payload is kept durably even if no session is listening. NOTIFY orders, 'order #4821 created'; Later, a consumer reconnects and reads the backlog. Then it acknowledges the last sequence it processed. RESUME SUBSCRIPTION orders_mailbox; -- returns (seq, payload, posted_at) for seq > last_ack ACK SUBSCRIPTION orders_mailbox UPTO 12; List the registered subscriptions and remove one when you no longer need it. SHOW SUBSCRIPTIONS; DROP SUBSCRIPTION [IF EXISTS] orders_mailbox;

sections.heading: Compatibility
sections.text: CREATE SUBSCRIPTION is not part of the SQL standard. PostgreSQL also has a CREATE SUBSCRIPTION statement. It does something very different. It is part of logical replication. It subscribes to a publication on a remote server through a CONNECTION string. EvolutionDB's command shares only the keyword. Here it is a local, single-server feature. It adds durability on top of LISTEN/NOTIFY. There is no remote connection, no publication, and no replication slot. EvolutionDB's grammar does not accept the PostgreSQL CONNECTION, PUBLICATION, and WITH options. The related verbs RESUME SUBSCRIPTION and ACK SUBSCRIPTION ... UPTO read and acknowledge the mailbox. They are EvolutionDB extensions. PostgreSQL has no equivalent.

sections.heading: See Also
sections.text: LISTEN / NOTIFY RECLAIM SHOW SELECT

sections.heading: Introduction
sections.text: CREATE TABLE Make a new base table in the current schema. You set its columns, their types, the column attributes, and the table constraints. You can also fill a new table from a query result.

sections.heading: Synopsis
sections.text: CREATE [ TEMPORARY | GLOBAL TEMPORARY ] TABLE [ IF NOT EXISTS ] [ schema . ]table_name ( { column_name data_type [ column_attribute [ ... ] ] | table_constraint } [ , ... ] ) [ table_option [ ... ] ] [ INHERITS ( parent_table ) ] -- Create a table from a query result (CTAS): CREATE [ TEMPORARY | GLOBAL TEMPORARY ] TABLE [ IF NOT EXISTS ] [ schema . ]table_name [ ( column_name [ , ... ] ) ] [ IGNORE | REPLACE ] [ AS ] select_statement -- Create a range partition child bound to an existing parent: CREATE [ TEMPORARY | GLOBAL TEMPORARY ] TABLE [ IF NOT EXISTS ] table_name PARTITION OF parent FOR VALUES FROM ( lo ) TO ( hi ) where column_attribute is one of: NOT NULL | NULL DEFAULT value DEFAULT { gen_random_uuid() | gen_random_uuid_v7() | snowflake_id() | CURRENT_TIMESTAMP | ( expr ) } AUTO_INCREMENT [ ( start [ , increment ] ) ] IDENTITY [ ( start [ , increment ] ) ] PRIMARY KEY | KEY UNIQUE [ KEY ] CHECK ( expr ) COMMENT 'string' CONSTRAINT name { UNIQUE | PRIMARY KEY | CHECK ( expr ) } GENERATED ALWAYS AS ( expr ) [ STORED | VIRTUAL ] AS ( expr ) [ STORED | VIRTUAL ] and table_constraint is one of: [ CONSTRAINT name ] PRIMARY KEY ( column [ , ... ] ) [ CONSTRAINT name ] UNIQUE ( column [ , ... ] ) [ CONSTRAINT name ] CHECK ( expr ) [ CONSTRAINT name ] FOREIGN KEY ( column [ , ... ] ) REFERENCES [ schema . ]ref_table ( ref_column [ , ... ] ) [ fk_action [ ... ] ] KEY ( column [ , ... ] ) | INDEX ( column [ , ... ] ) FULLTEXT { INDEX | KEY } ( column [ , ... ] ) and fk_action is one of: ON DELETE { CASCADE | SET NULL | RESTRICT | SET DEFAULT | NO ACTION } ON UPDATE { CASCADE | SET NULL | RESTRICT | SET DEFAULT | NO ACTION } MATCH { FULL | SIMPLE | PARTIAL } [ NOT ] DEFERRABLE [ INITIALLY { DEFERRED | IMMEDIATE } ] and table_option is one of: AUTO_INCREMENT [ = ] n ON COMMIT { DELETE ROWS | PRESERVE ROWS } SHARD BY HASH ( column ) SHARDS n SHARD BY RANGE ( column ) ( shard_def [ , ... ] ) PARTITION BY RANGE ( column ) WITH SYSTEM VERSIONING WITH ( ttl_column = 'column_name' )

sections.heading: Description
sections.text: CREATE TABLE makes a new base table in the current schema of the current database. The table starts empty. The user who runs the statement owns the table. Each column gets a name, a data type, and an optional list of column attributes. Table constraints and table options can come after the column list. EvolutionDB finds the current database and schema before it makes the table. You can write the table name as schema.table_name. Then the first name picks the target schema. If you do not write a schema, EvolutionDB uses the current schema of the session. See SET SCHEMA. The statement fails if the target schema does not exist. Columns and storage use EvolutionDB's binary tuple format. Every record starts with a sentinel byte and the table id. After that comes a header, a null bitmap, and the column data. A type code holds both a type family and a size. For example, VARCHAR(50) is encoded as 130050. A table can have up to 256 columns. The statement fails if a table with the same name already exists in the target schema. You get a "table already exists" error with SQLSTATE 42P07. To avoid this, add IF NOT EXISTS. Then EvolutionDB leaves the existing table as it is and the statement succeeds with no error. Create from a query (CTAS). You can put a select_statement after the table name. Then the new table is filled from the query result. If you give a column list, those names are used. If you do not, EvolutionDB takes the column names and types from the SELECT output. IGNORE and REPLACE control what happens to rows that break a uniqueness rule during the bulk load. Inheritance. INHERITS ( parent ) adds the parent table's columns at the front of the child's own columns. The parent must already exist. A child column cannot reuse the name of an inherited column. Partition child. The PARTITION OF parent FOR VALUES FROM (lo) TO (hi) form makes a range-partition child. It is bound to an existing range-partitioned parent. It covers the half-open integer range [lo, hi).

sections.heading: Parameters
sections.text: TEMPORARY / GLOBAL TEMPORARY Marks the table as temporary. TEMPORARY makes a temporary table for the current session only. GLOBAL TEMPORARY makes a global temporary table. Use the ON COMMIT table option to control how long rows live when a transaction commits. IF NOT EXISTS Turns off the "table already exists" error. If a table with the same name already exists in the target schema, no new table is made and no error is raised. The grammar uses the IF NOT EXISTS clause for this. It does not check that the columns match the existing table. schema.table_name / table_name The name of the table to create. You can add a schema in front of it. A name with no schema is created in the current schema of the session. column_name The name of a column in the new table. data_type The data type of the column. The integer family has BIT, TINYINT, SMALLINT, MEDIUMINT, INT/INTEGER, and BIGINT. Each one can take an optional UNSIGNED/ZEROFILL modifier. The numeric types are REAL, DOUBLE, FLOAT, and DECIMAL(p,s). The date and time types are DATE, TIME, TIMESTAMP, DATETIME, and YEAR. The character types are CHAR(n), VARCHAR(n), TINYTEXT, TEXT, MEDIUMTEXT, and LONGTEXT. The binary types are BINARY(n), VARBINARY(n), TINYBLOB, BLOB, MEDIUMBLOB, and LONGBLOB. There are also ENUM(...), SET(...), BOOLEAN, UUID, JSON, VECTOR(dim), and array types written as type[]. Character and text types can take an optional CHAR SET 'name' / COLLATE 'name' suffix. NOT NULL / NULL Says if the column can hold NULL. NOT NULL blocks NULL values. NULL allows them. NULL is the default. DEFAULT value Gives a default value. EvolutionDB uses it when an INSERT leaves out the column. The value can be a string, an integer, a float, or a boolean literal. You can also use the function defaults gen_random_uuid(), gen_random_uuid_v7(), and snowflake_id(). The special value CURRENT_TIMESTAMP works too. You can also write an expression default as DEFAULT ( expr ). AUTO_INCREMENT [ ( start [, increment ] ) ] / IDENTITY [ ( start [, increment ] ) ] Gives the column increasing integer values on its own. With no arguments, the values start at 1 and go up by 1. You can set your own start value and an optional increment. IDENTITY is another name for the same feature. PRIMARY KEY / KEY (column attribute) Marks the column as the primary key of the table. When you use KEY as a column attribute, EvolutionDB treats it as PRIMARY KEY. UNIQUE [ KEY ] (column attribute) Makes the column hold a different value in each row. CHECK ( expr ) Sets a check that each row must pass. You can write it on a single column or as a table constraint. COMMENT 'string' Adds a short note that describes the column. GENERATED ALWAYS AS ( expr ) [ STORED | VIRTUAL ] / AS ( expr ) [ STORED | VIRTUAL ] Defines a generated column. EvolutionDB works out its value from expr. STORED saves the computed value. VIRTUAL computes it each time you read the row. VIRTUAL is the default when you give neither keyword. CONSTRAINT name.. Gives a name to a UNIQUE, PRIMARY KEY, CHECK, or FOREIGN KEY constraint. This works for a column constraint or a table constraint. A CHECK constraint with no name gets the auto name table_check_n. PRIMARY KEY ( column [.. ] ) (table constraint) Sets one or more columns as the primary key. It can use more than one column. UNIQUE ( column [.. ] ) (table constraint) Sets a uniqueness rule. It can cover one column or several columns. FOREIGN KEY ( column [.. ] ) REFERENCES [ schema. ]ref_table ( ref_column [.. ] ) [ fk_action.. ] Sets a foreign key. It links columns in this table to columns in ref_table. The other table can be in a different schema. You set the referential actions with the fk_action clauses below. fk_action, ON DELETE / ON UPDATE { CASCADE | SET NULL | RESTRICT | SET DEFAULT | NO ACTION } Picks what happens to the linked rows when the referenced row is deleted or updated. fk_action, MATCH { FULL | SIMPLE | PARTIAL } Sets how matching works for foreign keys that use more than one column. fk_action, [ NOT ] DEFERRABLE [ INITIALLY { DEFERRED | IMMEDIATE } ] Says if the constraint check can wait until the transaction commits. It also sets the first check timing. KEY / INDEX ( column [.. ] ) Asks for an index on the listed columns inside the table definition. Here INDEX and KEY mean the same thing. FULLTEXT { INDEX | KEY } ( column [.. ] ) Asks for a full-text index on the listed columns. AUTO_INCREMENT [ = ] n (table option) Sets the start value for the auto-increment values of the table. ON COMMIT { DELETE ROWS | PRESERVE ROWS } (table option) For temporary tables, this picks if rows are deleted or kept when the transaction commits. SHARD BY HASH ( column ) SHARDS n / SHARD BY RANGE ( column ) (.. ) Spreads the table across n hash shards based on column. You can also spread it across set value ranges. You write each range shard as SHARD name VALUES LESS THAN { 'value' | MAXVALUE } ON NODE id. PARTITION BY RANGE ( column ) (table option) Sets the table as range-partitioned on column. You add child partitions with the PARTITION OF form. WITH SYSTEM VERSIONING (table option) Turns the table into a system-versioned table. EvolutionDB adds validity-period columns for you. It also links a table_history shadow table. That shadow table keeps old row versions. WITH ( ttl_column = 'column_name' ) (table option) Marks a TTL column. The auto-reclaim daemon removes rows when the value in this column is older than the current time. The named column must exist in the table. INHERITS ( parent_table ) Takes the parent table's columns. EvolutionDB puts them at the front of the child's own columns. The parent must exist. Duplicate column names are not allowed. PARTITION OF parent FOR VALUES FROM ( lo ) TO ( hi ) Makes a range-partition child of an existing partitioned parent. It covers the half-open integer range from lo up to hi. The range includes lo but not hi. [ IGNORE | REPLACE ] [ AS ] select_statement Fills the new table from the result of select_statement. This is CREATE TABLE AS. IGNORE skips rows that would conflict. REPLACE replaces them. The AS keyword is optional.

sections.heading: Examples
sections.text: Make a simple table with a primary key, defaults, and a check. CREATE TABLE users ( id INT PRIMARY KEY AUTO_INCREMENT, email VARCHAR(255) NOT NULL UNIQUE, age INT CHECK (age >= 0), status VARCHAR(20) DEFAULT 'active', created TIMESTAMP DEFAULT CURRENT_TIMESTAMP ); Create only if the table is not there yet, and use a UUID default. CREATE TABLE IF NOT EXISTS sessions ( id UUID DEFAULT gen_random_uuid() PRIMARY KEY, token TEXT NOT NULL ); This example uses a primary key on two columns and a named foreign key with referential actions. CREATE TABLE order_items ( order_id INT, product_id INT, qty INT NOT NULL DEFAULT 1, PRIMARY KEY (order_id, product_id), CONSTRAINT fk_order FOREIGN KEY (order_id) REFERENCES orders (id) ON DELETE CASCADE ); This example adds a generated column and a vector column. CREATE TABLE docs ( id BIGINT IDENTITY, title VARCHAR(200), title_len INT GENERATED ALWAYS AS (length(title)) STORED, embedding VECTOR(768) ); Make a table from a query result, and make a system-versioned table. CREATE TABLE active_users AS SELECT id, email FROM users WHERE status = 'active'; CREATE TABLE accounts ( id INT PRIMARY KEY, balance DECIMAL(12,2) ) WITH SYSTEM VERSIONING;

sections.heading: Compatibility
sections.text: The CREATE TABLE command in EvolutionDB mixes ideas from the SQL standard, PostgreSQL, and MySQL. It does not match any one of them exactly. From the SQL standard and PostgreSQL. EvolutionDB supports column definitions, PRIMARY KEY, UNIQUE, CHECK, and FOREIGN KEY ... REFERENCES with ON DELETE/ON UPDATE actions and MATCH/DEFERRABLE clauses. It also supports GENERATED ALWAYS AS, INHERITS, IF NOT EXISTS, and CREATE TABLE AS. From MySQL. These parts are MySQL-style. They are AUTO_INCREMENT, the KEY/INDEX and FULLTEXT declarations inside the column list, the COMMENT column attribute, and the UNSIGNED/ZEROFILL integer modifiers. The MySQL type names TINYINT, MEDIUMTEXT, LONGBLOB, ENUM, SET, DATETIME, and YEAR are also MySQL-style. So is the AS ( expr ) STORED|VIRTUAL short form for generated columns, and the table options written at the end. EvolutionDB extras. These parts belong to EvolutionDB only. They are VECTOR(dim) columns, the gen_random_uuid_v7() and snowflake_id() default functions, WITH SYSTEM VERSIONING, and WITH (ttl_column = ...). The hash and range sharding with SHARD BY is also EvolutionDB only. So is the integer-range PARTITION OF ... FOR VALUES child form. Differences and clauses that are not supported. Unlike PostgreSQL, there is no UNLOGGED, no OF type, no EXCLUDE constraints, and no tablespace clause. There is also no per-column COLLATE with an identifier. You write a quoted string after COLLATE instead. The grammar accepts the NULL column attribute. It does nothing on its own and only states that the column allows NULL.

sections.heading: See Also
sections.text: ALTER TABLE DROP TABLE CREATE INDEX TRUNCATE TABLE SELECT Data Types SET SCHEMA

sections.heading: Introduction
sections.text: CREATE TRIGGER Create a row-level trigger. Its body runs on its own before or after an INSERT, UPDATE, or DELETE on a table or memory store.

sections.heading: Synopsis
sections.text: CREATE TRIGGER name { BEFORE | AFTER } { INSERT | UPDATE | DELETE | PUT } ON { table_name | MEMORY STORE store_name } FOR EACH ROW BEGIN trigger_body END

sections.heading: Description
sections.text: CREATE TRIGGER adds a new trigger to a table that already exists. It also works on the backing table of a memory store. After you create it, the trigger fires for every row that the matching event touches. The body runs on the same engine that runs stored procedures. So you can use IF, WHILE, SIGNAL, and other procedural statements inside the body. EvolutionDB triggers are always row-level. FOR EACH ROW is required. It is the only form the grammar accepts. There is no statement-level form. Each trigger fires on one event and one timing only. To react to several events, you must create one trigger for each event. Inside the body, the special names OLD.column and NEW.column hold the row values before and after the operation. The engine replaces these names with real values just before it runs the body. OLD is the existing row. It is available for UPDATE and DELETE. NEW is the incoming row. It is available for INSERT and UPDATE. If a value is not known, the engine replaces the reference with NULL. In a BEFORE trigger, the engine gives special meaning to a statement like SET NEW.column = expression;. It works out the expression and writes the result back into that column of the row. The row is the one being inserted or updated. This lets the trigger change the row before it is stored. The engine does this rewrite for BEFORE triggers only. A trigger body can run its own DML. That DML can fire other triggers. There is a fixed limit on how deep this nesting can go. Past that limit, the engine quietly stops firing more triggers. If the body raises an error, the operation that fired it fails. The error then passes back to the caller. It carries its SQLSTATE when one is set. It fails if you create a trigger with a name that already exists. The error is SQLSTATE 42P07. It fails if you name a target table that does not exist. The error is SQLSTATE 42P01. It fails if you name a memory store that does not exist. The error is SQLSTATE 42704.

sections.heading: Parameters
sections.text: name The name of the new trigger. The engine looks up trigger names across all tables in the current schema. So a name must be unique within the schema. BEFORE | AFTER When the trigger runs in relation to the operation. BEFORE runs the body before the row change is applied. It also turns on SET NEW.column rewrites. AFTER runs the body after the row change is applied. INSERT | UPDATE | DELETE | PUT The one event that fires the trigger. PUT is an alias. It works the same as INSERT. You can give only one event per trigger. There is no multi-event form like INSERT OR UPDATE. You cannot limit UPDATE to a list of columns. ON table_name The table that the trigger is attached to. The table must already exist in the current database and schema. ON MEMORY STORE store_name Attach the trigger to a memory store instead of a normal table. The engine looks up the store name in the memory-store catalog. It then maps the name to its backing table named mem_store_name. The trigger fires on that backing table just like a normal table trigger. FOR EACH ROW Required. It sets row-level firing. The body runs once for each row that is touched. This is the only form supported. trigger_body The procedural statements between BEGIN and END. They run for each matching row. The body uses the same stored-procedure engine. It can read OLD.column and NEW.column. In BEFORE triggers it can also assign to NEW.column with SET.

sections.heading: Examples
sections.text: Set a value in a new row before it is inserted. This is a BEFORE trigger with SET NEW. CREATE TRIGGER trg_audit_stamp BEFORE INSERT ON orders FOR EACH ROW BEGIN SET NEW.status = 'pending'; END; Copy deleted rows into an audit table. This is an AFTER trigger that uses OLD. CREATE TRIGGER trg_archive_deleted AFTER DELETE ON orders FOR EACH ROW BEGIN INSERT INTO orders_archive (id, status) VALUES (OLD.id, OLD.status); END; Block an update with SIGNAL when a check is true. This is a BEFORE trigger that uses both OLD and NEW. CREATE TRIGGER trg_no_downgrade BEFORE UPDATE ON orders FOR EACH ROW BEGIN IF NEW.total < OLD.total THEN SIGNAL SQLSTATE '45000'; END IF; END; Attach a trigger to a memory store. Here PUT works the same as INSERT. CREATE TRIGGER trg_cache_touch AFTER PUT ON MEMORY STORE sessions FOR EACH ROW BEGIN -- runs against the backing table mem_sessions INSERT INTO session_log (sid) VALUES (NEW.sid); END;

sections.heading: Compatibility
sections.text: EvolutionDB's CREATE TRIGGER is close to the SQL standard and PostgreSQL. It is narrower and feels more like MySQL. Several PostgreSQL clauses are not supported. Inline body, not a function. You write the action straight into a BEGIN … END block. There is no EXECUTE FUNCTION or EXECUTE PROCEDURE clause. There is no separate trigger function. The body runs on the procedure engine. Row-level only. FOR EACH ROW is required. The PostgreSQL FOR EACH STATEMENT form is not available. Single event only. You give one of INSERT, UPDATE, DELETE, or PUT per trigger. PostgreSQL's INSERT OR UPDATE OR DELETE combinations are not accepted. UPDATE OF column is not accepted. TRUNCATE triggers are not supported. No WHEN condition. There is no WHEN (condition) clause. You must write the gate logic inside the body. For example, use IF … SIGNAL. No REFERENCING or transition tables. Old and new values come only through the OLD.column and NEW.column substitution. There are no named transition tables. PUT alias and MEMORY STORE target are EvolutionDB extensions. PostgreSQL and the SQL standard have no match for them. Row change with SET NEW.column in BEFORE triggers follows the MySQL way. It does not use PostgreSQL's "assign to NEW and RETURN" model.

sections.heading: See Also
sections.text: INSERT UPDATE DELETE CREATE TABLE

sections.heading: Introduction
sections.text: CREATE USER Make a new database user that can log in with a password.

sections.heading: Synopsis
sections.text: CREATE USER name PASSWORD 'password'

sections.heading: Description
sections.text: CREATE USER adds a new login account to the EvolutionDB instance. The account is stored in the system catalog. It lives in the CAT_SYS_USERS B+ tree inside the unified storage file. The account is keyed by user name. The password is kept only as a PBKDF2-SHA256 hash. The plain text password is never written to disk. It is also wiped from memory in a safe way after hashing. In EvolutionDB the statement always has the same fixed form. You write the user name, then the keyword PASSWORD, then a password value. The name and the password are both required. An empty name is rejected. An empty password is rejected too. There are no role attribute options. For example, you cannot use SUPERUSER, LOGIN, CREATEDB, or VALID UNTIL. These clauses are not part of EvolutionDB's grammar. They are not accepted. The new account can connect right away. It can use either wire protocol. That means PostgreSQL on port 5433 or the native EVO protocol on port 9967. A new user has no privileges at first. It only gets the access you give it later. Use GRANT to assign access. User lookup at login time is not case sensitive. The authenticator first looks for the lower-case name. If that is not found, it then looks for the name exactly as stored. Note. Most EvolutionDB statements go through the Flex/Bison SQL parser. CREATE USER does not. The protocol adaptor's catalog layer catches it first. It reads the statement as a plain command string. This is why only the exact CREATE USER name PASSWORD 'password' shape is accepted.

sections.heading: Parameters
sections.text: name The name of the new user. It is the first word after CREATE USER. A space ends the word. The name is stored in the system catalog exactly as written. The longest allowed name is 255 characters. Login matches the name in lower case first. So for login, the name is not case sensitive. PASSWORD 'password' Required. The login password for the new user. You can write it inside single quotes, like 'secret'. You can also write it as a plain word. A quoted value keeps any spaces up to the closing quote. The value is hashed with PBKDF2-SHA256. Each user gets a random salt, and the hash uses 100 000 iterations. It is stored as pbkdf2:<iterations>:<salt>:<hash>. An empty password is rejected with an invalid-parameter error.

sections.heading: Examples
sections.text: This example creates a user with a password in single quotes. CREATE USER reporting PASSWORD 'r3port!ng_2026'; Create a user, then grant it access to a table. Access is a separate step. CREATE USER analyst PASSWORD 'an4lyst'; GRANT SELECT ON orders TO analyst; This example connects as the new user with the EVO CLI. ./cli/evosql-cli -U analyst -W # prompts for the password Trying to create a user that already exists fails with an error. CREATE USER analyst PASSWORD 'whatever'; -- ERROR: User 'analyst' already exists (SQLSTATE 42710) Omitting the PASSWORD clause is a syntax error. CREATE USER guest; -- ERROR: Syntax: CREATE USER <name> PASSWORD '<pass>' (SQLSTATE 42601)

sections.heading: Compatibility
sections.text: The SQL standard has no CREATE USER statement. The standard defines roles with CREATE ROLE instead. EvolutionDB's form is a small part of the PostgreSQL extension. This was done on purpose. Password is required. PostgreSQL allows CREATE USER with no password and many optional attributes. EvolutionDB needs exactly PASSWORD 'password'. It accepts nothing else. No role attributes. PostgreSQL has options such as SUPERUSER, CREATEDB, CREATEROLE, LOGIN/NOLOGIN, CONNECTION LIMIT, VALID UNTIL, and IN ROLE. These are not part of EvolutionDB's grammar. They will not parse. No password-encryption keywords. PostgreSQL accepts PASSWORD '...' with optional ENCRYPTED/UNENCRYPTED hints. EvolutionDB always hashes the value with PBKDF2-SHA256. It stores only the hash. Users and roles. EvolutionDB also has a separate CREATE ROLE form. Roles made that way are marked as non-login. They cannot log in. A user made with CREATE USER can log in.

sections.heading: See Also
sections.text: GRANT CREATE DATABASE USE

sections.heading: Introduction
sections.text: DELETE Remove rows from a table.

sections.heading: Synopsis
sections.text: DELETE [ LOW_PRIORITY ] [ QUICK ] [ IGNORE ] FROM table_name [ WHERE condition ] [ ORDER BY column [ ASC | DESC ] [, ...] ] [ LIMIT count [ OFFSET start | , count ] ] [ RETURNING * | output_column [, ...] ] -- Multi-table form 1 (delete from joined targets) DELETE [ LOW_PRIORITY ] [ QUICK ] [ IGNORE ] target_table [ .* ] [, ...] FROM table_references [ WHERE condition ] -- Multi-table form 2 (USING) DELETE [ LOW_PRIORITY ] [ QUICK ] [ IGNORE ] FROM target_table [ .* ] [, ...] USING table_references [ WHERE condition ]

sections.heading: Description
sections.text: DELETE removes rows from table_name. It removes the rows that match the WHERE condition. If you leave out the WHERE clause, the statement deletes all visible rows in the table. The table stays valid but becomes empty. EvolutionDB accepts three forms of DELETE. A single-table delete. This is the form DELETE FROM table_name .... A multi-table delete. You can name the target tables before FROM with DELETE t1, t2 FROM .... You can also name them after FROM and give the source tables in a separate USING list. Both multi-table forms run a join over table_references. Then they delete the matched rows from each named target. The single-table form deletes in two steps. First it collects the rows that match the WHERE expression. Then it removes them. The removal is an MVCC soft delete. The row's xmax is set to the current transaction id. The primary-key index entry stays in place. This lets other readers still find the tuple and check whether it is visible to them. Space is freed later by the background reclaim process. This happens once xmax is committed and no snapshot can still see the row. Some tuples were written before MVCC existed and have no MVCC header. For those, the heap slot and index entry are removed right away. Each delete is transactional. The earlier version of every removed row is written to the undo log. So a ROLLBACK restores the rows. A disconnect also rolls back and restores them. An exclusive row lock is taken for each row. If a lock cannot be acquired, the statement fails with a lock or deadlock error. The whole statement is then rolled back. Referential integrity. EvolutionDB checks every foreign-key constraint that points to the row's table before it removes the row. The result depends on the constraint's ON DELETE action. The engine may reject the delete for RESTRICT or NO ACTION. It may delete the child rows that reference the row for CASCADE. It may set the child foreign-key columns to NULL for SET NULL. It may set them to their column default for SET DEFAULT. Cascades go down at most 32 levels. A deeper chain raises a foreign-key violation. A circular reference is one example. Triggers, indexes, and policies. BEFORE DELETE and AFTER DELETE triggers run once for each row when the table defines them. The secondary index entries for the row are removed. Row-level security may be enabled on the table. In that case the session user's DELETE USING policy is checked for each row. Rows that the policy does not expose are skipped without any error. So the deleted count counts only the rows the user is allowed to delete. ORDER BY is parsed but not applied. The single-table grammar accepts an ORDER BY clause for MySQL syntax compatibility. But EvolutionDB's execution engine does not order the rows it deletes. So ORDER BY has no effect on which rows are removed. Use WHERE to control the row set. Add LIMIT if you need to cap the count.

sections.heading: Parameters
sections.text: LOW_PRIORITY, QUICK, IGNORE MySQL-style delete options. The grammar accepts them for compatibility. They are parsed into an option bitmask. They do not change how EvolutionDB deletes rows. table_name The name of the table you delete rows from. This is used in the single-table form. The name is looked up in the system catalog in the current database and schema. If the table does not exist, the statement reports an error and removes no rows. target_table [ .* ] In the multi-table forms, a table whose matched rows are deleted. You can list one or more targets. Separate them with commas. The .* suffix after a target name is optional and is accepted. In the first form the targets come before FROM. In the second form they come after FROM and the join source comes after USING. table_references The join expression that supplies the rows to test in the multi-table forms. It uses the same table-reference syntax as SELECT, including joins. The rows that this join produces decide which rows are removed from each target_table. WHERE condition An expression that returns a boolean. Only rows where it is true are deleted. A result of true, t, or 1 counts as a match. Any non-zero numeric value counts as a match too. If you leave it out, every visible row qualifies. ORDER BY column [ ASC | DESC ] The single-table grammar accepts it. The executor does not honor it. See the callout above. LIMIT count [ OFFSET start ] Caps how many of the matched rows are deleted. This is used in the single-table form. Both the LIMIT count OFFSET start spelling and the MySQL LIMIT start, count spelling are accepted. The limit and offset window is applied to the collected set of matching rows before they are removed. RETURNING * | output_column Makes DELETE return values from the rows it removed. This is used in the single-table form. RETURNING * returns all columns of each deleted row. Otherwise it returns the columns you list. The values show the row as it was right before deletion.

sections.heading: Examples
sections.text: Delete one row by its primary key. DELETE FROM employees WHERE id = 42; Delete all rows that match a condition. DELETE FROM orders WHERE status = 'cancelled'; Empty a table with no WHERE clause. DELETE FROM session_log; Delete at most 100 matching rows. DELETE FROM events WHERE level = 'debug' LIMIT 100; Delete rows and return the rows you removed. DELETE FROM products WHERE discontinued = true RETURNING id, name; Multi-table delete with a join. This is the first form. It removes order lines whose parent order no longer exists. DELETE order_lines FROM order_lines LEFT JOIN orders ON order_lines.order_id = orders.id WHERE orders.id IS NULL; Multi-table delete with USING. This is the second form. DELETE FROM order_lines USING orders WHERE order_lines.order_id = orders.id AND orders.status = 'voided';

sections.heading: Compatibility
sections.text: The single-table DELETE FROM table WHERE ... form follows the SQL standard and PostgreSQL closely. The RETURNING clause is included. EvolutionDB's RETURNING works like PostgreSQL's. You use * or a list of column names. It gives back the row values from before the delete. The LOW_PRIORITY, QUICK, and IGNORE options are MySQL extensions. The multi-table DELETE t1, t2 FROM ... and DELETE FROM ... USING ... forms are MySQL extensions too. They are not part of the SQL standard. The option keywords are accepted for compatibility. They have no effect on execution. The LIMIT clause on DELETE is a non-standard extension. This includes the MySQL LIMIT start, count spelling. PostgreSQL does not allow LIMIT directly on DELETE. ORDER BY on DELETE is accepted for MySQL syntax compatibility. Unlike MySQL, it has no effect in EvolutionDB. EvolutionDB does not support the PostgreSQL WHERE CURRENT OF cursor form. It does not support the ONLY and inheritance qualifiers. It does not support a table alias on the delete target. The PostgreSQL join source keyword is USING. EvolutionDB's second multi-table form uses it too. ON DELETE referential actions are enforced. These are RESTRICT, NO ACTION, CASCADE, SET NULL, and SET DEFAULT. This matches the SQL standard.

sections.heading: See Also
sections.text: SELECT INSERT UPDATE TRUNCATE CREATE TABLE

sections.heading: Introduction
sections.text: DROP INDEX Remove a secondary index from the database. The storage it used is freed.

sections.heading: Synopsis
sections.text: DROP INDEX index_name

sections.heading: Description
sections.text: DROP INDEX removes an existing secondary index. The index is found by its name only. EvolutionDB searches every table for an index with that name. So you do not give the table that owns the index. You also cannot give it. When the index is found, its backing structure is destroyed. The way it is destroyed depends on the index type. A B+ tree index has its tree pages freed. This is the default for CREATE INDEX and CREATE UNIQUE INDEX. A hash index has its hash directory and bucket pages freed. You create this type with USING HASH. An HNSW approximate-nearest-neighbor index has its in-memory graph released. After the backing structure is destroyed, the matching entry is removed from the system catalog. On success the server reports Index 'index_name' dropped. You cannot drop a primary-key index with this command. If you try, you get an error. Drop the table or alter it to remove the primary key constraint instead. Note. The index name must match an index that really exists. If no index has that name, the command fails with index 'index_name' does not exist. EvolutionDB's grammar does not accept an IF EXISTS clause. So there is no built-in way to hide this error.

sections.heading: Parameters
sections.text: index_name The name of the index to remove. The lookup is by name across all tables. So the name finds the index on its own. You do not use a schema qualifier or the owning table. The name must point to an existing secondary index. It must not be a primary-key index.

sections.heading: Examples
sections.text: This example creates an index and then drops it by name. CREATE INDEX idx_users_email ON users (email); DROP INDEX idx_users_email; This example drops a hash index made with USING HASH. The drop command is the same for every index type. CREATE INDEX idx_orders_status ON orders (status) USING HASH; DROP INDEX idx_orders_status; Trying to drop a primary-key index fails with an error. -- raises: cannot drop primary key index '...' DROP INDEX users_pkey; Dropping an index that does not exist fails with an error. -- raises: index 'no_such_index' does not exist DROP INDEX no_such_index;

sections.heading: Compatibility
sections.text: DROP INDEX is not part of the core SQL standard. The standard leaves index management to each database. EvolutionDB's form is kept very small on purpose. Next to PostgreSQL, EvolutionDB accepts only DROP INDEX index_name. EvolutionDB's grammar does not support these PostgreSQL clauses. You must leave them out. IF EXISTS is not accepted. A drop of a missing index always raises an error. CONCURRENTLY is not accepted. The drop is not online or concurrent. CASCADE and RESTRICT are not accepted. You cannot drop many indexes in one statement. DROP INDEX a, b is not accepted. Use one index name per statement. Schema-qualified names are not used. schema.index does not apply. The index is found by its bare name across all tables. Unlike PostgreSQL, EvolutionDB will not drop a primary-key index with this command.

sections.heading: See Also
sections.text: CREATE INDEX CREATE TABLE DROP TABLE

sections.heading: Introduction
sections.text: DROP TABLE Remove a table from the current database. This also removes its rows, indexes, constraints, and access policies.

sections.heading: Synopsis
sections.text: DROP TABLE [ IF EXISTS ] table_name [ CASCADE | RESTRICT ]

sections.heading: Description
sections.text: DROP TABLE removes one table and everything attached to it. EvolutionDB finds table_name in the system catalog. Then it removes the storage and metadata in one operation. The steps are below. The primary-key B+ tree is destroyed. All heap pages that hold the table's rows are freed back to the page manager. Every secondary index is destroyed. This covers B+ tree indexes and hash and unique-hash indexes. The catalog entry is dropped. This also removes the table's columns, indexes, and constraints. The table's inheritance link is removed if the table was a child of another table. Any row-level security policies on the table are removed. You can name only one table per statement. EvolutionDB's grammar accepts a single table_name. It does not accept a comma-separated list. That form is only for TRUNCATE. Two dependency checks run before any storage is touched. Inheritance children. The drop is refused if the table has child tables that inherit from it. You must give CASCADE to allow it. With CASCADE, each child is dropped first, and so is each grandchild. The parent is dropped last. Views. The drop is refused if any view in the current database uses the table by name. CASCADE does not override this check. You must drop the view that depends on the table first. A successful DROP TABLE reports command(s) completed successfully!... A refused drop raises SQLSTATE 2BP01. This means that dependent objects still exist.

sections.heading: Parameters
sections.text: IF EXISTS Do not raise an error if the table does not exist. EvolutionDB reports success instead. Without this clause, dropping a missing table is an error. The message is table 'name' not found. table_name The name of the table to drop. The catalog finds the name in the current database and schema. Only one name is accepted. CASCADE Drop tables that inherit from this table first. This goes through the whole inheritance subtree. The named table is dropped after that. Note that CASCADE covers inheritance children only. It does not force the removal of dependent views. A view that uses the table still blocks the drop. RESTRICT Refuse to drop the table if any other object depends on it. This means an inheritance child. This is the default behavior when neither keyword is given.

sections.heading: Examples
sections.text: This example drops a table. DROP TABLE employees; Drop a table only if it exists. This hides the error when the table is missing. DROP TABLE IF EXISTS staging_import; This example drops a parent table along with every table that inherits from it. -- 'people' has child tables 'employees' and 'customers' DROP TABLE people CASCADE; Refuse to drop a table that still has children. This is the default behavior. DROP TABLE people RESTRICT; -- ERROR: cannot drop table "people" because other objects depend on it

sections.heading: Compatibility
sections.text: DROP TABLE is defined by the SQL standard. EvolutionDB supports part of it. Its behavior is closest to PostgreSQL. The real differences are below. Single table only. The SQL standard and PostgreSQL allow a comma-separated list of tables, such as DROP TABLE a, b, c. EvolutionDB's grammar accepts only one table_name per statement. CASCADE scope. In PostgreSQL, CASCADE also drops dependent objects such as views and foreign-key constraints in other tables. In EvolutionDB, CASCADE follows the inheritance tree only. This means child tables. A view that uses the table still blocks the drop. You must remove it first. RESTRICT is the default. This matches the SQL standard and PostgreSQL. IF EXISTS matches PostgreSQL. A missing table is reported as success and not as an error. EvolutionDB does not support the PostgreSQL-only DROP TABLE ... CASCADE CONSTRAINTS. It does not support schema-qualified multi-object drops. It also does not support the TEMPORARY keyword in this command.

sections.heading: See Also
sections.text: CREATE TABLE TRUNCATE DROP INDEX ALTER TABLE DELETE

sections.heading: Introduction
sections.text: GRANT Give a user access rights on a database, schema, or table. You can also make a user a member of a role.

sections.heading: Synopsis
sections.text: -- Grant privileges on a database object: GRANT privilege [ , ... ] ON { DATABASE | SCHEMA | TABLE } object_name TO username [ WITH GRANT OPTION ] -- Grant membership in a role (no ON clause): GRANT role_name TO username where privilege is one of: SELECT | INSERT | UPDATE | DELETE | CREATE | DROP ALL [ PRIVILEGES ]

sections.heading: Description
sections.text: GRANT gives privileges to a user. EvolutionDB has two forms of this command. The forms differ by whether an ON clause is present. Object privileges. The first form uses ON. It grants one or more privileges on a single database object to a named user. The object can be a DATABASE, a SCHEMA, or a TABLE. Each privilege maps to a SQL operation. These are SELECT, INSERT, UPDATE, DELETE, CREATE, and DROP. ALL is short for all six. You can also write it as ALL PRIVILEGES. Role membership. The second form has no ON. It makes username a member of role_name. When EvolutionDB checks privileges, a user gets every privilege held by its roles. The user also keeps its own privileges. Additive, merging semantics. You can grant privileges that overlap a grant that already exists for the same user and object. This does not replace the old grant. It merges with it. The new privilege bits are OR-ed into the bits that are already there. WITH GRANT OPTION is turned on if you ask for it. A re-grant never clears an existing grant option. A user's own grants and grants made to the special grantee PUBLIC are both additive. A user with no privileges still gains from any matching PUBLIC grant. How privileges are resolved. EvolutionDB checks access with a waterfall. It starts at the most specific scope. First it looks for a matching grant at TABLE scope. Then it checks SCHEMA scope. Then it checks DATABASE scope. A DATABASE grant on the wildcard name * applies to every database. EvolutionDB then runs the same waterfall for PUBLIC. Last, it runs it for each role the user belongs to. If any level gives the requested privilege, access is allowed. Who may run GRANT. The built-in admin user is a superuser. It may grant anything. It always holds WITH GRANT OPTION. A non-admin user may only pass on privileges it holds with the grant option on the same object. If not, the statement fails with "Permission denied". Only admin may grant role membership. EvolutionDB comes with one grant already set up. The admin user holds ALL on DATABASE *. New databases have no default PUBLIC grants. So non-admin users start with no access. They get access only after something is granted to them. The grant can be direct, through PUBLIC, or through a role.

sections.heading: Parameters
sections.text: privilege The privilege to grant. It is one of SELECT, INSERT, UPDATE, DELETE, CREATE, or DROP. Privilege names are not case-sensitive. You can list several privileges. Separate them with commas. EvolutionDB quietly ignores any word it does not know. If the list ends up empty, the statement fails with "Invalid privilege specification". ALL [ PRIVILEGES ] Short for all six privileges. These are SELECT, INSERT, UPDATE, DELETE, CREATE, and DROP. The PRIVILEGES keyword is optional. EvolutionDB accepts it for SQL-standard compatibility. It has no extra effect. ON DATABASE | SCHEMA | TABLE Picks the scope the grant applies to. The scope is the kind of object. Exactly one of these keywords must follow ON. In the waterfall, TABLE is the most specific scope. DATABASE is the broadest. object_name The name of the object you grant on. You may write it bare, single-quoted, or double-quoted. For a DATABASE grant the name is a database name. You can also use the wildcard *, which means all databases. For finer scopes the name is stored as given. Checks build the lookup key from the session's database, schema, and table. At table scope this key is db.schema.table. So write the name to match the level you want. TO username The user that gets the privileges or role membership. You may write it bare or single-quoted. Use the special name PUBLIC to grant to every user. This grant is additive. For an object grant the target user does not need to exist. For a role grant the target must be a user that exists. It cannot be a role. WITH GRANT OPTION Lets the recipient grant the same privileges on the same object to other users. It is valid only in the object-privilege form. Without it, the recipient may use the privileges but not pass them on. A non-admin grantor must hold the grant option to give it. role_name (role-membership form) In the form with no ON, this is the name of an existing role. Its membership is given to username. The role must exist. The target must be a user. The user must not already be a member. If any of these is not true, the statement fails. Only admin may run this form.

sections.heading: Examples
sections.text: This example gives a user read access on a table. GRANT SELECT ON TABLE orders TO analyst; This example gives several privileges at once and lets the recipient pass them on. GRANT SELECT, INSERT, UPDATE ON TABLE orders TO app_user WITH GRANT OPTION; This example gives every privilege on a schema to everyone. GRANT ALL PRIVILEGES ON SCHEMA reporting TO PUBLIC; Give create and drop rights across all databases. This uses the wildcard scope. GRANT CREATE, DROP ON DATABASE '*' TO dba; This example gives database-level read access to a single database. GRANT SELECT ON DATABASE testdb TO readonly; Grant role membership. There is no ON clause. Run this as admin. GRANT reporting_role TO analyst; This example shows how to look at the grants you made. SHOW GRANTS FOR analyst;

sections.heading: Compatibility
sections.text: EvolutionDB's GRANT follows the idea of the SQL standard and PostgreSQL. It is made simpler on purpose. A dedicated command interceptor handles the statement. The main SQL grammar does not. Same as the standard and PostgreSQL. The privilege set SELECT, INSERT, UPDATE, DELETE matches PostgreSQL. So does the ALL [PRIVILEGES] short form. So does the TO target with the special grantee PUBLIC. So does WITH GRANT OPTION. So do the additive PUBLIC rules. EvolutionDB specifics. CREATE and DROP are object privileges. They go in the same grant list as DML privileges. The object scope is one of exactly three keywords. These are DATABASE, SCHEMA, and TABLE. A DATABASE grant may use the wildcard name * to cover all databases. Privilege resolution uses a waterfall from most specific to least specific. The order is TABLE → SCHEMA → DATABASE. It also uses role inheritance. The admin user is an implicit superuser. Differences and clauses that are not supported. There is no ON ALL TABLES IN SCHEMA. There are no column-level privileges. There are no privileges on sequences, functions, types, or foreign objects. There is no GRANTED BY clause. There is no WITH ADMIN OPTION for role grants. The grant object must be a single named object. Lists of objects are not accepted. Privilege checks use the username and roles. For object grants the target user does not need to exist first.

sections.heading: See Also
sections.text: REVOKE SHOW GRANTS CREATE USER CREATE ROLE CREATE TABLE CREATE SCHEMA

sections.heading: Introduction
sections.text: INSERT Add one or more new rows to a table.

sections.heading: Synopsis
sections.text: INSERT [ LOW_PRIORITY | DELAYED | HIGH_PRIORITY | IGNORE ] [ INTO ] table_name [ ( column [, ...] ) ] VALUES ( { expression | DEFAULT } [, ...] ) [, ...] [ ON DUPLICATE KEY UPDATE column = expression [, ...] ] [ ON CONFLICT [ ( column ) ] { DO NOTHING | DO UPDATE SET column = expression [, ...] } ] [ RETURNING { * | column [, ...] } ] --- INSERT ... SET form --- INSERT [ LOW_PRIORITY | DELAYED | HIGH_PRIORITY | IGNORE ] [ INTO ] table_name SET column = { expression | DEFAULT } [, ...] [ ON DUPLICATE KEY UPDATE column = expression [, ...] ] --- INSERT ... SELECT form --- INSERT [ LOW_PRIORITY | DELAYED | HIGH_PRIORITY | IGNORE ] [ INTO ] table_name [ ( column [, ...] ) ] select_statement

sections.heading: Description
sections.text: INSERT adds new rows to table_name. There are three forms you can use. The VALUES form takes one or more value lists in parentheses. The SET form uses MySQL-style column = value assignments. The INSERT … SELECT form inserts the result of a subquery. You can give a list of column names in parentheses. Then each value goes into the named column. Columns you leave out get their declared DEFAULT. If there is no default, they get NULL. If you do not give a column list, the values go into the table's columns in their declaration order. The engine checks the row before it writes it. First it checks the types. Then it checks the NOT NULL, UNIQUE, and CHECK constraints. It only checks a constraint if the table actually declares it. A primary-key clash raises a duplicate-key error. This does not happen if you use an ON DUPLICATE KEY UPDATE or ON CONFLICT clause. Columns that have a DEFAULT get their value at insert time. Some defaults are function calls. Examples are gen_random_uuid(), gen_random_uuid_v7(), snowflake_id(), and CURRENT_TIMESTAMP. The engine runs these once per row. Expression defaults can use the rest of the row as context. A column with AUTO_INCREMENT gets its value from the table's running counter. The counter moves forward by its step when you do not give a value. Stored generated columns are computed from the inserted row. You cannot write an explicit value into a generated column. Only DEFAULT is allowed there. A statement may make a value for an AUTO_INCREMENT column. It may also make one with gen_random_uuid*() or snowflake_id(). The engine saves the first such value. You can then read it with LAST_INSERT_ID().

sections.heading: Parameters
sections.text: LOW_PRIORITY | DELAYED | HIGH_PRIORITY | IGNORE These keywords come from MySQL. The grammar accepts them. They have no effect when EvolutionDB runs the statement. They are here so that statements copied from MySQL can parse without an error. INTO An optional word before the table name. It is accepted for SQL and PostgreSQL compatibility. INSERT t … and INSERT INTO t … mean the same thing. table_name The name of an existing table to insert into. The engine removes a .dat suffix if the name has one. This gives the base table name. The table may be a RANGE-partitioned parent. In that case the row goes to the child partition whose bounds hold the partition-key value. In this release every row of a multi-row insert must go to the same partition. ( column [..] ) An optional list of target columns. When you give it, the engine matches the values to these columns by position and puts them in table order. Columns you do not list take their default value. If there is no default, they take NULL. Each VALUES row must have the same number of values as the number of named columns. If it does not, the engine rejects the statement. VALUES ( … ) [..] One or more rows of values. Each row is a comma-separated list in parentheses. When you give more than one group, the engine inserts many rows in one statement. expression A value expression that makes the column value. It can be a literal, a function call, arithmetic, and so on. DEFAULT In a value position, this asks for the column's declared default value. If the column has no default, the value is NULL. For a generated column, DEFAULT is the only value form you can use. SET column = { expression | DEFAULT } [..] The MySQL-style assignment form. Each named column is set to the given value or its default. You can only use the = operator. Any other comparison operator is a syntax error. select_statement A SELECT whose result rows are inserted. The engine matches its columns to the optional target column list. If there is no list, it matches them to the table's columns in order. ON DUPLICATE KEY UPDATE column = expression [..] The MySQL-style upsert. An inserted row may break the primary key or a UNIQUE constraint. When this happens, the engine updates the existing row with the listed assignments. It does not raise a duplicate-key error. ON CONFLICT [ ( column ) ] DO NOTHING | DO UPDATE SET … The PostgreSQL-style way to handle conflicts. With DO NOTHING, the engine skips a conflicting row in silence. With DO UPDATE SET, it updates the existing row with the given assignments. You can name an optional conflict target with one column. A target with more than one column such as (a, b) is not supported yet. It raises an error. RETURNING * | column [..] Returns data from the rows that were really inserted. RETURNING * returns every column. If you name columns, it returns only those columns. This works on the VALUES form.

sections.heading: Examples
sections.text: This example inserts one row with every column set. INSERT INTO users (id, name, email) VALUES (1, 'Ada', 'ada@example.com'); This example inserts several rows in one statement. One column falls back to its default. INSERT INTO users (id, name, role) VALUES (2, 'Lin', DEFAULT), (3, 'Bo', 'editor'); This example leaves out the column list. The values map to the columns in declaration order. INSERT INTO users VALUES (4, 'Mei', 'mei@example.com', DEFAULT); This example uses the MySQL-style SET form. INSERT INTO users SET id = 5, name = 'Sam', role = DEFAULT; This example inserts rows from a query. INSERT INTO archived_users (id, name) SELECT id, name FROM users WHERE active = 0; This example uses the MySQL ON DUPLICATE KEY UPDATE upsert. INSERT INTO counters (key, hits) VALUES ('home', 1) ON DUPLICATE KEY UPDATE hits = hits + 1; This example uses the PostgreSQL ON CONFLICT upsert and returns the result. INSERT INTO counters (key, hits) VALUES ('home', 1) ON CONFLICT (key) DO UPDATE SET hits = hits + 1 RETURNING *; This example skips a conflicting row in silence. INSERT INTO counters (key, hits) VALUES ('home', 1) ON CONFLICT DO NOTHING;

sections.heading: Compatibility
sections.text: The INSERT in EvolutionDB mixes the SQL standard, PostgreSQL, and MySQL. SQL standard and PostgreSQL. These parts follow PostgreSQL rules. They are the INSERT INTO … VALUES … and INSERT INTO … SELECT … forms, multi-row VALUES, the DEFAULT keyword in value positions, the ON CONFLICT … DO NOTHING / DO UPDATE SET clause, and RETURNING. MySQL extensions. These parts come from MySQL. They are the optional priority and IGNORE modifiers, the INTO keyword that you can leave out, the SET column = value assignment form, and ON DUPLICATE KEY UPDATE. The engine parses the priority and IGNORE modifiers but they have no effect when it runs. Differences from PostgreSQL. Some features are not supported. These are the standard OVERRIDING { SYSTEM | USER } VALUE clause, a DEFAULT VALUES clause, and naming a constraint as the conflict target with ON CONFLICT ON CONSTRAINT …. The WHERE predicate on ON CONFLICT … DO UPDATE is also not supported. Common table expressions with WITH … INSERT are not supported either. An ON CONFLICT target with more than one column is rejected. RETURNING works on the VALUES form.

sections.heading: See Also
sections.text: SELECT UPDATE DELETE REPLACE COPY CREATE TABLE

sections.heading: Introduction
sections.text: LISTEN / UNLISTEN / NOTIFY Subscribe to a named channel, stop subscribing, and send messages on it.

sections.heading: Synopsis
sections.text: LISTEN channel [ SELF ] UNLISTEN { channel | * } NOTIFY channel [ , 'payload' ]

sections.heading: Description
sections.text: LISTEN signs up the current session to listen on the notification channel named channel. NOTIFY sends a notification to every session that is listening on the same channel. You can also send an optional payload string with it. UNLISTEN removes the current session from one channel. Use * to remove it from all channels. The notification registry is kept only in server memory. It is shared between both front-end protocols. Connections that speak the PostgreSQL wire protocol use port 5433. Connections that speak the native EVO text protocol use port 9967. Both kinds of connection can listen and notify each other through the same channel names. When a connection closes, its listens are dropped automatically. Channel names are matched without regard to case. They are folded to lower case for lookup. A channel name may be at most 63 bytes long. Longer names are rejected. Transactional delivery. NOTIFY is commit-gated. A NOTIFY that runs inside an explicit transaction block is not delivered right away. Its channel and payload are queued on the session. The messages are sent only when the transaction commits. If the transaction rolls back, the queued notifications are dropped and nothing is sent. In auto-commit mode there is no open BEGIN. The statement commits on its own. So the notification is sent as soon as the NOTIFY statement finishes. LISTEN and UNLISTEN work differently. They take effect right away when the statement runs. They are not held back until commit. Self-notification. By default a session does not receive the notifications it sends on a channel it listens to itself. You can opt in to same-session delivery. To do this, add the SELF keyword when you listen. Write it as LISTEN channel SELF. You can run LISTEN again for a channel the session already listens on. This just updates the self-delivery flag in place. It does not create a second registration. Delivery format. On the PostgreSQL wire protocol a notification arrives as a NotificationResponse ('A') message. It carries the sending session id, the channel name, and the payload. On the EVO text protocol it arrives as an asynchronous NOTIFY <sender-session> <channel> <payload-length> line. The payload bytes follow that line.

sections.heading: Parameters
sections.text: channel The name of the notification channel. You give it as an identifier. It can be at most 63 bytes long. Matching is not case-sensitive. A name longer than the limit raises SQLSTATE 42622 (name too long). An otherwise invalid name raises the same error. SELF Valid only with LISTEN. It opts the current session in to receive its own NOTIFY messages on this channel. Without SELF, a session does not get its own notifications on a channel it listens to. This is the PostgreSQL default behavior. SELF is EvolutionDB's single-keyword way to say "deliver to self". There is no WITH (self = …) clause. * Valid only with UNLISTEN. It cancels every channel registration owned by the current session in one statement. payload An optional string literal sent with the notification. A comma separates it from the channel. It is delivered to every listener exactly as written. The payload can be at most 8000 bytes long. A longer payload raises SQLSTATE 22023 (payload string too long). When you do not give a payload, an empty payload is delivered. Note. EvolutionDB accepts a quoted string literal as the NOTIFY payload. The NOTIFY statement does not let you pass the payload as any other expression. Sometimes you want to build a payload from query values. For that, use the built-in function evo_notify(channel, payload). It queues a notification through the same commit-gated path. The companion function pg_listening_channels() returns the channels the current session is listening on.

sections.heading: Examples
sections.text: Set up a session to receive event notifications, then send to it. LISTEN orders_changed; NOTIFY orders_changed; NOTIFY orders_changed, 'order 4711 shipped'; Receive your own notifications on a channel. You opt in with SELF. LISTEN heartbeat SELF; NOTIFY heartbeat, 'ping'; -- this session also receives 'ping' Hold delivery until commit. The listener sees the notification only after COMMIT. It never sees it if the transaction is rolled back. BEGIN; NOTIFY orders_changed, 'pending'; COMMIT; -- 'pending' is delivered here Stop listening on one channel, then on everything. UNLISTEN orders_changed; UNLISTEN *; Build a payload from query values with the helper function. Then check the current subscriptions. SELECT evo_notify('orders_changed', 'order ' || id || ' shipped') FROM orders WHERE id = 4711; SELECT pg_listening_channels();

sections.heading: Compatibility
sections.text: The SQL standard has no LISTEN, UNLISTEN, or NOTIFY command. These commands follow PostgreSQL. These are the differences from PostgreSQL worth noting. EvolutionDB delivers same-session notifications only when the listen used the LISTEN channel SELF form. PostgreSQL handles this at NOTIFY time and has no SELF keyword. SELF is an EvolutionDB extension. The default with no SELF matches PostgreSQL. By default neither one echoes a session's own notifications. The NOTIFY payload must be a string literal. PostgreSQL also requires a string literal here. EvolutionDB adds the evo_notify() function for computed payloads. EvolutionDB's payload limit is 8000 bytes. Channel names are limited to 63 bytes. These match PostgreSQL's limits. Like PostgreSQL, notifications are commit-gated. Messages sent inside a transaction are delivered only on COMMIT. They are dropped on rollback. The notification channel registry is in memory only. It is shared across both the PostgreSQL and EVO protocols. It does not survive a server restart. For subscriptions that persist and can be replayed, see CREATE SUBSCRIPTION.

sections.heading: See Also
sections.text: BEGIN / COMMIT / ROLLBACK SAVEPOINT CALL SELECT SHOW

sections.heading: Introduction
sections.text: MEMORY DELETE Remove one key/value entry from a memory store. The entry is found by its namespace and key.

sections.heading: Synopsis
sections.text: MEMORY DELETE FROM store_name WHERE NS = 'namespace' AND KEY = 'key'

sections.heading: Description
sections.text: MEMORY DELETE removes exactly one entry from the memory store named store_name. Each entry has a unique identity. That identity is the pair of namespace and key. MEMORY DELETE targets the one row whose composite primary key matches the namespace and key you give. Behind the scenes, a memory store is held in an ordinary table named mem_<store_name>. The two parts of the entry identity are joined into one composite primary-key value. The namespace and key are joined by a record-separator byte. MEMORY DELETE rewrites the request as DELETE FROM mem_<store_name> WHERE pk = '...'. It then sends it through the engine's standard delete path. So the operation works just like a hand-written DELETE. Row-level security policies on the backing table still apply. These are also called RLS policies. Foreign-key cascade actions still apply. MVCC visibility and triggers still apply too. This RLS pass-through makes MEMORY DELETE safe in multi-tenant deployments. A caller can only delete rows that its policy allows. If the named store does not exist, the command fails with SQLSTATE 42704. This means the object is undefined. No data is changed. If the store exists but no entry matches the given namespace and key, the command still succeeds. It removes zero rows. Both predicates are required. The grammar accepts only the fixed form WHERE NS = '...' AND KEY = '...'. The comparison operator must be =. The two predicates must be joined by AND. Both the namespace and key values are string literals. You cannot delete a whole namespace with MEMORY DELETE. You cannot match a key prefix. You cannot leave out either predicate.

sections.heading: Parameters
sections.text: store_name The name of the target memory store. You write it after FROM. The store must already exist. See CREATE MEMORY STORE. If it does not exist, the command raises SQLSTATE 42704. NS = 'namespace' A string literal. It gives the namespace part of the entry to delete. Together with the key, it forms the unique identity of the entry. An empty string '' is a valid namespace value. KEY = 'key' A string literal. It gives the key part of the entry to delete, inside the given namespace.

sections.heading: Examples
sections.text: Delete one entry by namespace and key. MEMORY DELETE FROM chat_memory WHERE NS = 'user:42' AND KEY = 'last_topic'; Delete an entry stored under the empty namespace. MEMORY DELETE FROM kv_store WHERE NS = '' AND KEY = 'session_token'; Here is a common write-then-remove flow for one entry. -- store an entry MEMORY PUT INTO chat_memory VALUES ('user:42', 'last_topic', '{"text":"billing"}'); -- read it back MEMORY GET FROM chat_memory WHERE NS = 'user:42' AND KEY = 'last_topic'; -- remove it MEMORY DELETE FROM chat_memory WHERE NS = 'user:42' AND KEY = 'last_topic';

sections.heading: Compatibility
sections.text: MEMORY DELETE is an EvolutionDB extension. It is not part of the SQL standard. There is no matching statement in PostgreSQL. It is a simpler way to write the standard DELETE statement. The request is turned into DELETE FROM mem_<store_name> WHERE pk = '...'. It runs through the ordinary delete engine. So it keeps the standard delete behavior. This includes RLS, foreign-key actions, MVCC, and triggers. The MEMORY DELETE grammar is limited on purpose. A general-purpose DELETE is more flexible. MEMORY DELETE accepts only equality on both NS and KEY joined by AND. It supports no other WHERE shape. It supports no ORDER BY, LIMIT, or RETURNING clause. To delete by any other rule, work on the backing table with a standard DELETE statement.

sections.heading: See Also
sections.text: CREATE MEMORY STORE MEMORY PUT MEMORY GET DELETE CREATE POLICY

sections.heading: Introduction
sections.text: MEMORY GET Get one record from a memory store by its namespace and key.

sections.heading: Synopsis
sections.text: MEMORY GET FROM store_name WHERE NS = 'namespace' AND KEY = 'key'

sections.heading: Description
sections.text: MEMORY GET gets exactly one stored entry from a memory store. You make the store with CREATE MEMORY STORE. A memory store is held in a normal table named mem_<store_name>. Each entry has a composite primary key. That key is made from its namespace and its key. MEMORY GET builds that composite key and looks it up directly. It returns at most one row. The result set is a projection of the backing table. These columns always come back, in this order. They are mem_namespace, mem_key, mem_value, and created_at. The store can be made with a non-zero embedding dimension. In that case the embedding column also comes back. It sits between mem_value and created_at. The query is capped at one row inside the engine. Inside the engine the command turns into a SELECT against the backing table. The predicate has the form pk = 'namespace' || 0x1E || 'key'. The namespace and key are joined by an ASCII Record Separator. That byte is 0x1E. The command runs through the normal SELECT path. So row-level security policies, MVCC visibility, and triggers on the store work the same way as in a query you write by hand against mem_<store_name>. The command may find no entry for the given namespace and key. Then it still succeeds and returns an empty result set. If the named store does not exist, the command raises an error. The SQLSTATE is 42704. That code means undefined_object. The WHERE clause of MEMORY GET is fixed. It takes exactly two conditions. They are NS and KEY, joined by AND. The grammar accepts any comparison operator token in each spot. But the lookup is always an exact equality match on the composite key. A non-equality operator does not create a range scan. Always write = to keep things clear.

sections.heading: Parameters
sections.text: store_name The name of the memory store you want to read. You give the same name to CREATE MEMORY STORE. This is an identifier, not a quoted string. It points to the backing table mem_<store_name>. If the store does not exist, the command fails with SQLSTATE 42704. NS = 'namespace' The namespace part of the entry's identity, given as a string literal. The namespace lets one store hold separate parts of memory. For example, you can use one namespace per agent, session, or tenant. The outer quotes are stripped before the lookup. SQL-standard doubled inner quotes are collapsed into one quote too. KEY = 'key' The key part of the entry's identity inside the namespace, given as a string literal. Together NS and KEY point to one stored record. Quotes are handled the same way as for the namespace.

sections.heading: Examples
sections.text: Read back a value that you wrote with MEMORY PUT. MEMORY PUT INTO agent_mem VALUES ('session:42', 'last_intent', 'book_flight'); MEMORY GET FROM agent_mem WHERE NS = 'session:42' AND KEY = 'last_intent'; This example has a namespace and a key with a single quote. You write it with the SQL-standard doubled quote. MEMORY GET FROM agent_mem WHERE NS = 'user''s notes' AND KEY = 'draft'; The store can be made with an embedding dimension. A read from that kind of store also returns the embedding column. CREATE MEMORY STORE kb WITH (embedding_dim = 3, distance = 'cosine'); MEMORY PUT INTO kb VALUES ('docs', 'intro', 'welcome', '[0.1, 0.2, 0.3]'); -- returns mem_namespace, mem_key, mem_value, embedding, created_at MEMORY GET FROM kb WHERE NS = 'docs' AND KEY = 'intro';

sections.heading: Compatibility
sections.text: MEMORY GET is an EvolutionDB extension. It is not part of the SQL standard. PostgreSQL has no matching command. It is a short, easy form for a single-row SELECT … WHERE pk = … LIMIT 1 against the store's backing table. The set of clauses is kept small on purpose. A general SELECT is flexible, but here the WHERE clause cannot grow. It takes only the fixed NS + KEY equality pair. There is no column list. There is no ORDER BY. There is no LIMIT that you set yourself. The result is always capped at one row. To run any predicate you like over stored memory, query the backing table mem_<store_name> directly with a standard SELECT.

sections.heading: See Also
sections.text: CREATE MEMORY STORE MEMORY PUT MEMORY DELETE MEMORY SEARCH MEMORY LIST NAMESPACES DROP MEMORY STORE

sections.heading: Introduction
sections.text: MEMORY PUT Write one key/value memory item into a memory store. This can insert a new item or replace an old one.

sections.heading: Synopsis
sections.text: MEMORY PUT INTO store_name VALUES ( namespace, key, value [ , embedding ] )

sections.heading: Description
sections.text: MEMORY PUT stores one item in a memory store. You make the store first with CREATE MEMORY STORE. Each item has an identity. The identity is the pair of namespace and key. The item holds a value. The item can also hold an embedding vector. The vector is used for semantic search with MEMORY SEARCH. The command has upsert (PUT) semantics.
