The pair of namespace and key is the primary key of the store's backing table. So a write to a pair that already exists replaces the old value. It does not raise a duplicate-key error. The replacement is atomic inside the storage layer. The new tuple is inserted first. Then the old one is dropped. This way an item is never missing if the write fails partway through. Each memory store is built as a normal heap table. The table is named mem_<store_name> in the current schema. MEMORY PUT fills in that table's columns for you. It builds the composite primary key from namespace and key. It sets created_at to the current UTC timestamp. It leaves ttl_at NULL. The write goes through the engine's normal insert path. MVCC visibility rules apply. Constraints and policies on the backing table also apply. You can give the embedding argument only for stores that have an embedding dimension. This means embedding_dim > 0. For a store made without embeddings, use the three-value form. The embedding column does not exist, and the four-value form has no slot to fill.

sections.heading: Parameters
sections.text: store_name The name of the target memory store. You give it as a bare identifier after INTO. The store must already exist. If it does not, the command fails with SQLSTATE 42704. This means the memory store does not exist. namespace A string literal that groups related items. For example, it can be a tenant, an agent, or a conversation id. Together with key it forms the item's identity. It is stored in the mem_namespace column. key A string literal that names the item inside its namespace. The pair of namespace and key is unique within the store. Reusing a pair replaces the existing item. value The payload. It is written to the store's mem_value JSON column. Each argument is either a string literal or the keyword NULL. If you give NULL here, it stores a NULL value. embedding Optional. A string literal that holds the vector for this item in [f1,f2,...] form. It is stored in the embedding column. You can use it only when the store was created with an embedding dimension. Like the other arguments, it may be NULL to store the item without a vector. The argument syntax is positional. You do not name columns. Every value is a quoted string literal or NULL. Only the two forms shown in the Synopsis are accepted. You give three values without an embedding, or four values with one.

sections.heading: Examples
sections.text: Store a memory item in a plain key/value store with no embedding. MEMORY PUT INTO agent_mem VALUES ( 'user:42', 'favorite_color', '{"color": "blue"}' ); Overwrite the same item with a new value. It uses the same namespace and key. MEMORY PUT INTO agent_mem VALUES ( 'user:42', 'favorite_color', '{"color": "green"}' ); Store an item with its embedding vector. The store was created with an embedding dimension. MEMORY PUT INTO agent_mem VALUES ( 'user:42', 'note', 'Prefers concise answers', '[0.12, -0.04, 0.88, 0.31]' ); Store an item with a NULL value. MEMORY PUT INTO agent_mem VALUES ( 'session:abc', 'last_seen', NULL );

sections.heading: Compatibility
sections.text: MEMORY PUT is an EvolutionDB extension. It is not part of the SQL standard. It has no equivalent in PostgreSQL. It is built for long-term agent memory. It gives you a managed key/value-plus-vector layer over a normal backing table. The upsert behavior is like PostgreSQL's INSERT ... ON CONFLICT DO UPDATE. But the two are not the same in syntax. MEMORY PUT uses positional string-literal arguments and a fixed implicit schema. It does not use an explicit column list and conflict target. Unlike a standard INSERT, values are always string literals or NULL. Bind parameters and expressions are not accepted in the VALUES list. The matching hand-written statement would be an INSERT INTO mem_<store_name> (...) VALUES (...) on the backing table. You would also need to compute the primary key and the timestamp, and handle the conflict yourself.

sections.heading: See Also
sections.text: CREATE MEMORY STORE DROP MEMORY STORE MEMORY GET MEMORY SEARCH MEMORY DELETE MEMORY LIST NAMESPACES INSERT

sections.heading: Introduction
sections.text: MEMORY SEARCH MEMORY SEARCH sorts the rows of a memory store by how close they are to a query embedding. It then returns the closest matches.

sections.heading: Synopsis
sections.text: MEMORY SEARCH store_name USING VECTOR 'query' [ LIMIT count ]

sections.heading: Description
sections.text: MEMORY SEARCH runs a similarity search over a memory store. You create the store with CREATE MEMORY STORE. The command compares the query embedding you give against the embedding column of every row in the store's backing table. It computes a distance score for each row. The store's distance metric decides how the score is computed. The rows come back sorted from nearest to farthest. The smallest score is the nearest row. Each result row has four columns. mem_namespaceThe namespace the entry was stored under. mem_keyThe key inside that namespace. mem_valueThe stored JSON value. scoreThe distance between the row's stored embedding and the query embedding. The metric you chose at CREATE MEMORY STORE time computes it. Results are sorted by this column from low to high. The lowest score is the closest match. The distance function is set when you create the store. It comes from the store's distance option. You cannot change it for a single query. The options work like this. cosineCosine distance. This is the default for any store. It is used when distance was 'cosine' or was left out. l2Euclidean (L2) distance. It is used when distance was 'l2'. inner / ipInner-product distance. It is used when distance was 'inner' or 'ip'. l1Manhattan (L1) distance. It is used when distance was 'l1'. Inside the engine, the search runs as a SELECT over the backing table. So the same rules apply to the search results as to the table. This includes row-level security policies, MVCC visibility rules, and other engine-level guarantees. The memory store must have been created with a non-zero embedding_dim. A store made without an embedding column is a plain JSON key/value table. It cannot do semantic search. Searching such a store raises an error with SQLSTATE 42P01. Searching a store that does not exist raises SQLSTATE 42704.

sections.heading: Parameters
sections.text: store_name The name of the memory store to search. The store must exist. It must have been created with an embedding dimension greater than zero. USING VECTOR 'query' The query embedding. You give it as a string literal. It is read as a vector literal. A vector literal is a comma-separated list of floats inside brackets, for example '[0.1, 0.2, 0.3]'. It is compared against each row's stored embedding. This clause is required. There is no search form based on a key or text. LIMIT count Optional. It limits how many result rows come back. When you leave it out, the search returns the 10 closest matches. count is an integer literal.

sections.heading: Examples
sections.text: Return the 10 closest entries to a query embedding. This uses the default limit. MEMORY SEARCH agent_mem USING VECTOR '[0.12, 0.04, 0.91, 0.33]'; Return only the single best match. MEMORY SEARCH agent_mem USING VECTOR '[0.12, 0.04, 0.91, 0.33]' LIMIT 1; This example gets the top five nearest matches. Each row includes the computed distance in the score column. -- store created with: CREATE MEMORY STORE notes -- WITH (embedding_dim = 4, distance = 'l2') MEMORY SEARCH notes USING VECTOR '[1.0, 0.0, 0.0, 0.0]' LIMIT 5; -- columns: mem_namespace | mem_key | mem_value | score

sections.heading: Compatibility
sections.text: MEMORY SEARCH is an EvolutionDB extension. There is no matching statement in the SQL standard. It is not part of PostgreSQL's grammar. It works much like a nearest-neighbour ORDER BY query against a pgvector column in PostgreSQL. One such query is ORDER BY embedding <-> '[...]' LIMIT k. But it is one dedicated command, not a general SELECT operator. These behaviours are specific to EvolutionDB. The distance metric is set on the store when you create it. You cannot pick it per query. PostgreSQL and pgvector let you pick an operator per query, such as <->, <#>, or <=>. Results are always ordered by score from low to high. There is no user-supplied ORDER BY clause in this command. When you leave out LIMIT, the command uses a built-in default of 10 rows. It does not return all rows. The returned columns are fixed. They are mem_namespace, mem_key, mem_value, and score. There is no select-list in the grammar.

sections.heading: See Also
sections.text: CREATE MEMORY STORE MEMORY PUT MEMORY GET MEMORY DELETE MEMORY LIST NAMESPACES DROP MEMORY STORE SELECT

sections.heading: Introduction
sections.text: RECLAIM RECLAIM, remove dead row versions and tidy up a table's heap storage

sections.heading: Synopsis
sections.text: RECLAIM TABLE table_name

sections.heading: Description
sections.text: RECLAIM frees the storage used by dead row versions. It also reorganizes the heap pages of a single table on disk. EvolutionDB does not overwrite or remove rows right away on DELETE or UPDATE. Instead it keeps the old versions as MVCC tuples. It keeps them until no running transaction can still see them. RECLAIM cleans up those old versions. It also tightens the on-disk layout that they leave behind. RECLAIM TABLE does the following work on the named table, in this order. Dead tuple removal. EvolutionDB walks the heap page chain and removes every dead MVCC tuple. A tuple is dead in three cases. First, the transaction that inserted the row was aborted. Second, the transaction that inserted the row crashed and never committed, so the row was left in progress. Third, the transaction that deleted or updated the row has committed, and it is older than the oldest transaction that is still active. Each removed row is also purged from the primary-key B-tree and from every secondary index. Any TOAST overflow chain for the row is freed too. XID freezing. Some live rows were inserted by a transaction id that is old enough. It is older than the freeze cutoff. For those rows, the xmin value is rewritten to the frozen marker. This lets the old transaction ids be reused. It also protects the table against transaction-id wraparound. The table's frozen_xid statistic moves forward to match. Index compaction. The primary-key B-tree is compacted. The deletions above leave tombstones, and earlier lazy deletes leave them too. Those tombstones are physically dropped. Nodes that hold too few entries are merged. Index pages that become empty are freed. Page compaction. Each surviving heap page is compacted inside itself. The live records are packed together. The free space in the page is made contiguous. Page merging and freeing. Live records from sparse pages at the end are moved forward into earlier pages that have room. The primary-key entries for the moved rows are repointed to their new location. Pages that become completely empty are returned to the free list. The heap page chain is then rebuilt over the surviving pages. Dropped-column rewrite. A table may have columns that were logically removed by ALTER TABLE ... DROP COLUMN. If so, every row is rewritten without those columns. The dropped-column metadata is also removed from the catalog. This frees the per-row space that the dropped columns still used. When the operation finishes, the dead-tuple counter for the table is reset. A summary line is then printed. It reports how many dead tuples were removed, how many tuples were frozen, how many bytes were compacted, how many records were moved, how many pages were freed, and how many heap pages remain. A table may have never received any data, so it has no heap pages. In that case, RECLAIM reports that there is nothing to do and returns successfully. Automatic RECLAIM. EvolutionDB can run the same procedure on its own. A background path triggers RECLAIM for a table once its dead-tuple count crosses a threshold. When statistics are available, the threshold is more than 20% of the row count. When no statistics have been gathered, the threshold is more than 50 dead tuples. Running RECLAIM TABLE by hand does exactly the same work on demand.

sections.heading: Parameters
sections.text: table_name The name of the table to reclaim. Exactly one table name is required. The grammar does not accept a list of tables. It also does not accept any options or a parenthesized argument list. The name may not match an existing table. In that case, the command fails with an "undefined table" error.

sections.heading: Examples
sections.text: Reclaim a single table after a large batch of deletes and updates. RECLAIM TABLE orders; A common maintenance cycle. First delete the old rows. Then reclaim the space they used. DELETE FROM sessions WHERE last_seen < '2026-01-01'; RECLAIM TABLE sessions; Reclaim a table after dropping a column. This physically removes the dropped column from every row. ALTER TABLE products DROP COLUMN legacy_notes; RECLAIM TABLE products;

sections.heading: Compatibility
sections.text: The SQL standard has no RECLAIM statement. Storage maintenance of this kind is left fully to the implementation. RECLAIM TABLE is closest in idea to PostgreSQL's VACUUM. It also matches the freezing behavior of VACUUM FREEZE. It removes dead tuples, freezes old transaction ids, and reclaims index and heap space. It differs from PostgreSQL in some important ways. The keyword is RECLAIM TABLE, not VACUUM. EvolutionDB does not accept the VACUUM keyword. EvolutionDB's grammar accepts only the bare form RECLAIM TABLE table_name. It does not support a parenthesized option list like PostgreSQL's (FULL, ANALYZE, VERBOSE, FREEZE). It does not accept multiple table names. It does not accept a column list. PostgreSQL's ordinary VACUUM reclaims space lazily. It rarely returns pages to the operating system. RECLAIM TABLE works differently. It always compacts and merges heap pages, and it frees emptied pages. This behavior is closer to VACUUM FULL. Freezing happens in the same pass, so a separate FREEZE option is not needed. There is no ANALYZE built into RECLAIM. Statistics collection is a separate concern.

sections.heading: See Also
sections.text: TRUNCATE DELETE UPDATE CREATE TABLE

sections.heading: Introduction
sections.text: REPLACE REPLACE inserts a row. If a row with the same primary key already exists, it deletes that row first. Then it inserts the new one.

sections.heading: Synopsis
sections.text: REPLACE [ LOW_PRIORITY | DELAYED | HIGH_PRIORITY ] [ IGNORE ] [ INTO ] table_name [ ( column [, ...] ) ] VALUES ( { expression | DEFAULT } [, ...] ) [, ...] REPLACE [ LOW_PRIORITY | DELAYED | HIGH_PRIORITY ] [ IGNORE ] [ INTO ] table_name SET column = { expression | DEFAULT } [, ...] REPLACE [ LOW_PRIORITY | DELAYED | HIGH_PRIORITY ] [ IGNORE ] [ INTO ] table_name [ ( column [, ...] ) ] select_statement

sections.heading: Description
sections.text: REPLACE writes rows into table_name. It works just like INSERT, but with one difference. The new row may clash with an existing row on the table's primary key. When this happens, the old row is deleted and the new row takes its place. The statement does not fail with a duplicate-key error. Conflict detection uses only the table's primary key. EvolutionDB builds the primary-key value from the column values you give. It then checks the primary-key B+ tree. If no matching key is found, the row is inserted in the normal way. This is the same as INSERT. If a matching key is found, the engine deletes the old row. This is an MVCC delete under the current transaction. Then it inserts the new row. So a REPLACE is a delete plus an insert. It is not an in-place update. The replacement row is a brand-new tuple. Any columns you do not provide take their defaults or NULL. Any BEFORE INSERT triggers fire for the inserted row. The operation runs inside the statement's transaction. If the transaction aborts, it is rolled back with the rest of the work. Three forms are accepted. They all share the same conflict behavior. VALUES uses one or more rows in parentheses. You can add a column list before them. SET is the MySQL-style assignment form. It lists column = value pairs for a single row. SELECT fills the target from the result of a query. You can add a column list. Requires a primary key. REPLACE resolves conflicts using the primary-key index. A table without a usable primary key has nothing to collide on. In that case REPLACE acts as a plain insert. It raises a duplicate-key error only when the same key value comes up again.

sections.heading: Parameters
sections.text: LOW_PRIORITY | DELAYED | HIGH_PRIORITY Priority hints carried over from the INSERT grammar. They are accepted for MySQL syntax compatibility. They do not change scheduling in EvolutionDB. Statements run on the engine's single serialized write path no matter which hint you use. IGNORE A modifier shared with INSERT. It is parsed in the same option spot as the priority hints. REPLACE already resolves primary-key collisions by replacing the old row. So IGNORE has no extra effect on the conflict path. INTO An optional word before the table name. It is accepted for compatibility. REPLACE table and REPLACE INTO table mean the same thing. table_name The table that receives the rows. It must already exist. column [..] An optional list of target columns. You can use it with the VALUES and SELECT forms. When you give it, values are matched to these columns by position. Columns left out of the list take their default value or NULL. When you leave the list out, values are matched to the table's columns in the order they were defined. expression A value expression that gives the column value. Inside a VALUES row or a SET assignment, you can use the keyword DEFAULT instead of an expression. This asks for the column's declared default. DEFAULT Puts the column's default value in that spot. It uses NULL when no default is declared. select_statement A query whose result rows are replaced into the target. Each result row uses the same primary-key conflict handling as a VALUES row. SET column = value [..] The single-row assignment form. Each pair sets one column. Columns you do not list take their default value or NULL.

sections.heading: Examples
sections.text: This example creates a table with a primary key and then uses REPLACE on it. CREATE TABLE products ( id INT PRIMARY KEY, name VARCHAR(50), price INT ); -- No row with id = 1 yet: behaves like INSERT REPLACE INTO products (id, name, price) VALUES (1, 'Keyboard', 40); -- id = 1 already exists: old row is deleted, new row inserted in its place REPLACE INTO products (id, name, price) VALUES (1, 'Keyboard Pro', 65); A multi-row VALUES form. One key is new and one key collides. REPLACE INTO products (id, name, price) VALUES (2, 'Mouse', 25), (1, 'Keyboard Max', 80); The INTO keyword is optional. A column can be set to its default. REPLACE products (id, name, price) VALUES (3, 'Cable', DEFAULT); The SET form replaces a single row. REPLACE INTO products SET id = 1, name = 'Keyboard Ultra', price = 95; The SELECT form copies rows from another table. It replaces rows on key collisions. REPLACE INTO products (id, name, price) SELECT id, name, price FROM staging_products;

sections.heading: Compatibility
sections.text: REPLACE is not part of the SQL standard. PostgreSQL does not have a REPLACE statement. EvolutionDB's REPLACE follows the MySQL/MariaDB dialect. It accepts the same three forms VALUES, SET, and ... SELECT. It also accepts the optional INTO keyword and the priority and IGNORE modifiers in the MySQL syntax spots. These are the differences and limits to keep in mind. Primary key only. Like MySQL, EvolutionDB resolves the conflict by deleting the old row and inserting the new one. EvolutionDB does this against the table's primary key. It does not treat extra UNIQUE constraints as separate conflict targets. Priority hints do nothing. LOW_PRIORITY, DELAYED, and HIGH_PRIORITY are accepted for syntax compatibility. They do not affect scheduling. Portable alternative. For standard upsert behavior, use INSERT ... ON CONFLICT DO UPDATE in the PostgreSQL style or INSERT ... ON DUPLICATE KEY UPDATE in the MySQL style. EvolutionDB's INSERT supports both. Unlike REPLACE, those forms update the old row in place. They do not delete and re-insert it.

sections.heading: See Also
sections.text: INSERT UPDATE DELETE SELECT

sections.heading: Introduction
sections.text: REVOKE Take away access privileges or role membership that you gave to a database user before.

sections.heading: Synopsis
sections.text: REVOKE { ALL [ PRIVILEGES ] | privilege [, ...] } ON { DATABASE | SCHEMA | TABLE } scope_name FROM user_name -- Role membership form REVOKE role_name FROM user_name where privilege is one of: SELECT | INSERT | UPDATE | DELETE | CREATE | DROP

sections.heading: Description
sections.text: REVOKE takes away one or more privileges that a user got before. It can also take away a user's membership in a role. It does the opposite of GRANT. EvolutionDB keeps privileges in the system catalog. There is one grant entry for each user and scope. When you revoke privileges, EvolutionDB finds the matching grant for the user and scope. It removes the privilege bits you asked for. Then it does the following. If some privileges are left, EvolutionDB saves the grant entry again with the smaller set. If no privileges are left, EvolutionDB deletes the grant entry. If you revoke ALL or ALL PRIVILEGES, EvolutionDB also clears any WITH GRANT OPTION flag on the entry. The user and scope must have a grant entry. If there is no grant entry, the command fails with an error. The SQLSTATE is 42704, which means undefined object. The error says that no matching grant was found. Scopes. A privilege belongs to one of three scope kinds. These are DATABASE, SCHEMA, and TABLE. The scope_name names the object. The revoke must use the same scope kind and name that the original grant used. A revoke on a TABLE scope does not change a wider DATABASE or SCHEMA grant for the same user. PUBLIC grants are additive. EvolutionDB uses the PostgreSQL additive model. EvolutionDB checks privileges granted to PUBLIC on their own, apart from user grants. A revoke for one user removes only that user's own grant entry. It does not hide or override a privilege the user still has through a PUBLIC grant. To take a privilege away from everyone, revoke it from PUBLIC too. Role form. The command has no ON keyword in this form. EvolutionDB then reads it as a role-membership revoke. REVOKE role_name FROM user_name removes the user's membership in the named role. Who may revoke. The admin user can always revoke. A non-admin user can revoke a privilege only when that user holds the same privilege WITH GRANT OPTION on the same scope. If not, the command is denied with SQLSTATE 42501. Only admin can do role-membership revokes. On success the command reports a tag. It reports REVOKE for the privilege form. It reports REVOKE ROLE for the role-membership form.

sections.heading: Parameters
sections.text: ALL [ PRIVILEGES ] Revoke every privilege EvolutionDB tracks for the scope. These are SELECT, INSERT, UPDATE, DELETE, CREATE, and DROP. The keyword PRIVILEGES is optional. EvolutionDB accepts it but ignores it. This matches the noise-word usage in the SQL standard. Revoking ALL also clears the grant's WITH GRANT OPTION flag. privilege One privilege to remove. The allowed values are SELECT, INSERT, UPDATE, DELETE, CREATE, and DROP. You can list more than one privilege. Separate them with commas. Privilege names are case-insensitive. EvolutionDB quietly ignores tokens it does not know. If the final privilege set is empty, the command fails with an invalid privilege specification error. ON DATABASE | SCHEMA | TABLE scope_name Picks the scope kind and names the object the grant applies to. You must give exactly one of DATABASE, SCHEMA, or TABLE. You can write scope_name with no quotes. You can also use single quotes like 'name' or double quotes like "name". It must match the scope kind and name used by the original GRANT. FROM user_name The user whose privileges or role membership you are removing. You can also name PUBLIC here. You can write it with no quotes or with single quotes. role_name This is used in the role-membership form, which has no ON clause. It is the name of the role you take away from user_name. If the user is not a member of that role, the command fails. Not supported. EvolutionDB's REVOKE does not support some SQL-standard features. It does not accept the GRANT OPTION FOR clause. It does not accept the CASCADE or RESTRICT behavior keywords. It does not accept column-level privilege lists. There is no REVOKE ... FROM PUBLIC shorthand beyond naming PUBLIC as the user. You cannot revoke from more than one user in one statement. To revoke a single privilege from a grant that holds ALL, that privilege must be in the existing grant.

sections.heading: Examples
sections.text: Take away the right to insert into a table. The read access stays. REVOKE INSERT ON TABLE orders FROM reporting; Revoke several privileges at the same time. REVOKE INSERT, UPDATE, DELETE ON TABLE orders FROM reporting; Take away all privileges on a schema. This also clears any grant option the user had. REVOKE ALL PRIVILEGES ON SCHEMA sales FROM analyst; Revoke a database-wide privilege from everyone. Target PUBLIC to do this. REVOKE SELECT ON DATABASE testdb FROM PUBLIC; Use a quoted scope name when the name is not a plain identifier. REVOKE SELECT ON TABLE "Quarterly Report" FROM analyst; Remove a user's membership in a role. Only admin can do this. REVOKE analysts FROM alice;

sections.heading: Compatibility
sections.text: EvolutionDB's REVOKE handles the most common case in the SQL standard and in PostgreSQL. It removes object privileges from a user. But it differs in a few ways. The scope keyword is required. The SQL standard and PostgreSQL work out the object class on their own. EvolutionDB does not. It needs an explicit ON DATABASE, ON SCHEMA, or ON TABLE keyword. No CASCADE or RESTRICT. EvolutionDB does not accept the standard's behavior keywords for dependent privileges. A revoke does not also take away privileges that the user may have re-granted to others. No GRANT OPTION FOR. You cannot revoke only the grant option and keep the privilege itself. Revoking ALL clears the grant option. Revoking single privileges leaves the flag the same on whatever is left. No column-level privileges and no lists of more than one user. Privilege set. EvolutionDB tracks only SELECT, INSERT, UPDATE, DELETE, CREATE, and DROP. It does not model PostgreSQL privileges such as TRUNCATE, REFERENCES, TRIGGER, CONNECT, TEMPORARY, EXECUTE, and USAGE. Additive PUBLIC model. As in PostgreSQL, a revoke for one user does not override a privilege the user still has through a grant to PUBLIC. Role revoke is admin-only. It uses the same flat REVOKE role FROM user form as PostgreSQL. But it has no ADMIN OPTION FOR and no cascade behavior.

sections.heading: See Also
sections.text: GRANT CREATE USER

sections.heading: Introduction
sections.text: SAVEPOINT Create a new savepoint inside the current transaction. A savepoint marks a point you can roll back to later. You do not have to stop the whole transaction to do this.

sections.heading: Synopsis
sections.text: SAVEPOINT savepoint_name

sections.heading: Description
sections.text: SAVEPOINT sets a named marker inside the open transaction. After you set a savepoint, you have two choices. You can run ROLLBACK TO SAVEPOINT to undo every change made after the savepoint. The savepoint and the work done before it stay in place. You can also run RELEASE SAVEPOINT to drop the marker. Its work then becomes part of the transaction around it. In EvolutionDB, the protocol adaptor reads transaction control statements. The SQL grammar parser does not. The adaptor keeps an undo log for each session. This log holds the changes made since the transaction began. When you create a savepoint, the adaptor saves the current length of that undo log. When you roll back to the savepoint, the adaptor replays the undo entries in reverse. It stops when the log is back at the saved length. So a savepoint is just a saved position in the undo stack. It is not a nested sub-transaction with its own transaction id. You can only create a savepoint while a transaction is open. If no transaction is running, the command fails with SQLSTATE 25P01. The message is "SAVEPOINT can only be used in a transaction". A savepoint name is required. If you run SAVEPOINT with no name, it fails with SQLSTATE 42601. You can create a savepoint with the same name as one that already exists in the transaction. In that case EvolutionDB overwrites the old one in place. It moves the marker to the current position in the undo log. This is not how the SQL standard and PostgreSQL work. They keep both savepoints and hide the older one. See Compatibility for more. Savepoint names are compared without regard to case. They are cut to 127 characters. One transaction can hold up to 64 active savepoints. If you try to create more, the command fails with SQLSTATE 53000. The message is "too many savepoints". Note. The keyword and the name are read by position. The adaptor reads the word SAVEPOINT. Then it takes the next token as the savepoint name. A token ends at a space, a semicolon, or the end of the statement. There is no quoting and no parenthesized option syntax.

sections.heading: Parameters
sections.text: savepoint_name The name for the new savepoint. This is required. The name is one token with no spaces. Later you can use it with ROLLBACK TO SAVEPOINT or RELEASE SAVEPOINT. The match does not care about case. The name is stored cut to 127 characters. If you reuse a name that already exists in the transaction, it overwrites that savepoint. It does not create a second one.

sections.heading: Examples
sections.text: Set a savepoint. Then roll back to it to drop only the most recent change. BEGIN; INSERT INTO accounts VALUES (1, 'opening'); SAVEPOINT after_open; INSERT INTO accounts VALUES (2, 'oops'); -- undo the second insert, keep the first ROLLBACK TO SAVEPOINT after_open; INSERT INTO accounts VALUES (3, 'correct'); COMMIT; -- rows 1 and 3 are committed; row 2 is gone Release a savepoint once you are sure its work is good. After the release, the marker is gone. The changes stay part of the transaction. BEGIN; SAVEPOINT sp1; UPDATE inventory SET qty = qty - 1 WHERE id = 42; RELEASE SAVEPOINT sp1; COMMIT; Reusing a savepoint name moves the marker forward. It does not stack a new one. BEGIN; INSERT INTO log VALUES ('a'); SAVEPOINT mark; INSERT INTO log VALUES ('b'); -- re-pointing 'mark' here: a later ROLLBACK TO mark -- will only undo work done after this point SAVEPOINT mark; INSERT INTO log VALUES ('c'); ROLLBACK TO SAVEPOINT mark; -- undoes only 'c' COMMIT;

sections.heading: Compatibility
sections.text: EvolutionDB's SAVEPOINT follows the idea of the SQL standard's <savepoint statement>. For the common case of SAVEPOINT name, it works the same as the PostgreSQL form on the wire. The related commands ROLLBACK TO [SAVEPOINT] name and RELEASE [SAVEPOINT] name also work. The SAVEPOINT keyword is optional in them. This matches PostgreSQL. Here are the differences to know about. Duplicate names overwrite The SQL standard and PostgreSQL allow many savepoints with the same name. The newest one hides the older ones until it is released. EvolutionDB does it another way. It overwrites a same-named savepoint in place. So a transaction never has more than one savepoint per name. No nested sub-transactions A savepoint is a position in the undo log for the session. It is not a nested transaction with its own id. There is no separate sub-transaction view. There is no per-savepoint error isolation. The only thing a savepoint can do is undo the recorded changes. Fixed savepoint limit A transaction can hold at most 64 active savepoints. If you go past this limit, the command fails with SQLSTATE 53000. Plain identifier names only A name is read as one token with no quotes and no spaces. The match does not care about case. Quoted or delimited identifiers are not supported. Parenthesized option syntax is not supported either. Statement handled by the adaptor The protocol adaptor reads transaction control statements. This includes SAVEPOINT. The SQL grammar does not read them. They must stand on their own. You cannot put them inside other SQL.

sections.heading: See Also
sections.text: INSERT UPDATE DELETE SELECT

sections.heading: Introduction
sections.text: SELECT Read rows from one or more tables, subqueries, or set-returning sources. You can also filter, group, order, and limit the rows.

sections.heading: Synopsis
sections.text: SELECT [ ALL | DISTINCT | DISTINCTROW ] [ HIGH_PRIORITY ] [ STRAIGHT_JOIN ] [ SQL_SMALL_RESULT ] [ SQL_BIG_RESULT ] [ SQL_CALC_FOUND_ROWS ] { * | select_expr [ [ AS ] output_alias ] [, ...] } [ INTO new_table ] FROM from_item [, ...] [ FOR SYSTEM_TIME AS OF TRANSACTION xid ] [ WHERE condition ] [ GROUP BY expression [ ASC | DESC ] [, ...] [ WITH ROLLUP ] ] [ HAVING condition ] [ ORDER BY order_item [, ...] ] [ LIMIT { count | offset , count | count OFFSET offset } ] [ FOR UPDATE | FOR SHARE [ SKIP LOCKED ] ] [ INTO column [, ...] ] where from_item is one of: table_name [ [ AS ] alias ] [ index_hint ] ONLY table_name [ [ AS ] alias ] schema.table_name [ [ AS ] alias ] ( select_stmt ) [ AS ] alias LATERAL ( select_stmt ) [ AS ] alias UNNEST ( array_expr ) [ [ AS ] alias [ ( col ) ] ] from_item [ INNER | CROSS ] JOIN from_item [ join_condition ] from_item STRAIGHT_JOIN from_item [ ON condition ] from_item { LEFT | RIGHT } [ OUTER ] JOIN from_item join_condition from_item NATURAL [ { LEFT | RIGHT } [ OUTER ] ] JOIN from_item and join_condition is one of: ON condition USING ( column [, ...] ) and order_item is one of: column_name [ ASC | DESC ] table.column_name [ ASC | DESC ] column_position [ ASC | DESC ] vector_column <=> 'vector_literal' [ ASC | DESC ] and index_hint is one of: { USE | IGNORE | FORCE } KEY [ FOR JOIN ] ( index [, ...] )

sections.heading: Description
sections.text: SELECT reads rows from the database and returns them as a result set. EvolutionDB accepts two main shapes. Both are defined by the select_stmt rule in evoparser.y. A projection-only form, SELECT select_expr_list with no FROM. It evaluates the listed expressions once and returns a single row. This is how SELECT 1 and SELECT NOW() are handled. The full table query form. It adds FROM and the optional WHERE, GROUP BY, HAVING, ORDER BY, LIMIT, and row-locking clauses. A table query is processed in this order. Each from_item in FROM is scanned. Then joins are applied. The leftmost item is the driving relation. The other items default to INNER JOIN unless you give an explicit join type. If WHERE is present, its condition is checked against each candidate row. Rows that are not true are dropped. If GROUP BY is present, the remaining rows are collapsed into groups. Aggregate functions in the select list are computed for each group. WITH ROLLUP adds super-aggregate summary rows. If HAVING is present, its condition filters the groups. The select list is evaluated to form the output rows. Then DISTINCT and DISTINCTROW remove duplicate rows. If ORDER BY is present, rows are sorted. Non-numeric values are compared as text. Values that parse cleanly as numbers are compared as numbers. See CompareRecords in Select.c. LIMIT and OFFSET limit how many rows are returned. If FOR UPDATE or FOR SHARE is present, the returned rows are locked. The simple single-table path inside the engine works in a few steps. It finds the table in the catalog. It then does a primary-key lookup when a single equality on the PK is given, or a sequential table scan through the buffer pool. It decodes the binary tuple format. Finally it prints the matching rows. Note. The engine has a built-in text renderer for the SELECT * path. It buffers rows in fixed-size storage so it can sort ORDER BY in memory. The normal way clients connect is through the dual-protocol server. In that case the adaptor's query executor handles result streaming and richer expression work.

sections.heading: Parameters
sections.text: ALL / DISTINCT / DISTINCTROW These control how duplicate rows are handled. ALL is the default and returns every row. DISTINCT removes duplicate result rows. DISTINCTROW means the same thing. If you give the same option twice, you get a parse error called "duplicate … option". HIGH_PRIORITY, STRAIGHT_JOIN, SQL_SMALL_RESULT, SQL_BIG_RESULT, SQL_CALC_FOUND_ROWS These are MySQL-compatibility select options. The grammar accepts them as leading keywords. They parse without error so MySQL-style statements still load. In this position, only DISTINCT and DISTINCTROW change the result. * / select_expr [ [AS] output_alias ] This is the select list. * returns all columns of the source. Each other item is an expression. It can be a column reference, a literal, or a call to an arithmetic, string, aggregate, or window function. You can rename it with AS alias. The AS keyword is optional. from_item (FROM) One or more sources, separated by commas. A comma makes an implicit cross or inner join. A source can be a table, written as table_name or schema.table_name. It can also be a subquery in parentheses with a name, a LATERAL subquery, an UNNEST(array_expr) set-returning source, a join in parentheses, or an explicit join. The join forms are shown below. ONLY table_name In a table source, ONLY limits the scan to the named parent table. It hides rows that would otherwise come from child tables that inherit from it. JOIN clauses EvolutionDB accepts these joins. They are INNER JOIN, CROSS JOIN, plain JOIN which is treated as inner, STRAIGHT_JOIN, LEFT [OUTER] JOIN, RIGHT [OUTER] JOIN, and NATURAL joins. A NATURAL join may also use LEFT or RIGHT OUTER. You give the join predicate with ON condition or USING ( column [, ...] ). Outer joins need an explicit condition. index_hint An optional per-table hint. It can be USE KEY, IGNORE KEY, or FORCE KEY. Each one takes an index list in parentheses. You can also scope it to joins with FOR JOIN. FOR SYSTEM_TIME AS OF TRANSACTION xid This is a time-travel qualifier. It runs the query against the database snapshot at the given transaction id xid instead of the current snapshot. WHERE condition A boolean expression. Only rows where it is true are passed on. It supports comparisons, AND/OR/NOT, IN (...) and IN (subquery), EXISTS (subquery), scalar and quantified subqueries, and vector-distance operators. GROUP BY expression [ASC | DESC] [..] [WITH ROLLUP] This groups rows that share the same values over the listed expressions. Aggregate functions are then computed for each group. An optional ASC or DESC can follow each grouping expression. WITH ROLLUP adds super-aggregate subtotal and grand-total rows. HAVING condition A boolean condition applied to grouped rows. It usually refers to aggregates. It filters whole groups after GROUP BY. ORDER BY order_item [..] A list of sort keys. Each item is a column name, a qualified table.column, a 1-based column position such as ORDER BY 2, or a vector nearest-neighbor expression column <=> 'vector_literal' that uses the cosine-distance operator <=>. Each key can be followed by ASC, the default, or DESC. In ORDER BY expression position only the <=> operator is allowed. Other comparison operators are rejected. LIMIT { count | offset, count | count OFFSET offset } This limits how many rows are returned. Three forms are accepted. They are LIMIT count, the MySQL two-argument form LIMIT offset, count, and LIMIT count OFFSET offset. FOR UPDATE | FOR SHARE [ SKIP LOCKED ] This locks the returned rows at row level. FOR UPDATE takes an exclusive row lock. FOR SHARE takes a shared lock. The optional SKIP LOCKED modifier skips rows that are already locked instead of waiting for them. INTO column [..] This is the trailing variable-target list form. It sends the selected values into the named targets. It is different from the table-creating SELECT ... INTO new_table FROM ... form described below. SELECT.. INTO new_table FROM.. This is a PostgreSQL-style table-creating form. The query result is stored in a brand-new table new_table. Its columns are inferred from the select list. Inside the engine this uses the CREATE-TABLE-AS and inferred-schema path.

sections.heading: Examples
sections.text: This example selects every column from a table. SELECT * FROM employees; This example returns specific columns with an output alias and filters the rows. SELECT name, salary AS pay FROM employees WHERE dept_id = 10; This example eliminates duplicates and sorts the result in descending order. SELECT DISTINCT dept_id FROM employees ORDER BY dept_id DESC; Aggregate per group, filter the groups, and keep only the top rows. SELECT dept_id, COUNT(*) AS headcount, AVG(salary) AS avg_pay FROM employees GROUP BY dept_id HAVING COUNT(*) > 3 ORDER BY headcount DESC LIMIT 5; This example uses an inner join with an ON condition. SELECT e.name, d.dept_name FROM employees AS e INNER JOIN departments AS d ON e.dept_id = d.id; This example uses a left outer join with USING. SELECT e.name, d.dept_name FROM employees e LEFT OUTER JOIN departments d USING (dept_id); This example uses the MySQL-style two-argument LIMIT form (offset, count) for paging. SELECT name FROM employees ORDER BY name LIMIT 20, 10; This example runs a vector nearest-neighbor search using the cosine-distance operator. SELECT id FROM documents ORDER BY embedding <=> '[0.12, 0.84, 0.05]' LIMIT 5; This example runs a time-travel query against a past transaction snapshot. SELECT * FROM accounts FOR SYSTEM_TIME AS OF TRANSACTION 4200; This example locks the matched rows for update. It skips rows that are already locked. SELECT * FROM jobs WHERE status = 'pending' FOR UPDATE SKIP LOCKED; This example materializes a query result into a new table using the PostgreSQL-style SELECT INTO form. SELECT name, salary INTO high_earners FROM employees WHERE salary > 100000; This example expands an array into rows using UNNEST. SELECT v FROM UNNEST(ARRAY[1, 2, 3]) AS t(v);

sections.heading: Compatibility
sections.text: EvolutionDB's SELECT mixes SQL-standard, PostgreSQL, and MySQL spellings. SQL standard / PostgreSQL. Several parts follow standard and PostgreSQL conventions. These are DISTINCT, the join family with INNER, CROSS, LEFT/RIGHT [OUTER], NATURAL, and ON/USING, then GROUP BY, HAVING, and ORDER BY, LATERAL subqueries, UNNEST, row locking with FOR UPDATE or FOR SHARE and SKIP LOCKED, the SELECT ... INTO new_table table-creating form, and the SQL:2011 temporal opener FOR SYSTEM_TIME AS OF. Note that the temporal clause takes TRANSACTION xid, a transaction id, instead of a timestamp. MySQL extensions. Several parts are MySQL-style. These are DISTINCTROW, the leading options HIGH_PRIORITY, STRAIGHT_JOIN, SQL_SMALL_RESULT, SQL_BIG_RESULT, and SQL_CALC_FOUND_ROWS, the two-argument LIMIT offset, count form, WITH ROLLUP on GROUP BY, and the index hints USE/IGNORE/FORCE KEY. Several of the leading options are accepted for source compatibility but do not change the result. EvolutionDB-specific. The vector-distance operator <=> means cosine distance. It is allowed in ORDER BY expression position for nearest-neighbor search. Other comparison operators are rejected there. Not part of this command. The grammar's select_stmt rule does not define some clauses. These are WITH common table expressions, set operations such as UNION/INTERSECT/EXCEPT, WINDOW-clause named windows, FETCH FIRST, and OFFSET as a standalone clause. Only the LIMIT forms shown above are supported. Window functions appear as function expressions in the select list, not as a separate top-level clause.

sections.heading: See Also
sections.text: INSERT UPDATE DELETE CREATE TABLE

sections.heading: Introduction
sections.text: SET Set a session user variable. You can also change the active schema for the current connection.

sections.heading: Synopsis
sections.text: SET @variable_name { = | := } value_expression [ , @variable_name { = | := } value_expression [ , ... ] ] SET SCHEMA schema_name SET SCHEMA DEFAULT

sections.heading: Description
sections.text: SET has two uses in EvolutionDB. The word after the keyword tells them apart. User variables. SET @name = expr creates or overwrites a user variable for the session. The right-hand side is read as a full SQL value expression. This can include arithmetic, function calls, and other things the expression engine understands. The result is stored under the given name for the life of the connection. You can then use the variable anywhere an expression is allowed. For example, you can use it in a SELECT list or a WHERE clause by writing @name. A variable that was never set returns NULL instead of raising an error. You can set several variables in one statement. Separate the assignments with commas. You can use either = or := as the assignment operator. No other comparison operator is accepted. For example, SET @x > 1 is a syntax error. Schema selection. SET SCHEMA schema_name changes the active schema inside the current database. After this, unqualified table and object names resolve against that schema. The schema must already exist in the current database. If it does not, the statement fails with an undefined schema error. The active schema then stays the same. SET SCHEMA DEFAULT switches back to the schema named default. Both forms apply only to the connection that runs them. Variables and the chosen schema are not shared between connections. They do not survive after you disconnect. Note. User variables start with a leading @ sigil. For example, @x and @threshold. This is the only way to read or write them. There is no separate command to inspect them. A connection can hold up to 64 different user variables.

sections.heading: Parameters
sections.text: @variable_name The name of the session user variable to assign. Write it with a leading @. If you reuse an existing name, the old value is overwritten. = / := The assignment operator. Both forms mean the same thing. They are the only operators accepted in this position. value_expression Any SQL value expression. It is evaluated once when the SET statement runs. The computed value is stored in the variable. The stored value is then returned as is wherever you use @variable_name. schema_name The name of an existing schema in the current database. It becomes the active schema for the connection. After this, unqualified object names resolve against this schema. DEFAULT In the SET SCHEMA DEFAULT form, this picks the schema named default as the active schema.

sections.heading: Examples
sections.text: This example assigns an integer and reads it back. SET @x = 42; SELECT @x; -- 42 The right-hand side is a full expression. Arithmetic works too. SET @x = 10 + 20; SELECT @x; -- 30 This example uses the colon-equals form to store a string. SET @name := 'Alice'; SELECT @name; -- Alice This example references a variable inside a query. SET @threshold = 15; SELECT * FROM uv_test WHERE val > @threshold; You can overwrite a variable by assigning to the same name again. SET @x = 1; SET @x = 99; SELECT @x; -- 99 An unset variable evaluates to NULL. SELECT @undefined_var_xyz; -- NULL This example switches the active schema for the connection, then returns to the default. SET SCHEMA sales; SET SCHEMA DEFAULT;

sections.heading: Compatibility
sections.text: The SQL standard has no SET command for run-time parameters. The standard does define SET SCHEMA as part of session management. EvolutionDB's SET SCHEMA schema_name form follows that intent. It selects the schema used to resolve unqualified names. The SET SCHEMA DEFAULT spelling is an EvolutionDB shortcut. It selects the schema named default. EvolutionDB's user-variable form is SET @name = expr. You can also use := here. This form follows the MySQL session-variable convention, not PostgreSQL. It is not related to PostgreSQL's SET configuration_parameter TO value or SET ... = ... for run-time configuration. EvolutionDB does not implement that family of configuration parameters. It also does not implement the SET SESSION, SET LOCAL, SET TIME ZONE, or SET TRANSACTION clauses. PostgreSQL also has no equivalent of EvolutionDB's @-sigil user variables. In PostgreSQL, such per-session values are usually handled with customized run-time parameters or PL/pgSQL variables.

sections.heading: See Also
sections.text: SELECT USE CREATE SCHEMA

sections.heading: Introduction
sections.text: SHOW Show the value of a run-time configuration parameter. You can also list catalog objects such as databases, schemas, tables, users, grants, and stores.

sections.heading: Synopsis
sections.text: SHOW DATABASES SHOW SCHEMAS SHOW TABLES SHOW VIEWS SHOW CREATE VIEW view_name SHOW USERS SHOW ROLES SHOW GRANTS [ FOR user_name ] SHOW JOBS SHOW SUBSCRIPTIONS SHOW MEMORY STORES SHOW CHECKPOINT STORES SHOW ENTITY STORES SHOW GRAPH STORES SHOW DOCUMENT STORES SHOW MESSAGE LOGS SHOW EVO_SESSIONS SHOW EVO_PROCESSES SHOW REPLICATION STATUS SHOW REPLICATION LAG SHOW NODES SHOW TABLE PLACEMENT SHOW SHARDS table_name SHOW configuration_parameter

sections.heading: Description
sections.text: SHOW reports the value of a run-time configuration parameter. It can also return a result set that lists catalog objects. Most EvolutionDB statements are defined in the Bison grammar. SHOW is not defined there. The grammar file is evolution/parser/evoparser.y. Instead, the dual-protocol adaptor catches any statement whose first keyword is SHOW. This happens in adaptor/catalog.c. The adaptor handles the statement before the SQL engine ever sees it. Dispatch uses case-insensitive substring matching against the raw statement text. The first matching form wins. Three forms are handled by the distributed-command handler. These are SHOW NODES, SHOW TABLE PLACEMENT, and SHOW SHARDS. That handler runs ahead of the general SHOW handler. All forms return a result set tagged with the command tag SHOW. The object-listing forms behave like a SELECT. Each one defines its own columns. Matching is substring-based and order-sensitive. The keyword you use must match first in the dispatch chain. For example, a statement that contains the word tables is treated as SHOW TABLES. A SHOW statement that matches none of the listed forms falls through to the run-time parameter path. That path returns a single setting column. The value is an empty string when the parameter is unknown. Scope. The object listings use the session's current database and current schema. SHOW TABLES and SHOW VIEWS show only objects in the current schema. A temporary table is shown only to the session that owns it. SHOW DATABASES, SHOW USERS, SHOW ROLES, and the store, job, and subscription listings cover the whole cluster.

sections.heading: Parameters
sections.text: DATABASES Lists every database in the cluster. Returns one column, database. SCHEMAS Lists every schema in the current database. Returns one column, schema. TABLES Lists tables in the current schema of the current database. Returns one column, table. A temporary table appears only in the session that created it. VIEWS Lists views in the current schema. Returns view_name and view_sql. The view_sql column holds the stored query that defines the view. CREATE VIEW view_name Shows the rebuilt CREATE VIEW statement for the named view. Returns columns View and Create View. The definition is rebuilt as CREATE VIEW name AS <stored sql>. The view must exist in the current database and schema. If it does not, the statement returns SQLSTATE 42P01. That code means the view does not exist. USERS Lists all database users. Returns one column, username. ROLES Lists all roles. Returns one column, role_name. GRANTS [ FOR user_name ] Lists privilege grants. With no FOR clause, all grants are returned. With FOR user_name, the result is filtered to that user. The name may be unquoted or single-quoted. Returns user, scope, object, privileges, and grant_option. The scope value is TABLE, SCHEMA, or DATABASE. The grant_option value is YES or NO. JOBS Lists registered scheduled cron jobs. Returns name, schedule, sql, enabled, and last_run_unix. The schedule column is the cron expression. The sql column is the body it runs. The enabled column is a boolean. The last_run_unix column is the Unix timestamp of the last fire. A value of 0 means the job has never run. SUBSCRIPTIONS Lists durable subscriptions. Returns name, channel, last_ack_seq, next_seq, and ttl_days. MEMORY STORES Lists memory stores created with CREATE MEMORY STORE. Returns name, backing_table_id, embedding_dim, and distance. The distance value is cosine, l2, inner, or l1. The underscore spelling MEMORY_STORES is also accepted. CHECKPOINT STORES Lists checkpoint stores. Returns name, backing_table_id, and retention. The underscore spelling CHECKPOINT_STORES is also accepted. ENTITY STORES Lists entity stores. Returns name, backing_table_id, and ttl_days. The underscore spelling ENTITY_STORES is also accepted. GRAPH STORES Lists graph stores. Returns name, nodes_table_id, and edges_table_id. The underscore spelling GRAPH_STORES is also accepted. DOCUMENT STORES Lists document stores. Returns name, backing_table_id, embedding_dim, and distance_kind. The underscore spelling DOCUMENT_STORES is also accepted. MESSAGE LOGS Lists message logs. Returns name, backing_table_id, and ttl_days. The underscore spelling MESSAGE_LOGS is also accepted. EVO_SESSIONS Lists the sessions that are connected right now. Returns pid, username, database, state, and duration_ms. The pid column is the session id. The state value is active or idle. The duration_ms column is how many milliseconds the current query has been running. It is 0 when the session is idle. EVO_PROCESSES Works like EVO_SESSIONS. It adds a query column with the text of the session's current statement. The columns are pid, username, database, state, duration_ms, and query. REPLICATION STATUS Shows the streaming replication slots, one row per replica. Returns role, replica_id, my_lsn, confirmed_lsn, lag_bytes, active, and last_seen_us. The role value is primary, replica, or witness. When there are no replicas, a single summary row for the local role is returned. This form matches only when the statement text does not contain cluster. REPLICATION LAG Shows a short lag view, one row per slot. Returns replica_id and lag_bytes. NODES Lists nodes in a distributed cluster. Returns node_id, host, pg_port, and role. The role value is SELF or PEER. On a non-distributed server, it returns a single STANDALONE row for localhost:5433. The distributed-command path handles this form. TABLE PLACEMENT Lists every non-temporary table with the node that owns it. Returns table_name, schema, owner_node_id, and location. The location value is LOCAL or REMOTE. The distributed-command path handles this form. SHARDS table_name Lists the shards of a sharded table. Returns shard_name, ordinal, owner_node_id, range_bound, shard_type, and shard_key. The range_bound value is MAXVALUE for the open upper bound. The shard_type value is HASH or RANGE. It returns no rows if the table is not sharded. The distributed-command path handles this form. configuration_parameter Any SHOW form not matched above returns a single setting column. The known parameters are listed here. system_time_retention is the number of days of system-versioned history that is kept. A value of 0 means pruning is off. It is returned as system_time_retention_days. Other known parameters are max_connections, active_connections, statement_timeout / evo_statement_timeout, evo_lock_timeout, evo_idle_timeout, transaction_isolation, and search_path. server_version reports 15.0 (EvoSQL). max_identifier_length reports 63. standard_conforming_strings reports on. cluster reports the Raft status, which includes the role, term, leader, and LSN. buffer_pool_stats is a multi-column view of the hit rate and I/O. buffer_pool_size is also accepted as shared_buffers, innodb_buffer_pool_size, or db_cache_size. An unknown parameter does not raise an error. It returns one row with an empty setting value.

sections.heading: Examples
sections.text: List databases. Then list the schemas and tables in the current database. SHOW DATABASES; SHOW SCHEMAS; SHOW TABLES; Rebuild the definition of a view. SHOW CREATE VIEW active_users; Show all grants. Then show only the grants held by one user. SHOW GRANTS; SHOW GRANTS FOR 'analyst'; Look at the connected sessions and the queries they are running. SHOW EVO_PROCESSES; List the shards of a sharded table on a distributed cluster. SHOW SHARDS orders; Read run-time parameters. SHOW server_version; -- 15.0 (EvoSQL) SHOW max_connections; SHOW buffer_pool_stats; -- pool_pages, hits, misses, hit_rate, ... SHOW system_time_retention; -- system_time_retention_days

sections.heading: Compatibility
sections.text: The SQL standard has no SHOW statement. The run-time-parameter form of EvolutionDB is SHOW configuration_parameter. It follows the PostgreSQL convention. It supports several PostgreSQL-compatible names such as server_version, max_connections, standard_conforming_strings, search_path, and shared_buffers. To help other tools connect, SHOW server_version reports a PostgreSQL-style string. That string is 15.0 (EvoSQL). The object-listing and store-listing forms are EvolutionDB extensions. These forms include SHOW DATABASES, SHOW TABLES, SHOW SCHEMAS, SHOW GRANTS, and the various ... STORES forms. Their shape is closer to MySQL-style SHOW listings than to PostgreSQL. The exact keywords and result columns are specific to EvolutionDB. PostgreSQL's SHOW ALL is not implemented. The SHOW SESSION and SHOW LOCAL qualifiers are not implemented either. Matching is substring-based, so these qualifiers are simply ignored. EvolutionDB does not raise an error for an unknown parameter. It returns an empty setting value instead. PostgreSQL raises an error in this case.

sections.heading: See Also
sections.text: SET CREATE VIEW GRANT SELECT

sections.heading: Introduction
sections.text: BEGIN / COMMIT / ROLLBACK Open, commit, or undo a transaction with many statements. A group of changes either all take effect together or none of them do.

sections.heading: Synopsis
sections.text: BEGIN START TRANSACTION COMMIT END ROLLBACK SAVEPOINT savepoint_name ROLLBACK TO [ SAVEPOINT ] savepoint_name RELEASE [ SAVEPOINT ] savepoint_name

sections.heading: Description
sections.text: In EvolutionDB each statement runs in its own transaction. This transaction is implicit and commits on its own. This happens unless you first open an explicit transaction block. BEGIN starts such a block. START TRANSACTION is a synonym for it. After that, the server records every data-modifying statement in a per-connection undo log. The block stays open until you finish it with COMMIT or ROLLBACK. COMMIT saves all the changes made since BEGIN. The changes become durable. Other connections can now see them. Before the server confirms the commit, it does a few things. It flushes the transaction's dirty pages to the write-ahead log. It assigns a commit sequence number. It marks the transaction committed in the commit log. This commit log is called the CLOG. The server then discards the undo log. It also releases any locks the transaction held. END is an exact synonym for COMMIT. ROLLBACK throws away the transaction. The server replays the undo log in reverse. This restores every inserted, updated, or deleted row to its earlier state. The server marks the transaction aborted in the CLOG. It then releases its locks. Under MVCC, a rollback of an UPDATE or DELETE does not rewrite data. Instead it clears the soft-delete marker xmax on the original tuple. Pre-MVCC rows use a re-insert fallback. Each connection has its own transaction context. The undo log is thread-local. So transactions on different connections are independent. A connection may disconnect with a transaction still open. In that case the server rolls it back on its own. Note. A statement inside the block may raise an error. Then the transaction enters an aborted state. While aborted, the server accepts only ROLLBACK, COMMIT, SAVEPOINT, and RELEASE. It rejects other statements until the block is closed. A COMMIT on an aborted transaction performs an implicit ROLLBACK. All work is discarded. This matches PostgreSQL.

sections.heading: Isolation level
sections.text: You do not set the transaction isolation level on the BEGIN statement itself. EvolutionDB matches BEGIN and START TRANSACTION by prefix. So it accepts any words you type after the keyword, but it ignores them. To choose an isolation level, run SET [SESSION] TRANSACTION ISOLATION LEVEL first. Run it before you open the transaction. You can also run it before any query inside the transaction. The recognized levels are READ UNCOMMITTED, READ COMMITTED, REPEATABLE READ, and SERIALIZABLE. READ COMMITTED is the default. At READ COMMITTED each statement takes a fresh snapshot. At REPEATABLE READ and SERIALIZABLE, BEGIN takes one snapshot. The whole transaction sees that same snapshot. A SERIALIZABLE transaction also registers with the conflict guard. This checks for write-skew. Such a transaction may fail at COMMIT with SQLSTATE 40001. The message is could not serialize access due to read/write dependencies among transactions. Then the server rolls back the transaction. The command reports ROLLBACK.

sections.heading: Savepoints
sections.text: Inside an open transaction you may set named savepoints. SAVEPOINT name marks the current position in the undo log. ROLLBACK TO [SAVEPOINT] name replays the undo log back to that mark. It undoes everything done since the savepoint. It keeps earlier work and the transaction itself. It also clears the aborted state, so the transaction can continue. RELEASE [SAVEPOINT] name forgets the savepoint and all newer ones. It does not undo any work. If you define a savepoint with the name of an existing one, it overwrites the old one. Savepoint commands outside a transaction fail with SQLSTATE 25P01. A reference to a savepoint that does not exist fails with 3B001.

sections.heading: Parameters
sections.text: BEGIN Start an explicit transaction block on the current connection. It sets up a fresh undo log and savepoint stack. It also gives the transaction an MVCC transaction id. The command reports the tag BEGIN. A BEGIN while you are already inside a transaction logs a warning. Otherwise it does nothing. It does not start a nested transaction. START TRANSACTION An exact synonym for BEGIN. It behaves the same way. COMMIT End the current transaction and make all of its changes permanent. It flushes WAL. It records the commit in the CLOG with a commit sequence number. It releases locks. It discards the undo log. It delivers any pending notifications. It reports the tag COMMIT. Run outside a transaction, it does nothing and causes no harm. END An exact synonym for COMMIT. ROLLBACK End the current transaction and undo all changes made since BEGIN. It replays the undo log in reverse. It marks the transaction aborted. It releases locks. It reports the tag ROLLBACK. Run outside a transaction, it does nothing and causes no harm. SAVEPOINT savepoint_name Set a new savepoint at the current point inside the open transaction. Names are case-insensitive. If you reuse an existing name, it overwrites that savepoint. ROLLBACK TO [ SAVEPOINT ] savepoint_name Undo all changes made after the named savepoint was set. This does not end the transaction. The SAVEPOINT keyword is optional. The server discards any savepoints created after the target. It also clears the transaction's aborted flag if one is set. RELEASE [ SAVEPOINT ] savepoint_name Remove the named savepoint and all savepoints created after it. The work done since then is kept. The SAVEPOINT keyword is optional.

sections.heading: Examples
sections.text: This example moves money between two accounts as one atomic step. BEGIN; UPDATE accounts SET balance = balance - 100 WHERE id = 1; UPDATE accounts SET balance = balance + 100 WHERE id = 2; COMMIT; This example discards a set of changes instead of keeping them. BEGIN; DELETE FROM staging_rows; -- changed your mind: ROLLBACK; This example uses a savepoint to undo only part of a transaction. BEGIN; INSERT INTO orders (id, total) VALUES (1, 50); SAVEPOINT before_items; INSERT INTO order_items (order_id, sku) VALUES (1, 'BAD-SKU'); ROLLBACK TO SAVEPOINT before_items; -- keep the order, drop the bad item COMMIT; This example sets an isolation level before opening the transaction. SET TRANSACTION ISOLATION LEVEL REPEATABLE READ; BEGIN; SELECT count(*) FROM inventory; -- the same snapshot is seen for the whole transaction SELECT sum(qty) FROM inventory; COMMIT; This example shows that END is interchangeable with COMMIT. BEGIN; UPDATE settings SET value = 'on' WHERE key = 'feature_x'; END;

sections.heading: Compatibility
sections.text: EvolutionDB's transaction control follows the spirit of the SQL standard. It works well with PostgreSQL. It makes some changes to keep things simple on purpose. The server accepts both BEGIN and the SQL-standard START TRANSACTION. They behave the same way. END works as a synonym for COMMIT. This is a PostgreSQL extension. The standard only defines COMMIT. PostgreSQL allows optional noise words on these statements. These include WORK, TRANSACTION, and AND CHAIN. EvolutionDB does not parse them as separate clauses. It matches the leading keyword and ignores any text after it. So COMMIT WORK behaves the same as COMMIT. The extra words have no effect. AND CHAIN does not start a new transaction. PostgreSQL lets you set transaction options on the BEGIN line. These include ISOLATION LEVEL ..., READ ONLY, READ WRITE, and DEFERRABLE. EvolutionDB does not read these from the BEGIN statement. Set the isolation level first with SET [SESSION] TRANSACTION ISOLATION LEVEL. There is no read-only or deferrable transaction mode. A nested BEGIN does nothing. It emits a warning instead of an error. This matches PostgreSQL. A COMMIT or ROLLBACK outside a transaction does nothing and causes no harm. SAVEPOINT, ROLLBACK TO [SAVEPOINT], and RELEASE [SAVEPOINT] work with PostgreSQL-compatible behavior. A reused savepoint name overwrites the old one. A ROLLBACK TO clears the aborted state. A failed statement aborts the whole transaction. The server refuses later statements until you run ROLLBACK. A ROLLBACK TO a prior savepoint also works. This matches PostgreSQL's failed-transaction behavior. A COMMIT on an aborted transaction performs an implicit rollback. Two-phase commit is available through the separate XA command family. This family is XA START/END/PREPARE/COMMIT/ROLLBACK/RECOVER. There is no PREPARE TRANSACTION statement.

sections.heading: See Also
sections.text: INSERT UPDATE DELETE SELECT

sections.heading: Introduction
sections.text: TRUNCATE Remove all rows from one or more tables in a single statement.

sections.heading: Synopsis
sections.text: TRUNCATE TABLE table_name [ , table_name [, ... ] ] [ RESTART IDENTITY | CONTINUE IDENTITY ] [ CASCADE | RESTRICT ]

sections.heading: Description
sections.text: TRUNCATE removes all rows from a table. It does the same job as a DELETE with no WHERE clause. But it does not scan and remove rows one at a time. Instead it throws away the table's storage all at once. This makes it much faster on large tables. For each named table, EvolutionDB destroys the heap pages, the primary-key B+ tree, and every secondary index. This includes B+ tree, hash, and unique-hash indexes. It then builds them again as empty structures. It also resets the table's row and page statistics to zero. The table definition stays intact. This means the columns, constraints, indexes, and privileges all remain. Only the data is removed. The TABLE keyword is required. EvolutionDB's grammar accepts TRUNCATE TABLE. It does not accept the bare TRUNCATE form. You can truncate several tables in one statement. List them after the first table name and separate them with commas. You can name the first table plus up to seven more tables. That is eight tables in total. Any names beyond that limit are ignored without warning. The tables are processed from left to right. If any table fails, the statement stops and reports the error. For example, the table may not exist.

sections.heading: Foreign keys
sections.text: Before it truncates a table, EvolutionDB checks the other tables. It looks for any other table that has a foreign key pointing to this one and holds at least one row. Say such a referencing table exists and you did not give CASCADE. Then the operation is refused with SQLSTATE 23503. The message names the referencing table. With CASCADE, the referencing tables are truncated first. This happens recursively. So the dependent rows are removed along with the target. Self-references are ignored for this check. Note. If the named table does not exist, TRUNCATE fails with SQLSTATE 42P01. This means the table does not exist. On success the server reports command(s) completed successfully!...

sections.heading: Parameters
sections.text: table_name The name of a table to truncate. The first name is required. You can add more names after it, separated by commas. These truncate further tables in the same statement. You can add up to seven extra tables. Names are resolved through the current database and schema search path. RESTART IDENTITY Reset the table's auto-increment counter. The next inserted row then starts again from the beginning. This is EvolutionDB's default behavior. For any table that has an auto-increment column, the counter is reset to zero. The only exception is when you give CONTINUE IDENTITY. You can write this clause directly for SQL and PostgreSQL compatibility. CONTINUE IDENTITY Do not reset the auto-increment counter. The sequence continues from where it left off. So values are not reused even though the table is now empty. CASCADE Automatically truncate any non-empty tables that hold foreign keys pointing to a table being truncated. This continues recursively down the chain. Without this option, you cannot truncate a table that is still referenced by dependent tables that hold rows. That is an error. RESTRICT Refuse to truncate if other tables hold foreign keys pointing to the target. This is the default foreign key behavior. You get it simply by leaving out CASCADE. The grammar accepts this keyword for compatibility. It adds no behavior beyond the default.

sections.heading: Examples
sections.text: Remove all rows from a single table. TRUNCATE TABLE orders; Remove all rows from several tables in one statement. TRUNCATE TABLE orders, order_items, shipments; Remove all rows from a table but keep the auto-increment counter moving forward. TRUNCATE TABLE sessions CONTINUE IDENTITY; Remove all rows from a table and from all tables that point to it through foreign keys. -- customers is referenced by orders, which is referenced by order_items TRUNCATE TABLE customers CASCADE; Ask for the default identity reset in a direct way. TRUNCATE TABLE audit_log RESTART IDENTITY;

sections.heading: Compatibility
sections.text: The SQL standard defines TRUNCATE TABLE with optional RESTART IDENTITY and CONTINUE IDENTITY clauses. EvolutionDB follows that form. It also accepts PostgreSQL's CASCADE and RESTRICT keywords. Here are the differences to be aware of. EvolutionDB requires the TABLE keyword. PostgreSQL makes it optional and allows the bare TRUNCATE foo form. That bare form is not accepted here. PostgreSQL allows an explicit ONLY qualifier and table inheritance rules. EvolutionDB has no such clause. In EvolutionDB the identity default is RESTART IDENTITY. The auto-increment counter is reset unless you give CONTINUE IDENTITY. This matches PostgreSQL's default. CASCADE recursively truncates referencing tables that hold rows. This matches PostgreSQL. Without CASCADE, you cannot truncate a table that a non-empty table references. This fails with SQLSTATE 23503. RESTRICT simply names this default behavior.

sections.heading: See Also
sections.text: DELETE INSERT UPDATE SELECT

sections.heading: Introduction
sections.text: UPDATE UPDATE changes column values in the rows of a table that match a condition.

sections.heading: Synopsis
sections.text: UPDATE [ LOW_PRIORITY ] [ IGNORE ] table [ AS alias ] [ , table [ AS alias ] ] SET [ table. ]column = expression [ , [ table. ]column = expression ] ... [ WHERE condition ] [ ORDER BY column [ ASC | DESC ] [ , ... ] ] [ LIMIT count [ OFFSET start ] ] [ LIMIT start , count ] [ RETURNING * | output_column [ , ... ] ] The assignment operator in the SET list must be =. The parser rejects any other comparison operator in that spot. It reports "bad update assignment".

sections.heading: Description
sections.text: UPDATE changes the values of the named columns. It does this for every row of table that matches the WHERE condition. Only the columns named in the SET clause change. All other columns keep their current values. The server reports the number of changed rows back to the client. EvolutionDB applies each update without destroying the old data. This is the MVCC model. The engine does not overwrite a row in place. Instead it stamps the old version with the current transaction's xmax. This marks the old version as soft-deleted. The engine then writes the new version to a fresh heap location with a fresh xmin. It then points the primary-key B+ tree entry at the new RowID. The old version stays on the page. So other readers that hold an older snapshot still see the row before the update. The change can also be rolled back. The engine takes an exclusive row lock on each row before it changes the row. If the lock cannot be granted, the statement fails with a lock or deadlock error. There are two ways to run an update. The WHERE-expression form scans the table. It collects the matching rows. Then it applies the update to each one. For a simple primary-key range condition like WHERE id <= 5000, the engine walks only the matching B+ tree keys instead. The single-key form has no WHERE clause. It finds one row directly by its primary key. The statement becomes a multi-table update when the FROM-style table list names more than one table. It also becomes a multi-table update when a join is present. EvolutionDB checks the new values before it writes a row. It checks them against the column's declared type. It also checks the NOT NULL, UNIQUE, CHECK, and FOREIGN KEY constraints. It re-evaluates any STORED generated columns. It enforces row-level security USING and WITH CHECK policies. It fires BEFORE UPDATE and AFTER UPDATE triggers. A SET on a generated column is rejected. The engine enforces the configured ON UPDATE referential action on referencing child rows when a primary-key column changes. The action can be RESTRICT, NO ACTION, CASCADE, SET NULL, or SET DEFAULT. For system-versioned tables, the engine copies the old row to the history table. It sets the old row's valid_to to the current time. It also refreshes the live row's validity window automatically.

sections.heading: Parameters
sections.text: LOW_PRIORITY An optional update modifier that the grammar accepts. It is a MySQL-compatibility keyword. The parser reads it and records it. It does not change how EvolutionDB schedules work. IGNORE An optional update modifier that the grammar accepts. It is a MySQL-compatibility keyword. The parser reads it and records it as an option flag. table [ AS alias ] The target table whose rows you want to update. You can give an optional alias. You can list more than one table reference, separated by commas. Join syntax is also accepted. The statement is processed as a multi-table update when it references more than one table. column The name of a column in the target table. You can write it in the qualified form table.column. The qualified form is what drives multi-table assignments. The column cannot be a generated column. If you assign to one, the engine raises an error. The error says the column can only be updated to DEFAULT. expression The value to assign to the column. You write it as a SQL expression after =. The new value must match the column's declared type. The engine checks the type before it writes the row. WHERE condition An expression that returns a boolean result. Only rows where it is true are updated. In the single-key form you leave it out, and the engine finds the row by primary key. When you give a WHERE expression, every row that matches the condition is updated. A simple primary-key range or equality condition uses a B+ tree cursor walk instead of a full table scan. ORDER BY column [ ASC | DESC ] The grammar accepts this MySQL-style ordered update. It orders the candidate rows. It is most useful together with LIMIT. LIMIT count [ OFFSET start ] / LIMIT start, count Limits how many of the matched rows are actually updated. The engine slices the matched set by the OFFSET and LIMIT range first. Then it applies the updates. Rows outside the range are left unchanged. RETURNING * | output_column [.. ] Makes UPDATE return the affected rows. RETURNING * returns all columns. A column list returns just those columns. The returned values show the new row after the update.

sections.heading: Examples
sections.text: This example changes one column on one row found by its primary key. UPDATE employees SET salary = 75000 WHERE id = 42; This example updates several columns at once. One new value comes from an expression. UPDATE products SET price = price * 1.10, updated_by = 'admin' WHERE category = 'books'; This example uses a range condition over the primary key. It takes the B+ tree cursor fast path. UPDATE orders SET status = 'archived' WHERE id <= 5000; This example updates only the first few matching rows in order and returns the new values. UPDATE tickets SET priority = 'high' WHERE assignee IS NULL ORDER BY created_at ASC LIMIT 10 RETURNING id, priority; This example sets a column to NULL. UPDATE accounts SET closed_at = NULL WHERE id = 7;

sections.heading: Compatibility
sections.text: The core form UPDATE table SET col = expr [ , ... ] WHERE condition follows the SQL standard. It matches PostgreSQL closely. The optional RETURNING clause is a PostgreSQL extension. EvolutionDB supports it. It returns the column values after the update. A few clauses are MySQL-style extensions. They are not part of the SQL standard or PostgreSQL. These are the LOW_PRIORITY and IGNORE modifiers before the table name. They also include the ORDER BY and LIMIT clauses on UPDATE. The LIMIT start, count two-argument form is MySQL syntax. The LIMIT count OFFSET start form follows PostgreSQL. EvolutionDB's UPDATE differs from PostgreSQL in some ways. It does not implement PostgreSQL's UPDATE ... FROM from_list clause for joins. Instead, you write multi-table updates by listing tables and joins directly after the UPDATE keyword in a FROM-style table reference. You then use qualified SET table.column = ... assignments. This is the MySQL multi-table style. EvolutionDB also does not support WHERE CURRENT OF cursor targeting. It does not support table aliases used only for a FROM join. It does not support the standard SET (col1, col2) = (...) multi-column row-assignment form. You write assignments one column at a time. The assignment operator must be a plain =.

sections.heading: See Also
sections.text: INSERT DELETE SELECT CREATE TABLE

sections.heading: Introduction
sections.text: USE USE picks the active database for the current session.

sections.heading: Synopsis
sections.text: USE [ DATABASE ] database_name

sections.heading: Description
