The evaluator resolves a column reference by name. The match is case-insensitive. It returns string literals as they are. For any arithmetic or numeric node it calls the numeric evaluator and renders the result back as text. Integer results print without a decimal point. Other numbers use %g formatting. The string-valued design leads to two facts that come up again and again in this chapter. Values carry no static typeAt evaluation time, a column value, a literal, and a function result are all text. Each operation decides on its own whether to treat the operands as numbers or as text. It does this by looking at the operand strings. See Section 9.7. NULL is a separate return channelThe evaluator returns 1 when it produced a value. It returns 0 to signal NULL. A NULL literal evaluates to NULL. So does a column whose stored value is the internal null marker. So does an unresolved column name. Some functions hold NULL in their output. They use the engine's null marker string.

sections.heading: 9.2.1. Arithmetic and Bitwise Operators
sections.text: Arithmetic runs as C double. The engine recognizes these operators. OperatorOperationNotes +Addition -Subtraction. Also unary negation. *Multiplication /DivisionDivision by zero yields 0, not an error and not infinity. %ModuloInteger modulo. Both operands are truncated to long long first. Modulo by zero yields 0. << >>Bit shift left / rightOperands truncated to long long. & | ^Bitwise AND / OR / XOROperands truncated to long long. SELECT price * 1.1, qty % 12, flags & 4 FROM orders; NULL in arithmetic. Arithmetic runs through the numeric evaluator. That evaluator treats an unresolved or non-numeric operand as 0.0. It does not propagate NULL. So NULL + 1 on the numeric path resolves to 1. A non-numeric string contributes its strtod value. Text with no leading number contributes 0. This differs from the SQL standard. In the standard, any NULL arithmetic operand makes the whole expression NULL.

sections.heading: 9.2.2. Comparison Operators
sections.text: The six comparison operators return a boolean. The boolean is rendered as the single character t or f. =Equal <>Not equal < >Less than / greater than <= >=Less than or equal / greater than or equal A comparison first evaluates both sides. If either side is NULL, the result is NULL. The evaluator returns the null channel. This follows SQL three-valued logic. Otherwise the evaluator chooses how to compare. If both rendered values parse cleanly and fully as numbers, it compares them as numbers. Otherwise it compares them as byte strings with strcmp. See Section 9.7. SELECT * FROM orders WHERE total >= 100 AND status <> 'cancelled'; The <=> operator. EvolutionDB parses <=> as a vector cosine-distance operator. See Section 9.6.7. When the operands are not vector literals, it falls back to a plain equality test. That test returns t or f. So SELECT 1 <=> 1 returns t. In the general case it is not MySQL's null-safe equals.

sections.heading: 9.2.3. Logical Operators
sections.text: AND, OR, NOT, and XOR work on boolean values. They use full SQL three-valued logic. A value counts as true if it is t, the word true in any case, or a non-zero number. Anything else is false. The truth tables for NULL follow the standard. ExpressionResult FALSE AND NULLf TRUE AND NULLNULL NULL AND NULLNULL TRUE OR NULLt FALSE OR NULLNULL NOT NULLNULL NULL XOR xNULL (XOR requires both operands non-null)

sections.heading: 9.2.4. Pattern Matching: LIKE and REGEXP
sections.text: LIKE and NOT LIKE support the two SQL wildcards. % matches any run of characters. It can also match none. _ matches exactly one character. An iterative backtracking matcher does the matching byte by byte. SELECT name FROM users WHERE name LIKE 'A%' AND email NOT LIKE '%@test._'; If either the subject or the pattern is NULL, the result is NULL. REGEXP and NOT REGEXP match against a POSIX extended regular expression. On a NULL operand the result is NULL. An invalid pattern that fails to compile evaluates to f. That means false. It does not raise an error. Limitations. LIKE is byte-exact and case-sensitive. There is no ESCAPE clause and no ILIKE. So you cannot escape a literal % or _ in the pattern. On Linux and macOS, REGEXP uses the platform POSIX <regex.h>. It is compiled with REG_EXTENDED | REG_NOSUB. Windows (MinGW) builds lack POSIX regex. On those builds REGEXP always returns f.

sections.heading: 9.2.5. NULL Predicates, BETWEEN, IN
sections.text: IS NULL and IS NOT NULL test whether their operand is NULL. A column counts as null in two cases. The first is when its stored value is the engine's null marker. The second is when the named column is not in the row at all. A literal is never null. This holds for an integer, a float, a string, or a boolean. BETWEEN, NOT BETWEEN, IN, and NOT IN are not their own node types. The parser desugars them into the operators above. x BETWEEN a AND brewritten as (x >= a) AND (x <= b). x NOT BETWEEN a AND brewritten as (x < a) OR (x > b). x IN (a, b, c)rewritten as (x = a) OR (x = b) OR (x = c). The parts go into a balanced OR tree. This keeps a long list shallow. x NOT IN (a, b, c)rewritten as (x <> a) AND (x <> b) AND (x <> c). The parts go into a balanced AND tree. IN and NOT IN reduce to comparisons and logical operators. So they take the three-valued NULL behavior of those operators. An empty IN () list evaluates to false. An empty NOT IN () list evaluates to true.

sections.heading: 9.3.1. CASE
sections.text: Both forms of CASE are supported. The searched form tests a boolean condition in each branch. The simple form compares one operand against each WHEN value. The engine desugars the simple form into the searched form. It turns each WHEN v into the comparison operand = v. Both forms share one evaluator. -- searched CASE SELECT CASE WHEN score >= 90 THEN 'A' WHEN score >= 80 THEN 'B' ELSE 'F' END FROM grades; -- simple CASE (desugars to status = 'A', status = 'I', ...) SELECT CASE status WHEN 'A' THEN 'active' WHEN 'I' THEN 'inactive' END FROM accounts; Branches are evaluated top to bottom. The first WHEN whose condition is true selects its THEN result. A branch whose condition is NULL counts as not matched. It is skipped. If no branch matches, the ELSE result is returned. If there is no ELSE, the result is NULL.

sections.heading: 9.3.2. COALESCE, NULLIF, IFNULL, IF
sections.text: COALESCE(a, b)Returns a if it is non-null. Otherwise it returns b. If both are null, it returns NULL. The engine takes two arguments. NULLIF(a, b)Returns NULL when a and b are equal as strings. Otherwise it returns a. IFNULL(a, b)This is the MySQL form. It returns a if non-null. Otherwise it returns b. IF(cond, t, f)This is the MySQL three-argument conditional. It checks cond for truthiness. A value is true if it is t, true, 1, or any non-zero number. It returns the second or third argument by that test. A NULL condition takes the false branch.

sections.heading: 9.3.3. IS TRUE / IS FALSE
sections.text: x IS TRUE returns t only when x renders as t, true, or 1. Otherwise it returns f. A NULL operand also gives f. x IS FALSE returns t only when x renders as f, false, or 0.

sections.heading: 9.4. String Functions
sections.text: String functions evaluate their first argument. If that argument is NULL, they return NULL. A few functions are exceptions. They are noted below. FunctionReturnsBehavior UPPER(s) / LOWER(s)textASCII case fold per byte via toupper/tolower. LENGTH(s)integerByte length of the string via strlen. This is not the character count. SUBSTRING(s, pos [, len])textThe start position is 1-based. A position below 1 is clamped to 1. With no length, it returns the tail from pos. TRIM([LEADING|TRAILING|BOTH] [chars] FROM s)textTrims any of the characters in chars from the chosen end or ends. The default is a single space. CONCAT(a, b)textJoins two arguments. It is NULL-propagating in the MySQL style. If either argument is NULL, the result is NULL. REPLACE(s, from, to)textReplaces all non-overlapping occurrences of from with to. An empty from returns s unchanged. LEFT(s, n) / RIGHT(s, n)textFirst or last n bytes. n is clamped to [0, length]. LPAD(s, n, pad) / RPAD(s, n, pad)textPads to width n with the repeating pad string on the left or right. The default pad is a space. REVERSE(s)textReverses the bytes of the string. REPEAT(s, n)textJoins n copies of s. INSTR(s, sub)integer1-based position of the first occurrence of sub in s. Returns 0 if not found. LOCATE(sub, s)integerSame as INSTR with the arguments swapped. TOKEN_LENGTH(s)integerRough word count from a whitespace split. It stands in for a tokenizer. SELECT UPPER(LEFT(name, 3)), LENGTH(bio), REPLACE(slug, '-', '_'), SUBSTRING(sku, 2, 4) FROM profiles; Encoding caveat. All string functions work on bytes. LENGTH, SUBSTRING, LEFT/RIGHT, and REVERSE count and slice by byte. So a multi-byte UTF-8 character is several units. It can be split. UPPER/LOWER fold only ASCII letters. There is no separate CHAR_LENGTH. The evaluator's string buffers have a fixed size. It is often 512 bytes. So a function may truncate a very long value.

sections.heading: 9.5. Mathematical Functions
sections.text: Math functions evaluate their arguments as numbers. They render the result with %g. Some render as an integer, as noted. A NULL argument usually gives NULL. FunctionResult ABS(x)Absolute value. CEIL(x) / FLOOR(x)Round up or down to an integer value. ROUND(x [, d])Round to d decimal places. The default is 0. POWER(a, b)a raised to the power b. SQRT(x)Square root. MOD(a, b)Floating-point remainder via fmod. MOD(a, 0) returns NULL. LOG(x)Natural logarithm. LOG10(x)Base-10 logarithm. SIGN(x)The sign of x as an integer. It is 1, -1, or 0. RAND()Pseudo-random double in [0, 1]. PI()The constant π to 15 significant digits. SELECT ROUND(price * 1.08, 2), CEIL(weight), SIGN(balance) FROM items; Note. The MOD(a, b) function uses floating-point fmod. It returns NULL on a zero divisor. The % operator works differently. See Section 9.2.1. It truncates to integers and returns 0 on a zero divisor. SQRT, LOG, and LOG10 follow the C library on out-of-domain inputs. They may render as nan or -inf. They do not raise an error.

sections.heading: 9.6.1. Date and Time
sections.text: CURRENT_TIMESTAMP / NOW()Current local date-time as YYYY-MM-DD HH:MM:SS. CURRENT_DATECurrent local date as YYYY-MM-DD. CURRENT_TIMECurrent local time as HH:MM:SS. YEAR / MONTH / DAY / HOUR / MINUTE / SECOND(dt)Pull one field from a YYYY-MM-DD [HH:MM:SS] string. It parses the string with sscanf. DATEDIFF(d1, d2)Whole days between two dates, as d1 - d2. It uses mktime and difftime. DATE_ADD(dt, n unit) / DATE_SUB(dt, n unit)Add or subtract n of a unit. The known units are YEAR, MONTH, and HOUR. Any other unit is treated as DAY. The result is rendered as a date in YYYY-MM-DD form. SELECT YEAR(created_at), DATEDIFF(CURRENT_DATE, signup_date) FROM users; Note. Date functions work on the text YYYY-MM-DD form. They use the server's local time zone through the C library. There is no time-zone-aware TIMESTAMPTZ arithmetic here. DATE_ADD and DATE_SUB normalize through mktime. They emit a date even when you give an HOUR unit.

sections.heading: 9.6.2. Identifier and Session Functions
sections.text: gen_random_uuid()Random RFC 4122 version-4 UUID. It is formatted as 8-4-4-4-12 lowercase hex. gen_random_uuid_v7()Time-ordered RFC 9562 version-7 UUID. It has a 48-bit millisecond timestamp prefix. snowflake_id()A 64-bit Snowflake-style id. It has a 41-bit millisecond timestamp, a 10-bit machine id, and a 12-bit per-millisecond sequence. The custom epoch is 2026-01-01 UTC. The machine id comes from $EVOSQL_MACHINE_ID or from a hash of the hostname. last_insert_id()The last value taken from a UUID, Snowflake, or auto-increment default. It is 0 if there is none. CURRENT_USERThe session username from the active query context. It is unknown outside a session. evo_current_xid()The last-assigned MVCC transaction id. You most often use these generator functions as column DEFAULTs. See CREATE TABLE.

sections.heading: 9.6.3. Sequence Functions
sections.text: Sequence values come from functions. These functions resolve against the catalog of the current schema. See CREATE SEQUENCE. nextval(name)Advance the sequence and return its new value. It also updates the session's lastval tracking. currval(name)The sequence's current value without advancing. It errors if the sequence does not exist. It also errors if it has not been called yet in this session. setval(name, value [, is_called])Set the sequence's current value. An is_called of false or 0 makes the next nextval return value itself. lastval()The value most recently made by nextval in this session. It errors if no sequence has been called yet.

sections.heading: 9.6.4. JSON Functions and Operators
sections.text: JSON is stored and worked on as text. The arrow operators and a MySQL-compatible function set are available. json -> keyPull out a member or element. It returns a JSON value. json ->> keyPull out a member or element as unquoted text. JSON_EXTRACT(j, path)Extract by path. JSON_UNQUOTE(j) / JSON_QUOTE(s)Remove or add JSON string quoting. JSON_TYPE(j)Type name of the JSON value. JSON_LENGTH(j) / JSON_DEPTH(j)Element count or nesting depth. Returns NULL on invalid input. JSON_VALID(j)1 if the text is valid JSON, else 0. A NULL argument gives 0. JSON_KEYS(j)Keys of a JSON object. JSON_PRETTY(j)Indented rendering. JSON_SET / JSON_INSERT / JSON_REPLACE(j, path, value)Path-based change. JSON_SET sets the value. JSON_INSERT only inserts. JSON_REPLACE only replaces. JSON_REMOVE(j, path)Remove the element at a path. JSON_CONTAINS(target, candidate)Containment test. It returns 1 or 0. JSON_CONTAINS_PATH(j, mode, path)Test for a path. The mode is all or one. JSON_SEARCH(j, mode, str)Find a string value. The mode is all or one. JSON_OBJECT(k, v) / JSON_ARRAY(v1 [, v2 [, v3]])Build an object from one key/value pair, or an array from up to three values. A value that is already valid JSON is inserted as is. Other values are quoted. SELECT data->>'name', JSON_LENGTH(data->'tags') FROM events WHERE JSON_VALID(data) = 1 AND JSON_CONTAINS(data, '{"active":true}') = 1;

sections.heading: 9.6.5. Array Constructors and Functions
sections.text: Inside the engine, arrays are PostgreSQL-style brace text. An example is {e1,e2,NULL,...}. An element with a separator or whitespace is quoted. ARRAY[e1, e2, ...]Build an array literal. A NULL element is rendered as the unquoted token NULL. arr[i]1-based subscript. An out-of-bounds or malformed index gives NULL. This follows PostgreSQL semantics. array_length(arr)Element count. x <op> ANY(arr)True if the comparison holds for any element. If no element matches but the array holds a NULL, the result is NULL. Otherwise the result is false. Element comparison is numeric when both sides parse as numbers. Otherwise it is lexicographic. unnest(arr)In scalar context it returns the array text as is. True row-producing expansion needs a FROM clause. The join pipeline handles that, not this evaluator.

sections.heading: 9.6.6. Full-Text Match
sections.text: MATCH(col) AGAINST('query') tokenizes the row's column value and the query literal. It returns the count of query terms that appear in the row. You can use this count as a boolean in WHERE. You can also use it as a relevance score in ORDER BY. It is a simple any-term match. A positive count means a match. It is not a ranked full-text engine.

sections.heading: 9.6.7. Vector Distance Functions and Operators
sections.text: Vectors arrive as text. One form is bracketed, like [f1,f2,...] or {f1,f2,...}. Another form is a compact quantized form, like b64i8:<base64>. The quantized form is decoded back to floats. The table below shows the distance operators and their named equivalents. OperatorDistance <=>Cosine distance (1 - cosine similarity) L2 operatorEuclidean distance inner-product operatorNegative dot product L1 operatorManhattan distance If either operand is NULL, the result is NULL. If the two vectors have different dimensions, the result is also NULL. When the operands are not vector literals, the cosine operator falls back to a string-equality test. That test returns t or f. The other operators need vectors. They return NULL in that case. There are companion functions too. vector_dim(v) gives the element count. vector_norm(v) gives the L2 norm. vector_normalize(v) gives the unit vector as bracketed text. All three round-trip through the same parser. The approximate nearest-neighbor helpers hnsw_knn(...) and hnsw_filter_knn(...) return comma-separated primary-key lists. See CREATE INDEX.

sections.heading: 9.6.8. Subqueries, Notifications, and Timing
sections.text: (SELECT ...) scalar subqueryReturns the first value of the subquery. It returns NULL if the subquery returns no rows. x IN (SELECT ...) / x NOT IN (SELECT ...)Membership test against subquery results. It collects up to 256 values. [NOT] EXISTS (SELECT ...)True or false on whether the subquery returns any row. evo_notify(channel, payload)Put a notification on the session's channel and return the payload. See LISTEN / NOTIFY. pg_listening_channels()Array text of the session's current listens. evo_sleep(ms)An interruptible delay. It returns 0. evo_jitter(min_ms, max_ms)A random interruptible delay. It returns the actual milliseconds slept.

sections.heading: 9.7. Type Coercion and CAST
sections.text: Values are untyped text during evaluation. Each operation decides coercion on its own. There is no declared expression type. ComparisonsBoth operands are compared as numbers only when each renders as a full, valid number. The whole string must be consumed by strtod and must be non-empty. If either side is not a clean number, the comparison falls back to a byte-wise strcmp. So '10' = 10 and '2' < '10' both behave as numbers. But '10x' < '9' compares as text. ArithmeticOperands are coerced with strtod. Text with no leading number contributes 0. Boolean contextA value is true if it is t, true in any case, or a non-zero number. Inside CASE and IF, 1 is also taken as true. You convert types on purpose with CAST(expr AS type). The target type's family code drives the conversion. The type encoding is described in Chapter 4. There, typeVal / 10000 is the family. Target familyCAST behavior Integer typesParse with strtol and render as a base-10 integer. Float / double typesParse with strtod and render with %g. BooleanA non-zero numeric value renders as true. Otherwise it renders as false. VARCHAR / CHAR / TEXT / DATEThe string passes through unchanged. There is no reformatting. SELECT CAST(price AS INT), CAST(active AS BOOLEAN) FROM items; A CAST of a NULL value is NULL. For the family codes, see Chapter 4, Data Types. For where expressions can appear, see SELECT.

sections.heading: 9.8. NULL Handling Summary
sections.text: The treatment of NULL is not the same across operations. The table below gathers the rules from earlier sections and puts the differences side by side. ContextNULL behavior Comparison operators (=, <, …)Any NULL operand → result NULL. This is three-valued. AND / OR / NOT / XORStandard three-valued logic. See Section 9.2.3. LIKE / REGEXPAny NULL operand → NULL. String functionsNULL input usually → NULL. CONCATAny NULL argument → NULL. This is the MySQL style. It is not the PostgreSQL ignore-null style. Most math functionsNULL input → NULL. MOD(a,0) → NULL. Arithmetic operators (numeric path)A NULL or non-numeric operand is treated as 0. It does not propagate. CASE branch with NULL conditionTreated as not matched. It falls through. COALESCE / IFNULLReturn the first non-null argument. Array subscript out of rangeNULL. Vector distance, dimension mismatchNULL. Note. Honest differences from the SQL standard. Three behaviors differ from a standard engine. They are worth remembering. (1) Division and modulo by zero return 0. They do not raise a division_by_zero error. (2) On the numeric path, arithmetic treats a NULL operand as 0. It does not propagate NULL. (3) CONCAT propagates NULL like MySQL. PostgreSQL's || and concat() ignore nulls. String functions work on bytes. Case folding is ASCII only. LIKE has no ESCAPE clause. Treat all of this as engine-specific. Test the edge cases yourself.

sections.heading: 9.9. Expressions in CHECK Constraints
sections.text: You can store part of the expression language in a table's metadata. This is for use in CHECK constraints. The expression is serialized to a compact reverse-Polish token stream. That is postfix form. It is re-parsed when the constraint is evaluated. The serializable node set has several parts. It includes column references. It includes the four literal kinds plus NULL. It includes the comparison, logical, and arithmetic operators. The logical operators are AND, OR, NOT, and XOR. It includes IS NULL and IS NOT NULL. It includes LIKE and NOT LIKE. It includes the functions UPPER, LOWER, LENGTH, TOKEN_LENGTH, CONCAT, and COALESCE. It includes the JSON arrow operators. It includes the session functions CURRENT_USER, CURRENT_TIMESTAMP, CURRENT_DATE, and CURRENT_TIME. Node types outside this set are dropped during serialization. So a CHECK built from unsupported functions will not round-trip faithfully. Keep CHECK expressions within the list above. For constraint syntax, see CREATE TABLE. CREATE TABLE accounts ( id INT PRIMARY KEY, email VARCHAR(120) CHECK (LENGTH(email) > 3), balance DECIMAL(12,2) CHECK (balance >= 0), owner VARCHAR(60) CHECK (owner = CURRENT_USER) ); These expressions are used in several places. See SELECT for projection, WHERE, and HAVING. See Chapter 6, Data Manipulation for SET assignments, DEFAULT, and ON CONFLICT updates. See CREATE SEQUENCE for the sequence functions.

sections.heading: Introduction
sections.text: 10. Indexes EvolutionDB has four index access methods. They are the default B+ tree, an FNV-1a hash index, an in-memory HNSW graph for vector search, and an inverted full-text index. Each one has its own layout on disk or in memory. Each one encodes keys in its own way. Each one speeds up a different kind of query. An index is an extra structure. The engine can read it instead of scanning every row of a table. EvolutionDB builds indexes with CREATE INDEX and CREATE FULLTEXT INDEX. It removes them with DROP INDEX. Every table also has one index that you do not create yourself. This is its primary-key index. It is a B+ tree. Its entries point at the heap location of each live row. The location is a RowID, which is page_no:4 + slot_idx:2. Secondary indexes use the same RowID value. So a matched index entry reaches its row directly. It does not need a second primary-key lookup. Scope of this chapter. This chapter only covers the access methods that EvolutionDB 3.0 really has. It only describes the behavior found in the source code. The source files are btree2.c, hash_idx.c, hnsw.c, and FullText.c. EvolutionDB borrows some syntax from PostgreSQL and MySQL, but it does not always behave the same way. The HNSW graph lives only in memory. The full-text index is not kept up to date on DML. We point out these differences clearly. The full statement syntax and the option lists are on the CREATE INDEX reference page.

sections.heading: 10.1. Choosing an Access Method
sections.text: The four methods are not the same. Each one is keyed in a different way. Each one answers a different kind of query. The table below shows when to use each one. The rest of the chapter describes them in detail. MethodCreated withBest forCannot do B+ tree (default)CREATE INDEXEquality, range scans (<, <=, >, >=), ordered traversal, prefix lookups on secondary keys, HashCREATE INDEX … USING HASHSingle-value equality lookups on a secondary columnRange scans, ordered traversal HNSWCREATE INDEX … USING HNSWApproximate k-nearest-neighbor search over a VECTOR columnExact filtering, persistence across restart. In-memory only in v1 Full-textCREATE FULLTEXT INDEXWord/token matching in text columns via MATCH … AGAINSTPhrase/ranked search; auto-maintenance on DML Three of the four keep their pages in the same unified 4 KB page file as the table data. The Page Manager allocates these pages. The HNSW graph is different. It lives only in process memory.

sections.heading: 10.2. B+ Tree Indexes
sections.text: The B+ tree is EvolutionDB's default access method. It is also the most capable one. Every primary key uses it. CREATE INDEX builds it too, unless you ask for another method. Inside, it is a page-based B+ tree in btree2.c. Every node is one 4 KB page. Leaf nodes hold the real (key, RowID) entries. Internal nodes hold only separator keys and child page pointers. -- single-column B+ tree index CREATE INDEX idx_users_email ON users (email); -- enforce uniqueness while indexing CREATE UNIQUE INDEX idx_users_username ON users (username); -- composite index over two columns CREATE INDEX idx_orders_cust_date ON orders (customer_id, order_date);

sections.heading: 10.2.1. Node Layout and Capacity
sections.text: Each node page starts with the standard 16-byte page header. Next comes a 4-byte node header. The node header holds a 16-bit key count and a reserved 16-bit flags field. That leaves 4076 bytes of node space for entries. A leaf entry is [key_len:1][key:N][RowID:6]. An internal node mixes num_keys separator keys with num_keys + 1 child pointers. One byte holds each key length. An index key can be at most 255 bytes long. This limit is set by BT2_MAX_KEY_LEN. A longer key is rejected at insert time. Leaf pages are linked in both directions through the page header's prev_page and next_page fields. This sibling chain makes ordered scans and ranges cheap. The engine descends the tree once to find the starting leaf. After that, the cursor walks the chain. It does not descend again for each key.

sections.heading: 10.2.2. Key Comparison Is Numeric-Aware
sections.text: Keys are stored as length-prefixed byte strings. The comparison is not plain lexicographic. First the comparator tries to read both keys as 64-bit integers. If both read cleanly, it compares them as numbers. If that fails, it tries floating-point. If neither side is a number, it falls back to a byte-wise memcmp. On a common prefix, the shorter key comes first. Why this matters. Numeric-aware comparison means an integer key like 100 sorts after 20. That is the correct order. Both are stored as text. You do not get the lexicographic surprise of "100" < "20". There is a trade-off. Any value that looks like a number is ordered as a number. This happens no matter what type the column was declared as. The comparison buffers are limited to 256 bytes. So the numeric fast path only works on keys shorter than 255 bytes. Longer keys always use the lexicographic path.

sections.heading: 10.2.3. Secondary Keys and Composite Indexes
sections.text: A secondary B+ tree does not key on the column value alone. Many rows can share the same indexed value. To keep the entries unique, the engine builds a composite key. It joins the indexed value with the row's primary key. The join uses the ASCII unit separator \x1F. The key looks like indexed_value␟PK. So a lookup for one indexed value is a prefix scan. The engine sets the cursor at the first key whose prefix matches. Then it walks the leaf chain and collects entries until the prefix changes. A multi-column index works differently. It joins the column values with | into one key.

sections.heading: 10.2.4. Inserts, Splits, Deletes, and Compaction
sections.text: Inserts keep each leaf sorted. Sometimes an entry will not fit in a leaf's 4076 bytes. Then the leaf splits. The upper half of its entries move to a new page. The sibling chain is relinked. The first key of the new page is pushed up to the parent as a separator. A split can cascade up the tree. A split at the root creates a new root one level taller. So the tree grows from the leaves upward and stays balanced. Deletes use lazy tombstones. bt2_delete finds the entry and overwrites its RowID with {0,0}. It does not remove the entry from the page and it does not rebalance. This keeps the tree stable on purpose. The root never moves and no page is freed. So the system catalog and hot-path callers can safely hold leaf-page hints and live cursors. A later insert of the same key reuses a tombstoned slot. Searches and cursor scans skip tombstones. Reclaiming the physical space is a separate offline pass called bt2_compact. RECLAIM runs it. It drops tombstones. Then it borrows or merges entries across underflowing sibling nodes. The min-fill heuristic is half a page. It frees empty pages and collapses a single-child root. It can move the root. So it must run under the global DML lock. No concurrent reader may hold page hints or cursors. The RECLAIM path sets up exactly these conditions. Note. Compaction uses a min-fill target of half of NODE_CAPACITY. This is only a space-usage heuristic. It is not a correctness rule. There is just one hard rule. A node's bytes must never go over the 4076-byte capacity. Both the borrow path and the merge path keep this rule. Between compactions, a node may sit below min-fill, and that is fine.

sections.heading: 10.3. Hash Indexes
sections.text: A hash index uses USING HASH. It gives up the B+ tree's ordering and gives you direct equality lookup instead. It is built as static hashing with overflow chains in hash_idx.c. A single directory page holds a fixed array of bucket pointers. Each non-empty bucket is a chain of bucket pages packed with entries. -- equality-only index for token lookups CREATE INDEX idx_sessions_token ON sessions USING HASH (token);

sections.heading: 10.3.1. Buckets, Hashing, and Overflow
sections.text: Bucket routing uses the FNV-1a hash function. The hash is taken modulo the bucket count. A new index starts with 64 buckets by default. This default is set by HASH_DEFAULT_BUCKETS. The bucket count is capped at 1017 buckets. This cap is set by HASH_MAX_BUCKETS. It lets the whole bucket array fit in one 4 KB directory page. Bucket pages are allocated lazily. A bucket holds no page at all until its first entry arrives. An entry is [key_len:2][key:N][RowID:6]. Keys are limited to 255 bytes. This limit is set by HASH_MAX_KEY_LEN. When a bucket page fills up, a new overflow page is allocated. It is linked into the chain through the page header. Like a secondary B+ tree, a hash entry stores the composite key indexed_value␟PK. Only the part before the \x1F separator is hashed for bucket routing. So every row that shares an indexed value lands in the same bucket. An equality lookup hashes just the search value. It walks that one bucket chain. It returns every entry whose key begins with indexed_value␟.

sections.heading: 10.3.2. When a Hash Index Applies
sections.text: A hash index answers exactly one kind of query. That query is equality on the indexed value. The bucket layout has no ordering in it. So it cannot serve a range predicate, an ORDER BY, or a prefix-of-a-prefix search. To delete an entry, the engine shifts the other entries in its bucket page down over it. The bucket count is fixed. There is no rehash and no split. If you need ordering or ranges, use the default B+ tree instead. Static, not extendible. The bucket count is chosen at create time. It never changes after that. This version has no dynamic resizing and no extendible-hashing split. When a table grows far past the bucket count, lookups slow down. They move from near-constant time toward a per-bucket chain walk. Size the index for the table. If the cardinality is unknown or growing, use a B+ tree instead.

sections.heading: 10.4. HNSW Vector Indexes
sections.text: An HNSW index uses USING HNSW. It speeds up approximate nearest-neighbor search, also called ANN search, over a VECTOR(dim) column. It finds the rows whose embedding is closest to a query vector under a chosen distance metric. You build it with CREATE INDEX. It is not like the other three methods. It does not live in the page file. The graph and its dense vector store are held only in process memory, in hnsw.c. -- build an HNSW index, choosing the distance metric via opclass CREATE INDEX idx_docs_embedding ON docs USING HNSW (embedding vector_l2_ops) WITH (m = 32, ef_construction = 128);

sections.heading: 10.4.1. How the Graph Is Built
sections.text: At CREATE INDEX time the engine scans the table once. It decodes the vector column's float4 payload straight from each live tuple. No text-field buffer limits the dimension, so 1024-dimension embeddings work. It appends each (primary key, vector) pair to a dense, row-major store. Then it builds a multi-layer navigable graph over that store. Each node gets a random top level. Neighbor lists are filled by greedy beam search. The search uses the configured ef_construction candidate width. It keeps up to m links per node per level. At the base layer it keeps 2 × m links. Graphs are tracked in a small static table keyed by (table_id, index_name). There is a hard cap of 64 concurrent graphs. This cap is set by HNSW_MAX_GRAPHS. Primary keys can be up to 256 bytes. In-memory only. Rebuild required. In v1 the HNSW graph is not persisted. It is also not rebuilt automatically at server startup. After a restart, an existing HNSW index must be rebuilt with a fresh CREATE INDEX or a reindex. The DDL layer does record enough catalog metadata for automatic replay later. This metadata covers the column, the distance kind, m, and ef_construction. DROP INDEX frees the graph right away.

sections.heading: 10.4.2. Distance Metrics (opclasses)
sections.text: You write the opclass after the column. It picks the distance function the graph uses. The graph uses it for both build and search. EvolutionDB has four opclasses. vector_cosine_ops (default)Cosine distance, 1 − cos(a, b). A zero-norm vector on either side gives distance 1.0. This is the default when you give no opclass. It is also the fallback for any opclass the engine does not know. vector_l2_opsEuclidean (L2) distance. This is the straight-line distance between the two vectors. vector_ip_opsNegative inner product, −a·b. Smaller means nearer. This ordering matches the other metrics. vector_inner_ops works as a synonym. vector_l1_opsManhattan (L1) distance. This is the sum of the absolute per-component differences.

sections.heading: 10.4.3. Searching the Index
sections.text: You run vector search from SQL using helper functions and distance operators. The expression engine evaluates them. hnsw_knn returns a comma-separated list of the k nearest primary keys. They are ordered by distance from low to high. The value k defaults to 10 and is capped at 256. -- k nearest neighbors of a query vector, by primary key SELECT hnsw_knn('docs', 'idx_docs_embedding', '[0.12, 0.04, ...]', 5); -- the distance functions / operators rank directly in a query SELECT id, l2_distance(embedding, '[0.12, 0.04, ...]') AS dist FROM docs ORDER BY embedding <-> '[0.12, 0.04, ...]' LIMIT 5; The distance expressions are cosine_distance(a, b), l2_distance(a, b), inner_product(a, b), and l1_distance(a, b). There are also three operators. <-> is L2. <#> is negative inner product. <=> is cosine, and you can use it in ORDER BY emb <=> '[…]'. You can write a query vector in two ways. One way is a bracketed literal '[f1, f2, …]'. The other way is the client's b64i8:<base64> quantized form. This form is a float32 scale followed by an int8 array. It is more compact. It keeps a 1024-dimension query under the statement size cap. Inside, search picks one of two paths under the same interface. For a graph large enough to help, it does a multi-layer greedy descent. Then it does a base-layer beam search of width ef_search. The default width is 64. It is widened to at least k when needed. For very small graphs the engine takes the other path. It also takes it if graph construction hit an allocation failure. That path is an exact brute-force top-k scan over the dense store. The switch is silent. So results are always correct. The graph only changes how fast they come back.

sections.heading: 10.4.4. Hybrid Search (Vector + Filter)
sections.text: hnsw_filter_knn joins vector ranking with one equality predicate over another column. You supply the predicate as a 'col=val' spec. You can also write 'col=val:pre' or ':post' to force a strategy. SELECT hnsw_filter_knn('docs', 'idx_docs_embedding', '[0.12, ...]', 5, 'lang=en'); -- report which strategy the planner chose and the filter selectivity SELECT hnsw_hybrid_explain('docs', 'idx_docs_embedding', '[0.12, ...]', 5, 'lang=en'); In auto mode the engine measures the filter's selectivity. Selectivity is the number of matching rows divided by the total rows. Then it chooses a strategy. When fewer than 10% of rows match, it pre-filters. It ranks only the matching rows. Otherwise it post-filters. It ranks a wider, oversampled candidate set and then drops the non-matches. An empty filter falls back to plain hnsw_knn. In this version the filter predicate can only be equality.

sections.heading: 10.5. Full-Text Indexes
sections.text: A full-text index uses CREATE FULLTEXT INDEX. It supports word matching inside a text column. It is built as an inverted index on top of a B+ tree, in FullText.h. The text is tokenized. One entry is inserted per token. Each entry is keyed <word>␟<PK>, using the \x1F separator again. The value is the row's heap RowID. A query tokenizes the search string in the same way. Then it looks up each token's word prefix. -- build the inverted index over a text column CREATE FULLTEXT INDEX idx_articles_body ON articles (body); -- query it with MATCH ... AGAINST SELECT id, title FROM articles WHERE MATCH(body) AGAINST('storage engine');

sections.heading: 10.5.1. The Tokenizer
sections.text: The same tokenizer, ft_tokenize, runs at both build time and query time. So the two see the same token stream. Its rules are listed below. Word boundariesA token is a run of ASCII alphanumerics. Any byte ≥ 0x80 counts as part of a word. This keeps multi-byte UTF-8 letters attached to their words, such as Turkish characters. Any other byte ends the current token. LowercasingASCII letters are lowercased, so matching is case-insensitive. Non-ASCII bytes pass through unchanged. This is because tolower on raw UTF-8 bytes is undefined. So in this version, queries against non-ASCII text match case-sensitively. Turkish text is one example. A proper Unicode case-fold is future work. Short-token filterTokens shorter than 2 characters are dropped. Stop wordsA small built-in English stop-word list is removed. It holds 28 words, such as a, an, and, the, of, and to. LimitsA token is capped at 64 bytes. This limit is set by FT_MAX_TOKEN_LEN. Longer tokens are truncated. At most 256 tokens are taken from a single field. This limit is set by FT_MAX_TOKENS. Note. Not auto-maintained. In this version the full-text index is not updated automatically on INSERT, UPDATE, or DELETE. After bulk DML, drop it and re-create it with CREATE FULLTEXT INDEX to refresh the token entries. There is no stemming and no phrase search. There is no relevance ranking beyond whether a token is present. Matching uses the shared, normalized token stream described above.

sections.heading: 10.6. Compatibility
sections.text: In the SQL standard, indexing is implementation-defined. So none of these statements are part of standard SQL. EvolutionDB's surface follows PostgreSQL and MySQL closely. There are some clear differences that are worth knowing. Access methods. EvolutionDB offers the default B+ tree, USING HASH, and USING HNSW. It does not support PostgreSQL's gin, gist, spgist, and brin method names. It also does not support a generic USING method clause. Hash indexes are static. PostgreSQL's hash index grows on its own. EvolutionDB's does not. The bucket count is fixed at create time. The default is 64 and the max is 1017. It uses overflow chains. There is no rehash and no extendible split. HNSW. The opclass names vector_cosine_ops, vector_l2_ops, vector_ip_ops, and vector_l1_ops, plus the m and ef_construction build parameters, match the pgvector extension. EvolutionDB's graph is in-memory only. It is not auto-rebuilt at startup. You must use a single VECTOR column. Composite HNSW indexes are not supported. Full-text. EvolutionDB uses a dedicated MySQL-style CREATE FULLTEXT INDEX statement with MATCH … AGAINST queries. It does not use PostgreSQL's tsvector and GIN model. It is not auto-maintained on DML. It offers no stemming, no phrase search, and no ranked relevance. Unsupported B+ tree and hash clauses. On non-HNSW indexes, these clauses are all rejected. They are ASC/DESC, NULLS FIRST/LAST, COLLATE, per-column opclasses, INCLUDE, partial-index WHERE, TABLESPACE, and storage-parameter WITH (…).

sections.heading: 10.7. See Also
sections.text: CREATE INDEX. The full syntax synopsis, the parameters, and the complete option grammar. DROP INDEX. How to remove an index and free an HNSW graph. RECLAIM. The offline pass that compacts B+ tree tombstones. CREATE TABLE. How to declare primary keys and columns, including VECTOR. SELECT. The queries that indexes speed up.

sections.heading: Introduction
sections.text: 19. Scheduled Jobs EvolutionDB keeps named, cron-scheduled SQL statements in the system catalog. It checks each five-field cron pattern against the clock once per background tick. A scheduled job is a saved catalog object. It links a name to a cron expression and a single SQL statement. You create jobs with CREATE JOB. You pause or resume them with ALTER JOB. You remove them with DROP JOB. You list them with SHOW JOBS. The same background worker thread that runs auto-RECLAIM also checks the jobs. There is no separate scheduler daemon or outside process to set up. Maturity (3.0). The DDL commands, catalog storage, cron matching, the ENABLE/DISABLE flag, and SHOW JOBS are all finished. When a job fires, the server records the match. It stamps last_run_unix and writes a line to the server log. It does not yet run the stored SQL. Running the SQL is planned for a later release. See §19.6 for the exact current behavior.

sections.heading: 19.1. Overview
sections.text: Each job holds exactly five pieces of state. They all live in the CAT_SYS_SCHEDULED_JOBS system catalog. There is one B+ tree row per job. name The job name. Job names are global. They are not schema-qualified, and they must be unique. The maximum length is 127 characters. schedule (cron expression) A five-field, space-separated cron pattern. It is stored as a plain string. The maximum length is 63 characters. See §19.5. sql The single SQL statement the job will run. It is stored exactly as given. The maximum length is 1023 characters. Any single or double quotes around it are removed when it is stored. enabled A boolean. New jobs are enabled. ALTER JOB … DISABLE pauses the job. ALTER JOB … ENABLE starts it again. last_run_unix A 64-bit Unix timestamp. It marks the most recent tick where the job's pattern matched. It is 0 if the job has never matched. It is used for introspection. It also stops the same minute from matching twice. Not a PostgreSQL feature. Core PostgreSQL has no CREATE JOB statement and no built-in scheduler. Recurring tasks are usually handled by the outside pg_cron extension or by the host's cron or systemd. The SQL standard does not define scheduled jobs at all. EvolutionDB's CREATE JOB … ON SCHEDULE … DO syntax looks most like MySQL's CREATE EVENT. The behavior, the cron dialect, and the limits below are all specific to EvolutionDB. Not the same as the agent's daily brief. CREATE JOB schedules in-database SQL inside the engine catalog. Application-level recurring tasks belong somewhere else. EvolutionAgent's morning daily brief, for example, is not a SQL job and has no CREATE JOB behind it. It runs in the MCP server's own background scheduler (a job named brief_delivery, by default daily at 07:30 UTC), which collects and renders the brief and queues it to the user. It is opt-in and self-hosted. For how that scheduler works and how to enable it, see the EvolutionAgent developer docs.

sections.heading: 19.2. Creating a job
sections.text: See the CREATE JOB reference page for the full command synopsis. CREATE JOB [ IF EXISTS ] name ON SCHEDULE '<min> <hour> <dom> <mon> <dow>' DO '<sql>'; Both the cron expression and the SQL body come in as string literals. The engine removes the quotes around them before it stores them. A valid statement runs four checks in order. The name must be present. The cron expression must be valid. See §19.5. The DO body must not be empty. The name must not already be in use. -- Fire every day at 03:00 (UTC - see the note on time zones below) CREATE JOB nightly_rollup ON SCHEDULE '0 3 * * *' DO 'INSERT INTO daily_totals SELECT today(), count(*) FROM orders'; -- Fire at the top of every hour CREATE JOB hourly_ping ON SCHEDULE '0 * * * *' DO 'UPDATE heartbeat SET ts = now()';

sections.heading: 19.2.1. The IF EXISTS guard
sections.text: The optional IF EXISTS clause makes the create safe to repeat. If a job with that name already exists, the statement does nothing and still succeeds. It does not raise an error. The keyword here is IF EXISTS, not IF NOT EXISTS. It guards against a name that is already there. CREATE JOB IF EXISTS nightly_rollup ON SCHEDULE '0 3 * * *' DO 'INSERT INTO daily_totals SELECT today(), count(*) FROM orders'; -- second run with the same name: no error, no change Without the guard, creating a job with a name that is already taken raises SQLSTATE 42710 (duplicate object).

sections.heading: 19.3. Pausing and resuming a job
sections.text: ALTER JOB flips the enabled flag. There is no way to change a job's cron expression or SQL body in place. To change those, drop the job and create it again. ALTER JOB name { ENABLE | DISABLE }; ALTER JOB hourly_ping DISABLE; -- pause: skipped on every tick ALTER JOB hourly_ping ENABLE; -- resume Altering a job that does not exist raises SQLSTATE 42704 (undefined object). A disabled job stays in the catalog. It still shows up in SHOW JOBS. The scheduler skips it when it walks the job list.

sections.heading: 19.4. Dropping a job
sections.text: DROP JOB [ IF EXISTS ] name; DROP JOB hourly_ping; DROP JOB IF EXISTS maybe_gone; -- no error if it isn't there Dropping a job that does not exist raises SQLSTATE 42704. If you add IF EXISTS, the statement does nothing instead.

sections.heading: 19.5. Cron expression dialect
sections.text: EvolutionDB 3.0 uses a very small cron dialect on purpose. An expression has exactly five fields. They are separated by spaces or tabs. PositionFieldAllowed rangeNotes 1minute0–59 2hour0–23 3day of month1–31 4month1–12 5day of week0–7both 0 and 7 mean Sunday, as in POSIX Each field has one of exactly two forms. * A wildcard. It matches every value of that field. a single non-negative integer A fixed number. It matches only when the matching clock field is equal to it. That is the whole grammar. The forms below are common in standard cron. They are not supported in 3.0. They cause the expression to be rejected. Ranges, e.g. 1-5 Comma lists, e.g. 1,15,30 Step values, e.g. */5 Named months or weekdays, e.g. JAN, MON This handles the common cases. It covers every minute, every hour, and a fixed time each day. It does this without a full cron parser. The catalog stores the whole string as written. A later release can add a richer evaluator with no data migration. -- Accepted '* * * * *' -- every minute '30 * * * *' -- at minute 30 of every hour '0 0 1 * *' -- midnight on the 1st of every month '0 9 * * 0' -- 09:00 on Sundays -- Rejected → SQLSTATE 22023 '*/5 * * * *' -- step values unsupported '0 9-17 * * *' -- ranges unsupported '0 0 * * MON' -- named weekdays unsupported '0 0 * *' -- only four fields Time zone is UTC. The cron pattern is matched against UTC. It is not matched against the server's local time. A job set to 0 3 * * * fires when the UTC clock reads 03:00. The host time zone does not matter. Standard cron matches local time. EvolutionDB does not. Add any offset right into the hour field.

sections.heading: 19.6. How jobs are evaluated and fired
sections.text: There is no separate scheduler process. The background worker that runs auto-RECLAIM also calls the scheduler on each tick. The tick interval is 30 seconds by default. You can lower it to as little as 1 second with the EVOSQL_AUTO_RECLAIM_INTERVAL_SEC environment variable. The cron dialect's smallest step is one minute. So ticks shorter than a minute do not cause extra firings. On each tick the scheduler walks every job. It fires a job only when all of these are true. the job is enabled; its cron pattern matches the current UTC minute; and its last_run_unix is older than the start of the current minute. This stops a job from firing twice on two ticks inside the same minute. When a job fires, the scheduler stamps last_run_unix with the start of the current minute. It saves this to the catalog. It also writes a line to the server log to note the match. In 3.0 it does not run the stored SQL. The hooks to send the job's SQL through the engine are in place. Running any SQL from the background thread is blocked until that path is safe. That work is planned for a later release. What this means in practice. Today you can create, save, enable, disable, and watch scheduled jobs. You can confirm a schedule "ticked" by watching last_run_unix move forward in SHOW JOBS. Do not rely on a job's DO statement to change data yet. The statement is saved but not run until SQL execution is wired up.

sections.heading: 19.7. Inspecting jobs
sections.text: SHOW JOBS returns one row per job. It lists enabled and disabled jobs alike. See the SHOW reference page. SHOW JOBS; The result set has five columns. ColumnTypeMeaning nametextJob name scheduletextThe stored cron expression sqltextThe configured DO statement enabledbooleantrue if active, false if disabled last_run_unixbigintUnix timestamp of the most recent match, or 0 if never matched A non-zero last_run_unix that moves forward over time shows the scheduler is matching the pattern. This is the best way to check that a schedule fires.

sections.heading: 19.8. Error codes
sections.text: Job statements report failures through SQLSTATE. SQLSTATEConditionRaised by 42601Syntax error. The job name is missing, or the DO body is emptyCREATE JOB 22023Invalid cron expression (wrong field count, unsupported syntax, or out-of-range value)CREATE JOB 42710A job with that name already existsCREATE JOB (without IF EXISTS) 42704The named job does not existDROP JOB / ALTER JOB (without IF EXISTS) Notes and limitations (3.0). A job's DO SQL is recorded but not run. Only the match is logged, and last_run_unix is stamped. Treat jobs as schedulable metadata until execution lands. Cron matching uses UTC, not local time. Each cron field allows only * or a single integer. There are no ranges, lists, steps, or names. Job names are global. They are not schema-qualified, and they must be unique across the server. There is no ALTER JOB form to rewrite the schedule or the SQL. Drop the job and create it again to change them. The scheduler walks at most 64 jobs per tick. Evaluation runs on the auto-RECLAIM worker thread. If that worker is not started, no job is ever evaluated.

sections.heading: Introduction
sections.text: 13. JSON EvolutionDB stores JSON documents as text. It checks each document when you write it. It gives you a MySQL-compatible set of functions. It also gives you the -> and ->> path operators to read, change, and search documents. This chapter describes the JSON data type. It also describes every JSON operation that EvolutionDB supports. The JSON support is small and self-contained. It uses little memory and is built on a recursive-descent parser. It follows the MySQL function set and the MySQL $.path expression syntax. It does not follow the PostgreSQL json/jsonb operator set. It does not follow the SQL/JSON standard. When the behaviour differs from those systems, this chapter says so clearly. At a glance. EvolutionDB JSON is text-backed. A JSON column holds the original document bytes. EvolutionDB checks the bytes when you write them. There is no binary or decomposed storage form. There is no path indexing. You cannot change a document in place. The transformation functions return a new string. If you have used MySQL's JSON functions, this will feel familiar. If you have used PostgreSQL jsonb, the operators here are different.

sections.heading: 13.1. The JSON Data Type
sections.text: EvolutionDB has one JSON type. You can write it as JSON or JSONB. The parser accepts both spellings. Both map to the same type. There is no separate binary form. So JSONB is only an alias. It exists so tools that emit JSONB still work. PropertyValue Type namesJSON, JSONB (alias) Internal type code230000 (type family 23) On-disk formOriginal JSON text, stored in the binary tuple format like any variable-length value ValidationDocument is parsed and rejected if not well-formed JSON (on write) Maximum nesting64 levels (JSON_MAX_DEPTH) EvolutionDB checks a value before it goes into a JSON column. It uses the same parser as JSON_VALID. An invalid document is rejected at write time. CREATE TABLE events ( id INT PRIMARY KEY, payload JSON ); -- accepted: well-formed object INSERT INTO events VALUES (1, '{"user":"ada","tags":["x","y"],"active":true}'); -- rejected: trailing comma is not valid JSON INSERT INTO events VALUES (2, '{"user":"ada",}'); -- ERROR: invalid JSON: '{"user":"ada",}' (SQLSTATE data_exception) See CREATE TABLE for column declaration and INSERT for write semantics. Validation grammar. The parser accepts standard JSON. This means objects, arrays, and strings. Strings may use the escapes \" \\ \/ \b \f \n \r \t and \uXXXX. It also accepts numbers. A number may have an optional sign, an integer part, a fraction, and an exponent. It accepts the literals true, false, and null. It rejects integers with a leading zero. It rejects control characters inside strings. It rejects documents deeper than 64 levels. PostgreSQL jsonb removes duplicate object keys and reorders keys. EvolutionDB does not do this. It stores the document exactly as written. So key order and whitespace are kept.

sections.heading: 13.2. Path Expressions
sections.text: Some functions and operators point to a place inside a document. They use MySQL-style path expressions. A path starts with $. This is the document root. After it come zero or more steps. .keyReads a member of an object by its key name. [n]Reads an element of an array by its index. The index starts at zero. You can chain steps to any depth. For example $.user.name, $.tags[0], or $.items[2].sku. A bare $ means the whole document. -- given payload = {"user":"ada","tags":["x","y"],"address":{"city":"berlin"}} SELECT JSON_EXTRACT(payload, '$.user') FROM events; -- "ada" SELECT JSON_EXTRACT(payload, '$.tags[1]') FROM events; -- "y" SELECT JSON_EXTRACT(payload, '$.address.city') FROM events; -- "berlin" SELECT JSON_EXTRACT(payload, '$') FROM events; -- whole document Supported subset. Only two kinds of step work here. The first is a key step with .key. The second is a numeric index step with [n]. Some features are not supported. Wildcards like $.* and $[*] do not work. The recursive descent operator $** does not work. Quoted member names like $."weird key" do not work. Negative indexes and range indexes do not work. An index step needs at least one digit. A key step needs a name that is not empty. If not, the path is rejected. Key names are matched against an internal 256-byte buffer. Very long keys are cut short for the comparison.

sections.heading: 13.3. The -> and ->> Operators
sections.text: The arrow operators are a short way to extract a value. The left side is a JSON expression. The right side is a path. They are left-associative. json -> pathExtracts the value at path and keeps its JSON form. A string result keeps its quotes. json ->> pathExtracts the value at path as text. A JSON string result loses its quotes. A JSON null becomes SQL NULL. -- payload = {"user":"ada","age":31} SELECT payload -> '$.user' FROM events; -- "ada" (quoted JSON string) SELECT payload ->> '$.user' FROM events; -- ada (plain text) SELECT payload -> '$.age' FROM events; -- 31 Both operators return SQL NULL in two cases. The first is when either side is NULL. The second is when the path matches nothing in the document. The right side uses the same $.path syntax as the functions above. A bare key with no leading $ is also accepted. It is treated as a single object-key step. Contrast with PostgreSQL. In PostgreSQL, -> takes a single key or array index. That is one text value or one integer. The #> and #>> operators take a path array. The @>, ?, and jsonb_path_* operators complete the set. EvolutionDB is different. Its -> and ->> accept a whole MySQL $.path on the right. It has no #>, @>, or ? operators. For containment and existence checks, use the JSON_CONTAINS and JSON_CONTAINS_PATH functions instead.

sections.heading: 13.4. Inspection Functions
sections.text: These functions read a document. They do not change it. JSON_VALID(doc)Returns 1 if doc is well-formed JSON. Returns 0 if it is not. This is the same check used when you write to a JSON column. JSON_TYPE(doc)Returns the type of the top-level value. It is one of OBJECT, ARRAY, STRING, BOOLEAN, NULL, INTEGER, or DOUBLE. A number with ., e, or E is reported as DOUBLE. Any other number is reported as INTEGER. JSON_LENGTH(doc)For an object, this is the number of keys. For an array, it is the number of elements. For a scalar, it is 1. JSON_DEPTH(doc)The nesting depth. A scalar or an empty container has depth 1. Each nested object or array adds one level. JSON_KEYS(doc)Returns a JSON array of the top-level object's key names. The names keep their quotes. For example ["user","tags"]. This works only for objects. JSON_EXTRACT(doc, path)Returns the value at path in its JSON form. Returns NULL if the path matches nothing. This is the same as the -> operator. -- payload = {"user":"ada","tags":["x","y"],"meta":{"v":1.5}} SELECT JSON_VALID(payload) FROM events; -- 1 SELECT JSON_TYPE(payload) FROM events; -- OBJECT SELECT JSON_LENGTH(payload) FROM events; -- 3 SELECT JSON_DEPTH(payload) FROM events; -- 3 SELECT JSON_KEYS(payload) FROM events; -- ["user","tags","meta"] SELECT JSON_TYPE('1.5') FROM events; -- DOUBLE SELECT JSON_TYPE('42') FROM events; -- INTEGER

sections.heading: 13.5. Construction and Formatting Functions
sections.text: JSON_OBJECT(k1, v1[, k2, v2])Builds a JSON object from key and value pairs. It takes one or two pairs. A value that is itself valid JSON is kept as-is. Any other value is quoted as a JSON string. JSON_ARRAY(v1[, v2[, v3]])Builds a JSON array from one to three values. It uses the same rule for each element. Valid JSON is kept as-is. Anything else is quoted as a string. JSON_QUOTE(str)Wraps str in double quotes. It escapes special characters. These are ", \, newline, carriage return, tab, and control characters as \uXXXX. The result is a JSON string literal. JSON_UNQUOTE(doc)The reverse of JSON_QUOTE. If doc is a quoted JSON string, it removes the quotes and unescapes the text. If not, it returns the input unchanged. JSON_PRETTY(doc)Renders the document again with two-space indentation and newlines. If the input cannot be parsed, it returns the original text unchanged. SELECT JSON_OBJECT('name', 'ada', 'age', '31'); -- {"name":"ada","age":31} (31 is valid JSON, embedded; "ada" quoted) SELECT JSON_ARRAY('1', 'two', 'true'); -- [1,"two",true] SELECT JSON_QUOTE('a"b'); -- "a\"b" SELECT JSON_UNQUOTE('"a\nb"'); -- a<newline>b Argument-count limits. JSON_OBJECT takes one or two key and value pairs. A four-argument call is merged into one object. JSON_ARRAY takes one, two, or three elements. These argument counts are fixed in the grammar. They are not the variadic forms that MySQL and PostgreSQL have. To build larger objects or arrays, you must put the JSON text together in another way.

sections.heading: 13.6. Modification, Containment, and Search
sections.text: The modification functions return a new document. They never change stored data in place. Use them inside an UPDATE to save a change. JSON_SET(doc, path, val)Adds the key if it is missing. Replaces its value if the key is there. JSON_INSERT(doc, path, val)Adds the key only if it does not already exist. It leaves existing keys alone. JSON_REPLACE(doc, path, val)Updates the key only if it already exists. It does not add missing keys. JSON_REMOVE(doc, path)Deletes the key at path. JSON_CONTAINS(target, candidate)Returns 1 if candidate is inside target. The match can be scalar equality. It can be a subset of object keys and values. It can be array-element membership, checked recursively. Otherwise it returns 0. JSON_CONTAINS_PATH(doc, mode, path)Returns 1 if the path exists in doc. mode is 'all' or 'one' and is case-insensitive. Any value other than 'all' is treated as 'one'. JSON_SEARCH(doc, mode, str)Searches string values for an exact match of str. It returns the matching paths as JSON path strings. For example "$.user". With mode 'all' it returns every match, separated by commas. Any other mode stops at the first match. It returns NULL when nothing matches. -- payload = {"user":"ada","age":31} UPDATE events SET payload = JSON_SET(payload, '$.age', '32') WHERE id = 1; -- {"user":"ada","age":32} UPDATE events SET payload = JSON_REMOVE(payload, '$.age') WHERE id = 1; -- {"user":"ada"} SELECT JSON_CONTAINS('{"a":1,"b":2}', '{"a":1}'); -- 1 SELECT JSON_CONTAINS_PATH('{"a":1}', 'one', '$.a'); -- 1 SELECT JSON_SEARCH('{"u":"ada","v":"bob"}', 'one', 'ada'); -- "$.u" Single-key limitation on modification. JSON_SET, JSON_INSERT, JSON_REPLACE, and JSON_REMOVE work on top-level object keys only. The document must be an object. The path must be a single $.key step. Nested paths like $.a.b are rejected. Array indexes like $.a[0] are rejected. This is a planned simplification. The MySQL versions accept any path and many path and value pairs. JSON_SEARCH matches only string values. It skips numbers, booleans, and null. It needs an exact, full-string match. It does not use a LIKE pattern.

sections.heading: 13.7. Error Reference
sections.text: SituationBehaviour Invalid JSON written to a JSON columnThe write is rejected with invalid JSON: '…'. SQLSTATE is data_exception Path does not match any valueJSON_EXTRACT, ->, and ->> return NULL Malformed path expressionThe function returns NULL JSON_SET family with a nested path, an array index, or a non-object documentReturns NULL. The operation is not applied JSON null extracted with ->> or JSON_EXTRACT(text)Becomes SQL NULL Document deeper than 64 levelsTreated as invalid JSON

sections.heading: 13.8. Notes and Limitations
sections.text: Implementation notes. Text storage, not binary. JSON and JSONB are the same text-backed type. There is no decomposed or indexed binary form. So whitespace, key order, and duplicate keys are kept exactly as written. No JSON indexing. A query that filters on ->> or JSON_EXTRACT does a full scan. There are no expression or path indexes over JSON. Fixed buffers. JSON expression evaluation uses fixed-size buffers. A document can be up to about 4 KB per evaluation step. A key can be up to 256 bytes. The output is limited by the column or result buffer. Very large documents or values may be cut short. MySQL flavour, not PostgreSQL or SQL/JSON. The function names, the $.path syntax, and the JSON_TYPE labels follow MySQL. There is no jsonb. There are no @>, ?, or #> operators. There is no JSON_TABLE. There are no SQL/JSON JSON_VALUE or JSON_QUERY constructs. Modification is shallow. You can only set, insert, replace, or remove single top-level object keys. Related reference pages are CREATE TABLE, INSERT, UPDATE, and SELECT.

sections.heading: Introduction
sections.text: 15. Memory & Entity Stores EvolutionDB gives you two built-in stores for agent memory. The first is the memory store. It is a namespaced JSON key/value table. Each row can hold an embedding, and you can run a semantic SEARCH over it. The second is the entity store. It keeps one summary row per key and counts how often each key is mentioned. Both are simple SQL statements. They compile down to ordinary tables, B+ tree primary keys, MVCC tuples, and the engine's existing INSERT/SELECT/UPDATE/DELETE code.

sections.heading: 15.1. Overview
sections.text: A memory store and an entity store are not new storage engines. Each one is a named catalog object bound to a single regular backing table. The MEMORY ... and ENTITY ... statements are shortcuts. They build the right tuple and send it through the same code path a hand-written DML statement would use. Everything the engine already does to a normal table still works the same way. That includes primary-key indexing, MVCC visibility, row-level security policies, foreign-key cascades, and triggers. The two stores answer different questions. Memory store "Give me the value I filed under namespace + key." Or "find the rows whose embedding is closest to this query vector." It is a namespaced JSON KV store. Each row can also carry one VECTOR(N) column for semantic recall. See CREATE MEMORY STORE. Entity store "What do I know about this entity, and how often has it come up?" It is a per-key table. Each row holds a summary, an optional facts blob, an auto-incrementing mention_count, and a last_seen timestamp. See CREATE ENTITY STORE. Relationship to PostgreSQL. These statements are EvolutionDB extensions. They are not part of the SQL standard and have no PostgreSQL equivalent. In PostgreSQL you would model the same data with your own table, the pgvector extension, and upsert logic in your application. EvolutionDB puts the table layout, the upsert behavior, and the distance ranking into single statements. The vector distance itself reuses the engine's native operators. Those are described in the Vectors chapter.

sections.heading: 15.2. Creating a memory store
sections.text: You create a memory store with CREATE MEMORY STORE. The optional WITH list says whether the store carries embeddings. It also says which distance metric SEARCH should use. CREATE MEMORY STORE name [ WITH ( embedding_dim = N [, distance = 'cosine' ] ) ] embedding_dim = N The size of the per-row embedding. When N > 0, the backing table gets a VECTOR(N) column and the store supports MEMORY SEARCH. When you leave it out, the default is 0. In that case no embedding column is created at all. The store is then a plain JSON KV table. Running SEARCH against it is an error. distance = '...' The distance metric. It is saved in the catalog and used later by SEARCH. The accepted literals are listed in §15.5. The default is cosine. Any literal the engine does not recognize quietly falls back to cosine. -- semantic store with 1536-d embeddings, L2 distance CREATE MEMORY STORE agent_mem WITH (embedding_dim = 1536, distance = 'l2'); -- plain namespaced JSON KV store, no vectors CREATE MEMORY STORE scratch; The statement also supports CREATE MEMORY STORE IF NOT EXISTS name. If a store with that name already exists, this returns success. It does not raise 42710.

sections.heading: 15.2.1. The backing table
sections.text: When you create a memory store named foo, the engine creates a regular table named mem_foo in the current schema. It has the columns below. The embedding column is present only when embedding_dim > 0. ColumnTypeNotes mem_namespaceVARCHAR(255)NOT NULL mem_keyVARCHAR(255)NOT NULL pkVARCHAR(512)PRIMARY KEY, composite of namespace and key mem_valueJSONnullable embeddingVECTOR(N)only if embedding_dim > 0; nullable created_atTIMESTAMPset at PUT time, UTC ttl_atTIMESTAMPnullable; written NULL by every PUT (reserved) The primary key is a single derived column. It is not a two-column composite. The namespace and the key are joined into one string, separated by the ASCII Record Separator byte 0x1E. -- pk is: namespace + 0x1E + key -- 0x1E avoids the engine's internal field/row separators (0x1F, 0x02) The PK is an indexed VARCHAR. A point lookup by namespace and key is O(log n) through the B+ tree. The mem_namespace and mem_key columns also stay queryable on their own. You can use them in ordinary SQL against mem_foo.

sections.heading: 15.3. Writing memories, MEMORY PUT
sections.text: MEMORY PUT does an upsert by primary key. The full reference is at MEMORY PUT. MEMORY PUT INTO name VALUES (ns, key, value [, embedding]) Three positional values are required. They are the namespace, the key, and the JSON value. A fourth optional value gives the embedding. The embedding only matters for a store created with embedding_dim > 0. Each value may be a quoted string literal or NULL. -- KV-only write MEMORY PUT INTO scratch VALUES ('user:42', 'lang', '{"locale":"tr-TR"}'); -- write with an embedding (store created WITH embedding_dim = 4) MEMORY PUT INTO agent_mem VALUES ('user:42', 'fact:1', '{"text":"prefers dark mode"}', '[0.10, 0.22, 0.07, 0.91]'); The embedding literal is a list of floats in brackets, separated by commas. This is the same text form the engine accepts for VECTOR columns elsewhere. On a semantic store you can leave out the embedding or pass NULL. The row is still stored and you can still get it by key. It just will not give a meaningful SEARCH score. Upsert is atomic and crash-safe. PUT does not delete and then insert. It inserts first. If there is a primary-key collision, the PK is pointed at the new tuple and the old one is dropped. New comes first, the swap comes last. A failed re-insert can never leave the row in a deleted state. The created_at timestamp is written again on every PUT. The value shows the most recent write, not the first one.

sections.heading: 15.4. Reading by key, MEMORY GET
sections.text: MEMORY GET is an exact point lookup on namespace + key. See MEMORY GET. MEMORY GET FROM name WHERE NS = '<ns>' AND KEY = '<key>' The grammar is fixed. Both the NS predicate and the KEY predicate are required. They are joined by AND. Inside, this compiles to a single-row SELECT ... WHERE pk = '<ns>0x1E<key>' LIMIT 1. The returned columns are the namespace, the key, the value, and created_at. The embedding is also returned when the store has one. MEMORY GET FROM scratch WHERE NS = 'user:42' AND KEY = 'lang';

sections.heading: 15.5. Semantic recall, MEMORY SEARCH
sections.text: MEMORY SEARCH ranks every row in the store by its vector distance from a query embedding. The closest rows come first. See MEMORY SEARCH. MEMORY SEARCH name USING VECTOR '<query>' [ LIMIT n ] The query is a float list in brackets. It has the same form as a stored embedding. The statement returns (mem_namespace, mem_key, mem_value, score). The score is computed for each row using the distance function that was set for the store at CREATE time. The result is ordered by score ascending, so the nearest matches come first. If you leave out LIMIT, the engine sets no cap. Pass a value to bound the result set. It is best to always pass a small LIMIT. MEMORY SEARCH agent_mem USING VECTOR '[0.11, 0.20, 0.08, 0.90]' LIMIT 5; The distance metric is fixed when the store is created. See §15.2. It decides which native operator SEARCH uses for the score column. distance literalInternal kindOperator used by SEARCH 'cosine' (default)MEMORY_DIST_COSINEcosine distance 'l2'MEMORY_DIST_L2Euclidean (L2) distance 'inner' / 'ip'MEMORY_DIST_INNERinner-product distance 'l1'MEMORY_DIST_L1Manhattan (L1) distance SEARCH is a full scan, scored per row. It does not use an approximate vector index. The distance = ... metric set at creation time is what makes the ranking meaningful. Every row's distance is computed during the scan. Calling SEARCH against a store created without an embedding column raises error 42P01. A non-semantic store has no vector to compare. The ordering happens inside the engine using the score column's positional ordinal. This statement does not expose an ORDER BY <expression>.

sections.heading: 15.6. Enumerating namespaces, MEMORY LIST NAMESPACES
sections.text: To see which namespaces exist in a store, use MEMORY LIST NAMESPACES. It compiles to SELECT DISTINCT mem_namespace FROM mem_<name>. An optional prefix filter is added as a LIKE 'prefix%' predicate. MEMORY LIST NAMESPACES IN name [ PREFIX '<prefix>' ] MEMORY LIST NAMESPACES IN scratch; MEMORY LIST NAMESPACES IN scratch PREFIX 'user:';

sections.heading: 15.7. Removing memories, MEMORY DELETE
sections.text: MEMORY DELETE removes the single row that matches a namespace and key. See MEMORY DELETE. MEMORY DELETE FROM name WHERE NS = '<ns>' AND KEY = '<key>' Like GET, both predicates are required. The statement runs through the engine's standard DELETE path against mem_<name>, filtered on the composite pk. Any row-level security policy, foreign-key cascade, MVCC version, or trigger on the backing table applies just as it would to a hand-written DELETE FROM mem_<name> WHERE pk = '...'. This makes MEMORY DELETE safe in a multi-tenant database where each tenant is fenced off by an RLS policy. MEMORY DELETE FROM scratch WHERE NS = 'user:42' AND KEY = 'lang';

sections.heading: 15.8. Dropping a memory store
sections.text: DROP MEMORY STORE removes the catalog registration. It also removes the backing mem_<name> table. DROP MEMORY STORE name [ CASCADE ] DROP MEMORY STORE IF EXISTS name IF EXISTS hides the not-found error 42704 and returns success. The backing table is dropped if it is present.

sections.heading: 15.9. Entity stores
sections.text: An entity store keeps one row per entity key. It tracks a rolling summary and counts how many times the entity has been mentioned. You create and drop it the same way as a memory store. It takes no WITH options. See CREATE ENTITY STORE. CREATE ENTITY STORE name [ IF NOT EXISTS ] DROP ENTITY STORE name [ IF EXISTS ] When you create an entity store named foo, the engine builds a backing table named ent_foo. It has this fixed shape. ColumnTypeNotes entity_keyVARCHAR(255)PRIMARY KEY, NOT NULL summaryVARCHAR(8000)nullable factsVARCHAR(8000)nullable; a JSON-shaped string mention_countINTNOT NULL; auto-incremented on each PUT last_seenTIMESTAMPNOT NULL; advanced to the current UTC time on each PUT

sections.heading: 15.9.1. ENTITY PUT, upsert with mention tracking
sections.text: ENTITY PUT is an upsert keyed on entity_key. Unlike MEMORY PUT, it has behavior of its own. The first write inserts the row with mention_count = 1. Every later write to the same key updates the summary and facts, adds one to mention_count, and moves last_seen forward. ENTITY PUT INTO name VALUES ('key', 'summary' [, 'facts']) -- first mention: inserts with mention_count = 1 ENTITY PUT INTO people VALUES ('ada', 'Works on the storage layer'); -- second mention: updates summary, mention_count -> 2, last_seen bumped ENTITY PUT INTO people VALUES ('ada', 'Leads the B+ tree rewrite', '{"team":"core","tz":"UTC+3"}'); The optional third value is the facts blob. When you leave it out, it is stored as NULL. The existence check is a direct primary-key probe of the backing table's B+ tree. The old mention_count is read from the existing tuple before it is incremented. The counter only goes up for each key.

sections.heading: 15.9.2. ENTITY GET
sections.text: ENTITY GET fetches a single entity row by key. ENTITY GET FROM name WHERE KEY = 'k' ENTITY GET FROM people WHERE KEY = 'ada'; It returns (entity_key, summary, facts, mention_count, last_seen) for the matching key. It returns one row.

sections.heading: 15.9.3. ENTITY RANK
sections.text: ENTITY RANK returns the store's rows ordered by mention_count descending. Ties are broken by last_seen descending. The most-discussed and most-recent entities come first. ENTITY RANK FROM name [ LIMIT n ] -- top 10 entities by how often they've been mentioned ENTITY RANK FROM people LIMIT 10; The engine sorts the result set internally. LIMIT bounds the output when you supply it.

sections.heading: 15.10. Status & error codes
sections.text: The store statements set SQLSTATE codes the same way as the rest of the engine. Below are the codes you will see most often with these stores. SQLSTATERaised when 42601A required name is missing (e.g. CREATE MEMORY STORE with no store name). 42710Creating a store whose name already exists (without IF NOT EXISTS). 42704Referencing a store (PUT / GET / SEARCH / DELETE / RANK / DROP) that does not exist. 42P01MEMORY SEARCH against a store created without an embedding column.

sections.heading: 15.11. Notes & limitations
sections.text: SEARCH is exact, not indexed. MEMORY SEARCH scans and scores every row. It does not use an approximate-nearest-neighbour vector index. For large stores the cost grows with the number of rows. The distance metric is fixed at creation. You cannot change it after CREATE MEMORY STORE. There is no ALTER for stores. To switch metrics, create the store again. No ORDER BY <expr> surface. SEARCH sorts inside the engine by its computed score column using a positional ordinal. You cannot give your own ordering expression on the statement. ttl_at is reserved. The memory backing table has a ttl_at column. The catalog descriptor has a ttl_days field. But MEMORY PUT always writes ttl_at as NULL. No automatic expiry pass is wired up. Treat TTL as not yet implemented. Fixed predicate shapes. MEMORY GET and DELETE require exactly WHERE NS = '...' AND KEY = '...'. ENTITY GET requires exactly WHERE KEY = '...'. These are not general WHERE clauses. For other queries, run ordinary SQL against the backing mem_<name> or ent_<name> table directly. Entity facts is a string, not a JSON column. It is stored as VARCHAR(8000) that holds a JSON-shaped string. The engine does not parse it or validate it as JSON. Stores live in the current schema. The backing table is created in the schema from the current database and schema context. A store name is unique within the catalog's store registry. Timestamps are UTC. created_at and last_seen are written as YYYY-MM-DD HH:MM:SS in UTC at write time.

sections.heading: Introduction
sections.text: 11. Transactions & MVCC How EvolutionDB groups statements into atomic transactions. How it keeps connections apart with multi-version snapshots. How it guards rows from writers that conflict. This chapter explains how EvolutionDB 3.0 handles many things at once. It covers explicit transaction blocks, savepoints, and the four SQL isolation levels. It also covers the snapshot rule that lets readers and writers run at the same time without blocking each other. And it covers the row locks and gap locks that writers use, and that the SERIALIZABLE level uses. For the exact syntax of each statement, follow the links to the transaction, savepoint, SET, and SELECT reference pages.

sections.heading: 11.1. Overview
sections.text: Every statement in EvolutionDB runs inside a transaction. If you have not opened an explicit block, each statement runs in its own auto-committed transaction. The change is applied and made durable as soon as the statement succeeds. Opening a block with BEGIN holds back that commit until you say so. A group of changes either all take effect together or none of them do. Concurrency control uses two separate parts. Undo log (rollback) The undo log is a stack of undo entries. Each connection has its own. Every INSERT, UPDATE, and DELETE inside an open block pushes an entry. The entry records how to reverse the change. COMMIT throws the log away. ROLLBACK replays it in reverse. This gives atomicity per connection. MVCC (isolation) Each transaction gets a 32-bit transaction id (XID). Row versions carry the XID that created them (xmin) and the XID that deleted them (xmax). A transaction reads through a snapshot. The snapshot decides which versions are visible. Reads never block writes and writes never block reads. The two layers work together. An UPDATE or DELETE does not overwrite or remove a row on disk.
