It sets the old version's xmax to the deleting XID. This is a "soft delete". For an update, it also inserts a new version. Rollback then just clears xmax to bring the original version back. It only falls back to a re-insert for rows written before MVCC existed. Note. A write-ahead log gives durability. Before a COMMIT is acknowledged, the transaction's dirty pages are flushed to the WAL. Then a commit sequence number (CSN) is assigned. Then the transaction is marked committed in the commit log (CLOG). You can turn on the optional synchronous-commit mode with EVOSQL_SYNC_COMMIT=1. When it is on, the commit also waits for a replica to acknowledge its WAL position. If that wait times out, the commit quietly falls back to asynchronous commit.

sections.heading: 11.2. Transaction control statements
sections.text: The session layer recognizes transaction control commands by their prefix. They do not go through the SQL parser. These are the verbs it knows. BEGIN START TRANSACTION COMMIT END ROLLBACK SAVEPOINT savepoint_name ROLLBACK TO [ SAVEPOINT ] savepoint_name RELEASE [ SAVEPOINT ] savepoint_name

sections.heading: 11.2.1. BEGIN / START TRANSACTION
sections.text: BEGIN and its synonym START TRANSACTION open an explicit block. On BEGIN the server creates a new undo log. It sets up an empty savepoint stack. It assigns the transaction's XID. And it marks the transaction as active. At REPEATABLE READ and SERIALIZABLE the server takes one snapshot right away. See 11.4 for more. At READ COMMITTED and READ UNCOMMITTED no snapshot is taken for the whole transaction. Each statement takes its own snapshot. BEGIN; INSERT INTO accounts VALUES ('alice', 100); UPDATE accounts SET balance = balance - 25 WHERE name = 'alice'; COMMIT; Note. The isolation level is not read from words typed after BEGIN. The verb is matched by its prefix. Any extra text is accepted but ignored. For example, BEGIN ISOLATION LEVEL SERIALIZABLE ignores the extra words. Use SET TRANSACTION ISOLATION LEVEL first instead. If you run BEGIN while already inside a block, the server logs a warning and does nothing else. The existing block continues. It does not open a nested transaction.

sections.heading: 11.2.2. COMMIT / END
sections.text: COMMIT finishes the block. Its synonym is END. The server flushes dirty pages to the WAL. It gets a CSN. It marks the XID committed in the CLOG. It releases all row and gap locks the transaction held. It removes the XID from the active list. And it throws away the undo log. From that moment the changes are durable. They are visible to other connections that take their snapshots after the commit.

sections.heading: 11.2.3. ROLLBACK
sections.text: ROLLBACK drops the block. The undo log is replayed in reverse. This restores every inserted, updated, or deleted row. The XID is marked aborted in the CLOG. And its locks are released. Deletes and updates are soft. Rollback usually just clears the xmax marker on the affected tuples. It does not rewrite any data. DML in the blockUndo entryWhat ROLLBACK does INSERTTX_OP_INSERTDelete the inserted row and remove its primary-key index entry. UPDATETX_OP_UPDATEDelete the new version. Restore the old version by clearing its xmax. Re-insert the old data if the old slot was already gone. DELETETX_OP_DELETEClear xmax to bring the tuple back. Re-insert the old data for pre-MVCC rows. Note. If a statement inside the block raises an error, the transaction enters an aborted state. While aborted, the server only accepts ROLLBACK, COMMIT, END, SAVEPOINT, and RELEASE. It rejects any other statement until the block is closed. If you run COMMIT on an aborted transaction, the server does a ROLLBACK instead. All work is discarded. This matches PostgreSQL. If a connection disconnects with a block still open, the server rolls it back on its own.

sections.heading: 11.3. Savepoints
sections.text: Savepoints mark a position in the undo-log stack. They let you roll back part of a transaction without dropping the whole block. They only work inside an open transaction. See the SAVEPOINT reference for the full syntax. SAVEPOINT name Records the current undo-log depth under name and pushes it on the savepoint stack. If you re-use an existing name, it is overwritten in place. This matches PostgreSQL. Names are matched without case. Up to 64 savepoints may be active at once. ROLLBACK TO [SAVEPOINT] name Replays undo entries down to the recorded depth. This undoes all work done after the savepoint. Then it destroys any savepoints made after it. The savepoint itself stays and you can roll back to it again. This also clears the aborted state. You can keep going after a statement error as long as you set a savepoint before it. RELEASE [SAVEPOINT] name Forgets name and all newer savepoints. It does not undo any work. The data changes stay part of the still-open transaction. BEGIN; INSERT INTO log VALUES (1, 'start'); SAVEPOINT sp1; INSERT INTO log VALUES (2, 'maybe'); ROLLBACK TO sp1; -- row (2,'maybe') is undone; row (1,'start') stays INSERT INTO log VALUES (3, 'final'); COMMIT; -- rows (1,'start') and (3,'final') persist Note. In EvolutionDB a savepoint is a position in the undo log of one connection. It rolls back data changes made since the savepoint. It does not take a separate MVCC sub-snapshot. The visibility snapshot in use stays the transaction's snapshot or the statement's snapshot. It is not tied to the savepoint. If you run SAVEPOINT, ROLLBACK TO, or RELEASE outside a transaction, the server raises SQLSTATE 25P01. If you name a savepoint that does not exist, it raises SQLSTATE 3B001 (savepoint does not exist).

sections.heading: 11.4. Isolation levels
sections.text: EvolutionDB supports all four standard isolation levels. Set the level for the session and for the next transaction before you open a block. Use SET to do this. SET [ SESSION ] TRANSACTION ISOLATION LEVEL { READ UNCOMMITTED | READ COMMITTED | REPEATABLE READ | SERIALIZABLE } The default level for a new connection is READ COMMITTED. If you set the level after a transaction has already run a query, the server raises SQLSTATE 25001. If the level is not one it knows, it raises SQLSTATE 22023. LevelInternal codeSnapshot strategyExtra machinery READ UNCOMMITTED0One snapshot per statement. It is treated like READ COMMITTED.None. READ COMMITTED (default)1A new snapshot is taken at the start of every statement.None. REPEATABLE READ2One snapshot is taken at BEGIN and used for the whole transaction.None. SERIALIZABLE3One snapshot is taken at BEGIN, like REPEATABLE READ.Conflict guard for write-skew detection, plus gap locks and a serializer mutex. Here is an honest comparison with the SQL standard and PostgreSQL. READ UNCOMMITTED. The standard allows dirty reads at this level. EvolutionDB does not do dirty reads. Uncommitted versions are never made visible. This level acts exactly like READ COMMITTED. This matches PostgreSQL. PostgreSQL also turns READ UNCOMMITTED into READ COMMITTED. READ COMMITTED. Each statement sees a snapshot taken when it starts. Two statements in the same transaction can see different committed data. Non-repeatable reads and phantoms are possible. This is the standard meaning of the level. REPEATABLE READ. The whole transaction reads through one snapshot. Rows it has already read do not change under it. EvolutionDB does not do first-updater-wins abort detection at this level. It does not give the stricter "snapshot isolation" anomaly set that PostgreSQL's REPEATABLE READ gives. SERIALIZABLE. This adds a conflict guard. The guard tracks read and write dependencies. It rejects a commit that forms a dangerous shape known as write skew. It also adds gap locks to block phantom inserts into a scanned range. To keep a single serial order across the whole system, it takes a process-wide mutex for the life of the transaction. DML from other connections waits until this transaction commits or rolls back. A failed serialization check aborts the commit with SQLSTATE 40001 (could not serialize access due to read/write dependencies among transactions). Note. SERIALIZABLE holds a global DML mutex for the whole life of the transaction. It serializes writes across all connections. It does not allow the optimistic concurrency that PostgreSQL's SSI allows. Use it as a correctness tool for critical sections. Do not use it as a high-throughput level. The conflict guard tracks a limited number of read interests and predicate locks per transaction. It catches common write-skew patterns. But it is not a full dependency-graph analysis.

sections.heading: 11.5. Snapshots & visibility
sections.text: A snapshot is a view of transaction activity at one moment in time. It records the following fields. xminThe lowest XID still active when the snapshot was taken. Every XID below this is finished. xmaxOne past the highest XID assigned so far. Every XID at or above this is "in the future" and invisible. active_xids[]The list of XIDs that were in progress at snapshot time. Up to 256 transactions running at once are tracked. my_xidThe XID of the transaction that took the snapshot. It is used to recognize its own writes. snapshot_csnThe highest committed CSN at snapshot time. It gives an O(1) "committed before me" fast path. A heap tuple stores its creator XID (xmin) and deleter XID (xmax) in its MVCC header. The visibility rule decides whether a tuple is visible to a snapshot. It works roughly like this. Tuples without an MVCC header are pre-MVCC rows. They are always visible. A frozen xmin is the reserved value 1. It is treated as committed long ago and is always visible. If xmin is the snapshot's own XID, the tuple is the reader's own insert. It is visible unless the same transaction also deleted it. In other cases xmin must be committed in the CLOG, below xmax, and not in the active list. Only then is the creating transaction's work visible. If xmax is 0, the tuple was never deleted and is visible. If the deleter is this transaction, it is invisible. The tuple is still visible if the deleter has not committed, or committed after the snapshot, or was active at snapshot time. The tuple counts as dead only when the deleter committed and is visible in this snapshot. The engine avoids repeating CLOG and CSN lookups. It uses hint bits and a CSN ring-buffer cache. The hint bits are XMIN_COMMITTED and XMIN_ABORTED. They are stamped on the tuple after the first successful check. None of this changes the result of the rule. It only makes it faster.

sections.heading: 11.5.1. Temporal queries (FOR SYSTEM_TIME)
sections.text: You can read the database as it was at a past transaction. The engine builds a snapshot placed just after a given XID committed. Every committed XID up to and including that value is visible. Anything assigned later is invisible. EvolutionDB offers this with a SQL:2011-style clause on SELECT. -- current transaction id of this session SELECT evo_current_xid(); -- read a table as it stood right after transaction 42 committed SELECT * FROM orders FOR SYSTEM_TIME AS OF TRANSACTION 42; Note. The historical view does not track concurrency. It has no active list. It shows a clean image with only committed data at the chosen XID. How far back you can go is limited by retention. The CSN cache and commit-timestamp cache use a ring buffer. If a snapshot's XID has dropped out of the kept window, the server rejects it. It does not return a misleading result.

sections.heading: 11.6. Row locks & explicit locking
sections.text: MVCC means readers and writers do not block each other. But writers to the same row must run one at a time. EvolutionDB has a row-level lock manager. It is keyed by the table_id and the primary-key value. Locks are held by XID. They are released on their own at COMMIT, at ROLLBACK, at the end of an auto-commit statement, or at disconnect. ModeAcquired byCompatibility EVO_LOCK_EXCLUSIVEUPDATE, DELETE, SELECT ... FOR UPDATEOne holder only. It conflicts with everything. EVO_LOCK_SHAREDSELECT ... FOR SHAREIt works with other shared holders. It conflicts with exclusive. Locking is re-entrant. If the same XID locks a row it already holds, nothing happens. The same holder can also upgrade a shared lock to exclusive. If a request conflicts with another transaction's lock, it waits on a condition variable. It waits until the lock is released or the timeout runs out. BEGIN; SELECT balance FROM accounts WHERE name = 'alice' FOR UPDATE; UPDATE accounts SET balance = balance - 10 WHERE name = 'alice'; COMMIT; -- row lock released here

sections.heading: 11.6.1. Lock waits, timeout & deadlocks
sections.text: The default lock wait timeout is 5000 ms. You can override it per session with SET EVO_LOCK_TIMEOUT = ms. A value of 0 restores the default. If a waiter does not get the lock within the timeout, the operation fails with a lock-timeout error (EVO_LOCK_TIMEOUT). Before it sleeps, the lock manager runs a wait-for-graph cycle check. It follows the chain of who each holder is waiting for. If that chain leads back to the requester, it reports a deadlock right away with EVO_LOCK_DEADLOCK. It does not wait for the timeout. The search has a fixed depth limit. EVO_LOCK_OK (0)Lock granted (or no lock needed). EVO_LOCK_TIMEOUT (-1)Wait exceeded the timeout. EVO_LOCK_ERROR (-2)Internal lock-manager error. EVO_LOCK_DEADLOCK (-3)Granting the wait would create a wait-for cycle. Note. Shared locks use a single holder slot per row. The last writer wins for the recorded holder. FOR SHARE is treated as compatible, but it is not fully multi-holder. SKIP LOCKED is accepted on FOR UPDATE and FOR SHARE for syntax compatibility. Row locks are keyed by primary key. A table without a usable primary key cannot take a row lock. In that case the request does nothing.

sections.heading: 11.6.2. Gap locks (phantom prevention)
sections.text: Gap locks stop phantom inserts in SERIALIZABLE transactions. A range scan can take a gap lock. The lock covers a key interval (lo, hi] on a table. An empty bound means −∞ or +∞. If another transaction tries to INSERT a key that falls inside an active gap lock held by a different XID, the insert waits. It uses the same timeout as row locks. It waits until the gap lock is released at the holder's commit or rollback. Gap locks do not conflict with each other. They conflict only with inserts. They are taken on their own during SERIALIZABLE range scans. There is no explicit statement for them.

sections.heading: 11.7. XID lifecycle & reserved ids
sections.text: Transaction ids are 32-bit values. A counter that only goes up hands them out. The counter is kept in the database file header. The ids survive a restart. A few values are reserved. 0, pre-MVCC sentinelA tuple with no MVCC header. It is always visible. It is also used as "no deleter" in xmax. 1, XID_FROZENIt means "committed long ago". An xmin of 1 is always visible. The server does not check the CLOG for it. 2+, normalThe first transaction id that can be assigned. Ids are assigned in increasing order. The CLOG stores two status bits per XID. 00 means in-progress. 01 means committed. 10 means aborted. The bits are packed into 4 KB pages. Each page covers 16,320 ids and is chained for overflow. At startup the server records the first XID it assigns. Any XID below that value still marked in-progress belongs to a session that crashed. It is treated as aborted. Its half-written rows never become visible. Note. For auto-commit DML, XID assignment is lazy. A transaction id is not given out until the first write. Read-only statements do not use up ids. The active transaction array holds up to 256 entries. This sets the limit on how many transactions running at once a snapshot can track exactly.

sections.heading: 11.8. See also
sections.text: BEGIN / COMMIT / ROLLBACK. The transaction control reference. SAVEPOINT. The reference for savepoints, ROLLBACK TO, and RELEASE. SET. How to set the isolation level and the lock timeout. SELECT. Covers FOR UPDATE, FOR SHARE, and FOR SYSTEM_TIME AS OF. UPDATE · DELETE. Soft-delete writers that take row locks.

sections.heading: Introduction
sections.text: 17. LISTEN / NOTIFY EvolutionDB has a small, in-memory publish and subscribe channel. A connection signs up for a channel with LISTEN. Any connection can raise an event with NOTIFY. The server then pushes an async message to every listener. This works on both the PostgreSQL wire protocol and the native EVO text protocol.

sections.heading: 17.1. Overview
sections.text: Async notification lets applications signal each other without polling a table. A connection subscribes to a named channel. Later, the same or another connection publishes an event on that channel. The event can carry a short text payload. The server delivers the event to every subscribed connection as an out-of-band message. It arrives on the listener's socket even while the listener is running a query. EvolutionDB has three statements and a small set of helper functions. LISTEN Subscribe the current session to a channel. See LISTEN / NOTIFY. UNLISTEN Cancel one subscription, or all of them with UNLISTEN *. NOTIFY Publish an event on a channel. A payload string is optional. The event is delivered when the transaction commits. The registry is a process-local hash map. It is a djb2-hashed bucket array of 1024 slots. Each bucket is a linked list of channel records. Each channel is a linked list of listeners. The registry is not persisted and is not shared across server processes. Subscriptions live and die with the running server and with the connection that created them. For events that must survive a disconnected consumer, see §17.8 Durable subscriptions. Identity and "PID". PostgreSQL reports the process id of the backend that sent the notification. EvolutionDB reports the sender's internal session id instead. The session id is a per-connection integer. It is not an operating-system PID. On the PG wire protocol it is sent in the 4-byte PID field of the NotificationResponse ('A') message. On the EVO protocol it is the first field of the NOTIFY async frame.

sections.heading: 17.2. LISTEN, subscribing to a channel
sections.text: LISTEN channel LISTEN channel SELF A LISTEN has two effects. Both happen right away when the statement runs. They are not deferred to commit. First, the (session, channel) pair is added to the global registry. Second, the channel is recorded in the session's own listen mirror so helper functions can list it. If the session already listens on that channel, the registration is an idempotent upsert. In that case only the self-delivery flag is updated. The flag is explained below. LISTEN orders; -- session is now subscribed to channel "orders"

sections.heading: 17.2.1. Self-delivery (the SELF keyword)
sections.text: By default a session does not receive notifications that it raised itself. This matches PostgreSQL's default behavior. EvolutionDB lets you turn on self-delivery with a special keyword instead of a parameter clause. LISTEN orders SELF; -- now this session WILL be delivered its own NOTIFY orders events Honest contrast with PostgreSQL. PostgreSQL has no per-channel "self" switch. Same-session delivery is controlled globally by client_min_messages and queue mechanics. A backend simply does not see its own notifications. EvolutionDB's LISTEN ... SELF is an EvolutionDB-specific extension. The grammar comment notes one reason for the keyword choice. It was chosen over a WITH (self = true) clause to avoid a token conflict on = in the LISTEN context.

sections.heading: 17.3. UNLISTEN, cancelling subscriptions
sections.text: UNLISTEN channel UNLISTEN * UNLISTEN channel removes this session's subscription to one channel. UNLISTEN * removes every subscription owned by the session. Both forms take effect right away. Both report the command tag UNLISTEN. Cancelling a channel you were not listening on is not an error. It does nothing. UNLISTEN orders; -- stop listening on one channel UNLISTEN *; -- stop listening on everything When a channel's last listener is removed, the empty channel record is freed from the registry automatically. You do not need to call UNLISTEN before disconnecting. When a connection closes, the server runs a disconnect hook. The hook removes all of that session's subscriptions. It also drops any pending notifications the session had queued but not yet committed.

sections.heading: 17.4. NOTIFY, publishing an event
sections.text: NOTIFY channel NOTIFY channel, 'payload' A NOTIFY raises an event on a channel. It can take an optional single-quoted payload string. NOTIFY does not fire right away. Unlike LISTEN and UNLISTEN, it is enqueued onto the session's pending list. It is delivered when the surrounding transaction commits. See §17.5. The command tag is NOTIFY. NOTIFY orders; NOTIFY orders, 'order 4711 shipped'; Payloads are plain text. The parser strips the surrounding quotes and stages the payload for the executor. An empty payload and a missing payload are the same. Both deliver an empty string to listeners.

sections.heading: 17.5. Transactional behavior
sections.text: Notifications respect transaction boundaries. A NOTIFY is buffered until the transaction that issued it commits. If the transaction rolls back, the buffered notifications are discarded. They are never delivered. Autocommit. Outside an explicit BEGIN, a NOTIFY is flushed and delivered right after the statement. A listener sees the message before the client's next response. Explicit transactions. Inside BEGIN … COMMIT, all enqueued notifications are delivered at COMMIT. A ROLLBACK discards every pending notification for the session. Savepoints. Each pending notification records the savepoint depth where it was raised. ROLLBACK TO SAVEPOINT discards only the notifications enqueued after that savepoint. Earlier ones stay queued for the eventual commit. BEGIN; NOTIFY orders, 'a'; -- queued (depth 0) SAVEPOINT sp1; NOTIFY orders, 'b'; -- queued (depth 1) ROLLBACK TO sp1; -- 'b' discarded, 'a' still queued COMMIT; -- only 'a' is delivered

sections.heading: 17.5.1. Commit-time de-duplication
sections.text: At commit, before fan-out, the pending list is collapsed. Duplicate (channel, payload, sender) triples raised in the same transaction are merged into a single delivery. Channel names are compared without case. Payloads are compared exactly, with case. This matches PostgreSQL's rule that identical notifications within a transaction are folded. A tight loop of NOTIFY orders, 'x' delivers one message, not many.

sections.heading: 17.6. Delivery model and self-filtering
sections.text: At commit, the server handles each de-duplicated pending notification in turn. It takes a read-lock on the registry. It copies the current listener list for the channel into a fixed-size snapshot. It releases the lock. Only then does it write to each listener's connection. This snapshot-then-release pattern means network I/O never happens while the registry lock is held. That avoids deadlocks against per-connection write locks. The self-delivery filter is applied during the snapshot. A listener whose session_id matches the sender's is skipped. The exception is a listener that subscribed with LISTEN ... SELF. See §17.2.1. Each listener receives the message in its protocol's native async form. ProtocolFrameFields delivered PostgreSQL wire (5433)NotificationResponse (message type 'A')sender session id (in the PID field), channel name, payload EVO text (9967)NOTIFY <session_id> <channel> <payload_len>\n<payload>\nsender session id, channel, payload length, then the payload bytes Both protocols share the same in-memory registry. A NOTIFY from a psql client can wake an EVO-protocol listener. The reverse also works.

sections.heading: 17.7. Helper functions
sections.text: Two function-call forms are available inside SQL expressions. You can use them in code, for example from a stored procedure body or a trigger. See CALL. EVO_NOTIFY(channel, payload) Enqueues a notification. It works the same as the NOTIFY statement. PG_NOTIFY(channel, payload) is a PostgreSQL-compatible alias for the same function. Like the statement form, the event is buffered and delivered at commit. PG_LISTENING_CHANNELS() Returns the channels the current session is listening on. The list is read from the session's own listen mirror. -- raise a notification from inside a procedure / trigger SELECT EVO_NOTIFY('orders', 'inserted'); -- inspect this session's subscriptions SELECT PG_LISTENING_CHANNELS();

sections.heading: 17.8. Identifiers, limits, and error codes
sections.text: Channel names are case-insensitive. They are compared with a folded hash and strcasecmp. So orders and ORDERS are the same channel. The fixed limits are listed below. Channel name length Maximum 63 characters. The buffer is 64 bytes including the terminator. This mirrors PostgreSQL's NAMEDATALEN. Over-long or empty names are rejected. Payload length Maximum 8000 bytes. An over-long payload is rejected before delivery. Per-session LISTEN cap A session may listen on at most 32 channels at once. The thirty-third distinct LISTEN is refused. Snapshot fan-out bound A single delivery copies up to 1024 listeners per channel into its snapshot. Errors are reported with these SQLSTATE codes. SQLSTATECondition 42622Channel name too long (≥ 64 bytes) or invalid; or per-session LISTEN cap exceeded. 22023Payload string too long (over 8000 bytes).

sections.heading: 17.9. Durable subscriptions
sections.text: Plain LISTEN and NOTIFY are fire-and-forget. A notification is delivered only to connections that are subscribed at the moment of commit. A consumer that is offline when the event fires misses it. EvolutionDB adds a separate durable subscription feature on top of the same channels. It gives you an at-least-once, replayable queue. At commit, after the in-memory fan-out, each notification is also appended to every persistent mailbox bound to that channel. A consumer that reconnects later can drain the backlog. This is described in the Chapter on durable subscriptions and the CREATE SUBSCRIPTION command. The fast path is not affected. When a channel has no durable subscribers, no extra work is done. Notes and limitations The listener registry is in-memory and per-process. It is not written to evosql.db. It is not recovered after a restart. It is not visible to other server processes. Use durable subscriptions when an event must survive a disconnect or restart. The "PID" reported to listeners is the sender's internal session id. It is not an OS process id. Same-session notifications are filtered out by default. Turn them on per channel with LISTEN ... SELF. This is an EvolutionDB extension, not standard PostgreSQL syntax. NOTIFY is commit-gated. A rolled-back transaction delivers nothing. The same is true for a ROLLBACK TO SAVEPOINT that covers the statement. Identical notifications within one transaction are collapsed to a single delivery. Channel names are compared without case. Payloads are compared with case for de-duplication. NOTIFY currently accepts only a channel name and an optional quoted string literal. The statement form does not yet support arbitrary expression payloads. Disconnecting auto-cancels all of a session's listens. It also discards the session's uncommitted pending notifications.

sections.heading: 17.10. End-to-end example
sections.text: Connection A subscribes and waits. Connection B publishes. -- connection A LISTEN orders; -- A now blocks/polls for async messages on the channel -- connection B BEGIN; INSERT INTO orders (id, status) VALUES (4711, 'shipped'); NOTIFY orders, '4711 shipped'; -- queued COMMIT; -- delivered to A here -- connection A receives (PG wire): channel "orders", payload "4711 shipped", -- PID = B's session id. Then it can stop: UNLISTEN orders; See also: LISTEN / NOTIFY reference, CREATE SUBSCRIPTION, transaction control, SAVEPOINT, and CALL.

sections.heading: Introduction
sections.text: 7. Queries This page shows how EvolutionDB turns a SELECT into rows. It covers projection, filtering with WHERE, joins, subqueries, grouping, aggregates, and finally ORDER BY and LIMIT. It describes how the engine really works. This chapter describes the read path. The full grammar and a clause-by-clause reference live on the SELECT command page. Here we focus on behavior. We show what each stage computes, in what order it runs, and where EvolutionDB differs from PostgreSQL or the SQL standard. Everything below matches the engine source. The main files are evolution/db/Select.c, evolution/db/expression.c, and the adaptor's join pipeline in adaptor/join.c.

sections.heading: 7.1. Overview & Processing Order
sections.text: A table query runs stage by stage. These are the stages, in order. FROM / join. The leftmost source is the driving relation. Each next source is joined onto the row so far. Rows are produced one at a time. See §7.3. WHERE. The boolean condition runs against each candidate row. Rows that do not come out true are dropped. See §7.2. GROUP BY / aggregates. The rows that are left are collapsed into groups. Aggregate functions are computed for each group. See §7.5. HAVING. A boolean condition filters whole groups. Projection. The select list runs to build the output rows. Then DISTINCT removes duplicates. Window functions. These run after grouping and before sorting. ORDER BY. Rows are sorted. See §7.6. LIMIT / OFFSET. The number of returned rows is limited. Inside the core engine there are two physical shapes. A single-table query finds the table in the catalog. Then it does one of two things. It does a primary-key lookup when the WHERE is a single equality on the PK. Otherwise it does a sequential table scan through the buffer pool and decodes each binary tuple as it goes. A multi-table query goes to the join pipeline. Both paths decode rows with tup_extract_fields() and stream them. The engine does not build a separate query-plan tree. It has no cost-based optimizer. Note. EvolutionDB runs expressions on the text form of each column value. This is the same form the wire protocol returns. Comparisons try a numeric reading first. If that fails, they fall back to byte-wise string comparison. There is no separate typed expression engine. This keeps the code small. It also means a few behaviors differ from a strictly typed planner. They are called out in each section below.

sections.heading: 7.1.1. Projections (the select list)
sections.text: Each select-list item is an expression node. Its type is ExprNode. The parser builds it. The following forms are supported. * projects every column of the source. Plain column references. These are col or qualified alias.col. Qualified names resolve by exact match first. If that fails, the engine drops the qualifier and matches the bare column name. See expr_evaluate, case EXPR_COLUMN. Literals. These are integer, float, string, boolean, and NULL. Arithmetic and bitwise operators. These are + - * / %, << >>, and & | ^. String functions. These are SUBSTRING, TRIM, UPPER, LOWER, LENGTH, CONCAT, REPLACE, and COALESCE. CASE in both searched and simple forms. The simple form is rewritten into equality comparisons. JSON accessors -> and ->>, plus the JSON_* function family. Aggregate and window functions. See §7.5. You can rename an item with AS alias. The AS keyword is optional. The alias is stored as the node's display name through expr_store_select(). The same name is used to find the value again in HAVING and in window evaluation. SELECT name, salary * 12 AS annual, UPPER(dept) FROM employees; Note. Integer division uses floating-point math inside. Division or modulo by zero returns 0 and does not raise an error. See expr_evaluate_num, cases EXPR_DIV and EXPR_MOD. PostgreSQL would raise division_by_zero. In the scan path, field buffers are fixed at 256 bytes per column. Very long projected values are cut off.

sections.heading: 7.2. The WHERE Clause
sections.text: The WHERE condition is an expression tree. expr_evaluate() runs it for each row. A row is kept only when the result is the boolean true string "t". The join pipeline drops the row when the result is "0", "false", "f", or NULL. A NULL here means the value could not be evaluated. See the end-of-pipeline test in pipeline_recurse.

sections.heading: Supported predicates
sections.text: Comparisons =, <>, <, >, <=, >=. Each side is turned into a string. If both sides parse fully and cleanly as numbers, the comparison is numeric. Otherwise it is a strcmp string comparison. See expr_evaluate and EXPR_CMP_*. Logical AND, OR, NOT, XOR. NULL operands use SQL three-valued logic. For example, TRUE AND NULL → NULL, and FALSE AND NULL → FALSE. NULL tests IS NULL and IS NOT NULL. A column counts as NULL when it is missing from the row or carries the internal null marker. Pattern match LIKE and NOT LIKE. Use % for any sequence and _ for any single character. A small in-engine wildcard matcher does the work. Range / membership BETWEEN low AND high is rewritten to x >= low AND x <= high. NOT BETWEEN becomes x < low OR x > high. IN (list) and NOT IN (list) are rewritten into a balanced OR/AND tree. This keeps long lists from overflowing the recursive evaluator. Subqueries IN (SELECT ...), NOT IN (SELECT ...), EXISTS and NOT EXISTS, and the scalar form (SELECT ...). See §7.4. Full-text & vector MATCH(col) AGAINST('terms') matches on any term token and returns a hit count. Vector-distance operators are also supported. A comparison yields NULL when either side is NULL. NULL is not the same as false. This follows SQL rules, so such a row is not returned. SELECT * FROM orders WHERE status = 'open' AND total BETWEEN 100 AND 500 AND note LIKE '%urgent%';

sections.heading: PK lookup & push-down
sections.text: For a single table, the engine looks for a WHERE that is a lone equality on the primary-key column. A simple comparison on the PK also counts. expr_is_simple_pk_range() detects this case. It is served by a direct B+ tree lookup with bt2_search instead of a full scan. The join pipeline uses the same idea for first-table push-down. An alias.col = literal term that targets the driving table is pulled out by extract_eq_for_alias. It is used as a PK lookup or as an early per-row column filter. This runs before the rest of the WHERE tree is applied to the full merged row. Note. The choice between numeric and string comparison is made per value at run time. It does not depend on the declared column type. Two operands that both look numeric are compared as numbers, even if the column is text. Mixed cases fall back to strcmp. There is no implicit collation beyond byte order. String comparison is case-sensitive. Column-name resolution, on the other hand, is case-insensitive.

sections.heading: 7.3. Joins
sections.text: Multi-table queries run through the iterator pipeline engine in adaptor/join.c. It uses Volcano-style recursion in pipeline_recurse. No table is fully loaded into memory. Each table gives one row at a time. The merged row grows depth-first until the deepest level emits a complete joined tuple. Memory use is O(number of tables), not O(table size).

sections.heading: Join types
sections.text: The parser turns each join type into a small integer. The pipeline reads it back. SyntaxEncoded codeBehavior JOIN (plain)100Inner join. INNER JOIN101Inner join. CROSS JOIN102Cartesian product (no key filter). STRAIGHT_JOIN200Inner join (MySQL ordering hint; treated as inner). LEFT [OUTER] JOIN301 / 305Left outer: emit NULL-filled right side when no match. RIGHT [OUTER] JOIN302 / 306Parsed and accepted; see note below. NATURAL [LEFT|RIGHT] JOIN400+Parsed and accepted; see note below.

sections.heading: How a join row is produced
sections.text: Before the pipeline starts, the engine reads each ON condition with extract_eq_columns(). It finds the equality left.col = right.col. It maps this to two keys. The left key is a column index in the row built so far. The right key is a column in the table being joined. For each table after the first, one of the following happens. If the right key is the joined table's primary key, the match is found with an O(log n) B+ tree lookup. The function is bt2_search. If not, the joined table is scanned. Rows are kept where the right-key column equals the left key value. This is a nested-loop scan. If there is no usable key, the joined table is scanned with no filter. CROSS JOIN is one example. This is a full Cartesian expansion. For a LEFT join with codes 301 or 305, the right side may have no matching row. In that case its columns are filled with NULLs and the merged row is still emitted. Output columns are named alias.column when the source has an alias. MVCC visibility is checked for each scanned row. So joins see the same snapshot as the rest of the statement. SELECT e.name, d.dept_name FROM employees AS e INNER JOIN departments AS d ON e.dept_id = d.id;

sections.heading: LATERAL and UNNEST sources
sections.text: A LATERAL ( subquery ) source runs again once per outer row. The merged outer row is published into the query context. This lets correlated column references resolve against it. The inner statement is run with query_execute. Each result row is appended. See pipeline_recurse_lateral. A UNNEST(array_expr) source expands an array into one row per element. Both fit in as join slots and follow LEFT rules. A lateral or unnest source that yields no rows still emits NULLs under a left join. Note on join limits. Only an equality ON condition between two columns is used for key-based matching. A non-equi ON or one with expressions falls back to a full scan of the joined table. The broader WHERE tree still filters the final merged row. RIGHT and NATURAL joins are parsed. But the NULL-fill branch in pipeline_recurse only runs for the LEFT codes 301 and 305. Do not rely on right-outer NULL extension. Do not rely on NATURAL deriving the join key on its own the way PostgreSQL does. FULL OUTER JOIN is not built as a NULL-extending join. Use an explicit LEFT JOIN ... ON for portable outer-join behavior.

sections.heading: 7.4. Subqueries
sections.text: Subqueries are stored as raw SQL text on the expression node. The field is subquery_sql. They are run through a callback g_qctx->subquery_fn. The callback runs the inner statement and returns its values. expr_evaluate handles the following forms. Scalar subquery, (SELECT ...) Run and reduced to a single value. You can use it anywhere a scalar expression is allowed. expr IN (SELECT ...) and NOT IN The subquery's column is loaded into memory, up to 256 values. Then it is tested for membership. EXISTS (SELECT ...) and NOT EXISTS The result depends on whether the subquery returns any row. Correlated subqueries use the same outer-scope mechanism as LATERAL. A column reference inside the subquery that is not local resolves against the published outer row. SELECT name FROM employees e WHERE EXISTS ( SELECT 1 FROM orders o WHERE o.emp_id = e.id );

sections.heading: 7.5. Grouping & Aggregates
sections.text: The executor switches to the grouping path in two cases. The first is when the select list has an aggregate function. The second is when the query has a GROUP BY. Rows are then put into buckets. With GROUP BY, each row's grouping-key value places it in a group. With no GROUP BY but an aggregate present, all rows form one group. Aggregates are computed by walking the rows of each group again.

sections.heading: Aggregate functions
sections.text: COUNT(*) Number of rows in the group. COUNT(expr) Number of rows where expr is not NULL. SUM(expr) / AVG(expr) The total or mean over the non-NULL values. A group with only NULL values returns NULL. AVG shows an integer when the mean is whole. Otherwise it shows four decimal places. MIN(expr) / MAX(expr) The lowest or highest value. The comparison is numeric when every value parses as a number. Otherwise it is a string comparison. GROUP_CONCAT(expr) Joins the non-NULL values of the group with a separator. The default separator is ,. This works the MySQL way. Non-aggregate select-list expressions in a grouped query run against the first row of each group. A HAVING condition then runs for each group. Aggregates used in HAVING are matched by display name to the computed select-list aggregates. Any extra aggregates that appear only in HAVING are computed on the fly. SELECT dept_id, COUNT(*) AS headcount, AVG(salary) AS avg_pay FROM employees GROUP BY dept_id HAVING COUNT(*) > 3; Note. SQL says every non-aggregated select-list column must appear in GROUP BY. EvolutionDB does not enforce this rule. An ungrouped column just takes its value from the group's first row. This is the permissive MySQL way. PostgreSQL has a strict check instead. COUNT(DISTINCT ...) and other DISTINCT aggregate arguments are not part of this aggregate path.

sections.heading: DISTINCT
sections.text: SELECT DISTINCT removes duplicate result rows after projection. DISTINCTROW works as a synonym. Duplicate removal compares the rendered output rows.

sections.heading: ORDER BY
sections.text: Rows are sorted with qsort over the result set. Each sort key may be a column name, a qualified table.column, or a 1-based column position like ORDER BY 2. Each key may end with ASC (the default) or DESC. The comparison is adaptive. When both values parse fully and cleanly as numbers, the order is numeric. Otherwise it is by string. See CompareRecords in Select.c. DESC just flips the comparison result. There is a special form, ORDER BY vector_col <=> 'literal'. It ranks rows by cosine distance for nearest-neighbor search. The executor can take an approximate-nearest-neighbor fast path. This needs a ready HNSW index on the column and a LIMIT on the query. It also needs no disqualifying clause. Disqualifying clauses are DISTINCT, OFFSET, a join, or extra sort keys. If the fast path does not apply, the engine brute-force sorts by distance. SELECT id FROM documents ORDER BY embedding <=> '[0.12, 0.84, 0.05]' LIMIT 5;

sections.heading: LIMIT / OFFSET
sections.text: Three forms are accepted. The first is LIMIT count. The second is the MySQL two-argument form LIMIT offset, count. The third is LIMIT count OFFSET offset. LIMIT and OFFSET are applied last. They run after ORDER BY, DISTINCT, and grouping. In the streaming single-table path, OFFSET skips the first N matching rows. LIMIT stops the scan once N rows have been sent. SELECT name FROM employees ORDER BY name LIMIT 20, 10; -- skip 20, return 10 Note. The engine's built-in text renderer holds rows in fixed-size storage for in-memory ORDER BY sorting. In the SELECT * path this is up to a few hundred rows. Large result sets are sorted and streamed by the adaptor's query executor when clients connect through the server. There is no standalone OFFSET clause without LIMIT. The grammar also has no FETCH FIRST, no set operations UNION, INTERSECT, and EXCEPT, and no CTEs with WITH.

sections.heading: 7.7. Common Errors
sections.text: 42P01, relation does not exist A table named in FROM or a join was not found in the catalog. tapi_resolve failed. 53000, out of memory The join pipeline could not allocate its per-table or merged-row buffers.

sections.heading: See Also
sections.text: SELECT, the full clause-by-clause command reference and synopsis CREATE INDEX, the primary-key and HNSW indexes that enable the lookup and ANN fast paths INSERT · UPDATE · DELETE

sections.heading: Introduction
sections.text: 20. Row-Level Security Row-Level Security (RLS) lets you add per-table policies. These policies limit which rows a session user can read, insert, update, or delete. The engine enforces them on every DML path. Your application does not have to do this work. EvolutionDB supports RLS as a small subset of the PostgreSQL model. A table carries an RLS-enabled flag. You can attach any number of named policies to that table. A policy has an optional row-filter expression with USING. It also has an optional write-validation expression with WITH CHECK. Each policy has a command scope and an optional role list. When the flag is on, the engine filters reads through the matching USING expressions. It also checks written rows against the matching WITH CHECK expressions. The SQL commands and their storage are handled in evolution/db/Policy.c. The runtime helpers live in the same place. These are the read overlay and the write check. The SELECT, INSERT, UPDATE, and DELETE paths call them.

sections.heading: 20.1. Enabling and Disabling RLS on a Table
sections.text: You define policies on their own. But they only start to work once Row-Level Security is enabled on the table that owns them. The flag does not create, drop, or change any policy. It only controls whether the engine uses them. ALTER TABLE table_name ENABLE ROW LEVEL SECURITY; ALTER TABLE table_name DISABLE ROW LEVEL SECURITY; The grammar accepts only the exact phrase ROW LEVEL SECURITY. If you misspell the middle word, you get a syntax error. The message is "expected LEVEL after ROW…". The flag is a single per-table value named rls_enabled. It is stored in the system catalog. The function cat_update_table_rls_flag() sets it. -- turn enforcement on; existing policies (if any) now apply ALTER TABLE accounts ENABLE ROW LEVEL SECURITY; -- turn enforcement off; policies remain in the catalog, just dormant ALTER TABLE accounts DISABLE ROW LEVEL SECURITY; Default-deny. A table can have rls_enabled = 1 while no policy matches a non-superuser's command. In that case access is denied. Reads return zero rows. Writes are rejected. If you enable RLS before you define any policy, every non-superuser is locked out. The admin user is a superuser. It skips RLS fully. See §20.6.

sections.heading: 20.2. CREATE POLICY
sections.text: Creates a named policy and attaches it to a table. See also the command reference page at CREATE POLICY. CREATE POLICY policy_name ON table_name [ AS { PERMISSIVE | RESTRICTIVE } ] [ FOR { ALL | SELECT | INSERT | UPDATE | DELETE } ] [ TO role [, ...] ] [ USING ( using_expression ) ] [ WITH CHECK ( check_expression ) ]; Only the name and the target table are required. Every other clause is optional. The clauses must come in the order shown above. The order is AS, then FOR, then TO, then USING, then WITH CHECK. policy_name The policy name. It must be unique within one table. In the catalog, policies are keyed by table_id:policy_name. So you can reuse the same name on different tables. If you create a duplicate name on the same table, it fails with SQLSTATE 42710. AS PERMISSIVE / AS RESTRICTIVE Sets how the policy combines with other policies. PERMISSIVE policies are OR'd together. RESTRICTIVE policies are AND'd on top. The default is PERMISSIVE. See §20.5. FOR The command scope that the policy applies to. The default is FOR ALL. Inside the engine, each scope maps to a one-character command code. See §20.4. TO role [..] The roles that the policy applies to. You can list up to 8 roles. The limit is CAT_MAX_POLICY_ROLES. Any extra entries are ignored. If you leave the list out, the policy applies to every role. A role named public in the list also matches every role. Role matching is case-insensitive. It is compared against the session user name. USING ( using_expression ) A boolean expression that is checked on each existing row. A row is visible to SELECT when this expression is true. The same row can then be used by UPDATE and DELETE. The expression AST is stored in the catalog. It is hex-encoded and can be up to 2048 bytes. The limit is CAT_MAX_POLICY_EXPR. WITH CHECK ( check_expression ) A boolean expression that new or updated rows must pass on INSERT and UPDATE. If you leave it out, the engine reuses the USING expression for the write check. This matches PostgreSQL. -- each user only sees and writes their own rows CREATE POLICY owner_rows ON documents FOR ALL USING (owner = 'alice') WITH CHECK (owner = 'alice'); -- read-only visibility policy scoped to one role CREATE POLICY public_published ON documents AS PERMISSIVE FOR SELECT TO reader USING (status = 'published'); -- a restrictive guard that ANDs on top of any permissive policy CREATE POLICY not_archived ON documents AS RESTRICTIVE FOR SELECT USING (archived = false); Memory-store sugar. EvolutionDB also accepts CREATE POLICY name ON MEMORY STORE store. It also accepts the matching ALTER MEMORY STORE … ENABLE/DISABLE ROW LEVEL SECURITY. These point at the store's backing table. That table is named mem_<store>. In every other way they work the same. If the store does not exist, the engine uses the literal name. It then raises the usual "relation does not exist" error.

sections.heading: 20.3. DROP POLICY
sections.text: Removes a policy from a table. See also DROP POLICY. DROP POLICY policy_name ON table_name; DROP POLICY public_published ON documents; The engine resolves the target table first. If the table does not exist, the error is SQLSTATE 42P01. This means undefined table. If the table exists but has no such policy, the error is SQLSTATE 42704. This means undefined object. Dropping a policy does not change the table's rls_enabled flag. So if you drop the last policy while RLS is still on, the table goes back to default-deny for non-superusers.

sections.heading: 20.4. Command Scopes
sections.text: The FOR clause stores a single command-code byte on the policy. A policy takes part in enforcement only when its scope matches the current operation. A scope of ALL always matches. FOR clauseCodeApplies to FOR ALL (default)'*'SELECT, INSERT, UPDATE, DELETE FOR SELECT'S'Read path (USING overlay) FOR INSERT'I'Write check (WITH CHECK) FOR UPDATE'U'Read overlay for candidates + write check FOR DELETE'D'Write check on deletion candidates

sections.heading: 20.5. How Policies Are Combined and Enforced
sections.text: Enforcement works in two different ways. The way depends on whether the operation reads rows or writes rows.

sections.heading: 20.5.1. Read overlay (SELECT, and UPDATE/DELETE row selection)
sections.text: For reads, the engine builds an overlay expression. It uses every policy that matches the command and the session user. It then AND's this overlay into the statement's existing WHERE clause. The combination rule works like this. All matching PERMISSIVE USING expressions are OR'd together. All matching RESTRICTIVE USING expressions are AND'd on top of that OR group. If no permissive policy matches, the overlay is the constant false. That means zero rows. A restrictive policy on its own never makes any row visible. There must be a permissive grant for any row to pass. The result is folded into the same WHERE expression that every scan path uses. So plain table scans, fast paths, and expression indexes all see the same filtered view. The original WHERE is put back after the statement runs. In multi-table queries, the same overlay is applied to each joined table. -- with the three documents policies above, a non-superuser SELECT -- effectively runs as: -- WHERE (<your WHERE>) -- AND ( owner = 'alice' OR status = 'published' ) -- PERMISSIVE OR-group -- AND ( archived = false ) -- RESTRICTIVE AND SELECT * FROM documents WHERE created > '2026-01-01';

sections.heading: 20.5.2. Write check (INSERT, UPDATE, DELETE)
sections.text: For writes, the engine checks the matching policies' WITH CHECK expression. When no WITH CHECK is present, it uses the USING expression instead. It runs this check against the candidate row's column values. The acceptance rule works like this. If any matching RESTRICTIVE check is false, the write is rejected right away. At least one matching PERMISSIVE check must be true. If no permissive policy matches at all, the write is rejected. This is default-deny. A failed check raises SQLSTATE 42501. The message is new row for relation "…" violates row-level security policy. An expression result counts as true when it equals t, true, or 1. It also counts as true when it is a non-zero number. -- rejected: WITH CHECK (owner = 'alice') is false for this row INSERT INTO documents (id, owner, status) VALUES (1, 'bob', 'draft'); -- ERROR: new row for relation "documents" violates row-level security policy

sections.heading: 20.6. Superuser Bypass
sections.text: The admin user counts as a superuser. It skips both the read overlay and the write check fully. RLS is never applied to its statements. This is true no matter what the table's flag is or what policies exist. The engine identifies the superuser by a case-insensitive match of the session user name against admin. EvolutionDB has no per-table "force RLS" mode right now. So there is no way to make the superuser follow its own policies.

sections.heading: 20.7. Error Codes
sections.text: RLS uses the following SQLSTATE values. SQLSTATECondition 42601Syntax error. The policy name or target table is missing. Or the ENABLE/DISABLE ROW LEVEL SECURITY phrase is wrong. 42P01 / 42E01Undefined table. The target relation does not exist. 42704Undefined object. DROP POLICY ran on a policy or memory store that does not exist. 42710Duplicate object. A policy with the same name already exists on the table. 42501Insufficient privilege. A written row broke a WITH CHECK policy. EX000Internal error. The RLS flag could not be updated.

sections.heading: 20.8. Notes and Limitations
sections.text: EvolutionDB's RLS is a chosen subset of the SQL and PostgreSQL model. These are the main differences from PostgreSQL. No ALTER POLICY. To change a policy, DROP it and CREATE it again. No FORCE ROW LEVEL SECURITY. The owner and superuser admin always skip policies. You cannot turn on enforcement for the superuser. Superuser detection is name-based. Only the literal user admin counts as a superuser for RLS. This does not come from a role privilege system. Role matching is by session user name. The TO list is compared against the current user. The match is case-insensitive. The value public is special and means all roles. There is no role-membership or inheritance resolution. Fixed limits. A policy can have at most 8 roles. The limit is CAT_MAX_POLICY_ROLES. Each serialized USING or WITH CHECK expression is capped at 2048 bytes. The limit is CAT_MAX_POLICY_EXPR. Clause order is fixed. CREATE POLICY clauses must come as AS → FOR → TO → USING → WITH CHECK. Disabling RLS does not drop policies. Policies stay in the catalog. They become active again when you re-enable RLS. See also: CREATE POLICY, DROP POLICY, ALTER TABLE.

sections.heading: Introduction
sections.text: 16. Document, Graph & Log Stores EvolutionDB has four special stores. They are the document store, the graph store, the message log, and the checkpoint store. Each one is a thin set of verbs on top of a normal engine table. MVCC visibility, row-level security, and the standard error path all work the same way.

sections.heading: 16.1. Overview
sections.text: The stores in this chapter are not separate storage engines. Each one is a small layer on top of the same SELECT, INSERT, UPDATE, and DELETE machinery that runs normal SQL. When you create a store, EvolutionDB creates one or more normal backing tables in the current schema. These tables have a fixed column layout. EvolutionDB also records the store in the system catalog. Each store has its own verbs. Examples are DOCUMENT WRITE, GRAPH UPSERT NODE, and LOG APPEND. These verbs turn their arguments into the same internal request structures that a hand-written DML statement would build. Then they call the normal processor. This design has the same results for all four stores. Documents, nodes, edges, log entries, and checkpoints are normal tuples in the binary tuple format. They are stored in slotted pages and indexed by B+ trees. Transaction behavior, MVCC snapshot visibility, and the setjmp/longjmp error path work the same way for store operations and for plain DML. The backing tables are real tables. Once you know how they are named, you can read them directly with SELECT. This helps when you debug. The backing-table name for each store comes from the store name in a fixed way. Store kindCatalog commandBacking table(s) Document storeCREATE DOCUMENT STOREdoc_<name> Graph storeCREATE GRAPH STOREkg_<name>_nodes, kg_<name>_edges Message logCREATE MESSAGE LOGml_<name> Checkpoint storeCREATE CHECKPOINT STOREck_<name> Note. None of these store types are in the SQL standard. PostgreSQL has no direct equivalent. In PostgreSQL you would use a jsonb column, the third-party pgvector extension, recursive CTEs for graph traversal, or external services. EvolutionDB puts these patterns into dedicated verbs. The comparisons in each section below are there to help you. They are not claims that the features are equal.

sections.heading: 16.2. Document Store
sections.text: A document store is a collection of JSON documents. It can also have a vector embedding column for similarity search. You create it with CREATE DOCUMENT STORE. This builds a backing table named doc_<name> with a fixed layout. idVARCHAR(255), NOT NULL, primary key. contentVARCHAR(8000), the document body. metaVARCHAR(8000), a JSON-shaped metadata string; may be NULL. embeddingVECTOR(N), created only when embedding_dim > 0. created_atTIMESTAMP, NOT NULL, set to the UTC time of each write. The store also records a distance kind. You choose it when you create the store. It is used later to rank vector searches. The accepted values are not case-sensitive. WITH distanceInternal kindScoring expression 'cosine' (default)MEMORY_DIST_COSINEcosine distance 'l2'MEMORY_DIST_L2Euclidean (L2) distance 'inner' / 'ip'MEMORY_DIST_INNERinner product 'l1'MEMORY_DIST_L1Manhattan (L1) distance Any unknown distance value falls back to 'cosine' without warning.

sections.heading: 16.2.1. Writing documents
sections.text: DOCUMENT WRITE runs through INSERT. The verb supplies id and content. It also supplies meta, or a NULL marker for meta. It supplies the embedding when the store has a vector column. It always supplies a server-generated created_at timestamp. An embedding is only sent when two things are true. The store must have an embedding column, and you must supply a non-empty embedding literal. DOCUMENT WRITE INTO articles VALUES ('a1', 'Vector databases explained', '{"lang":"en","tags":["db"]}'); For a store with an embedding column, give the vector literal as the fourth value. DOCUMENT WRITE INTO articles VALUES ('a2', 'Nearest-neighbor search', '{"lang":"en"}', '[0.12, 0.04, 0.99]');

sections.heading: 16.2.2. Filtering documents
sections.text: DOCUMENT FILTER returns id, content, meta, and created_at. It also returns embedding when that column is present. It runs through SELECT. The WHERE argument is a Mongo-style filter in JSON. The engine parses it into the same whereExpr tree that a normal WHERE clause produces. DOCUMENT FILTER FROM articles WHERE '{"lang":"en"}' LIMIT 10; The empty filter '{}' is the all-documents wildcard. It parses with no error but makes no clauses. So the SELECT below it runs with no WHERE restriction. A LIMIT is applied only when it is greater than zero.

sections.heading: 16.2.3. Vector search
sections.text: DOCUMENT SEARCH ranks documents by similarity. It only works on a store that has an embedding column. If the store has no embedding column, it fails with SQLSTATE 42P01. The verb returns id, content, meta, and a computed score column. The score uses the store's distance kind. It compares the embedding column with the query vector you supply. You can add a Mongo-style filter to narrow the set of candidates. DOCUMENT SEARCH articles USING VECTOR '[0.10, 0.05, 0.98]' WHERE '{"lang":"en"}' LIMIT 5; Note, client-side ordering. In this release DOCUMENT SEARCH returns the score column. It does not run a server-side ORDER BY on it. The caller must sort on the score column on the client side. A server-side ORDER BY on the score expression is planned. So a LIMIT caps the candidate rows that get a score. It does not give you the top-K by distance.

sections.heading: 16.2.4. Deleting documents
sections.text: DOCUMENT DELETE runs through DELETE. It requires a filter argument. If you leave the filter out, it raises SQLSTATE 42601. This is a deliberate guardrail. To wipe a whole store you must pass the wildcard '{}' on purpose. The engine turns it into a 1 = 1 sentinel predicate. This lets the delete path tell "delete all on purpose" apart from "missing WHERE clause". -- delete one matching document DOCUMENT DELETE FROM articles WHERE '{"lang":"de"}'; -- intentionally wipe the whole store DOCUMENT DELETE FROM articles WHERE '{}'; You remove a document store and its backing table with DROP DOCUMENT STORE. This drops the doc_<name> table and removes the store from the registry.

sections.heading: 16.3. Graph Store
sections.text: A graph store is a bitemporal knowledge graph. You create it with CREATE GRAPH STORE. It keeps two backing tables. kg_<name>_nodes id VARCHAR(255) PK NOT NULL, type VARCHAR(64), props VARCHAR(8000) (JSON-shaped, may be NULL), valid_from TIMESTAMP NOT NULL. kg_<name>_edges id BIGINT PK AUTO_INCREMENT, src / dst VARCHAR(255) NOT NULL, rel VARCHAR(128) NOT NULL, props VARCHAR(8000), valid_from TIMESTAMP NOT NULL, and invalid_at TIMESTAMP where NULL means "currently active". The invalid_at column is the heart of the temporal model. Edges are never updated in place to change their meaning. Instead, you replace an edge. The old version is closed by stamping its invalid_at. A fresh row then records the new fact. This lets you answer "what did the graph look like at time T".

sections.heading: 16.3.1. Upserting nodes
sections.text: GRAPH UPSERT NODE sets the latest type and props for a node id. First it looks up the id in the nodes table's primary-key B+ tree. If the node exists, it runs an UPDATE in place. This avoids a delete-then-insert round-trip. That round-trip works badly with same-transaction MVCC visibility. If the node does not exist, it runs an INSERT for a new row. When you give no type, the node type defaults to the literal node. The valid_from stamp is set to the current UTC time on every upsert. GRAPH UPSERT NODE INTO kg VALUES ('person:alice', 'Person', '{"name":"Alice"}');

sections.heading: 16.3.2. Upserting edges
sections.text: GRAPH UPSERT EDGE records a directed, typed relationship. It stores it as a bitemporal triple (src, rel, dst). It does two steps. It runs an UPDATE. This stamps invalid_at = NOW() on any still-live row with the same (src, rel, dst). Still-live means rows where invalid_at IS NULL. This closes the old version of the fact. Then it runs an INSERT for a fresh edge row with valid_from = NOW() and invalid_at = NULL. The edge id is auto-incremented. You do not supply it. GRAPH UPSERT EDGE INTO kg VALUES ('person:alice', 'WORKS_AT', 'org:acme', '{"since":2021}'); Old versions are kept. So the edge table grows as facts change. This is the cost of a graph you can fully audit and travel back in time through.

sections.heading: 16.3.3. Querying neighbors
sections.text: GRAPH NEIGHBORS OF follows outgoing edges from a node. It runs through SELECT. It returns src, rel, dst, props, valid_from, and invalid_at. The OF '<id>' clause is required. If it is missing, you get SQLSTATE 42601. The temporal filter depends on whether you give AS OF. No AS OF Only currently-live edges are returned. The filter is src = id AND invalid_at IS NULL. With AS OF ts Edges that were live at that moment are returned. The filter is src = id AND valid_from <= ts AND (invalid_at IS NULL OR invalid_at > ts). -- current neighbors GRAPH NEIGHBORS OF 'person:alice' IN kg; -- neighbors as the graph stood at a past moment GRAPH NEIGHBORS OF 'person:alice' IN kg AS OF '2024-01-01 00:00:00'; Note, depth is single-hop in this release. The grammar accepts a DEPTH N clause. The default is 1. For now the engine only runs depth-1 traversal as a single SELECT. Multi-hop BFS expansion is planned. Until then, you build depth-2 traversals by making two NEIGHBORS calls and joining the results on the client side. You remove a graph store and both of its backing tables with DROP GRAPH STORE.

sections.heading: 16.4. Message Log
sections.text: A message log is an append-only chat-history structure. You create it with CREATE MESSAGE LOG. It is backed by a table named ml_<name>. seqBIGINT PRIMARY KEY AUTO_INCREMENT, the monotonic sequence number. session_idVARCHAR(255), NOT NULL. roleVARCHAR(64), for example user or assistant. contentVARCHAR(8000). metaVARCHAR(8000), JSON-shaped, may be NULL. created_atTIMESTAMP, NOT NULL, UTC write time. The store can also record an optional ttl_days retention hint when you create it. The content column is VARCHAR(8000) on purpose. It is not an unbounded type. This keeps each row small enough to fit inline in the slotted-page format. If you need larger payloads, split the content into parts on the client side.

sections.heading: 16.4.1. Appending entries
sections.text: LOG APPEND runs through INSERT with an explicit column list. The columns are session_id, role, content, meta, and created_at. The engine fills the seq column through its auto-increment path. It sets created_at to the current UTC time. So the caller never supplies them. LOG APPEND INTO chat VALUES ('sess-42', 'user', 'Hello there', '{"client":"web"}');

sections.heading: 16.4.2. Reading entries
sections.text: LOG READ returns seq, role, content, meta, and created_at. It filters by session_id. It runs through SELECT. The WHERE SESSION = '<sid>' clause is required. If it is missing, you get SQLSTATE 42601. You can add a LAST n form to cap the result to the most recent n rows. LOG READ FROM chat WHERE SESSION = 'sess-42'; LOG READ FROM chat WHERE SESSION = 'sess-42' LAST 20;

sections.heading: 16.4.3. Truncating and counting
sections.text: LOG TRUNCATE runs through DELETE. It also requires a WHERE SESSION clause. Without a BEFORE clause it deletes every row for the session. With BEFORE n it deletes only rows whose seq < n. This is the natural way to prune old turns and keep recent history. -- drop the whole session LOG TRUNCATE FROM chat WHERE SESSION = 'sess-42'; -- keep only seq >= 100 LOG TRUNCATE FROM chat WHERE SESSION = 'sess-42' BEFORE 100; LOG COUNT returns the number of entries for a session. It uses a COUNT(*) that runs through SELECT. LOG COUNT FROM chat WHERE SESSION = 'sess-42'; Note, append-only by convention. The log is "append-only" in a simple sense. The verbs only add rows with LOG APPEND or bulk-prune rows with LOG TRUNCATE. There is no in-place update verb. The backing table is a normal table. So the ttl_days retention hint is recorded, but these verbs do not enforce it on their own. You prune on purpose with LOG TRUNCATE.

sections.heading: 16.5. Checkpoint Store
sections.text: A checkpoint store is a native backend for agent state snapshots. Its design follows LangGraph's checkpoint saver. You create it with CREATE CHECKPOINT STORE. It is backed by a table named ck_<name> with a fixed eight-column layout. thread_idVARCHAR(255), NOT NULL. checkpoint_nsVARCHAR(255), the namespace. It can be NULL. An empty string maps to NULL in this engine. checkpoint_idVARCHAR(255), NOT NULL, primary key. parent_idVARCHAR(255). ck_valuesJSON, the serialized checkpoint state. It is named ck_values and not values to avoid the SQL reserved word. metadataJSON. parent_configJSON. created_atTIMESTAMP, set by the engine on each PUT. CREATE CHECKPOINT STORE accepts an optional WITH (retention = '...') clause. The retention string is recorded with the store. DROP CHECKPOINT STORE accepts CASCADE. In this release the drop always removes the backing table and the registry entry.

sections.heading: 16.5.1. Writing checkpoints (PUT)
sections.text: CHECKPOINT PUT writes one eight-column row through INSERT. You supply seven fields. They are thread_id, checkpoint_ns, checkpoint_id, parent_id, ck_values, metadata, and parent_config. The engine stamps the eighth field, created_at. Any field may be sent as NULL. There is also a PUT WRITES variant. In this release the application handles the difference between PUT and PUT WRITES. CHECKPOINT PUT INTO agent_state VALUES ('thread-1', '', 'ckpt-001', NULL, '{"step":1}', '{"source":"input"}', NULL);

sections.heading: 16.5.2. Reading checkpoints (GET / LIST)
sections.text: Both read verbs run through SELECT. They use a star projection over the backing table. They use a WHERE thread_id = '...' filter. They use ORDER BY created_at DESC, so the newest row comes first. The two verbs differ as follows. CHECKPOINT GET Returns the latest checkpoint for a thread with LIMIT 1. You can add an AT '<checkpoint_id>' clause to narrow the filter to one checkpoint. That picks a single fixed row. So the LIMIT is dropped when you give AT. CHECKPOINT LIST Returns the full history for a thread. You can cap it with a positive LIMIT. CHECKPOINT GET FROM agent_state WHERE THREAD = 'thread-1'; CHECKPOINT GET FROM agent_state WHERE THREAD = 'thread-1' AT 'ckpt-001'; CHECKPOINT LIST FROM agent_state WHERE THREAD = 'thread-1' LIMIT 50; Note, direct table access. The DDL commands CREATE and DROP CHECKPOINT STORE and the PUT, GET, and LIST verbs all work on the same ck_<name> table. The backing table uses a ck_ prefix on purpose. It does not use a leading-underscore form. The lexer's identifier pattern is [A-Za-z][A-Za-z0-9_]*, which does not allow a leading underscore. This also means you can use the table directly with plain INSERT and SELECT. See also the CHECKPOINT reference page.

sections.heading: 16.6. Common Errors
sections.text: All four stores raise errors through the standard engine error path. Each error has a SQLSTATE code. You will see these codes most often. SQLSTATEMeaning in a store context 42601Syntax or usage error. The store name is missing, or a required clause is left out. For example, DOCUMENT DELETE without a filter, GRAPH NEIGHBORS without OF, or LOG READ, COUNT, or TRUNCATE without WHERE SESSION. 42704The store does not exist. Every DML verb can raise this. DROP without IF EXISTS can also raise it. 42710A store with the same name already exists. This happens on CREATE without the idempotent form. 42P01DOCUMENT SEARCH on a store created without an embedding column. 22023Invalid Mongo-style filter JSON passed to DOCUMENT FILTER, DOCUMENT SEARCH, or DOCUMENT DELETE.

sections.heading: 16.7. Compatibility
sections.text: None of the four store types are in the SQL standard. PostgreSQL has no direct counterpart for any of them. They are EvolutionDB extensions. Here are some honest points of contrast. Idempotency keyword. The CREATE ... STORE statements use IF EXISTS as the idempotent form. They do not use PostgreSQL's IF NOT EXISTS. The meaning is the same. It skips creation when the store is already there. Fixed schemas. Every backing table has a fixed column layout. You cannot define it yourself. You cannot add your own columns through the store commands. Documents vs. jsonb / pgvector. PostgreSQL applications join a jsonb column with the third-party pgvector extension. EvolutionDB puts JSON storage and vector search into a single store with DOCUMENT verbs. HNSW build parameters and similar tuning knobs are not exposed. Graph traversal. PostgreSQL writes graph queries with recursive CTEs over user tables. EvolutionDB ships a dedicated bitemporal model. It has invalid_at versioning and an AS OF snapshot filter. Multi-hop traversal is single-hop in this release. Vector ordering. Top-K ranking for DOCUMENT SEARCH is done on the client side for now. Server-side ORDER BY on the score is planned.

sections.heading: 16.8. See Also
sections.text: CREATE DOCUMENT STORE CREATE GRAPH STORE CREATE MESSAGE LOG CREATE CHECKPOINT STORE CHECKPOINT INSERT · SELECT · UPDATE · DELETE

sections.heading: Introduction
sections.text: 18. Subscriptions & CDC Durable subscriptions turn EvolutionDB's short-lived LISTEN/NOTIFY channels into change-data-capture streams that last and can be replayed. When a notification commits on a channel, it is saved to a mailbox on disk. A consumer can read that mailbox, acknowledge it, and replay it after reconnecting.

sections.heading: 18.1. Overview
sections.text: EvolutionDB has an in-memory LISTEN/NOTIFY registry. It sends a payload to every connection that is listening on a channel right now. This delivery is best-effort. If no client is connected when the notification commits, the notification is lost. A durable subscription fixes this gap. It registers a named mailbox that is saved and bound to one channel. Each time a NOTIFY commits on that channel, the payload is added to the mailbox with a sequence number that always goes up. A consumer that was offline can connect again later. It can replay everything that arrived in the meantime with RESUME SUBSCRIPTION. It can also move an acknowledgement watermark forward with ACK SUBSCRIPTION. A background daemon then prunes what the consumer has already processed. Durable subscriptions are EvolutionDB's change-data-capture (CDC) building block. Application code or triggers emit NOTIFY on a channel. One or more durable subscribers read that stream at their own pace. Delivery is at-least-once. The stream can be replayed after a reconnect. How this relates to in-memory NOTIFY. Durable saving runs after the in-memory listener fan-out. It runs inside the same broadcast that happens at commit time. Live listeners are served first. The catalog walk that fills durable mailboxes only happens after the in-memory broadcast for the channel is delivered. So the fast path costs nothing when no subscription is set up. See NOTIFY and LISTEN.

sections.heading: 18.2. The subscription model
sections.text: A subscription is a catalog object. It has a unique name and is bound to exactly one channel. Each subscription owns a private backing table. This table acts as its mailbox.

sections.heading: 18.2.1. Backing table
sections.text: When you create a subscription named s, EvolutionDB creates a backing heap table named sub_s in the current schema. The table always has this fixed layout. CREATE TABLE sub_s ( seq BIGINT PRIMARY KEY, -- monotonic, server-assigned payload VARCHAR(8000), -- the NOTIFY payload; '' for payload-less NOTIFY posted_at TIMESTAMP NOT NULL -- UTC append time, 'YYYY-MM-DD HH:MM:SS' ); One row is written for each published NOTIFY. The seq column is the primary key. The backing table's B+ tree keeps rows in ascending order by sequence. You do not need an ORDER BY to read them in arrival order. The posted_at stamp is set on the server in UTC at publish time.

sections.heading: 18.2.2. Subscription catalog fields
sections.text: The system catalog stores one descriptor for each subscription. SHOW SUBSCRIPTIONS returns these columns. name text Unique subscription name. Acts as the catalog key. channel text The single NOTIFY channel that this subscription is bound to. Channel matching at publish time is case-insensitive. last_ack_seq bigint The acknowledgement watermark. It starts at 0, which means nothing is acknowledged yet. RESUME returns only rows with seq > last_ack_seq. The prune daemon deletes rows with seq <= last_ack_seq. next_seq bigint The sequence number to give to the next published row. It starts at 1. After each successful append, it goes up by one and is saved. ttl_days int How long unacknowledged rows are kept, in days. The default is 7. A value of 0 turns off the TTL prune. The acknowledged-row prune still runs.

sections.heading: 18.3.1. CREATE SUBSCRIPTION
sections.text: CREATE SUBSCRIPTION name FOR CHANNEL channel CREATE SUBSCRIPTION IF NOT EXISTS name FOR CHANNEL channel This registers a durable subscription. It also creates its sub_<name> backing table in the same step. The watermark starts at 0. The next sequence starts at 1. The TTL starts at the 7-day default. Both name and channel are required. If you leave out either one, you get a syntax error. The error code is 42601. CREATE SUBSCRIPTION orders_cdc FOR CHANNEL order_events; If a subscription with that name already exists, the statement fails with 42710. If you add IF NOT EXISTS, the statement does nothing instead.

sections.heading: 18.3.2. DROP SUBSCRIPTION
sections.text: DROP SUBSCRIPTION name DROP SUBSCRIPTION IF EXISTS name This unregisters the subscription and drops its backing table. Without IF EXISTS, dropping a subscription that does not exist fails with 42704. With IF EXISTS, the statement does nothing instead. DROP SUBSCRIPTION IF EXISTS orders_cdc;

sections.heading: 18.4.1. RESUME SUBSCRIPTION
sections.text: RESUME SUBSCRIPTION name This returns the backlog for the subscription. The result set has the columns (seq, payload, posted_at). It includes every mailbox row whose seq is strictly greater than the current last_ack_seq. The rows come back in ascending seq order. RESUME is read-only and idempotent. It never moves the watermark forward and never deletes rows. You get the same backlog until you acknowledge it. Resuming a subscription that does not exist fails with 42704. RESUME SUBSCRIPTION orders_cdc; -- seq | payload | posted_at -- -----+--------------------------+--------------------- -- 1 | {"id":42,"op":"insert"} | 2026-06-12 09:14:03 -- 2 | {"id":43,"op":"insert"} | 2026-06-12 09:14:05

sections.heading: 18.4.2. ACK SUBSCRIPTION
sections.text: ACK SUBSCRIPTION name UPTO seq This moves the acknowledgement watermark to seq. The operation only moves last_ack_seq forward. It is idempotent. ACK ... UPTO 5 followed by ACK ... UPTO 3 leaves the watermark at 5. The acknowledged sequence does not have to match a row that is actually stored. The watermark just moves, and the prune daemon catches up on its next tick. Acknowledging a subscription that does not exist fails with 42704. ACK SUBSCRIPTION orders_cdc UPTO 2;

sections.heading: 18.5. End-to-end lifecycle
sections.text: A full CDC loop joins the publisher side with the durable consumer side. The publisher side is NOTIFY. -- 1. enroll a durable subscriber on a channel CREATE SUBSCRIPTION orders_cdc FOR CHANNEL order_events; -- 2. producers emit notifications (e.g. from application code or a trigger) NOTIFY order_events, '{"id":42,"op":"insert"}'; NOTIFY order_events, '{"id":43,"op":"insert"}'; -- 3. a consumer reconnects later and drains the backlog RESUME SUBSCRIPTION orders_cdc; -- rows seq 1, 2 -- 4. after processing, acknowledge progress so the daemon can prune ACK SUBSCRIPTION orders_cdc UPTO 2; -- 5. inspect every subscription's watermark and TTL SHOW SUBSCRIPTIONS;

sections.heading: 18.6. Retention & pruning
sections.text: A background auto-reclaim daemon trims each subscription's backing table on a fixed interval. On every tick it walks the mailbox. It deletes a row when one of these conditions is true. Acknowledged. The row's seq is <= last_ack_seq. This prune always runs, no matter what the TTL is. TTL-expired. ttl_days > 0 and the row's posted_at is older than ttl_days × 86 400 seconds. This catches unacknowledged rows from consumers that never came back. Rows with a NULL timestamp are not TTL-pruned. The daemon collects the rows to remove and deletes them in bulk. It uses the same write lock as the other retention passes. It removes each row from both the heap and the PK B+ tree. It handles at most 256 rows per subscription per tick. A very large backlog is cleared over several ticks, not in one pass. Tuning the interval. The reclaim interval is 30 seconds by default. You can lower it for tests with the EVOSQL_AUTO_RECLAIM_INTERVAL_SEC environment variable. It accepts a value from 1 to 30 seconds.

sections.heading: 18.7. Error codes
sections.text: Subscription statements set these SQLSTATE values. SQLSTATEConditionRaised by 42601Missing subscription name, or missing FOR CHANNELCREATE SUBSCRIPTION 42710Subscription name already exists (without IF NOT EXISTS)CREATE SUBSCRIPTION 42704Subscription does not exist (without IF EXISTS)DROP, RESUME, ACK

sections.heading: 18.8. Comparison with PostgreSQL
