sections.text: EvolutionDB's syntax copies PostgreSQL's CREATE SUBSCRIPTION keyword on purpose. But the meaning is completely different. These are not logical-replication subscriptions. Source of data PostgreSQL subscriptions read a logical replication stream from a publisher's WAL. EvolutionDB subscriptions read committed NOTIFY payloads on a named channel. There is no upstream node, publication, or replication slot. Binding PostgreSQL binds a subscription to a PUBLICATION on a remote CONNECTION. EvolutionDB binds a subscription to a local channel with FOR CHANNEL. There is no publication object and no connection string. Delivery & replay EvolutionDB uses a pull-based mailbox with a cursor you control. RESUME replays. ACK ... UPTO moves a watermark forward. A daemon prunes. PostgreSQL applies changes on its own with apply workers. It has no client-driven RESUME/ACK like this. Payload An EvolutionDB payload is the free-form text of a NOTIFY. The backing column caps it at 8000 bytes. It is not a decoded row change. EvolutionDB does not read the payload. The CDC structure is whatever the producer chooses to encode. This is usually JSON. It can include the operation, keys, and columns. Standard SQL Subscriptions, RESUME, and ACK are EvolutionDB extensions. They are not part of the SQL standard. RESUME and ACK are EvolutionDB-specific verbs with no PostgreSQL equivalent. Notes & limitations. A subscription binds to exactly one channel. The channel is set at creation and cannot be changed. There is no ALTER SUBSCRIPTION. To rebind, drop the subscription and create it again. ttl_days defaults to 7. You cannot set it through DDL in this release. The prune daemon honors it, but SHOW SUBSCRIPTIONS only shows it read-only. The backing table is named sub_<name> in the current schema. It is an internal detail. Use only RESUME and ACK to read it. Do not query or change it directly. Delivery is at-least-once. There is no automatic deduplication. A consumer that does not ACK will see the same rows again on the next RESUME. Durable saving happens at NOTIFY commit time. Notifications sent in a transaction that rolls back are never saved. Channel matching is case-insensitive. Subscription and channel names follow the catalog name-length limit.

sections.heading: 18.9. See also
sections.text: NOTIFY. Emit a payload on a channel. This is the publisher side of CDC. LISTEN. Subscribe a live connection to a channel. This is transient and in-memory. CREATE SUBSCRIPTION, DROP SUBSCRIPTION, RESUME SUBSCRIPTION, ACK SUBSCRIPTION. These are the command reference pages. SHOW SUBSCRIPTIONS. List registered subscriptions and their watermarks.

sections.heading: Introduction
sections.text: 4. SQL Syntax This page shows how EvolutionDB reads SQL text. It covers tokens, identifiers, literals, comments, operators, and keywords. It also shows how statements start and end. This chapter describes the lexical structure of the SQL dialect that EvolutionDB 3.0 accepts. These rules turn a stream of characters into the tokens the grammar reads. Everything here comes from the Flex scanner (evolution/parser/evolexer.l) and the Bison grammar (evolution/parser/evoparser.y). When the dialect differs from the SQL standard or from PostgreSQL, this page notes the difference. Lineage. The scanner is case-insensitive and reentrant. Its keyword set mixes MySQL and PostgreSQL spellings. For example, both INT8 and BIGINT map to the same type. This mix is on purpose, for better compatibility. EvolutionDB speaks the PostgreSQL wire protocol. It also accepts many MySQL-style forms at the lexical level. A token can exist here without full PostgreSQL meaning. To see what each form really does, read the per-command pages under SQL Commands.

sections.heading: 4.1. Lexical Overview
sections.text: SQL input is a sequence of tokens. Whitespace or comments separate them when needed. The scanner knows these token classes. KeywordsReserved words such as SELECT, FROM, CREATE. Matched case-insensitively. See §4.7. IdentifiersNames of tables, columns, indexes, and aliases, either bare or backtick-quoted. See §4.2. Literals (constants)String, numeric, boolean, NULL, hex, and bit-string constants. See §4.3. OperatorsArithmetic, comparison, logical, bitwise, JSON, and vector operators. See §4.5. User variablesSession variables prefixed with @. See §4.4. Comments and whitespaceDiscarded before reaching the grammar. See §4.6. The scanner moves a byte cursor named g_lex_pos forward on every token. This lets the parser copy out the exact text of a subquery. Correlated and LATERAL subqueries use this cursor so the engine can re-execute them. You do not see this at the syntax level. It is why subquery text comes back exactly as you wrote it.

sections.heading: 4.2. Identifiers and Names
sections.text: An unquoted identifier (token NAME) must begin with an ASCII letter. It may continue with letters, digits, and underscores. [A-Za-z][A-Za-z0-9_]* This is the exact pattern in the scanner. The results differ from the SQL standard and from PostgreSQL in a few ways. An identifier cannot start with an underscore. So _tmp is not a valid bare identifier. PostgreSQL does allow a leading _. Only ASCII letters work in bare identifiers. Non-ASCII and Unicode letters are not part of the NAME pattern. The lexer sets no length cap. But identifiers go into fixed-size buffers later. Each part of a qualified table.column reference has a 127-character limit. See §4.2.2.

sections.heading: 4.2.1. Backtick-Quoted Identifiers
sections.text: To use a reserved word as a name, or a name that does not fit the bare pattern, wrap it in backticks. This is the MySQL convention. It is not the SQL-standard double quote. `any chars except backtick, slash, backslash, dot, newline` The scanner removes the outer backticks and returns the inner text as a NAME. If a backtick has no closing backtick before the end of the line, the scanner raises "unterminated quoted name". CREATE TABLE `orders` ( `select` INTEGER, -- reserved word used as a column name `order date` DATE -- embedded space is legal inside backticks ); Double quotes are string-like, not identifier-like. In the SQL standard and PostgreSQL, "foo" is a delimited identifier. EvolutionDB is different. Its scanner treats a double-quoted run as a string literal. It uses the same lexer rule as single-quoted strings. To quote an identifier, use backticks. See §4.3.1.

sections.heading: 4.2.2. Qualified Names
sections.text: A name can have a dotted prefix. The grammar accepts two-part names in expression and table positions. -- column qualified by table/alias SELECT o.total FROM orders o; -- schema-qualified table name SELECT * FROM public.orders; In expression position, a.b becomes one column reference of the form a.b. Each part is clamped to 127 characters. The special pseudo-table EXCLUDED works in the EXCLUDED.col form. You use it inside INSERT … ON CONFLICT … DO UPDATE.

sections.heading: 4.3.1. String Constants
sections.text: A string constant is a character sequence bounded by single quotes or double quotes. '( \\. | '' | [^'\n] )*' "( \\. | "" | [^"\n] )*" A string supports two ways to escape characters. Doubled quote. Write '' for a literal single quote. This is the SQL-standard form. Write "" for a literal double quote inside a double-quoted string. The parser turns the doubled quote into a single character. Backslash escape. The scanner accepts a backslash followed by any character. The pattern is \\.. A string may not span a newline. A quote opened with no closing quote before the end of the line raises "Unterminated string". SELECT 'it''s here'; -- doubled quote -> it's here SELECT "a double-quoted literal"; -- double quotes = string, not identifier No dollar-quoting, no E'' prefix. This lexer does not support PostgreSQL dollar-quoted strings like $tag$ … $tag$. It also does not support the escape-string prefix E'…'. Long literals do work. A multi-kilobyte vector written as text is one example. The literal buffer grows on the heap to fit the token. But the literal must still stay on a single line.

sections.heading: 4.3.2. Numeric Constants
sections.text: The scanner distinguishes exact integers from approximate (floating-point) numbers. INTNUM, integerPattern -?[0-9]+, parsed with atoi. An optional leading minus sign is part of the integer token itself. APPROXNUM, approximateA number with a decimal point, an exponent, or both. Examples are 3.14, .5, 1E10, and 2.5e-3. Parsed with atof. SELECT 42, -7, 3.14159, .5, 6.022E23; Precision caveat. Integers are read with atoi, so they use the C int range. Approximate numbers are read with atof, so they use the C double range. The lexer does not promote values outside those ranges to arbitrary precision. Pick your column types with this in mind. See §4.7.2.

sections.heading: 4.3.3. Boolean and NULL Constants
sections.text: The keywords TRUE, FALSE, and UNKNOWN become a boolean constant. TRUE has the value 1. FALSE has the value 0. UNKNOWN has the internal value -1 for three-valued logic. The keyword NULL is its own token named NULLX. SELECT TRUE, FALSE, NULL; SELECT * FROM t WHERE active IS UNKNOWN; -- IS UNKNOWN is treated as IS NULL

sections.heading: 4.3.4. Hexadecimal and Bit-String Constants
sections.text: The scanner knows two non-decimal literal forms. It returns them as string tokens. Later stages read them. FormPatternExample Hex stringX'[0-9A-F]+' or 0X[0-9A-F]+X'1F4', 0XFF Bit stringB'[01]+' or 0B[01]+B'1010', 0B1101 The pattern matches hex digits in uppercase. The whole scanner is case-insensitive. So lowercase hex digits work too.

sections.heading: 4.3.5. Array Literals
sections.text: An array constant uses the ARRAY[…] constructor. The elements are expressions separated by commas. You write an empty array as ARRAY[]. SELECT ARRAY[1, 2, 3]; INSERT INTO t (tags) VALUES (ARRAY['a', 'b']); SELECT ARRAY[]; You can subscript array elements with expr[ index ]. The subscript binds tighter than any arithmetic operator. To declare an array column type, add [] to a base type. See §4.7.2.

sections.heading: 4.4. User (Session) Variables
sections.text: A user variable begins with @. It holds a value for the session. The scanner accepts four spellings. @nameBare form with the pattern @[0-9a-z_.$]+. The bare form matches only lowercase letters, digits, _, ., and $. @"name" / @`name` / @'name'Quoted form. It allows spaces or mixed case in the variable name. You assign variables with SET, using = or :=. You read them inside expressions. SET @threshold := 100; SELECT * FROM orders WHERE total > @threshold; A quoted variable with no closing quote raises "unterminated quoted user variable". See SET for assignment details.

sections.heading: 4.5. Operators
sections.text: The scanner returns the operator tokens below. Single-character punctuation in the set - + & ~ | ^ / % * ( ) , . ; ! [ ] is returned as itself. Multi-character operators have their own tokens.

sections.heading: 4.5.1. Comparison Operators
sections.text: All comparison operators collapse to one COMPARISON token. The token carries a sub-code. The parser then branches on that sub-code. This means the = in a WITH (m = 16) index option and the = in a WHERE clause are the same lexical token. OperatorMeaningSub-code =equal4 <less than1 >greater than2 != / <>not equal3 <=less than or equal5 >=greater than or equal6 <=>NULL-safe equal / cosine-distance in ORDER BY12

sections.heading: 4.5.2. Logical, Bitwise, JSON, and Vector Operators
sections.text: TokenSpelling(s)Role Logical ANDAND, &&boolean conjunction Logical OROR, ||boolean disjunction. Here || is OR, not string concatenation Logical XOR / NOTXOR, NOT, !exclusive-or / negation Arithmetic+ - * / %, MODadd, subtract, multiply, divide, modulo Bitwise& | ^and / or / xor Shift<<, >>left / right bit-shift JSON->, ->>JSON member access. The ->> form returns text Vector<->, <#>L2 distance / negative inner product Assignment:=variable assignment, used in SET and in @v := expr MySQL-isms to watch. Two operators behave the way MySQL defines them. They do not follow PostgreSQL or the SQL standard. First, || is logical OR. It is not string concatenation. Use CONCAT(...) for that. Second, ^ is bitwise XOR. It is not exponentiation. Use POWER(...) for that. The <-> and <#> vector operators are EvolutionDB and pgvector-style extensions.

sections.heading: 4.5.3. Operator Precedence
sections.text: The grammar fixes the precedence. The list goes from lowest binding to highest. := (assignment, right-associative) OR XOR AND IN, IS, LIKE, REGEXP (non-associative) NOT, ! BETWEEN COMPARISON (= <> < > <= >= <=>) <->, <#> (vector distance) | then & (bitwise) <<, >> (shift) +, - *, /, %, MOD ^ (bitwise XOR) ->, ->> (JSON) unary minus [ (array subscript, binds tightest) Use parentheses when your intent matters. The form (expr) is always valid for grouping.

sections.heading: 4.6. Comments and Whitespace
sections.text: The scanner knows three comment styles. It drops all of them. StyleFormNotes Hash line comment# … (to end of line)MySQL-style Double-dash line comment-- … (to end of line)Requires a space or tab immediately after -- Block comment/* … */May span multiple lines; an unclosed block raises "unclosed comment" The -- rule is stricter than PostgreSQL. The scanner pattern is "--"[ \t].*. A dash-dash comment counts only when a space or tab follows it. So --comment with no space is not a comment. The scanner reads it as operators. But -- comment with a space is a comment. PostgreSQL accepts -- with no space after it. Block comments do not nest here. PostgreSQL does nest them. Whitespace means a space, a tab, or a newline. It separates tokens. Other than that, it is ignored. Any character that matches no rule raises "mystery character".

sections.heading: 4.7. Keywords
sections.text: Keywords match case-insensitively. So SELECT, select, and Select are the same. The scanner has a few context-sensitive behaviors worth knowing. They help it tell similar tokens apart.

sections.heading: 4.7.1. Context-Sensitive Tokenization
sections.text: BETWEEN … ANDWhen the scanner reads BETWEEN, the lexer enters a special mode. In that mode the next AND comes back as the range-connector token, not the logical-AND operator. Outside that mode, AND is the boolean operator. NOT EXISTS / NOT INThe scanner reads NOT EXISTS as a single token. It is an EXISTS with a "negated" sub-flag. This has a side effect. CREATE … IF NOT EXISTS reaches the grammar as IF EXISTS with the negation flag set. The grammar comments point this out. Function-vs-keyword lookaheadNames like COUNT, SUM, AVG, MIN, MAX, RANK, IF, MOD, REPLACE, and YEAR peek at the next character. If it is (, they become a function token. If not, they come back as an ordinary identifier or keyword. So rank can be a column name while RANK() is a window function. Trailing-context functionsMany built-ins match only when ( follows them right away. This uses a trailing-context rule. Examples are CAST/"(", SUBSTRING/"(", and L2_DISTANCE/"(". Without the parenthesis, they fall through to the identifier rule.

sections.heading: 4.7.2. Data-Type Keywords
sections.text: The grammar's data_type rule encodes each type as an integer typeVal. The expression typeVal / 10000 gives the type family. The remainder carries length and precision. The table below lists the accepted type keywords and their MySQL and PostgreSQL aliases. FamilyKeyword(s) acceptedNotes IntegerTINYINT/INT1, SMALLINT/INT2, MEDIUMINT/MIDDLEINT, INT/INT4/INTEGER, BIGINT/INT8optional (n), UNSIGNED, ZEROFILL ApproximateREAL, DOUBLE/FLOAT8, FLOAT/FLOAT4optional length/precision Exact decimalDECIMAL/DEC/NUMERIC(p) or (p,s) BitBIToptional length CharacterCHAR/CHARACTER, VARCHAR/VARCHARACTERVARCHAR requires (n) TextTINYTEXT, TEXT, MEDIUMTEXT, LONGTEXToptional BINARY BinaryBINARY, VARBINARY, TINYBLOB, BLOB, MEDIUMBLOB, LONGBLOB Date/timeDATE, TIME, TIMESTAMP, DATETIME, YEAR EnumeratedENUM(...), SET(...)string value list BooleanBOOLEAN/BOOL Identifier/docUUID, JSON/JSONBJSONB aliases JSON VectorVECTOR(n)fixed dimension, works with HNSW indexes Arraytype[]append [] to any base type See CREATE TABLE for how these types work in column definitions. See §4.8.2 for column attributes.

sections.heading: 4.7.3. A Note on Reserved Words
sections.text: The keyword set is large because the dialect covers many features. These include window functions, vector search, document stores, graph stores, memory stores, scheduled jobs, and pub/sub. Not every keyword is fully reserved in every position. The grammar intentionally keeps some common words usable as names. For example, LEVEL is not reserved, so … AS level works. The scanner reads VALUE but returns it as an ordinary NAME. If you are unsure, wrap any name that might collide in backticks. See §4.2.1.

sections.heading: 4.8.1. Statement Termination
sections.text: The grammar's start symbol is a semicolon-terminated statement list. stmt_list : stmt ';' | stmt ';' stmt_list ; A semicolon (;) ends every statement. You can send many statements in one batch. Separate them with semicolons. The semicolon is part of the grammar and is required. It is not just a separator that the client strips. CREATE TABLE t (id INTEGER PRIMARY KEY); INSERT INTO t VALUES (1); SELECT * FROM t;

sections.heading: 4.8.2. Column Attributes (Lexical Forms)
sections.text: Inside a column definition, the grammar accepts these attribute spellings. See CREATE TABLE for what they mean. NOT NULL / NULLnullability DEFAULT …a string/number/boolean literal, CURRENT_TIMESTAMP, a function default (gen_random_uuid(), gen_random_uuid_v7(), snowflake_id()), or a parenthesized expression DEFAULT (expr) AUTO_INCREMENT / IDENTITYboth accepted, optionally with (start) or (start, step) PRIMARY KEY / KEY / UNIQUE / UNIQUE KEYkey constraints CHECK (expr)check constraint, optionally named via CONSTRAINT name CHECK (...) GENERATED ALWAYS AS (expr) [STORED|VIRTUAL] / AS (expr) [STORED|VIRTUAL]generated columns. A bare AS (expr) defaults to VIRTUAL COMMENT 'text'column comment. It is parsed and kept as metadata

sections.heading: 4.8.3. Statement Categories
sections.text: The grammar covers statements across these areas. Each one links to its reference page. Queries. SELECT with JOIN, GROUP BY, HAVING, ORDER BY, LIMIT/OFFSET, window functions, FOR UPDATE/FOR SHARE, and FOR SYSTEM_TIME AS OF. Data modification. INSERT with ON CONFLICT / ON DUPLICATE KEY UPDATE, UPDATE, DELETE, REPLACE, COPY, TRUNCATE. Schema. CREATE TABLE, ALTER TABLE, DROP TABLE, CREATE INDEX with USING HASH and USING HNSW, DROP INDEX, CREATE DOMAIN, CREATE SEQUENCE. Database, schema, and session. CREATE DATABASE, USE, SET, SHOW. Programmability. CREATE PROCEDURE, CALL, CREATE TRIGGER. Transactions and durability. BEGIN/COMMIT/ROLLBACK, SAVEPOINT, CHECKPOINT, RECLAIM, ANALYZE. Access control. CREATE USER, GRANT, REVOKE, CREATE POLICY for RLS. Async and specialized stores. LISTEN/NOTIFY, CREATE SUBSCRIPTION, CREATE JOB, CREATE MESSAGE LOG, CREATE DOCUMENT STORE, CREATE GRAPH STORE, CREATE ENTITY STORE, CREATE MEMORY STORE. Lexical notes and limits. Identifiers must start with an ASCII letter. A bare name cannot start with an underscore and cannot use Unicode letters. Use backticks for anything else. Double quotes mean strings, not identifiers. Use backticks to quote identifiers. String and quoted-name literals cannot cross a newline. A literal with no closing quote is a scan error. It does not continue on the next line. -- comments need a space or tab after them. --like-this is not a comment. Block comments do not nest. || is logical OR and ^ is bitwise XOR. This follows MySQL. Use CONCAT() for concatenation and POWER() for exponentiation. A leading - is part of a numeric literal at the lexical level. With operator precedence this is usually invisible. It is why negative numbers tokenize as single constants. No dollar-quoting and no E'…' escape-string prefix.

sections.heading: Introduction
sections.text: 21. Triggers & Procedures EvolutionDB stores procedures, functions, and triggers as raw SQL bodies. One text-driven engine runs all of them. The engine replaces parameters and OLD./NEW. column references with text. It splits the body into statements that follow the control flow. Then it runs each statement against the engine. Implementation note. The procedure language in EvolutionDB is not a compiled PL like PostgreSQL's PL/pgSQL. A body is kept word for word between BEGIN and the matching END. At run time the engine splits it on ; and on control-flow keyword boundaries. Then it runs each statement one by one. There is no type system for local variables. There is no plan caching. There is no overloading. A procedure or function is found by its name alone within a schema.

sections.heading: 21.1. Overview
sections.text: This page covers three kinds of stored objects. The system catalog of the current database and schema holds all of them. Procedure A named block of SQL with parameters. You run it with CALL. It is stored with its parameter list and body. The executor supports OUT/INOUT parameters and local DECLARE variables. Function You create it with CREATE FUNCTION … RETURNS type. Functions use the same catalog and the same body engine as procedures. The return type you declare is saved. Functions are stored next to procedures. The catalog flag is_function tells them apart. Trigger A BEFORE or AFTER block tied to one table and one of the INSERT/UPDATE/DELETE events. It fires once for each affected row. To run a trigger body, the engine wraps it in a temporary procedure and calls it. So the trigger reuses the full procedure engine. This includes IF, WHILE, SIGNAL, and more.

sections.heading: 21.2. CREATE PROCEDURE / FUNCTION
sections.text: See the reference page CREATE PROCEDURE for the full grammar. The synopsis below shows exactly what the parser accepts. CREATE [ OR REPLACE ] PROCEDURE name ( [ param_list ] ) [ AS ] BEGIN body END CREATE [ OR REPLACE ] FUNCTION name ( [ param_list ] ) RETURNS return_type [ AS ] BEGIN body END -- param_list element: [ IN | OUT | INOUT ] param_name data_type If you leave out the parameter mode, it defaults to IN. Functions accept a fixed set of return types. These are INT/INTEGER, VARCHAR, TEXT, BOOLEAN, FLOAT, DOUBLE, BIGINT, DATE, and TIMESTAMP. The engine stores up to 16 parameters (MAX_PROC_PARAMS). It quietly ignores extra parameters at parse time. The body is captured as raw text. The engine scans g_lex_input for the first BEGIN keyword and the matching standalone END. It tracks the depth of nested BEGIN…END blocks. It skips END IF, END WHILE, END LOOP, END FOREACH, and END CASE. A body can be at most 8192 bytes. The parameter list can be at most 1024 bytes. CREATE PROCEDURE add_user(IN uname VARCHAR(50), IN uage INT) AS BEGIN INSERT INTO users(name, age) VALUES (uname, uage); END; CREATE OR REPLACE FUNCTION double_it(n INT) RETURNS INT AS BEGIN RETURN n * 2; END; OR REPLACE semantics. When you give OR REPLACE and a procedure of the same name already exists, the engine drops the old definition and creates it again. Without OR REPLACE, a duplicate name fails with SQLSTATE 42P07 ("procedure/function already exists"). Lookup is by name only. So CREATE FUNCTION and CREATE PROCEDURE share one namespace per schema.

sections.heading: 21.3. Parameter and variable substitution
sections.text: EvolutionDB does not bind parameters as typed values. Instead it uses textual substitution before it runs each statement in the body. The engine walks the SQL. It replaces each parameter name with the matching argument text. It also replaces the positional markers $1, $2, and so on. The substitution skips text inside single-quoted string literals. So a literal that holds a parameter name is left alone. The quoting around an argument matters. A string argument keeps its quotes. For example, it arrives as 'alice'. A numeric argument arrives as bare digits. NULL arrives as the keyword NULL. A boolean arrives as TRUE/FALSE. The engine then parses the substituted text. So you must write the body to expect substituted SQL, not bound values. Security caveat. Arguments are spliced into SQL text. So a procedure call is really string interpolation. Pass only trusted or already-escaped values. There is no separate bind layer to stop injection. This is a chosen limit of the text-driven engine. It is not the parameterized-statement model that the SQL standard describes.

sections.heading: 21.4. Control flow inside a body
sections.text: The body splitter divides the body on ;. Then it splits again at control-flow keyword boundaries. A block map records the matching END markers. This lets the interpreter jump. The engine recognises the following constructs. ConstructForm ConditionalIF cond THEN … [ ELSEIF cond THEN … ] [ ELSE … ] END IF Pre-tested loopWHILE cond DO … END WHILE Unconditional loop[ label: ] LOOP … END LOOP Counted loopFOR var IN start..end DO … END FOR Query loopFOREACH vars IN (query) DO … END FOREACH Multi-branchCASE expr WHEN val THEN … [ ELSE … ] END CASE Loop controlLEAVE [label], ITERATE [label] Local variableDECLARE var TYPE [DEFAULT value], then SET var = expr CursorDECLARE c CURSOR FOR query, OPEN c, FETCH c INTO …, CLOSE c HandlerDECLARE { CONTINUE | EXIT } HANDLER FOR cond … Return (functions)RETURN expr Raise errorSIGNAL … (see §21.7) A loop can take an optional leading label:. This lets LEAVE/ITERATE target a loop that wraps it. The engine tracks up to 32 local variables, 8 cursors, and 8 handlers per call. A body is split into at most 128 statement units. CREATE PROCEDURE classify(score INT) AS BEGIN DECLARE grade VARCHAR(2) DEFAULT 'F'; IF score >= 90 THEN SET grade = 'A'; ELSEIF score >= 70 THEN SET grade = 'C'; END IF; INSERT INTO results(score, grade) VALUES (score, grade); END;

sections.heading: 21.5. CALL
sections.text: You run a stored procedure with CALL. See CALL. The parser only records the call name and arguments. The body runs in the adaptor layer after parsing returns. This avoids re-entering the parser mutex. CALL name ( [ arg [, arg ... ] ] ) An argument can be an integer literal, an approximate-number literal, a string literal, NULL, a boolean, a bare name, or a session variable (@var). The argument count must match the parameter count exactly. If it does not, the call fails with SQLSTATE 42883 ("procedure … expects N arguments, got M"). The engine accepts at most 16 arguments (MAX_CALL_ARGS). A procedure name that does not exist also raises 42883. CALL add_user('alice', 30); OUT / INOUT parameters. A call argument for an INOUT parameter can be a session variable (@var). In that case the engine reads the current value of the variable into the body before it runs. It can also write a result back to the caller's variable. This is how a procedure returns scalar data to the calling session.

sections.heading: 21.6. CREATE TRIGGER / DROP TRIGGER / ALTER TRIGGER
sections.text: The full grammar is on CREATE TRIGGER. A trigger ties a body to one table, one timing, and one event. It always fires FOR EACH ROW. CREATE TRIGGER name { BEFORE | AFTER } { INSERT | UPDATE | DELETE } ON table_name FOR EACH ROW BEGIN body END DROP TRIGGER [ IF EXISTS ] name ALTER TRIGGER name { ENABLE | DISABLE } The FOR EACH ROW clause is required. There is no statement-level (FOR EACH STATEMENT) trigger. There is no WHEN condition or column list on the event. The target table must exist. If it does not, creation fails with SQLSTATE 42P01. A duplicate trigger name raises 42P07. A new trigger is enabled by default. ALTER TRIGGER … DISABLE flips the catalog enabled flag. The trigger is then skipped at firing time, but it is not dropped. DROP TRIGGER is name-only. Unlike PostgreSQL, you do not name the table in DROP TRIGGER / ALTER TRIGGER. EvolutionDB scans every table in the current schema to find a trigger by name. So trigger names are scoped to the schema. The first match wins. OLD / NEW row references. Inside a trigger body, OLD.col and NEW.col refer to the row before and after the change. Before the body runs, the engine replaces these references with text. It uses the matching column value for the affected row. A numeric value is inserted bare. Everything else is single-quoted, and any embedded quotes are doubled. A missing value becomes NULL. Which values you get depends on the event. INSERT fills NEW. DELETE fills OLD. UPDATE fills both. BEFORE-trigger field assignment. A BEFORE trigger can change the row that is being written. It does this by assigning to a column with SET NEW.col = expr. The engine pulls these assignments out of the body. It first does the OLD/NEW substitution. Then it evaluates each expression with a SELECT. It writes the result back into the row before the row is stored. The engine honours up to 16 such assignments per trigger. CREATE TRIGGER stamp_created BEFORE INSERT ON orders FOR EACH ROW BEGIN SET NEW.created_at = NOW(); END; CREATE TRIGGER audit_del AFTER DELETE ON accounts FOR EACH ROW BEGIN INSERT INTO deleted_accounts(id, name) VALUES (OLD.id, OLD.name); END; Memory-store triggers. You can attach a trigger to a memory store with ON MEMORY STORE name. The parser stages the store name with an internal @mem: sentinel. At creation, the engine resolves the store through the catalog. It rewrites the store to its backing table mem_<name>. After that, the trigger fires just like a normal table trigger. A PUT event on a memory store is mapped to the INSERT ('I') event inside the engine. A store that does not exist raises SQLSTATE 42704.

sections.heading: 21.7. Trigger firing model, errors, and recursion
sections.text: The engine fires matching triggers from the INSERT/UPDATE/DELETE hook points. It fires them once per affected row. It filters them by timing, event, and the enabled flag. For each fired trigger, the engine wraps the substituted body as a temporary procedure. It calls the procedure, then drops it. So triggers and procedures run through the same engine. The steps are shown below. -- conceptual expansion performed internally per fired trigger CREATE OR REPLACE PROCEDURE __trig_<id>__() AS BEGIN <substituted body> END; CALL __trig_<id>__(); DROP PROCEDURE IF EXISTS __trig_<id>__; Recursion limit. A depth counter guards trigger execution. Nesting beyond 16 levels (MAX_TRIGGER_DEPTH) quietly stops further trigger firing. No error is raised. This prevents runaway recursion. Raising errors with SIGNAL. A body can stop with a user-defined error. This works for both procedures and triggers. SIGNAL [ SQLSTATE 'xxxxx' ] [ SET MESSAGE_TEXT = 'message' ] If you leave out SQLSTATE, it defaults to 45000 (unhandled user-defined exception). The default message is "Unhandled user-defined exception". A DECLARE … HANDLER FOR SQLEXCEPTION can catch the signal. A CONTINUE handler resumes after the failed statement. An EXIT handler unwinds the body. If there is no handler, the error passes to the caller. When a trigger body errors, the whole firing fails. The DML statement that started it is aborted. CREATE TRIGGER no_negatives BEFORE INSERT ON ledger FOR EACH ROW BEGIN IF NEW.amount < 0 THEN SIGNAL SQLSTATE '45000' SET MESSAGE_TEXT = 'amount must be non-negative'; END IF; END;

sections.heading: 21.8. SQLSTATE reference
sections.text: These are the error codes that the trigger and procedure paths raise. 42P01Trigger target table does not exist. 42P07Duplicate object. A procedure or function already exists (without OR REPLACE), or a trigger name already exists. 42883Procedure or function does not exist on CALL or DROP. The trigger does not exist on DROP without IF EXISTS. The argument count does not match on CALL. 42704Memory store named in ON MEMORY STORE does not exist. 45000Default SQLSTATE for an unqualified SIGNAL (user-defined exception). 53200Out of memory while preparing a CALL body for execution.

sections.heading: 21.9. Notes & limitations
sections.text: What EvolutionDB does not implement. Here is how it differs from PostgreSQL and the SQL standard. No row-vs-statement choice. Triggers are always FOR EACH ROW. There is no statement-level trigger. No WHEN condition, column list, or INSTEAD OF. The event is a single bare INSERT/UPDATE/DELETE with no filter. Views cannot carry triggers. One event per trigger. There is no INSERT OR UPDATE multi-event form. Create separate triggers. Table is not named on DROP/ALTER TRIGGER. Names are resolved by scanning the schema. So trigger names should be unique per schema. Textual parameter substitution, not value binding. Arguments and OLD/NEW references are spliced into SQL text. There is no typed parameter binding and no protection against injection. No procedure/function overloading. Lookup is by name within a schema. Functions and procedures share that namespace. Functions are not callable inline. A CREATE FUNCTION body runs through the same engine as a procedure. The return type is recorded. But the database does not let you call user functions inside a SELECT the way PostgreSQL does. Fixed capacities. Body ≤ 8192 bytes. ≤ 16 parameters/arguments. ≤ 128 statement units. ≤ 32 local variables, ≤ 8 cursors, ≤ 8 handlers per call. Trigger recursion depth ≤ 16. No trigger ordering guarantees by name. There are also no transition tables (REFERENCING … OLD/NEW TABLE).

sections.heading: 21.10. See also
sections.text: CREATE PROCEDURE. The full reference for CREATE/DROP PROCEDURE and FUNCTION. CREATE TRIGGER. The full reference for CREATE/DROP/ALTER TRIGGER. CALL. How to run a stored procedure. INSERT, UPDATE, DELETE. The DML statements whose hook points fire triggers. CREATE MEMORY STORE. The target of ON MEMORY STORE triggers.

sections.heading: Introduction
sections.text: 14. Vectors & Hybrid Search EvolutionDB stores dense embeddings in VECTOR(N) columns. Each vector has a fixed number of dimensions. You can rank vectors with four built-in distance metrics. An in-memory HNSW index makes nearest-neighbor queries faster. One hybrid-search call lets you mix an equality filter with vector ranking.

sections.heading: 14.1. Overview
sections.text: Vector support in EvolutionDB is built from four parts that work together. The VECTOR(N) data type. It is a fixed-size array of single-precision floats. It is stored inline in the binary tuple. Distance functions and operators. These are COSINE_DISTANCE, L2_DISTANCE, INNER_PRODUCT, and L1_DISTANCE. There are also the <-> and <#> operators. The HNSW index. You create it with CREATE INDEX … USING HNSW. You query it through the HNSW_KNN function or a cosine ORDER BY. Hybrid search with HNSW_FILTER_KNN. It folds a single equality predicate into vector ranking. It picks a pre-filter or post-filter strategy on its own. Relationship to PostgreSQL. The syntax is made to look like the pgvector extension. The engine recognizes VECTOR(N), USING HNSW, the vector_cosine_ops opclass family, the WITH (m=…, ef_construction=…) parameters, and the <-> and <#> operators. But the implementation is EvolutionDB's own. It uses an in-memory graph backed by brute force. See the Notes for details. EvolutionDB is not pgvector. It does not promise the same recall, persistence, or operator coverage. This chapter only describes what the engine really does.

sections.heading: 14.2. The VECTOR(N) Type
sections.text: A VECTOR(N) column holds exactly N single-precision float4 components. You set the dimension when you run CREATE TABLE. The dimension is part of the column's type. Rows cannot store vectors of a different length. VECTOR ( dimension ) Inside the engine, the dimension is folded into the type code as 260000 + N. The type family is 26. For example, VECTOR(3) is encoded as 260003 and VECTOR(1536) as 261536. The payload is stored inline in the tuple. It is N 4-byte little-endian floats in a row with no length prefix. The dimension comes from the column's type code, not from the stored bytes. StorageN × 4 bytes. The values are float4 little-endian. They are stored inline in the binary tuple. A VECTOR(1536) value takes 6144 bytes of column data. Maximum dimension16384. This limit is VECTOR_MAX_DIM. The engine rejects any dimension above it, whether declared or inserted. Element type32-bit IEEE float. You give values as decimal text. The engine converts them to float4 on insert. NULLA vector column can be NULL like any other column. The null bitmap marks it and no payload bytes are written.

sections.heading: 14.2.1. Vector literals
sections.text: You give a vector value as a string literal. There are three accepted forms. [f1,f2,…]A list of decimal floats in brackets. This is the standard text form. The engine uses it when it prints a vector back out. {f1,f2,…}A list in braces. The engine accepts it as the same kind of input. b64i8:<base64>A small quantized form. It is a little-endian float32 scale factor, then an int8[] array, all base64-encoded. On decode, each byte is dequantized as byte × (scale / 127). This keeps a 1024-dimension query literal near 1.4 KB. That stays well under the statement-size cap. It is meant for queries built by a machine. Whitespace inside a list is ignored. A NaN inside a list is rejected as a parse error. -- Declare and populate a table of embeddings CREATE TABLE docs ( id INTEGER PRIMARY KEY, title VARCHAR(200), topic VARCHAR(40), emb VECTOR(3) ); INSERT INTO docs (id, title, topic, emb) VALUES (1, 'intro to storage', 'storage', '[0.10, 0.20, 0.97]'), (2, 'btree internals', 'storage', '[0.11, 0.19, 0.98]'), (3, 'wire protocol', 'protocol','[0.90, 0.05, 0.10]'); See CREATE TABLE for the full column grammar. See INSERT for value syntax.

sections.heading: 14.3. Distance Functions and Operators
sections.text: Four scalar distance functions are available. You can use them anywhere an expression is allowed. That includes a projection, a WHERE clause, and an ORDER BY clause. Each one takes two vectors of equal length. Each one returns a DOUBLE distance. A smaller distance means more similar. FunctionOperatorReturnsDefinition COSINE_DISTANCE(a, b)<=> (in ORDER BY only)double1 − (a·b) / (‖a‖·‖b‖). Returns 1.0 if either vector is all-zero. L2_DISTANCE(a, b)<->doubleEuclidean distance, √Σ(aᵢ−bᵢ)². INNER_PRODUCT(a, b)<#>doubleNegated dot product, −Σ(aᵢ·bᵢ). It is negated so the most similar rows sort first. This matches the other metrics. L1_DISTANCE(a, b), doubleManhattan distance, Σ|aᵢ−bᵢ|. Three more vector helper functions complete the set. VECTOR_DIM(v)The number of components in v. VECTOR_NORM(v)The L2 norm of v. This is its magnitude. VECTOR_NORMALIZE(v)The unit vector that points the same way as v. -- Exact (un-indexed) nearest neighbors via a distance function in ORDER BY SELECT id, title, L2_DISTANCE(emb, '[0.10, 0.20, 0.97]') AS dist FROM docs ORDER BY dist ASC LIMIT 5; -- The L2 operator form is equivalent to L2_DISTANCE() SELECT id, emb <-> '[0.10, 0.20, 0.97]' AS dist FROM docs ORDER BY dist; Operator availability differs by metric. Only two infix operators exist. They are <-> for L2 and <#> for inner product. You can use both anywhere an expression is allowed. Cosine has no general infix operator. The <=> spelling works only in the ORDER BY position. For cosine anywhere else, call COSINE_DISTANCE(). There is no L1 operator at all. Use L1_DISTANCE() instead. This is narrower than pgvector. pgvector gives a full set of operators for each metric.

sections.heading: 14.3.1. Cosine ORDER BY
sections.text: You can rank a column by cosine distance against a query literal right inside ORDER BY. Use the <=> operator as shown below. ORDER BY column <=> '[f1,f2,…]' [ ASC | DESC ] SELECT id, title FROM docs ORDER BY emb <=> '[0.10, 0.20, 0.97]' ASC LIMIT 3; Only the cosine sub-operator works in this position. If you use another comparison operator with a string literal in ORDER BY, such as =, <, or >, the grammar rejects it with a clear error. It is not ignored without warning.

sections.heading: 14.4. The HNSW Index
sections.text: For larger collections, use an HNSW index. HNSW stands for Hierarchical Navigable Small World. It finds nearest neighbors without scanning every row at query time. You create it with the USING HNSW access method on a single VECTOR(N) column. CREATE INDEX [ IF NOT EXISTS ] name ON table USING HNSW ( column [ opclass ] ) [ WITH ( m = int [, ef_construction = int ] ) ]

sections.heading: 14.4.1. Operator classes (metric selection)
sections.text: The opclass is optional. It names the distance metric the index ranks by. If you leave it out, the index uses cosine. If you give an opclass name the engine does not know, it falls back to cosine. It does not raise an error. OpclassMetricInternal kind vector_cosine_ops (default)cosine distanceHNSW_DIST_COSINE (0) vector_l2_opsL2 / EuclideanHNSW_DIST_L2 (1) vector_ip_ops / vector_inner_opsinner productHNSW_DIST_INNER (2) vector_l1_opsL1 / ManhattanHNSW_DIST_L1 (3)

sections.heading: 14.4.2. Build parameters
sections.text: mThe most neighbors a node can have above layer 0. Nodes on layer 0 can have up to 2·m neighbors. The default is 16. A larger m connects the graph more and raises recall. It also uses more memory and more build time. ef_constructionThe beam width used while the engine inserts nodes during the build. The default is 64. Larger values build a better graph but take more build time. A bare CREATE INDEX … USING HNSW with no WITH clause uses ef_construction = 64. The brute-force build helper has its own default of 200. That default does not apply through DDL. The query-time beam width is ef_search. It is fixed at 64 in this release. You cannot yet set it per query. If your k is larger than ef_search, the engine widens the beam to k for that query. -- Cosine HNSW index with default m=16, ef_construction=64 CREATE INDEX docs_emb_idx ON docs USING HNSW (emb); -- L2 metric with explicit build parameters CREATE INDEX docs_emb_l2 ON docs USING HNSW (emb vector_l2_ops) WITH (m = 32, ef_construction = 200); See CREATE INDEX and DROP INDEX for the general index grammar.

sections.heading: 14.4.3. How the index is built and stored
sections.text: When you run CREATE INDEX, the engine scans the table. It decodes the vector column from every live row into a dense in-memory store. Then it builds a multi-layer navigable graph over that store. Each indexed table and column pair is identified by (table_id, index_name). The graph and its vectors live only in process memory. There is no on-disk copy of the graph right now. Search walks the graph when two things are true. The graph must have built without errors. The collection must be large enough to gain from it. The walk is a sub-linear greedy beam walk. Some collections have 2·ef_search rows or fewer. For those collections, or when graph construction hit an allocation failure, the engine falls back on its own to an exact brute-force top-k scan over the dense store. The brute-force path is always exact. The graph path is approximate.

sections.heading: 14.4.4. Querying with HNSW_KNN
sections.text: You run nearest-neighbor lookups against the index through the HNSW_KNN table function. HNSW_KNN ( table_name, index_name, query_vector, k ) table_name, index_nameThe table and the HNSW index to search. query_vectorA vector literal in any accepted form. The accepted forms are […], {…}, and b64i8:. Its dimension must match the indexed column. A mismatch returns no result. kAn integer literal. It is the number of nearest neighbors to return. They come back ordered by ascending distance. Each result row holds the matching row's primary-key value and its distance to the query vector. SELECT * FROM HNSW_KNN( 'docs', 'docs_emb_idx', '[0.10, 0.20, 0.97]', 5 ); The index is not kept up to date or rebuilt on its own. An HNSW graph is a snapshot from the moment you ran CREATE INDEX. Rows you insert, update, or delete after that do not show up until you rebuild the index. This release also does not reload existing HNSW indexes when the server starts. To refresh the graph, run CREATE INDEX again. You can use REINDEX once it is available. This is very different from PostgreSQL indexes. PostgreSQL indexes are kept up to date inside each transaction on every write.

sections.heading: 14.5. Hybrid (Lexical + Vector) Search
sections.text: Hybrid search joins two things in one call. It uses a single equality predicate on a normal column. It also ranks by vector. This is the common "filter then rank by similarity" pattern. For example, you might want the documents most similar to an embedding. But you only want them among rows whose topic = 'storage'. HNSW_FILTER_KNN ( table_name, index_name, query_vector, k, 'column=value[:mode]' ) The fifth argument is a string literal. It has the form column=value. You can add a :mode suffix to pick the execution strategy. If the filter is empty or missing, the call drops to a plain HNSW_KNN. auto (default)The engine measures filter selectivity. Selectivity is matching_rows / total_rows. It uses pre-filter when selectivity is below 10%. Otherwise it uses post-filter. preForce pre-filter. The engine first keeps only rows where column = value. Then it computes distances and takes top-k. This is best when the filter is tight and has few matches. postForce post-filter. The engine ranks all vectors first. Then it drops hits whose column value does not match. It oversamples to make up for the dropped rows. This is best when the filter is loose and matches most rows. -- Most-similar docs, restricted to topic = 'storage', auto strategy SELECT * FROM HNSW_FILTER_KNN( 'docs', 'docs_emb_idx', '[0.10, 0.20, 0.97]', 5, 'topic=storage' ); -- Force the pre-filter strategy SELECT * FROM HNSW_FILTER_KNN( 'docs', 'docs_emb_idx', '[0.10, 0.20, 0.97]', 5, 'topic=storage:pre' ); The filter compares against the column's text value. NULL column values never match. The predicate is a single equality only. Ranges, OR, and multi-column predicates are not part of the hybrid call. For richer filtering, use plain HNSW_KNN with a WHERE clause in a surrounding SELECT.

sections.heading: 14.5.1. Inspecting the chosen strategy
sections.text: There is a diagnostic sibling called HNSW_HYBRID_EXPLAIN. It takes the same arguments as HNSW_FILTER_KNN. Instead of results, it tells you which strategy the planner picked. It also reports the selectivity stats it used. Those stats are the candidate count, the number of filter matches, and the measured selectivity. The candidate count is the total number of rows scanned. HNSW_HYBRID_EXPLAIN ( table_name, index_name, query_vector, k, 'column=value[:mode]' ) SELECT HNSW_HYBRID_EXPLAIN( 'docs', 'docs_emb_idx', '[0.10, 0.20, 0.97]', 5, 'topic=storage' );

sections.heading: 14.6. Reference: error and edge-case behavior
sections.text: SituationBehavior Query vector dimension ≠ indexed dimensionThe query returns no rows. A mismatch can never be satisfied. Vector literal contains NaN or a non-numeric tokenParse error. Declared/inserted dimension > 16384Rejected. Cosine distance with an all-zero operandDefined as 1.0, the maximum distance. It is not a division error. HNSW_KNN with k ≤ 0Error. Empty table at CREATE INDEXBuilds fine with zero vectors. Searches return no rows. Graph build allocation failureFalls back to exact brute-force search without warning. HNSW_FILTER_KNN with empty filter stringDrops to plain HNSW_KNN. NULL value in the hybrid filter columnNever matches the equality predicate.

sections.heading: 14.7. Notes and limitations
sections.text: In-memory only. The HNSW graph and its dense vector store live in process memory. There is no on-disk copy of the graph. Indexes are not reloaded on startup. Rebuild with CREATE INDEX after a restart. Snapshot semantics. The graph reflects the table only as of build time. Later DML is not kept up to date in the index. Re-create the index to pick up changes. Approximate vs. exact. The graph path is an approximate nearest-neighbor search. Small collections with 2·ef_search rows or fewer use an exact brute-force scan instead. Build-failure fallbacks use the same exact scan. Fixed query beam width. ef_search is fixed at 64. It is widened to k when k is larger. There is no per-query SET ef_search yet. One column per HNSW index. An HNSW index covers a single VECTOR(N) column. Single equality in hybrid search. HNSW_FILTER_KNN supports exactly one column = value predicate. It is not a general WHERE engine. Bounded internal capacity. The active HNSW graph registry holds a fixed number of graphs. That number is 64. It holds primary-key keys up to 256 bytes. Very long composite keys are truncated for index bookkeeping. Operator coverage. Only <-> for L2 and <#> for inner product are general operators. Cosine through <=> works only in ORDER BY. L1 has no operator. PostgreSQL and pgvector give a full operator family per metric and push distance operators into the index. EvolutionDB does not do either.

sections.heading: 14.8. See also
sections.text: CREATE TABLE for declaring VECTOR(N) columns CREATE INDEX · DROP INDEX for managing the HNSW index INSERT · SELECT for loading and querying vector data

sections.heading: Introduction
sections.text: 3. Advanced Features EvolutionDB does more than plain tables and queries. It has transactional snapshot isolation. It has several kinds of index. It also has agent-memory and vector tools built right into SQL. This chapter shows what those features do in version 3.0. The earlier chapters covered creating tables and running queries. This chapter is a tutorial for the parts that make EvolutionDB more than a single-file SQL engine. It covers multi-version transactions. It covers the index types you can build on a table. It also covers the vector and memory features made for AI-agent work. Every example below runs against a live server. Connect with the CLI client evosql-cli. You can also use any PostgreSQL wire-protocol client on port 5433. When a behaviour differs from PostgreSQL or the SQL standard, the text says so plainly. About this chapter. The features here are described as they really work today. This includes their version-1 limits. Some parts have caveats written inline. The HNSW vector index and the agent-memory SEARCH path are two examples. Read the "Notes" callouts before you depend on a feature in production.

sections.heading: 3.1. Transactions and MVCC
sections.text: EvolutionDB uses Multi-Version Concurrency Control (MVCC). It does not lock rows for readers. Instead, each changed row stores the transaction that created it and the transaction that deleted it. A query sees one steady snapshot of the database. It skips rows that were not yet committed as of that snapshot. It also skips rows that were already deleted as of that snapshot. This works in the same way PostgreSQL does. The visibility rules are PostgreSQL-style on purpose.

sections.heading: 3.1.1. Transaction IDs and the commit log
sections.text: Every writing transaction gets a 32-bit transaction ID (XID). The number always goes up. The counter is saved in the database file header. So XIDs keep going up even after a restart. Two XIDs are reserved. XID_FROZEN = 1 A marker that means "committed long ago". If a row has a frozen inserting XID, it is always visible. The engine does not need to check the commit log for it. XID_NORMAL_MIN = 2 The first XID that a normal transaction can get. Commit status is kept in a packed on-disk commit log (CLOG). It uses two bits per XID. So each 4 KB page tracks 16,320 transactions. Pages are chained together when one page is full. There are three states. StateValueMeaning CLOG_IN_PROGRESS0The transaction has not committed or aborted yet. CLOG_COMMITTED1The transaction committed. Its rows can now be visible. CLOG_ABORTED2The transaction rolled back. Its rows are never visible. Two fast paths keep reads from touching the CLOG most of the time. First, each row header has hint bits. They are XMIN_COMMITTED and XMIN_ABORTED. They get set once the engine knows what happened to the inserting transaction. Second, a Commit Sequence Number (CSN) cache maps recently committed XIDs to a commit order. It uses a 4096-entry ring buffer. This gives an O(1) check for "did this commit before my snapshot?". The CSN cache also stores each commit's wall-clock time. The temporal query feature below uses this time. It limits how far back you can look.

sections.heading: 3.1.2. Snapshots and isolation levels
sections.text: A snapshot stores three things. It stores the highest XID at the moment it is taken, called xmax. It stores the lowest XID that is still active, called xmin. It also stores the list of transactions that were running at that moment. A row is visible when two things are true. Its inserting transaction committed before the snapshot. And its deleting transaction had not committed as of the snapshot. Up to 256 transactions can run at the same time. This limit is MVCC_MAX_ACTIVE. The session's isolation level controls when the snapshot is taken. Set it with SET TRANSACTION ISOLATION LEVEL. A new connection uses READ COMMITTED by default. READ UNCOMMITTED It is accepted, but it works like READ COMMITTED. EvolutionDB never shows uncommitted rows. READ COMMITTED (default) A new snapshot is taken at the start of each statement. So a transaction can see rows that others committed between its own statements. REPEATABLE READ One snapshot is taken at BEGIN. The same snapshot is reused for every statement in the transaction. So reading the same data again returns the same rows. SERIALIZABLE Visibility works the same as REPEATABLE READ. On top of that, it adds a conflict guard. The guard records predicate and gap locks during scans. This detects write skew. To make this safe, the transaction is serialized against other writers. It holds the engine's parse lock until it commits or rolls back. How this differs from PostgreSQL. PostgreSQL's SERIALIZABLE uses fully optimistic Serializable Snapshot Isolation. It allows a lot of concurrency. It may abort a transaction at commit time. EvolutionDB's SERIALIZABLE is stricter in practice. It holds a writer-exclusive lock the whole time. So it gives up concurrency to get a simpler guarantee. Pick REPEATABLE READ if you want snapshot stability without that serialization cost.

sections.heading: 3.1.3. Running a transaction
sections.text: Transaction control statements are handled at the protocol layer. A transaction starts with BEGIN. You can also use START TRANSACTION. It ends with COMMIT or ROLLBACK. See BEGIN / COMMIT / ROLLBACK for the full reference. SET TRANSACTION ISOLATION LEVEL REPEATABLE READ; BEGIN; INSERT INTO accounts (id, balance) VALUES (1, 100); UPDATE accounts SET balance = balance - 25 WHERE id = 1; SELECT balance FROM accounts WHERE id = 1; -- sees 75 COMMIT; Two behaviours match PostgreSQL on purpose. Implicit rollback on disconnect. If a connection drops in the middle of a transaction, the open transaction is rolled back for you. COMMIT in an aborted transaction. If a statement fails inside a transaction, a later COMMIT acts like a ROLLBACK.

sections.heading: 3.1.4. Savepoints
sections.text: Inside a transaction you can set a savepoint. Then you do more work. Then you can roll back to that point. You do not lose the whole transaction. See SAVEPOINT. BEGIN; INSERT INTO log (msg) VALUES ('step one'); SAVEPOINT sp1; INSERT INTO log (msg) VALUES ('step two'); ROLLBACK TO sp1; -- undoes 'step two' only COMMIT; -- 'step one' is durable

sections.heading: 3.1.5. Temporal queries (time travel)
sections.text: Old row versions stay in the heap until they are cleaned up. Because of this, EvolutionDB can rebuild the database as it looked just after a given transaction committed. The grammar borrows the SQL:2011 keyword FOR SYSTEM_TIME AS OF. But the point you give is a transaction ID, not a timestamp. SELECT * FROM accounts FOR SYSTEM_TIME AS OF TRANSACTION 42; This builds a synthetic snapshot. Its xmax sits one past XID 42. So every transaction up to and including 42 is visible. Anything newer is not visible. The historical view has no active list. It is a clean point-in-time read. It is not a concurrency snapshot. Notes, transactions. Some rows were written before MVCC existed. They have no MVCC flag in the tuple header. These rows are always visible. This keeps older database files working. The active-transaction array has a fixed size of 256 entries. If it fills up, the engine logs a warning. The extra transaction is not tracked. This can make its visibility wrong. Keep concurrency below that limit. "AS OF TRANSACTION" can only reach back as far as the old row versions still exist. The commit timestamp in the CSN ring sets the retention window. If a CSN slot has been evicted, it reports an unknown commit time. The value you give is a transaction ID, not a clock value. There is no AS OF TIMESTAMP form.

sections.heading: 3.2. Indexes
sections.text: EvolutionDB makes a primary-key index for every table on its own. This section covers the secondary indexes you add with CREATE INDEX. A secondary index is a separate structure. It maps an indexed value back to the heap row. So equality and prefix lookups can skip a full table scan.

sections.heading: 3.2.1. Index types
sections.text: The structure that gets built depends on the USING clause. It also depends on the UNIQUE and fulltext modifiers. Inside, each index stores a single-character type code. Type codeStructureCreated by PPrimary-key B+ treeAutomatic, per table (cannot be dropped) NB+ tree (non-unique)CREATE INDEX (default) UB+ tree (unique)CREATE UNIQUE INDEX HHash (non-unique)CREATE INDEX ... USING HASH VHash (unique)CREATE UNIQUE INDEX ... USING HASH FFull-text inverted B+ treeCREATE FULLTEXT INDEX AHNSW vector (ANN), in-memoryCREATE INDEX ... USING HNSW (see §3.3) B+ tree and hash secondary indexes are stored on disk in the unified page file. A composite index key joins the indexed column values with the row's primary key. An internal Unit Separator byte sits between them. Its value is 0x1F. That separator sorts before any printable character. This keeps each row's key unique. It also lets a prefix scan on "value␟" find every row that has that value. -- ordinary B+ tree index CREATE INDEX idx_email ON users (email); -- unique constraint enforced at build and on insert CREATE UNIQUE INDEX uq_sku ON products (sku); -- hash index - equality lookups only CREATE INDEX idx_status ON orders USING HASH (status);

sections.heading: 3.2.2. Composite and expression indexes
sections.text: An index can cover several columns. It can also index the result of an expression. A composite index joins the column values with a | separator in key order. An expression index stores the serialized expression. It re-evaluates the expression for each row at build time and on later writes. -- composite: keyed on (last_name, first_name) CREATE INDEX idx_name ON people (last_name, first_name); -- expression index CREATE INDEX idx_lower_email ON users (LOWER(email)); Limitation. You cannot create an index on an ARRAY column in version 1. Doing so raises error 0A000. This means the feature is not supported.

sections.heading: 3.2.3. Building an index without blocking writers
sections.text: Add CONCURRENTLY to build an index while other sessions keep writing to the table. The build runs in three phases. Phase 1 registers the index in the catalog with a building state. Phase 2 scans the existing rows in batches of 100. It releases the engine lock between batches so other queries can run. At the same time, a modification log records the primary keys of any rows that concurrent DML touches. Phase 3 reconciles that log. It removes stale entries. It inserts current values for each touched key. Only then does it mark the index ready. CREATE INDEX CONCURRENTLY idx_created ON events (created_at); The query planner ignores an index until it is marked ready. So a concurrent build never returns partial results. The batch-unlock design is safe for two reasons. B+ tree splits only move entries to the right. So the in-progress cursor stays valid. Deletes are lazy markers. They do not move entries.

sections.heading: 3.2.4. Full-text indexes
sections.text: A FULLTEXT index breaks a text column into tokens. It stores one (word, primary-key) pair per token in a B+ tree. This supports word-level MATCH search. Notes, full-text. In version 1, a full-text index is not kept up to date on INSERT, UPDATE, or DELETE. The MATCH evaluator rescans and re-tokenizes the heap for each row. So the results stay correct. But the index itself can drift after a lot of DML. Re-create the index after a large load to keep it fresh. To remove any secondary index, use DROP INDEX. The primary-key index has type P. It is protected and cannot be dropped. DROP INDEX idx_email;

sections.heading: 3.3. Vector search with HNSW
sections.text: EvolutionDB has a native VECTOR(N) column type. It also has an approximate-nearest-neighbour (ANN) index. You build it with CREATE INDEX ... USING HNSW. This is the building block for semantic search over embeddings.

sections.heading: 3.3.1. Creating an HNSW index
sections.text: CREATE INDEX name ON table USING HNSW (vector_column) [WITH (m = N, ef_construction = N)] CREATE TABLE docs ( id INT PRIMARY KEY, body TEXT, embedding VECTOR(1024) ); CREATE INDEX idx_doc_vec ON docs USING HNSW (embedding) WITH (m = 16, ef_construction = 64); Two build parameters tune the graph. m (default 16) The number of neighbour links per node. Layer 0 holds up to 2·m links per node. Higher layers hold up to m. ef_construction (default 64) The beam width used while inserting nodes during the build. A larger value builds a better graph, but it is slower. You pick the distance metric with an optional opclass after the column name. If you leave it out, it uses cosine. These metrics are supported. MetricConstantDistance Cosine (default)HNSW_DIST_COSINE = 01 − cos(a, b) Euclidean (L2)HNSW_DIST_L2 = 1‖a − b‖₂ Inner productHNSW_DIST_INNER = 2−(a · b) Manhattan (L1)HNSW_DIST_L1 = 3Σ|aᵢ − bᵢ| At CREATE INDEX time the engine scans the table. It reads each vector's float payload straight from the tuple. It loads each one into a dense per-graph store. Then it builds a multi-layer greedy graph over them. The vector column must be a real VECTOR type. The index supports one vector column.

sections.heading: 3.3.2. Searching
sections.text: Queries call the vector functions directly. You can pass a query vector in two ways. One way is a bracketed list [f1, f2, ...]. The other way is the compact b64i8:<base64> form. This form is a float32 scale followed by int8 values. It keeps a 1024-dimension query literal small enough to fit the statement size cap. -- k-nearest neighbours by the index's configured metric SELECT id, body FROM docs WHERE HNSW_KNN(embedding, idx_doc_vec, '[0.12, 0.07, ...]', 10); For larger datasets the search walks the graph. For small sets it falls back to an exact brute-force top-k scan. It also falls back this way if the graph could not be built. Either way, the results come back in ascending distance order. A search beam width controls the graph walk. It is ef_search and it is 64 by default.

sections.heading: 3.3.3. Hybrid (filtered) search
sections.text: Hybrid search joins an equality filter on a scalar column with vector ranking. The engine picks a strategy on its own. It looks at how selective the filter is. Pre-filter Used when fewer than 10% of rows match. The engine computes distances only for the matching rows. Then it ranks them. Post-filter Used when the filter is loose. The engine ranks all vectors first. Then it drops the hits that do not match. It takes extra candidates to make up for the dropped ones. -- restrict to lang='en', then rank by vector distance SELECT id FROM docs WHERE HNSW_FILTER_KNN(embedding, idx_doc_vec, '[...]', 10, 'lang=en'); -- EXPLAIN-style: report which strategy fired and its selectivity SELECT HNSW_HYBRID_EXPLAIN(embedding, idx_doc_vec, '[...]', 10, 'lang=en'); Notes, HNSW. HNSW graphs live in memory only. The catalog stores the build parameters in the index's metadata. These are m, ef_construction, and distance. At startup the engine rebuilds every HNSW graph from that metadata. If a graph fails to rebuild, it is left empty. Queries then fall back to brute force until you re-create it. An HNSW index covers one VECTOR column. Multi-column ANN indexes are not supported in version 1. Up to 64 HNSW graphs can exist at once across the whole server. This is approximate search. Recall depends on m, ef_construction, and the search beam width. For very small tables the engine uses the exact brute-force path. So results there are exact.

sections.heading: 3.4. Agent memory stores
sections.text: A memory store is a namespace, key, and value store. It can also hold optional vector embeddings. It is made for long-term agent memory. It is a thin, easy layer over the engine. Under the hood, a memory store is a normal table named mem_<store>. So every memory operation gets MVCC, row-level security, triggers, and foreign-key cascade. It works the same way a hand-written query would.

sections.heading: 3.4.1. Creating a store
sections.text: Create a store with CREATE MEMORY STORE. With no options it is a plain JSON key-value store. If you supply embedding_dim, it adds a vector column. This turns on semantic search. -- KV-only store CREATE MEMORY STORE notes; -- store with 1024-d embeddings, cosine distance CREATE MEMORY STORE recall WITH (embedding_dim = 1024, distance = 'cosine'); The backing table has a fixed shape. It has a mem_namespace and a mem_key. It has a composite primary key. The key joins the namespace and key with a Record Separator byte. That byte is 0x1E. It has a JSON mem_value. It has a created_at timestamp and a nullable ttl_at timestamp. It has an embedding vector column too, but only when embedding_dim > 0. The distance option accepts cosine, l2, inner, ip, or l1. The default is cosine.

sections.heading: 3.4.2. Storing and reading memories
sections.text: MEMORY PUT is an upsert. It is keyed on (namespace, key). When there is a collision, the engine replaces the old row and links the primary key to the new one. A failed re-insert can never leave the row missing. The embedding argument is optional. It only matters for stores that were created with an embedding dimension. -- upsert a value (no embedding) MEMORY PUT INTO notes VALUES ('user:42', 'tone', '{"prefers":"concise"}'); -- upsert with an embedding for semantic recall MEMORY PUT INTO recall VALUES ('user:42', 'fact-1', '{"text":"ships to Berlin"}', '[0.01, 0.4, ...]'); -- point lookup by namespace + key MEMORY GET FROM recall WHERE NS = 'user:42' AND KEY = 'fact-1'; -- delete one memory MEMORY DELETE FROM recall WHERE NS = 'user:42' AND KEY = 'fact-1'; -- enumerate namespaces, optionally by prefix MEMORY LIST NAMESPACES IN recall PREFIX 'user:'; MEMORY GET, DELETE, and LIST compile down to ordinary SELECT and DELETE statements against the backing table. So any row-level security policy on that table applies on its own. This is what makes a memory store safe for multi-tenant use. See the per-command pages. MEMORY PUT, MEMORY GET, MEMORY SEARCH, and MEMORY DELETE.

sections.heading: 3.4.3. Semantic search
sections.text: MEMORY SEARCH ranks stored memories by their distance from a query embedding. It uses the store's configured distance metric. It adds a score column. It orders the results so the closest matches come first. MEMORY SEARCH recall USING VECTOR '[0.02, 0.39, ...]' LIMIT 5; If you do not give a LIMIT, the search returns up to 10 results by default. Notes, memory stores. MEMORY SEARCH needs a store created with embedding_dim > 0. Searching a KV-only store raises an error. Search computes the score for each row over the backing table. Then it orders by that score. It does not yet route through the HNSW index. So for very large stores it scales linearly. The result ranking is still correct. ttl_at is stored, and it is NULL by default. But the store layer does not enforce expiry on its own. Treat it as advisory metadata for now. A store is a table. So you can always query mem_<store> directly with standard SQL. Do this when you need joins, aggregates, or filters that the memory verbs do not offer.

sections.heading: 3.5. Where to go next
sections.text: The command pages under Reference give the exact grammar, options, and error codes for each statement used above. Start with CREATE INDEX, BEGIN / COMMIT / ROLLBACK, and MEMORY SEARCH.

sections.heading: Introduction
sections.text: 2. The SQL Language A hands-on tour of the SQL that EvolutionDB speaks. You will create tables, insert rows, and read them back. You will filter rows with WHERE and order results with ORDER BY. Every example here is based on what the engine actually does, not on what the SQL standard allows. This chapter assumes you have a running server. The PostgreSQL wire protocol runs on port 5433. The native EVO protocol runs on port 9967. You also need a session connected to a database. Every example below uses only features the EvolutionDB engine truly supports. When EvolutionDB works differently from PostgreSQL or the SQL standard, the difference is pointed out clearly. Conventions. SQL keywords are shown in upper case so they are easy to read. The EvolutionDB lexer does not care about case for keywords. So SELECT, select, and Select all mean the same thing. Table and column names are also compared without regard to case. The engine matches columns with strcasecmp.

sections.heading: 2.1. Tables and the Storage Model
sections.text: An EvolutionDB table is an ordered set of typed columns. Every table lives inside one page-based file. The file is named evosql.db and uses 4 KB pages. The table definition is kept in the system catalog. The catalog stores the name, the columns, the types, and the constraints. Rows are stored as binary tuples in a heap. A B+ tree on the primary key indexes that heap. Two limits are worth knowing up front. They are hard caps in the engine, not soft advice. Maximum columns per table The limit is set by CAT_MAX_COLUMNS. CREATE TABLE rejects any table that has more columns than this. The same limit also covers inherited columns and system-versioning shadow columns. Record size One row's value buffer must fit within RECORD_BUF_SIZE. If the row's text is too big, INSERT raises a data_exception. The check happens before the tuple is built.

sections.heading: 2.2. Creating a New Table
sections.text: You create a table with the CREATE TABLE command. You name each column and give it a data type. CREATE TABLE employees ( id INTEGER PRIMARY KEY, name VARCHAR(50) NOT NULL, dept VARCHAR(30), salary INTEGER, active BOOLEAN DEFAULT true ); When the command works, the engine reports command(s) completed successfully!... Whitespace and line breaks do not matter. The statement ends with a semicolon.

sections.heading: 2.2.1. Data Types
sections.text: EvolutionDB stores each column type as a single integer. The part typeVal / 10000 is the type family. The part typeVal % 10000 is the size for that family. For a VARCHAR, the size is the maximum length. The grammar assigns these codes directly. The table below lists the families you will use in everyday tutorial SQL. SQL writtenFamily baseStored asNotes SMALLINT20000integerThe value is checked as a base-10 integer. INT / INTEGER / INT440000 / 50000integerThe plain keyword has no length. Storage uses a default width. BIGINT / INT860000int64 little-endianThe value is checked as an integer. REAL / DOUBLE / FLOAT70000 / 80000 / 90000numericThe value is checked with strtod. DECIMAL110000stringThe value is checked as numeric. CHAR(n)120000 + nlength-prefixed stringThe length is checked against n. VARCHAR(n)130000 + nlength-prefixed stringA value longer than n raises string_data_right_truncation (22001). DATE / TIME / TIMESTAMP100001 / 100002 / 100003stringThe format is not checked at insert time. BOOLEAN / BOOL220001booleanIt accepts true, false, 0, and 1. The words are case-sensitive. UUID18003616-byte valueIt must be a 36-char hyphenated hex UUID. JSON230000stringThe value is checked as well-formed JSON. Honest contrast with PostgreSQL. EvolutionDB checks the type of the text value at insert time. These checks are narrow on purpose. The integer and float families check that the text reads as a number. VARCHAR(n) and CHAR(n) check length. BOOLEAN, UUID, and JSON have format checks. The DATE, TIME, TIMESTAMP, TEXT, and BLOB families are accepted without any value check. PostgreSQL rejects a bad timestamp. EvolutionDB stores it as given. Do not count on the engine to reject a bad date.

sections.heading: 2.2.2. Column Constraints and Defaults
sections.text: A column definition can carry inline constraints. These are the ones used on the INSERT path. PRIMARY KEY This marks the key column or columns. EvolutionDB builds a B+ tree on the primary key. It rejects duplicate keys with a unique violation. A table-level PRIMARY KEY (a, b) makes a composite key. You must give a value for primary key columns. NOT NULL An INSERT that leaves the column empty or sets it to NULL raises not_null_violation (23502). UNIQUE The engine checks uniqueness by scanning the existing rows. A duplicate non-NULL value raises unique_violation (23505). Two NULL values do not clash with each other. DEFAULT value The default is used when you leave a column out of a column-list INSERT. Surrounding quotes are stripped from string literals. A few function defaults are run at insert time. These include CURRENT_TIMESTAMP and gen_random_uuid(). CHECK (expr) The expression is tested against each new row. A false result raises check_violation (23514). See CREATE TABLE for the full set of constraints. That set includes foreign keys, composite uniqueness, generated columns, and system versioning.

sections.heading: 2.3. Populating a Table With Rows
sections.text: Use INSERT to add rows. In the simplest form you give a value for every column. The values follow the order the columns were declared in. INSERT INTO employees VALUES (1, 'Ada Lovelace', 'Engineering', 9200, true); String literals use single quotes. Booleans accept true or false, and also 0 or 1. The number of values you give must match the number of columns. If it does not, the engine raises an error and reports both numbers. The error reads INSERT has N value(s) but table has M column(s).

sections.heading: 2.3.1. Naming Columns Explicitly
sections.text: You can name the target columns in any order and leave out the rest. A column you leave out takes its DEFAULT. If it has no default, it takes NULL. INSERT INTO employees (id, name) VALUES (2, 'Grace Hopper'); Here dept and salary become NULL. The column active takes its declared default of true. EvolutionDB puts the values back into the table's physical column order. It checks that every named column exists. It rejects any write that targets a generated column. If the number of values does not match the number of named columns, the insert fails with INSERT has N value(s) but N column(s) specified.

sections.heading: 2.3.2. Inserting Several Rows at Once
sections.text: A single INSERT can carry several value groups. You separate the groups with commas. INSERT INTO employees VALUES (3, 'Alan Turing', 'Research', 8800, true), (4, 'Edsger Dijkstra', 'Research', 8800, false), (5, 'Barbara Liskov', 'Engineering',9500, true); Each row is checked and stored on its own. Every value is checked against its column's type. See §2.2.1 for types. The value is also checked against the NOT NULL, UNIQUE, and CHECK constraints. All of these checks run first. Then the binary tuple is written to the heap and the primary key is added to the B+ tree. Note on control bytes. Inside the engine, EvolutionDB separates row data with control bytes. One byte marks the end of a field. Another byte marks the end of a row. A crafted value could break these field boundaries. To stop that, the engine removes those bytes from your values during insert. For normal text you never see this. But it means you cannot store those exact control characters.

sections.heading: 2.4. Querying a Table
sections.text: The SELECT command reads rows. To read a whole table, select all columns with *. SELECT * FROM employees; The engine looks up the table's columns in the catalog. It prints a header row of column names. Below that it prints a separator line. Then it scans the heap and decodes each binary tuple back into its fields. A column whose stored value is NULL is shown as the word NULL.

sections.heading: 2.4.1. Looking Up a Single Row by Key
sections.text: When a query asks for a row by its primary key, EvolutionDB answers straight from the B+ tree. The heap is not scanned. The engine searches the index for the key and reads exactly that one tuple. The lookup is O(log n). A query on other columns falls back to a full scan.

sections.heading: 2.5. Filtering Rows With WHERE
sections.text: A WHERE clause keeps only the rows where a condition is true. The condition is tested one row at a time. It runs against that row's decoded column values. SELECT name, salary FROM employees WHERE dept = 'Research'; A condition can mix column names, literals, and comparison operators. It can also use the boolean words AND, OR, and NOT. SELECT * FROM employees WHERE salary >= 9000 AND active = true; The engine compares numerically when both sides read as numbers. Otherwise it compares as text, character by character. This matters for mixed data. If a value is compared as text, it matches and sorts by character order, not by number value. Numeric comparison only happens when both sides are valid numbers. Honest contrast. EvolutionDB runs WHERE on the text form of each row. There is no cost-based optimizer that picks among many indexes. The primary key B+ tree is used for direct key lookups. All other conditions are handled by scanning. Think of WHERE as a row filter over a scan. The one exception is when you pin the primary key to a single value.

sections.heading: 2.6. Sorting Results With ORDER BY
sections.text: By default, rows come back in heap-scan order. Treat that order as undefined. To set an order, add ORDER BY with a column name. SELECT name, salary FROM employees ORDER BY salary; The default direction is ascending. Add DESC for descending order. Add ASC if you want to state ascending order explicitly. SELECT name, salary FROM employees ORDER BY salary DESC; The sort uses the same rule as the WHERE comparison. If both values in a pair read cleanly as numbers, the sort is by number. Otherwise it sorts as text. So an INTEGER column sorts 2 < 10 as you expect. A VARCHAR column that holds "10" and "2" sorts "10" before "2". Note on the 500-row sort cap. The SELECT * path loads rows into a fixed in-memory buffer before it sorts them. That buffer holds at most 500 rows. If you use ORDER BY on a table with more than 500 rows on this path, the engine collects and sorts only the first 500 rows it scans. The rest are left out of the sorted output. This is an engine limit, not standard SQL behavior. PostgreSQL would sort the whole result set. If a column named in ORDER BY does not exist, the engine reports the error and falls back to an unordered scan instead of stopping.

sections.heading: 2.7. Putting It Together
sections.text: Here is a full tutorial session. It creates a table, adds rows, filters them, and orders them. -- 1. Define the table CREATE TABLE employees ( id INTEGER PRIMARY KEY, name VARCHAR(50) NOT NULL, dept VARCHAR(30), salary INTEGER ); -- 2. Add rows INSERT INTO employees VALUES (1, 'Ada Lovelace', 'Engineering', 9200), (2, 'Alan Turing', 'Research', 8800), (3, 'Barbara Liskov','Engineering', 9500); -- 3. Filter and order SELECT name, salary FROM employees WHERE dept = 'Engineering' ORDER BY salary DESC; The last query returns Barbara Liskov with 9500 before Ada Lovelace with 9200. Alan Turing is left out because his department does not match the filter.

sections.heading: 2.8. Common Errors in This Chapter
